*(Alternative valid remedies: install acoustic diffusers; use heavy drapery; break parallelism between opposite walls.)*

**Marks Distribution:**
- (1) Sub-step 1 + 2: 1 mark
- (2) Sub-step 2 (calculation): 1 mark
- (3) Sub-step 3 (comparison & conclusion): 1 mark
- (4) Numerical echo confirmation: 1 mark
- (5) Two remedies: 2 marks
- (6) Neat sketch with ray diagram: 1 mark

---

### Question B (Choice 2): 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the formation of an echo in a rectangular hall. State the conditions necessary for an echo to be perceived. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A speaker in a hall produces a sound intensity level of 75 dB at a distance of 2 m. Calculate:
   1. The sound intensity level at 20 m from the speaker.
   2. Whether an echo will be perceived if the reflecting wall is at 20 m.
   3. State three causes of echo formation in buildings. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

### Part (a) — Sketch & Conditions (7 marks)

**Neat Sketch (3 marks):** (Refer to Section 4.4 schematic above. Must include: source S, rear wall, incident ray, reflected ray, distance d, formula Δt = 2d/v, label for echo threshold.)

**Conditions for echo to be perceived (4 marks):**

1. **Hard reflecting surface:** The surface must have a low Sound Absorption Coefficient (α < 0.05) so that most of the sound energy is reflected back, not absorbed. Examples: bare concrete, marble, glass. **(1 mark)**

2. **Sufficient distance:** The reflecting surface must be at least 11.3 m away from the source/listener so that the round-trip travel time exceeds 1/15 second. **(1 mark)**

3. **Audibility of reflected sound:** The intensity of the reflected sound at the listener's position must exceed the background noise and the threshold of hearing. **(1 mark)**

4. **No obstruction in path:** The direct and reflected sound paths must be clear so that the listener can hear both. **(1 mark)**

---

### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Sound intensity level at $r_1 = 2 \text{ m}$: $L_1 = 75 \text{ dB}$
- Required: SIL at $r_2 = 20 \text{ m}$ and echo check for wall at 20 m.

**Step 1: Apply the Inverse Square Law in dB form**

$$L_2 = L_1 - 20 \log_{10}\left( \frac{r_2}{r_1} \right)$$

$$L_2 = 75 - 20 \log_{10}\left( \frac{20}{2} \right)$$

$$L_2 = 75 - 20 \log_{10}(10)$$

$$L_2 = 75 - 20 \times 1$$

$$\boxed{L_2 = 55 \text{ dB}} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 2: Echo check for wall at 20 m**

$$\Delta t = \frac{2d}{v} = \frac{2 \times 20}{340} = \frac{40}{340} = 0.1176 \text{ s}$$

Since $0.1176 \text{ s} \gg \dfrac{1}{15} \text{ s} = 0.0667 \text{ s}$:

$$\boxed{\text{An echo WILL be perceived.}} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 3: Three causes of echo formation (3 marks)**

1. **Large, hard, parallel reflecting surfaces** at sufficient distance (e.g., rear wall of a long hall).
2. **Distant flat surfaces** such as domed or vaulted ceilings in churches and auditoriums.
3. **Extensive use of low-absorption materials** like polished stone, glass, or bare concrete on large wall/floor areas without acoustic treatment.

*(Valid alternative answers: parallel bare walls, high ceilings, large glazed facades, lack of absorbent furniture in a large room.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 KTU Board Exam — Rapid Revision Checklist: Acoustical Defects – Echoes**

- ✅ **Echo Definition:** A *single, distinct* repetition of sound arriving at the listener after $\Delta t \geq 1/15$ s.
- ✅ **Echo Threshold (Haas Threshold):** $\Delta t_{\min} = \dfrac{1}{15} \text{ s} \approx 0.0667 \text{ s}$.
- ✅ **Speed of Sound (use in problems):** $v = 340 \text{ m/s}$ (or 343 m/s for higher precision).
- ✅ **Core Formula:** $\Delta t = \dfrac{2d}{v}$ — used to find time gap *or* distance.
- ✅ **Minimum Echo Distance:** $d_{\min} = 11.3 \text{ m}$ (memorize this number).
- ✅ **Inverse Square Law:** Intensity falls as $1/r^2$; in dB, sound drops **6 dB per doubling of distance**.
- ✅ **Three Conditions for Echo:** Hard surface + Sufficient distance (≥ 11.3 m) + Audibility.
- ✅ **Three Main Remedies:** (1) Absorption (acoustic panels, curtains, mineral wool), (2) Diffusion (diffusers, splayed walls), (3) Geometry (tilted surfaces, break parallelism).
- ✅ **Common Mistake:** Don't confuse $d$ (one-way) with $2d$ (round-trip). The 1/15 s threshold applies to **round-trip time**.
- ✅ **Distinguish from Reverberation:** Echo = single reflection, ≥ 1/15 s delay. Reverberation = multiple reflections, continuous decay.
- ✅ **Distinguish from Resonance:** Resonance = standing wave amplification at room's natural frequency; not a reflected sound.
- ✅ **Standard Board-Exam Sketch:** Always draw — speaker S, reflecting wall, incident ray (d), reflected ray (d), label "Δt = 2d/v".
- ✅ **For 14-mark problems:** Always conclude with practical remedies (at least 2) to score full marks on the "application" component of the question.
- ✅ **Acoustic Material Reference:** Memorize α values — bare concrete (0.02), glass (0.03), acoustic tile (0.70), heavy curtain (0.60), carpet (0.30).
- ✅ **Rule of Thumb for dB:** $L_2 = L_1 - 20 \log_{10}(r_2/r_1)$ — used in nearly every numerical.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reverberation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET834 - FUNDAMENTALS OF BUILDING PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Reverberation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Reverberation** is the persistence of sound in an enclosed space (a room, hall, or auditorium) *after* the original sound source has ceased to produce sound. It occurs because the sound waves emitted by a source undergo multiple reflections off the hard interior surfaces — walls, ceiling, and floor — before their acoustic energy is fully dissipated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reverberation Time (RT or T₆₀):** As per **Wallace Clement Sabine** (the founder of architectural acoustics), the **Reverberation Time** is defined as the time required (in seconds) for the sound pressure level (SPL) to decay by **60 dB** after the sound source is abruptly switched off. It is the single most important numerical parameter used in the acoustic design of any building.

The standard symbol is **T** (in **seconds**), and the standard frequency of measurement is **500 Hz** or **1000 Hz** (mid-band speech frequencies).

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you drop a single **marble (sound source)** into a calm, empty **swimming pool (room)**:
- If the pool has smooth, hard tiled walls → ripples (sound) bounce back and forth for a *long time* before dying out. This is **HIGH reverberation** (echoey, "boomy" hall).
- If the pool is filled with floating foam mats and thick curtains → ripples die out almost instantly. This is **LOW reverberation** (dry, "dead" hall).

The **engineer's job** in building planning is to choose the wall/ceiling/floor materials so that the reverberation time is *just right* — not too long (speech becomes muddy) and not too short (speech sounds thin and lifeless).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is Reverberation part of "Building Planning"?** Because the geometric volume of a hall, the choice of wall finishes (plaster, wood, carpet, acoustic tiles), and the seating arrangement *all directly* determine the acoustic quality. A planning engineer MUST co-design the room's acoustics along with its layout, lighting, and ventilation.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Terminology

| Term | Symbol | Unit | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reverberation Time | **T** | **seconds (s)** | Time for 60 dB decay |
| Volume of Hall | **V** | m³ | Total enclosed air space |
| Surface Area | **S** | m² | Area of a reflecting surface |
| Absorption Coefficient | **α (alpha)** | dimensionless (0 to 1) | Fraction of sound energy absorbed |
| Total Absorption | **A** | **sabins** (or m² sabin) | Σ Sᵢαᵢ |
| Sound Pressure Level | **SPL** | dB | Loudness measure |
| Sabine's Constant (for air at 20°C) | **0.161** | s/m | Conversion constant in SI units |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Reverberation — Step-by-Step Logic

When a sound source stops, the sound energy inside a room does **not** vanish instantly. It undergoes a chain of physical events:

1. **Sound Emission:** The source radiates acoustic energy into the room's air.
2. **Reflection:** The spherical sound waves strike the hard boundaries (walls/ceiling/floor) and a portion of the energy bounces back into the room.
3. **Partial Absorption:** At every reflection, the boundary material *absorbs* a fraction (α) of the incident energy and *reflects* the remainder (1 − α).
4. **Multi-Path Decay:** The reflected waves strike *other* surfaces, lose more energy, and continue bouncing. After each reflection, the residual energy shrinks geometrically.
5. **60 dB Threshold:** Sabine defined RT as the time taken for the cumulative decay to reach **one-millionth (10⁻⁶)** of the original intensity — equivalent to a 60 dB drop in SPL — because below this level the sound is **inaudible to the human ear**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULAS FOR KTU EXAMS** — Memorize the table below verbatim.

| # | Formula Name | Equation (SI Units) | Equation (FPS / CGS Units) | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Sabine's Formula** (Most Important) | $$T = \frac{0.161 \, V}{A}$$ | $$T = \frac{0.05 \, V}{A}$$ | T = seconds, V = m³, A = sabins |
| 2 | **Total Absorption (Absorbency)** | $$A = \sum_{i=1}^{n} S_i \, \alpha_i$$ | Same | Sᵢ in m², αᵢ dimensionless |
| 3 | **Equivalent Absorption Area** (per surface) | $$a = S \times \alpha$$ | Same | "Sabine" of that surface |
| 4 | **Eyring's Formula** (more accurate for low α) | $$T = \frac{0.161 \, V}{-S \ln(1 - \bar{\alpha})}$$ | $$T = \frac{0.05 \, V}{-S \ln(1 - \bar{\alpha})}$$ | S = total surface area, ᾱ = avg absorption coeff. |
| 5 | **Average Absorption Coefficient** | $$\bar{\alpha} = \frac{\sum S_i \alpha_i}{\sum S_i} = \frac{A}{S}$$ | Same | Used in Eyring's |
| 6 | **Reverberant Sound Level (Steady-State)** | $$L_p = L_w + 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4}{A}\right)$$ | — | Lₚ = SPL (dB), Lw = Sound Power (dB) |
| 7 | **Sabine's Air Absorption Correction** | $$T = \frac{0.161 \, V}{A + 4mV}$$ | — | m = attenuation coefficient (Np/m), V in m³ |
| 8 | **Volume of Common Shapes** | Box: V = L × B × H ; Dome: V = (2/3)πr²H | — | L, B, H in m |

> [!WARNING]
> **Unit Trap for KTU Exams:** The constant is **0.161** if V is in **m³** and A is in **m² sabins** (SI). It becomes **0.05** if V is in **ft³** and A is in **ft² sabins** (FPS). Always check the question's units first!

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Parameter

- **Numerator 0.161 V:** This represents the *acoustic energy storage capacity* of the room. Larger rooms store more sound energy → longer reverberation.
- **Denominator A (Total Absorption):** Represents the room's *ability to drain away* sound energy. More absorption (carpets, acoustic tiles, audience) → shorter reverberation.
- **α (Absorption Coefficient):** A material property. α = 0 means a perfect reflector (polished marble); α = 1 means a perfect absorber (open window). Most building materials lie between **0.02 and 0.80**.
- **Audience as Absorbers:** People are excellent sound absorbers. A seated audience contributes roughly **4.5 sabins per person** at mid-frequencies. This is why an *empty* auditorium sounds dramatically more reverberant than a *full* one.

## 2.4 Optimum Reverberation Time (For Design Practice)

| Type of Hall / Building | Optimum T (seconds) at 500 Hz |
|---|---|
| Lecture rooms, classrooms | **0.4 – 0.6 s** |
| Drama theatres, conference halls | **0.8 – 1.0 s** |
| Concert halls (classical music) | **1.5 – 2.0 s** |
| Cinema halls | **0.8 – 1.0 s** |
| Churches (with organ) | **2.0 – 3.0 s** |
| Auditoriums (multi-purpose) | **1.0 – 1.4 s** |

> [!NOTE]
> The optimum reverberation time **increases with room volume** up to a limit. A simple empirical rule: **T_optimum (s) ≈ 0.3 × ∛V (m³)** (this is a planning guideline, not a hard law).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hospital Operating Theatres:** Very low RT (~0.3 s) so surgeons hear commands clearly.
- **Recording Studios:** Very low RT to avoid coloration of recorded tracks.
- **Concert Halls:** High RT for symphonic "bloom" and richness.
- **Open-Plan Offices:** Low RT to preserve speech privacy.
- **Swimming Pools:** Special corrosion-resistant acoustic panels needed.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Sabine's Formula (Energy Decay Model)

Sabine built his theory on the assumption that in a *diffuse* sound field, the **rate of decay of sound energy** is **proportional** to the **average energy density** remaining in the room at any instant.

**Step 1 — Define the Energy Decay Rate**

Let $E(t)$ be the total sound energy in the room at time $t$ (after the source is switched off at $t=0$). The decay follows a first-order differential equation:

$$\frac{dE}{dt} = -k \, E$$

where $k$ is the decay constant (units: s⁻¹).

**Step 2 — Solve the Differential Equation**

Separating variables and integrating from $E_0$ (initial energy at $t=0$) to $E$ (energy at time $t$):

$$\int_{E_0}^{E} \frac{dE}{E} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{E}{E_0}\right) = -k t$$

$$E(t) = E_0 \, e^{-kt}$$

**Step 3 — Express $k$ in Terms of Room Properties**

The decay constant $k$ depends on how fast sound is absorbed. Sabine found it is proportional to the **total absorption A** and inversely proportional to the **room volume V**:

$$k = \frac{c \, A}{4V}$$

where $c$ = speed of sound in air (≈ 343 m/s at 20°C). The factor **4** arises from the geometry of diffuse reflection (statistical energy average over a sphere).

**Step 4 — Apply the 60 dB Definition**

By definition, RT = T = time for intensity to drop by a factor of $10^6$ (since $10 \log_{10}(10^{-6}) = -60$ dB).

$$\frac{E(T)}{E_0} = 10^{-6} = e^{-kT}$$

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$-kT = \ln(10^{-6}) = -6 \ln(10)$$

$$T = \frac{6 \ln(10)}{k}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute k and Simplify**

$$T = \frac{6 \ln(10) \cdot 4V}{c \, A} = \frac{24 \ln(10) \cdot V}{c \, A}$$

Now plug in $c = 343$ m/s and $\ln(10) \approx 2.3026$:

$$T = \frac{24 \times 2.3026}{343} \cdot \frac{V}{A} = \frac{55.26}{343} \cdot \frac{V}{A} \approx 0.161 \cdot \frac{V}{A}$$

Therefore, the final canonical form is:

$$\boxed{\,T = \frac{0.161 \, V}{A}\,}$$

This is **Sabine's Reverberation Formula** — the foundational equation of architectural acoustics.

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (Standard KTU Pattern)

> **[Example — KTU Board Exam Style]**
> A lecture hall measures **20 m × 15 m × 6 m** (L × B × H). The walls are plastered (α = 0.03), the ceiling is acoustic tile (α = 0.55), and the floor is wooden (α = 0.09). There is a wooden door of area 4 m² (α = 0.10) and a glass window of area 6 m² (α = 0.18). The hall seats **80 students** (each contributing 4.5 sabins). Calculate the **reverberation time** when the hall is fully occupied.

### **Solution — Step-by-Step**

**Step 1 — Compute the Volume V**

$$V = L \times B \times H = 20 \times 15 \times 6 = 1800 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Individual Surface Areas**

- Two long walls (L × H): $2 \times (20 \times 6) = 240$ m²
- Two short walls (B × H): $2 \times (15 \times 6) = 180$ m²
- Ceiling area: $20 \times 15 = 300$ m²
- Floor area: $20 \times 15 = 300$ m²
- Door area: $4$ m² (subtract from wall total — but here we will treat it as a separate item and subtract its area from the wall it replaces)
- Window area: $6$ m² (same treatment)

**Plastered Wall Area (net of door and window):**

$$S_{\text{wall,net}} = (240 + 180) - 4 - 6 = 410 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Compute Absorption A = Σ Sᵢ αᵢ**

| Surface | Area Sᵢ (m²) | αᵢ | Absorption Sᵢαᵢ (sabins) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Plastered walls (net) | 410 | 0.03 | $410 \times 0.03 = 12.30$ |
| Acoustic tile ceiling | 300 | 0.55 | $300 \times 0.55 = 165.00$ |
| Wooden floor | 300 | 0.09 | $300 \times 0.09 = 27.00$ |
| Wooden door | 4 | 0.10 | $4 \times 0.10 = 0.40$ |
| Glass window | 6 | 0.18 | $6 \times 0.18 = 1.08$ |
| Seated audience (80 × 4.5) | — | — | $360.00$ |
| **Total A** | | | **565.78 sabins** |

**Step 4 — Apply Sabine's Formula**

$$T = \frac{0.161 \, V}{A} = \frac{0.161 \times 1800}{565.78} = \frac{289.80}{565.78}$$

$$\boxed{\,T \approx 0.512 \text{ seconds}\,}$$

**Step 5 — Verify Against Optimum**

For a lecture room of 1800 m³, the optimum T is roughly **0.4 – 0.6 s** at 500 Hz. Our computed value of **0.51 s** is comfortably within the desirable range. ✓

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Methods

| Aspect | Sabine's Formula | Eyring's Formula |
|---|---|---|
| **Best Suited For** | Rooms with α < 0.20 (typical) | Rooms with very high α (heavily treated) |
| **Assumes** | Sound uniformly distributed | Mean free path is well-defined |
| **Equation** | $T = 0.161 V / A$ | $T = 0.161 V / [-S \ln(1-\bar{\alpha})]$ |
| **Accuracy** | Good for ordinary halls | Better for studios, anechoic chambers |
| **KTU Exam Frequency** | ★★★★★ (Asked in nearly every paper) | ★★★☆☆ (Asked as a sub-part) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mathematical Tie-In:** When $\bar{\alpha}$ is small, the Taylor expansion gives $-\ln(1-\bar{\alpha}) \approx \bar{\alpha} + \bar{\alpha}^2/2 + \ldots \approx \bar{\alpha}$. Since $\bar{\alpha} = A/S$, Eyring's formula **reduces to Sabine's** for low-absorption rooms. This is why Sabine's works for almost all real buildings.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Reverberation Process — Conceptual Flow Diagram

The following block diagram maps the **energy flow path** of sound inside an enclosed room, showing the iterative reflection–absorption cycle that defines reverberation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["SOURCE EMITS SOUND ENERGY E0"] --> B["SOUND PROPAGATES THROUGH AIR VOLUME V"]
    B --> C["SOUND STRIKES SURFACE AREA S"]
    C --> D{"ABSORPTION COEFFICIENT ALPHA"}
    D -- "FRACTION ALPHA ABSORBED" --> E["ACOUSTIC ENERGY DRAINED FROM ROOM"]
    D -- "FRACTION 1 MINUS ALPHA REFLECTED" --> F["SOUND BOUNCES BACK INTO ROOM VOLUME"]
    F --> C
    E --> G["REMAINING ENERGY E T DECREASES"]
    G --> H{"ENERGY DECAYED BY 60 DB"}
    H -- "NO LOOP CONTINUES" --> I["REVERBERATION TIME T = 0.161 V BY A"]
    H -- "YES LOOP STOPS" --> J["SOUND IS INAUDIBLE TO HUMAN EAR"]
```

## 4.2 Reverberation Time Decision Matrix — Block Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the **designer's decision logic** when a building planner selects materials to achieve a target reverberation time.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K["START PLANNING A HALL"] --> L["DETERMINE HALL USE LECTURE CONCERT CINEMA"]
    L --> M["SELECT TARGET REVERBERATION TIME T TARGET"]
    M --> N["COMPUTE HALL VOLUME V L TIMES B TIMES H"]
    N --> O["LIST ALL INTERIOR SURFACES AND MATERIALS"]
    O --> P["LOOK UP ALPHA VALUE FOR EACH MATERIAL AT 500 HZ"]
    P --> Q["COMPUTE TOTAL ABSORPTION A EQUALS SUM S SUB I ALPHA SUB I"]
    Q --> R["APPLY SABINE FORMULA T EQUALS 0.161 V BY A"]
    R --> S{"T EQUALS T TARGET WITHIN PLUS MINUS 10 PERCENT"}
    S -- "NO" --> T["ADD OR REMOVE ABSORPTIVE MATERIALS CARPETS CURTAINS ACOUSTIC TILES"]
    T --> Q
    S -- "YES" --> U["ACOUSTIC DESIGN FINALIZED T APPROVED"]
```

## 4.3 Frequency-Dependent Absorption Spectrum (Schematic Block)

Real-world absorption coefficients vary **strongly with frequency**. A building planner must check that the hall sounds balanced at low, mid, and high frequencies.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1["LOW FREQUENCY 125 HZ"] --> G1["MATERIAL A ALPHA APPROX 0.05 THIN"]
    F2["MID FREQUENCY 500 HZ"] --> G2["MATERIAL A ALPHA APPROX 0.25 STANDARD"]
    F3["HIGH FREQUENCY 2000 HZ"] --> G3["MATERIAL A ALPHA APPROX 0.60 HIGH"]
    G1 --> H["REVERBERATION TIME T LOW IS LONG USUALLY"]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Implication:** Bass frequencies (125–250 Hz) are always the *hardest* to absorb because acoustic waves are long (wavelengths of 1–3 m). This is why thick, porous, resonant absorbers (e.g., 100 mm mineral wool panels) are needed in concert halls.

## 4.4 Schematic of a Typical Reverberation Problem Input-Output

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INPUT["INPUTS V S SUB I ALPHA SUB I N PERSONS PER PERSON SABINS"] --> ENGINE["SABINE ENGINE T EQUALS 0.161 V BY A"]
    ENGINE --> OUTPUT["OUTPUT REVERBERATION TIME T IN SECONDS"]
    OUTPUT --> DECISION{"IS T WITHIN OPTIMUM RANGE"}
    DECISION -- "YES" --> OK["HALL ACOUSTICALLY SUITABLE"]
    DECISION -- "NO" --> FIX["REVISE MATERIALS OR GEOMETRY"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[Question A1] [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> **Define the term "Reverberation". What is meant by "Reverberation Time"?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Reverberation is the **persistence of sound** in an enclosed space (such as a room, hall, or auditorium) caused by the **multiple reflections** of sound waves from the interior surfaces (walls, ceiling, and floor) after the original sound source has stopped.

**Reverberation Time (T)** is defined by **W.C. Sabine** as the time, measured in **seconds**, required for the **sound intensity** (or sound pressure level) inside a room to fall to **one-millionth (10⁻⁶) of its original value** — which corresponds to a decay of **60 dB** — after the sound source is abruptly switched off. The standard measurement frequency is **500 Hz**. *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the 60 dB criterion, 1 mark for Sabine's name and 500 Hz.)*

---

> **[Question A2] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **State Sabine's formula for reverberation time. Explain the significance of each term.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Sabine's formula is:

$$T = \frac{0.161 \, V}{A}$$

where:
- **T** = Reverberation Time in **seconds**.
- **V** = Volume of the room in **cubic metres (m³)**.
- **A** = Total absorption of the room in **sabins (or m² sabin)**, given by $A = \Sigma S_i \alpha_i$.
- **0.161** is a constant derived from the speed of sound (343 m/s) and $\ln(10)$.

The formula indicates that **reverberation time is directly proportional to the volume** (bigger halls ring longer) and **inversely proportional to the total absorption** (more absorptive surfaces = shorter reverberation). *(1 mark formula, 1 mark for each term's significance.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

> **(a)** A conference hall has dimensions **25 m × 18 m × 7 m (L × B × H)**. The internal finishes are:
> - Plastered ceiling (α = 0.04)
> - Carpet floor (α = 0.30)
> - Plastered walls (α = 0.03)
> - 6 wooden doors, each 2 m × 1 m (α = 0.10)
> - 8 glass windows, each 2 m × 1.5 m (α = 0.18)
> - 100 cushioned chairs (each chair = 2.0 sabins)
>
> Calculate the **reverberation time** of the hall when fully occupied. Is it suitable for a conference?
> **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Volume**

$$V = 25 \times 18 \times 7 = 3150 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Surface Areas**

- Ceiling: $25 \times 18 = 450$ m²
- Floor: $25 \times 18 = 450$ m²
- Two long walls: $2 \times (25 \times 7) = 350$ m²
- Two short walls: $2 \times (18 \times 7) = 252$ m²
- Total gross wall area: $350 + 252 = 602$ m²
- Doors total area: $6 \times (2 \times 1) = 12$ m²
- Windows total area: $8 \times (2 \times 1.5) = 24$ m²
- Net plastered wall area: $602 - 12 - 24 = 566$ m²

**Step 3 — Total Absorption A**

| Surface | Area (m²) | α | Sα (sabins) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Plastered ceiling | 450 | 0.04 | 18.00 |
| Carpet floor | 450 | 0.30 | 135.00 |
| Plastered walls (net) | 566 | 0.03 | 16.98 |
| Wooden doors | 12 | 0.10 | 1.20 |
| Glass windows | 24 | 0.18 | 4.32 |
| Cushioned chairs | — | — | $100 \times 2.0 = 200.00$ |
| **TOTAL A** | | | **375.50 sabins** |

**Step 4 — Apply Sabine's Formula**

$$T = \frac{0.161 \times 3150}{375.50} = \frac{507.15}{375.50} = 1.351 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 5 — Verdict**

For a conference hall, optimum T is **0.8 – 1.0 s**. Our computed T = 1.35 s is **slightly long**, indicating the hall is mildly over-reverberant. **Recommendation:** Add more absorptive material (e.g., acoustic ceiling baffles or wall panels) to bring T down by ~0.35 s. *(All 7 marks: 1 for V, 1 for areas, 2 for A-table, 2 for T calc, 1 for verdict.)*

---

> **(b)** List and explain **any four factors affecting reverberation time** of a hall. How does the presence of an audience influence the reverberation time? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Four factors affecting Reverberation Time:**

1. **Volume of the Hall (V):** RT is directly proportional to V. Doubling the room volume doubles the RT (if absorption is unchanged). Larger halls require careful acoustic design to avoid excessive ringing. *(1.5 marks)*

2. **Total Absorption (A):** RT is inversely proportional to A. Adding absorptive materials (acoustic tiles, carpets, curtains, upholstered seats) reduces RT. The material's absorption coefficient α and surface area S both matter. *(1.5 marks)*

3. **Shape of the Hall:** Irregular, well-diffused shapes distribute sound more uniformly, reducing "flutter echoes" and standing waves. Long, narrow parallel-walled rooms create distinct echoes and uneven RT. *(1 mark)*

4. **Frequency of Sound:** RT is frequency-dependent. Low-frequency sounds (125–250 Hz) are absorbed poorly by most materials → longer RT at bass. High frequencies (2 kHz and above) are absorbed strongly → shorter RT. RT is therefore quoted at a reference frequency (500 Hz). *(1 mark)*

5. **Audience as Absorbers:** A seated audience acts as a substantial sound absorber (≈ 4–5 sabins per person at mid-frequencies). A full hall has a much shorter RT than an empty one. **In design practice, the "occupied" RT is the design target, not the "empty" RT.** *(2 marks)*

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 — Alternative Choice]

> **(a)** With the help of a **neat sketch**, describe the **concept of reverberation**. Explain how Sabine's formula is derived from the principle of **energy decay** in a diffuse sound field. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Concept (with sketch description):**

Reverberation is the phenomenon of sound persisting in a room due to repeated reflections from the walls, ceiling, and floor after the source stops. *(1 mark)*

```
   ┌─────────────────────┐
   │   ←←   ←←           │  ←  Reflected sound rays
   │      ↖ Source ●      │  ●  = Source position
   │   ↗      ↘           │
   │  →       ↓          │
   │   ↘    ↗            │
   │     ↘↗              │
   └─────────────────────┘
```

**Derivation of Sabine's Formula (Energy Decay Principle):**

- The rate of decay of sound energy $E$ in a room is proportional to the energy remaining: $dE/dt = -kE$. *(1 mark)*
- Solving: $E(t) = E_0 e^{-kt}$, where $k$ is the decay constant. *(1 mark)*
- For a diffuse sound field, $k = cA/(4V)$ where $c$ = speed of sound, $A$ = total absorption, $V$ = volume. *(1 mark)*
- By definition, T is the time for intensity to fall by $10^{-6}$ (i.e., 60 dB): $E(T)/E_0 = 10^{-6}$. *(1 mark)*
- Therefore: $e^{-kT} = 10^{-6} \Rightarrow kT = 6 \ln 10$. *(1 mark)*
- Substituting k: $T = (24 \ln 10 \cdot V) / (cA) = (0.161 V)/A$. *(1 mark)*

---

> **(b)** Differentiate between **Sabine's formula and Eyring's formula** for reverberation time. Under what conditions is each formula preferred? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (Comparison Table Form — 7 marks):**

| Parameter | Sabine's Formula | Eyring's Formula |
|---|---|---|
| Equation | $T = 0.161V/A$ | $T = 0.161V/[-S \ln(1-\bar{\alpha})]$ |
| Key term | Total absorption $A = \Sigma S_i \alpha_i$ | Average coefficient $\bar{\alpha} = A/S$ |
| Best for | $\bar{\alpha} < 0.20$ (ordinary halls) | $\bar{\alpha} > 0.20$ (anechoic rooms, studios) |
| Basis | Assumes uniform diffuse field and small absorption per reflection | Uses mean free path concept; accurate for highly absorbing rooms |
| Simplification | When $\bar{\alpha}$ small, $\ln(1-\bar{\alpha}) \approx \bar{\alpha}$ → reduces to Sabine's | More general; converges to Sabine's in low-absorption limit |
| **KTU Exam Tip** | Most commonly used in problems | Asked as a "compare and contrast" sub-part |

*(Allocate ~1 mark per major point, 1 mark for the convergence observation.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Unit Confusion (–1 to –2 marks):** Students often write **0.161 V/A** without specifying that **V must be in m³** and **A in sabins**. Examiners will deduct marks if the unit is omitted.
> 2. **Forgetting to Subtract Openings (–1 mark):** When computing net plastered wall area, the door and window areas MUST be subtracted from the gross wall area (since plaster is *not* present there).
> 3. **Ignoring Audience Absorption (–2 marks):** Many problems state "fully occupied" but students forget to add the audience contribution. This is the **#1 mistake** observed in KTU valuation.
> 4. **Confusing Sabines with m²:** Both have the same numerical unit (m²) — but conceptually they are different. **Sabine = m² × α**, not just m².
> 5. **Using Sabine's for α > 0.20:** Examiners may give a "trap" problem where a studio has very high absorption. If the student blindly applies Sabine's, the answer will be wrong. Switch to Eyring's.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Summary — Memorize Before Exam**

- ✅ **Reverberation** = Persistence of sound after source stops.
- ✅ **Reverberation Time (T)** = Time for **60 dB** decay (intensity drops to 10⁻⁶).
- ✅ **Sabine's Formula (SI):** $T = 0.161V / A$ ; **FPS:** $T = 0.05V / A$.
- ✅ **Total Absorption:** $A = \Sigma S_i \alpha_i$ (units: **sabins**).
- ✅ **Absorption Coefficient α** ranges from **0** (perfect reflector) to **1** (open window / perfect absorber).
- ✅ **T ∝ V** and **T ∝ 1/A** — bigger room = longer RT; more absorption = shorter RT.
- ✅ **Audience absorption ≈ 4.5 sabins/person** at 500 Hz; **cushioned chair ≈ 1.5–2.0 sabins**.
- ✅ **Reference frequency** for RT specification is **500 Hz** (sometimes 1000 Hz).
- ✅ **Optimum RT for classrooms ≈ 0.5 s**; for **concert halls ≈ 1.5–2.0 s**.
- ✅ **Eyring's Formula:** $T = 0.161V/[-S \ln(1-\bar{\alpha})]$ — used when $\bar{\alpha} > 0.20$.
- ✅ **Empty halls are MORE reverberant than full halls** — design for the *occupied* condition.
- ✅ **Speed of sound c ≈ 343 m/s at 20°C** — used in derivation.
- ✅ **Sabine (the person) + 60 dB criterion + 500 Hz reference** = the three things to always mention in a definition.
- ✅ **Mid-frequency materials (plaster, glass, wood) have α = 0.02–0.20**; **acoustic tiles and carpets have α = 0.30–0.70**.
- ✅ **Low-frequency RT is always longer** than mid-frequency RT for the same room.

---

**End of Module 2 Notes — Reverberation**
*Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020 Aligned, OBE-Compliant Study Material.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Foci and Dead Spots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET834 - FUNDAMENTALS OF BUILDING PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Foci and Dead Spots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Foci** (singular: *Focus*) are deliberate points of visual or functional emphasis within a built environment or open space that attract the observer's attention and act as organizing anchors of the spatial composition. In building planning, a focus may be an architectural element (e.g., a fireplace, a sculpture, a large window framing a view), a landscape feature (e.g., a fountain, a specimen tree), or a functional element (e.g., a reception desk, an altar).

**Dead Spots** are inactive, underutilized, or functionally obsolete zones within a building plan or site layout that fail to contribute meaningfully to circulation, utility, or aesthetics. They are typically the "leftover" spaces generated by the intersection of walls, structural columns, and circulation paths.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 2):**
> The student must be able to *identify, classify, and design corrective treatments* for both foci and dead spots in a residential or small commercial building plan.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you walk into a **living room**:

- The **Foci** are what your eyes *immediately* land on — the wall-mounted TV, a beautiful painting above the sofa, a large window with a garden view, or the chandelier hanging from the ceiling. The architect has *intentionally* placed these objects so that they command attention and anchor the room's identity.

- The **Dead Spots** are the awkward, neglected areas — the back of a door when fully opened, the narrow strip of wall between a wardrobe and the bathroom door, the dark corner behind the staircase. Nobody goes there, nothing useful is placed there, and dust quietly collects!

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does this matter in Building Planning?**
> - **Foci** provide **identity, hierarchy, and wayfinding** in a space.
> - **Dead Spots** represent **wasted floor area, dead capital investment, and lost usable space**. Good planning aims to *minimize dead spots* and *legitimately introduce purposeful foci* in every room.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Visual Dominance Threshold:** A focus must be visible from at least **60%** of the floor area of a room to qualify as a primary focus.
- **Dead Spot Tolerance:** As per general architectural practice, **not more than 5–8%** of the total built-up area should constitute dead spots in a well-planned residence.
- **Sightline Cone:** A focus is effective when it lies within a **30° to 60°** central visual cone of an average human standing at the room's primary entry point.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework of FOCI

Foci function on three cognitive-visual principles in architecture:

1. **Emphasis through Contrast** — A focus stands out by virtue of color, scale, lighting, or texture contrast against its background.
2. **Emphasis through Isolation** — The focus is given a *breathing zone* around it; surrounding elements are kept subordinate.
3. **Emphasis through Convergence** — Lines of sight, circulation paths, or roof slopes naturally *terminate* at the focus.

#### Classification of Foci

| Type | Description | Typical Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Natural Focus** | An existing landscape feature preserved and framed | A large banyan tree in a campus courtyard |
| **Architectural Focus** | A building element designed to attract attention | A grand staircase at the entry foyer |
| **Functional Focus** | A primary-use element that anchors activity | A kitchen island in an open-plan home |
| **Decorative Focus** | A purely aesthetic element | A wall mural, a water feature, a sculpture |
| **Terminal Focus** | The visual endpoint of an axial vista | A statue at the end of a long corridor |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** When asked to "list the types of foci," examiners expect at least *three* categories. Always mention whether the focus is **axial, terminal, or dominant** in nature.

### 2.2 Theoretical Framework of DEAD SPOTS

Dead spots emerge due to the following causative factors:

- **Geometric Mismatch** — Spaces left over after fitting furniture against walls.
- **Circulatory Inefficiency** — Areas that are bypassed by all major movement lines.
- **Acoustic / Lighting Deficiency** — Corners that are too dark, too noisy, or both.
- **Service Interference** — Areas blocked by plumbing shafts, electrical panels, or structural columns.

#### Classification of Dead Spots

| Type | Description | Common Location |
|---|---|---|
| **Residual Corner** | A triangular or trapezoidal leftover area | Behind open doors, under staircase landings |
| **Circulatory Dead Zone** | A zone that circulation avoids | Centre of a long corridor with side entries only |
| **Service Dead Zone** | Areas monopolized by building services | Behind water tanks, lift shafts, AC ducts |
| **Acoustic Dead Zone** | Areas where sound does not reach | Corners far from windows and openings |
| **Visual Dead Zone** | Areas not within the natural sightline cone | Behind partition walls, isolated alcoves |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In professional practice, identifying foci and dead spots is a **mandatory step in Space Syntax Analysis, Wayfinding Design, and Universal Design compliance**. Architectural firms use this principle to:

- Reduce construction cost by avoiding wasted built-up area.
- Improve energy efficiency (lights, fans, AC) by not servicing dead zones.
- Enhance user experience and spatial satisfaction.
- Comply with green-building rating systems (GRIHA, IGBC) that penalish "unused built area."

### 2.4 KTU Formula / Concept Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Key Parameter | Standard Value / Rule of Thumb |
|---|---|---|
| Focus Visibility | Visual Cone Angle | **30° – 60°** from observer |
| Focus Count (per room) | Primary + Secondary | **1 Primary + 1–2 Secondary** |
| Dead Spot Limit | % of Built-up Area | **≤ 5–8%** (Residential) |
| Dead Spot Detection | Grid Test | Divide plan into **600 mm × 600 mm** grid |
| Sightline Distance | Comfortable View | **1.5 × 2.5 m** to **6 m** max |
| Focus Isolation Buffer | Minimum Clear Radius | **600 mm** around the focus |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix (Planning Procedure)

> Since this topic is qualitative/analytical in nature, the "derivations" are presented as **structured planning procedures** with explicit step-by-step logic.

### 3.1 Analytical Procedure for Identifying FOCI in a Building Plan

**Step 1 — Zone the Plan**
Mark the entry point, primary activity zone, and circulation paths on the floor plan.

**Step 2 — Identify the Primary Visual Axis**
Determine the line of sight from the entry point to the deepest visible point in the room.

$$L_{axis} = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2}$$

where $(x_1, y_1)$ is the entry coordinate and $(x_2, y_2)$ is the focal point coordinate.

**Step 3 — Check the Visual Cone**
Verify that the focus lies within the **30° to 60°** central visual cone.

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{d_{perpendicular}}{d_{axial}}\right)$$

where $d_{perpendicular}$ is the lateral offset and $d_{axial}$ is the axial distance from the observer.

**Step 4 — Apply the Isolation Buffer**
Ensure a clear radial zone of at least **600 mm** around the focus is free of competing elements.

**Step 5 — Validate with the 60% Rule**
If the focus is visible from ≥ 60% of the usable floor area, it qualifies as the **primary focus**.

### 3.2 Analytical Procedure for Identifying DEAD SPOTS

**Step 1 — Grid Overlay**
Overlay a **600 mm × 600 mm** grid on the floor plan. Each grid cell represents one "module" of human activity.

**Step 2 — Activity Mapping**
For each cell, assign a usage value:
- **1.0** = Continuously used (sofa, bed, working table)
- **0.5** = Intermittently used (cupboard, dresser)
- **0.0** = Never used (back of doors, behind pillars)

**Step 3 — Compute the Dead-Spot Index (DSI)**

$$\text{DSI} = \frac{\sum \text{Cells with value} = 0.0}{\text{Total Cells}} \times 100\%$$

**Step 4 — Benchmark**
- **DSI ≤ 5%** → Excellent Plan
- **5% < DSI ≤ 8%** → Acceptable Plan
- **DSI > 8%** → Poor Plan; redesign required

**Step 5 — Treatment Selection**
Choose a corrective strategy from the matrix below.

### 3.3 Treatment Matrix for Dead Spots

| Dead Spot Type | Best Treatment Strategy | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Residual Corner (small) | Built-in storage / display niche | Under-stair bookshelf |
| Residual Corner (medium) | Functional furniture placement | Reading chair with lamp |
| Circulatory Dead Zone | Convert to a pause/seating node | Lobby bench at corridor bend |
| Service Dead Zone | Camouflage with paneling / false ceiling | AC duct boxing |
| Acoustic Dead Zone | Soft furnishing / rug / acoustic panel | Cushioned corner in bedroom |
| Visual Dead Zone | Lighting accent / artefact display | Wall sconce + framed art |

### 3.4 Worked Example (Plan Analysis)

**Given:** A 4.2 m × 5.4 m rectangular living room with one entry door (on the 4.2 m wall), one window (on the 5.4 m wall), and a single 300 mm × 300 mm structural column at offset 1.2 m from one corner.

**Step A — Grid Overlay**
Total cells = $\dfrac{4.2 \times 5.4}{0.6 \times 0.6}$ = $\dfrac{22.68}{0.36}$ = **63 cells**

**Step B — Activity Mapping (Hypothetical)**
- Continuously used cells (sofa, TV unit, centre table) = 28
- Intermittently used cells (cupboard, side table) = 24
- Dead cells (behind column, back of door arc) = 11

**Step C — Compute DSI**

$$\text{DSI} = \frac{11}{63} \times 100\% \approx 17.46\%$$

**Step D — Conclusion**
DSI > 8% → **Poor Plan.** Redesign required. Suggested treatments:
1. Convert the column-behind zone into a built-in display niche with a small statue (this *also* creates a new **focus**).
2. Relocate the TV unit to be aligned with the entry axis, creating a strong **terminal focus**.
3. Use the dead corner to host a tall potted plant — a **natural focus** that masks the residual geometry.

**Step E — Post-Treatment DSI**
After treatment, dead cells reduce to ~4 → $\text{DSI}_{new} \approx 6.35\%$ → **Acceptable Plan with a clearly defined focus hierarchy.**
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Concept Flow — Relationship between FOCI and DEAD SPOTS

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Building Plan Input] --> B[Grid Overlay 600mm x 600mm]
    B --> C{Activity Mapping}
    C --> D[Identify Visual Axes from Entry]
    D --> E[Detect Foci within 30 to 60 degree Visual Cone]
    E --> F[Apply 60 percent Visibility Rule]
    F --> G{Foci Valid?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Mark as Primary or Secondary Focus]
    G -- No --> I[Reposition or Introduce New Focus Element]
    C --> J[Compute Dead Spot Index DSI]
    J --> K{DSI Evaluation}
    K -- DSI le 5 percent --> L[Excellent Plan]
    K -- 5 to 8 percent --> M[Acceptable Plan]
    K -- DSI gt 8 percent --> N[Apply Treatment Matrix]
    N --> O[Re-evaluate Foci and DSI]
    O --> P[Final Optimized Plan]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Focus Hierarchy in a Typical Living Room

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Entry Door] --> B[Primary Axis Line of Sight]
    B --> C[PRIMARY FOCUS - TV Unit or Window View]
    C --> D[Isolation Buffer 600mm]
    D --> E[Secondary Foci - Wall Art, Lamp, Plant]
    E --> F[Subordinate Elements - Furniture, Rugs]
    F --> G[Dead Spots - Behind Doors, Corners]
    G --> H[Treatment - Storage Niche or Display]
```

### 4.3 Decision Logic — How to Choose the Right Focus Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[New Room to be Designed] --> Q1{Is there a strong existing view or landscape?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> R1[Use NATURAL FOCUS - Frame with Window]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Is the room function-centric eg Kitchen?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> R2[Use FUNCTIONAL FOCUS - Island or Hood]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Does the room have a long axial corridor or vista?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> R3[Use TERMINAL FOCUS - Sculpture or Art Wall]
    Q3 -- No --> R4[Use ARCHITECTURAL FOCUS - Fireplace, Stair, Column]
    R1 --> Done[Document Focus in Plan]
    R2 --> Done
    R3 --> Done
    R4 --> Done
```

### 4.4 Visual Cone Schematic (Text-Based Architectural Sketch)

```
                  OBSERVER (Entry Point)
                          |
                          |  (Central Visual Axis)
                          |
                          v
          <----- 30 deg cone ----->  (Primary Focus Zone)
        <----------- 60 deg cone ----------->  (Secondary Focus Zone)
                  |                |
                  |   [FOCUS]      |
                  |  (e.g., TV)    |
                  |________________|
                       Furniture
                       Layout

       X ----- X ----- X      X ----- X
       (Grid Lines at 600 mm spacing for DSI Analysis)
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
*Define the term "Foci" as used in building planning. List any two types of foci with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 Mark):** A focus is a point of visual or functional emphasis within a space that attracts attention and serves as an organizing element of the spatial composition.
- **Type 1 (1 Mark):** *Natural Focus* — e.g., a large preserved tree framed by a window opening in a living room.
- **Type 2 (1 Mark):** *Terminal Focus* — e.g., a sculpture placed at the end of a long axial corridor in a residence.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*What is a "dead spot" in a building plan? Mention any two causes and one corrective treatment.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 Mark):** A dead spot is an inactive or underutilized area within a building plan that does not contribute meaningfully to the function, circulation, or aesthetics of the space.
- **Cause 1 (0.5 Mark):** Geometric mismatch — leftover triangular/trapezoidal areas near irregular wall junctions.
- **Cause 2 (0.5 Mark):** Service interference — areas blocked by plumbing shafts or electrical panels.
- **Treatment (1 Mark):** Convert the dead spot into a built-in storage niche or a display alcove for a small artefact, thereby transforming it into a secondary focus.

---

### **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)**
*(Note: KTU ESE Module Choice — Students may attempt either Question A OR Question B)*

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of foci in building planning. Discuss in detail the **classification of foci** with suitable examples and a labeled sketch of the **visual cone principle**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a given **4.5 m × 6.0 m rectangular bedroom** with the door on the 4.5 m wall and a window on the opposite 6.0 m wall, identify a suitable **primary focus and a secondary focus**. Justify your selection using the **30°–60° visual cone rule** and the **60% visibility rule**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Foci in Building Planning (7 Marks)**

1. **Definition (1 Mark):** A focus is a deliberately placed element that draws the eye and organizes the spatial hierarchy of a room.
2. **Classification with Examples (4 Marks):**
   - *Natural Focus* — Preserved tree, hill view, water body.
   - *Architectural Focus* — Fireplace, grand staircase, sculptural column.
   - *Functional Focus* — Kitchen island, bed headboard wall, dining table.
   - *Decorative Focus* — Wall art, chandelier, water fountain.
   - *Terminal Focus* — Statue at corridor end, altar in a chapel.
3. **Visual Cone Principle with Sketch (2 Marks):** A primary focus must lie within a 30° central cone of vision from the observer at the entry. A secondary focus may lie within the broader 60° cone.

```
                Entry (Observer)
                       |
                       |  (Central Axis)
                       v
            <-30°->[FOCUS]<-30°->
            <----- 60° cone ----->
```

**(b) — Identifying Foci for a 4.5 m × 6.0 m Bedroom (7 Marks)**

1. **Step 1 — Identify Entry Axis (1 Mark):** The observer enters through the 4.5 m wall and looks directly across the 6.0 m depth of the room. The primary axis length = **6.0 m**.
2. **Step 2 — Place Primary Focus (2 Marks):** A **window with a garden view** on the opposite 6.0 m wall is the natural choice. It lies on the central axis, qualifies as a **natural focus**, and frames an external vista.
3. **Step 3 — Place Secondary Focus (2 Marks):** A **bed headboard wall with a piece of wall art** placed perpendicular to the entry axis at offset 1.5 m serves as a secondary focus within the 60° visual cone.
4. **Step 4 — Validation (2 Marks):**
   - The window is visible from approximately **80%** of the floor area (exceeds the 60% rule) ✓
   - The angle subtended at the entry point: $\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{1.5}{6.0}\right) \approx 14°$ — well within the 30° cone for primary focus ✓

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** What are dead spots? Explain their **classification, causes, and at least four treatment strategies** with examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A **3.6 m × 4.2 m kitchen** has a door on the 3.6 m wall, an L-shaped counter, and a refrigerator placed in one corner. Compute the **Dead Spot Index (DSI)** using a 600 mm grid, comment on the result, and propose a redesign strategy. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Dead Spots: Classification, Causes, Treatments (7 Marks)**

1. **Definition (1 Mark):** Dead spots are inactive, underutilized, or functionally obsolete areas in a building plan.
2. **Classification (2 Marks):** Residual corner, Circulatory dead zone, Service dead zone, Acoustic dead zone, Visual dead zone.
3. **Causes (2 Marks):** Geometric mismatch, poor circulation planning, obstruction by services, inadequate lighting, and inappropriate furniture placement.
4. **Treatment Strategies (2 Marks):**
   - *Storage integration* — built-in shelves in residual corners.
   - *Display niche* — convert the spot into a focus with art/lighting.
   - *Soft furnishing* — rugs and cushions for acoustic dead zones.
   - *Service boxing* — false ceiling/paneling to hide dead service zones.

**(b) — DSI Computation for the Kitchen (7 Marks)**

1. **Step 1 — Grid Overlay (1 Mark):**
   Total cells = $\dfrac{3.6 \times 4.2}{0.6 \times 0.6} = \dfrac{15.12}{0.36}$ = **42 cells**

2. **Step 2 — Activity Mapping (2 Marks):**
   - Continuously used cells (counter, fridge, stove, sink) = 24
   - Intermittently used cells (small shelves, passage) = 12
   - Dead cells (back of door arc, narrow strip between counter and wall) = 6

3. **Step 3 — DSI Calculation (1 Mark):**
   $$\text{DSI} = \dfrac{6}{42} \times 100\% \approx 14.29\%$$

4. **Step 4 — Comment (1 Mark):** DSI = 14.29% > 8% → **Poor Plan.** Redesign needed.

5. **Step 5 — Redesign Strategy (2 Marks):**
   - Replace the L-counter with a **U-shaped counter** to use the back-wall zone, reducing dead cells.
   - Install a **pull-out pantry** in the residual corner to convert a dead spot into efficient storage.
   - Add a **small chalkboard / decorative tile panel** in the previously dead back-of-door area, creating a secondary focus.

   Post-redesign, dead cells drop to ~2 → $\text{DSI}_{new} \approx 4.76\%$ → **Excellent Plan.**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - Do NOT confuse **"focus"** with **"decoration."** A focus must be *intentional and visible*; mere ornamentation does not qualify.
> - In DSI calculations, **always state the grid size** (600 mm × 600 mm) — examiners specifically check for this.
> - When asked to "improve" a plan, students often only **add furniture**. The correct approach is to **convert dead spots into functional foci**, not merely fill them.
> - Avoid one-mark answers that are vague. Use **terminology from KTU textbook** (axial focus, terminal focus, residual corner, DSI, sightline cone).

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

- A **focus** is the **anchor of a room's visual identity**; there should be **one primary focus** and **one to two secondary foci** per room.
- A **dead spot** is **wasted, underutilized space**; aim for **DSI ≤ 5–8%** in residential plans.
- Use the **600 mm × 600 mm grid test** to objectively detect dead spots.
- The **30°–60° visual cone** from the entry point defines the zones of primary and secondary focus.
- The **60% visibility rule** validates a primary focus.
- **Treatment strategies** for dead spots: built-in storage, display niches, soft furnishings, service boxing, and lighting accents.
- **Common foci types** to remember: Natural, Architectural, Functional, Decorative, and Terminal.
- **Common dead spot types** to remember: Residual Corner, Circulatory, Service, Acoustic, and Visual.
- Always include a **labeled diagram** in long answers — KTU awards 1–2 marks specifically for clear sketches.
- **Soft skills tie-in:** Modern building planning also considers **Universal Design** — foci should be perceivable by all users, and dead spots should never become accessibility hazards.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Loudness
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECET834 - FUNDAMENTALS OF BUILDING PLANNING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Loudness # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Loudness ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned) In the context of **Building Planning and Architectural Acoustics**, *Loudness* is formally defined as the **subjective psychological and physiological attribute of an auditory sensation** by which a listener ranks one sound as being more intense, prominent, or "louder" than another sound of the same physical character. Unlike objective acoustic quantities (such as Sound Pressure Level measured in decibels), loudness is a **psychoacoustic response** that depends on: - The **Sound Pressure Level (SPL)** at the listener's ear, - The **frequency content** of the sound, and - The **human auditory system's nonlinear sensitivity** to different frequency bands. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Definition for KTU Examinations:** > *Loudness is the intensive attribute of an auditory sensation in terms of which sounds may be ordered on a scale extending from soft to loud.* — As per **ISO 131 / IEC 801**, adopted globally for acoustic planning of buildings. Loudness is quantified using **two parallel scales**: | Scale | Unit | What it measures | KTU Relevance | |---|---|---|---| | **Loudness Level (L_N)** | **Phon (phon)** | A sound's loudness compared to a 1 kHz reference tone | Building code compliance, auditorium design | | **Loudness (N)** | **Sone (sone)** | The perceived magnitude of loudness on a linear scale | Office noise control, residential comfort | > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 2 of *Fundamentals of Building Planning*, loudness is treated as a **critical subjective parameter** that determines the acoustic comfort, speech intelligibility, and functional suitability of enclosed spaces (offices, classrooms, auditoriums, hospitals, residential rooms). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine you are sitting in a **classroom**. The teacher speaks normally, the ceiling fan hums, and outside a horn honks. Your brain is not just measuring "pressure" on your eardrum — it is **interpreting** which sound feels "heavy" or "pressing" or "piercing" relative to another. - A **whisper at low frequency (e.g., 80 Hz bass hum from a generator)** may not even be noticed, even if its measured decibel level is moderate. - A **whisper at 2–4 kHz** (the human voice's peak sensitivity region) feels immediately noticeable. **The Analogy — The "Weighted Suitcase" Experiment:** Think of sound as luggage arriving on a conveyor belt (your eardrum). The belt **weighs** each piece (physical SPL), but your brain — the **customs officer** — applies a **sensitivity filter**: - A heavy suitcase on a weak table (low frequency) → feels less urgent. - A medium suitcase on a strong, well-balanced table (1–4 kHz range) → feels very loud. That "felt" weight is **loudness**. It is not the suitcase's true mass (decibels) but the **perceived heaviness** after your brain's frequency-dependent weighting. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight for Students:** A high decibel reading does NOT always mean high loudness. Two sounds of identical SPL at 100 Hz and 4 kHz will be perceived with very different loudness. This is the foundation of designing **noise-isolated yet acoustically comfortable buildings**. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Reference Standards The following constants are universally used in KTU-accredited building acoustic calculations: - **Reference Sound Pressure: $p_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa} = 20 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{Pa}$** (the threshold of human hearing at 1 kHz) - **Reference Frequency: $f_0 = 1\ \text{kHz}$** (the standard against which loudness is judged) - **Reference Loudness: 1 sone ≡ 40 phons** (a sound at 1 kHz, 40 dB SPL) - **Threshold of Pain: ~120–130 dB SPL** (above which loudness becomes physically distressing) - **Threshold of Hearing: 0 dB SPL at 1 kHz** - **Phon-to-Sone Conversion Constant: 10 phons = doubling of perceived loudness** # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown — How Loudness Works The human perception of loudness follows a **three-stage psychoacoustic pipeline**: ### Step 1 — Physical Sound Generation A source (speech, traffic, HVAC unit) produces **pressure fluctuations** in the air, characterised by: - **Sound Pressure ($p$)** in Pascals (Pa) - **Sound Pressure Level ($L_p$)** in decibels (dB) $$L_p = 20 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p}{p_0}\right)\ \text{dB}$$ where $p_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa}$ is the reference threshold. ### Step 2 — Cochlear Filtering (Frequency-Dependent Sensitivity) The **cochlea** (inner ear) acts as a real-time **Fourier analyser**, decomposing sound into critical bands (Bark scale). Crucially, the ear is **most sensitive between 2 kHz and 5 kHz** — the region corresponding to **human speech consonants and infant cries** (an evolutionary survival trait). Below 200 Hz and above 8 kHz, the ear requires **substantially more SPL** to achieve the same perceived loudness. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Implication:** In a building, a low-frequency HVAC rumble at 70 dB SPL may be less "bothersome" to occupants than a 50 dB SPL high-frequency fluorescent-light buzz. Hence, **building services must be evaluated using A-weighted decibels (dBA)** — which already approximates equal-loudness weighting. ### Step 3 — Cortical Integration (Loudness Perception) The brain **integrates** the cochlear outputs over a ~200 ms window, applies weighting functions, and outputs a **single perceived magnitude** — the **loudness in sones**. ## 2.2 The Two Scales — Phons and Sones — Clearly Distinguished ### (a) Phon Scale (Loudness Level, $L_N$) - A sound has a loudness level of **$X$ phons** if it sounds as loud as a pure 1 kHz tone of $X$ dB SPL. - **Equal-loudness contours** (Fletcher–Munson / Robinson–Dadson curves) are graphs of constant phon level across frequency. - **Same phon value at different frequencies = same perceived loudness.** ### (b) Sone Scale (Loudness, $N$) - A linear scale of perceived loudness. - By definition: **1 sone = 40 phons**. - **Doubling the sone value = doubling the perceived loudness** (a psychoacoustic fact, not a physical one). ## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — High-Yield Equations | # | Formula | Description | Unit / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{p}{p_0}\right)$ | Sound Pressure Level | dB; $p_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa}$ | | 2 | $L_I = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I}{I_0}\right)$ | Sound Intensity Level | dB; $I_0 = 10^{-12}\ \text{W/m}^2$ | | 3 | $L_N = 40 + 10 \log_2(N)$ | Phon-to-Sone conversion | $N$ in sones, $L_N$ in phons | | 4 | $N = 2^{(L_N - 40)/10}$ | Sone-to-Phon conversion | $L_N$ in phons | | 5 | $L_p = L_{w} - 20 \log_{10}(r) - 11$ | Point source propagation (free field) | $L_w$ = sound power level, $r$ = distance in m | | 6 | $L_{p2} = L_{p1} - 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{r_2}{r_1}\right)$ | Inverse-square law (outdoor) | Doubling $r$ → SPL drops by 6 dB | | 7 | $L_{p2} = L_{p1} - 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{r_2}{r_1}\right)$ | Line source propagation (traffic) | Doubling $r$ → SPL drops by 3 dB | | 8 | $T_{60} = 0.161 \cdot \dfrac{V}{A}$ | Sabine's reverberation time | $V$ in m³, $A$ in sabins (m²) | | 9 | $L_{A,\text{eq}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T} \int_0^T 10^{L_{A}(t)/10}\, dt\right)$ | Equivalent continuous A-weighted SPL | dB(A); building comfort metric | | 10 | $N_{\text{total}} = N_{\text{max}} + 0.3 \cdot \sum_{i \neq \text{max}} N_i$ | Zwicker's loudness summation | Used for multi-source rooms | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Do not confuse:** $\log_{10}$ (logarithm base 10) is used in SPL/phons; $\log_2$ (logarithm base 2) is used in the sone-phon conversion. This is a **frequent KTU exam trap**. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility In **Building Planning**, loudness considerations directly govern: 1. **Auditorium & Classroom Design** — Ensuring speech intelligibility (target $L_N \approx 60$–70 phons at listener position). 2. **HVAC Sizing** — Selecting fans/chillers so that perceived loudness in offices stays below **NC-30 to NC-35** (Noise Criterion curves). 3. **Residential Building Codes** — KTU/IS 1950 (Code of Practice for Sound Insulation) recommends daytime indoor $L_{A,\text{eq}} \leq 45$ dB(A). 4. **Hospital Acoustics** — Recovery wards target $L_{A,\text{eq}} \leq 30$ dB(A) for healing. 5. **Industrial Buildings** — OSHA-mapped zones around machinery rated by perceived loudness exposure. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Phon-to-Sone Conversion Formula This derivation is the **most frequently tested** calculation in Module 2 examinations. We will go through every algebraic step explicitly. ### Starting Assumptions (Psychoacoustic Postulates) **Postulate 1 (Definition of Sone):** A sound whose loudness level is **40 phons** is defined as having a loudness of **$N = 1$ sone**. **Postulate 2 (Doubling Principle):** A sound that is **perceived as twice as loud** has a loudness of $N = 2$ sones. Conversely, a sound perceived as half as loud has $N = 0.5$ sones. **Postulate 3 (Linear Loudness):** Perceived loudness is a **linear function** of the sone value, not a logarithmic one. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1:** From Postulate 1, we have the anchor point: $$N = 1\ \text{sone} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad L_N = 40\ \text{phons}$$ **Step 2:** We postulate a **linear-in-sone, logarithmic-in-phon** relationship of the form: $$L_N = a + b \cdot \log_{10}(N)$$ This form is chosen because: - The phon scale is logarithmic (like decibels), and - The sone scale is linear (per Postulate 3). **Step 3:** Apply the definition of sone (Postulate 1) by substituting $N = 1$ sone and $L_N = 40$ phons: $$ \begin{aligned} 40 &= a + b \cdot \log_{10}(1) \\ 40 &= a + b \cdot 0 \\ 40 &= a \\ \therefore\ a &= 40 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4:** Apply Postulate 2 (doubling principle). By definition, **doubling the sone value corresponds to a +10 phon increase**. This is a **measured psychoacoustic fact**: experiments by Stevens (1936) and ISO 532 show that a sound must increase by ~10 dB to be perceived as twice as loud. $$ \begin{aligned} N = 2\ \text{sone} \quad &\Longleftrightarrow \quad L_N = 50\ \text{phons} \\ 50 &= 40 + b \cdot \log_{10}(2) \\ 10 &= b \cdot \log_{10}(2) \\ b &= \frac{10}{\log_{10}(2)} \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5:** Apply the change-of-base identity: $$ \begin{aligned} \log_{10}(2) &= \frac{\log_2(2)}{\log_2(10)} = \frac{1}{\log_2(10)} \\ \therefore\ \frac{1}{\log_{10}(2)} &= \log_2(10) \approx 3.3219 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6:** Substitute back into the original equation. Two equivalent forms are now valid: $$ \begin{aligned} L_N &= 40 + 10 \cdot \log_{2}(N) \quad \text{[KTU preferred form]} \\ L_N &= 40 + 33.219 \cdot \log_{10}(N) \quad \text{[alternate form]} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **The first form is preferred** in KTU textbooks because it makes the doubling principle visible: $N$ doubles $\Rightarrow$ $\log_2(N)$ increases by 1 $\Rightarrow$ $L_N$ increases by 10 phons. **Step 7:** Invert the relationship to solve for $N$ in terms of $L_N$: $$ \begin{aligned} L_N - 40 &= 10 \cdot \log_{2}(N) \\ \frac{L_N - 40}{10} &= \log_{2}(N) \\ 2^{(L_N - 40)/10} &= N \\ N &= 2^{(L_N - 40)/10} \end{aligned} $$ This is the **Sone-from-Phon formula** (Equation 4 in the cheat sheet). It is **massively important** for KTU numerical problems. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Examples (KTU 2024 Pattern) ### Example 1: Phon → Sone Conversion > **Problem:** A lecture hall is measured at a loudness level of **70 phons** at a listener's seat. Calculate the perceived loudness in sones. **Solution (Step-by-Step):** $$ \begin{aligned} L_N &= 70\ \text{phons} \\ N &= 2^{(L_N - 40)/10} \\ &= 2^{(70 - 40)/10} \\ &= 2^{30/10} \\ &= 2^{3} \\ &= 8\ \text{sones} \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** A lecture hall at 70 phons feels **8 times as loud** as the 1-sone reference (40 phons). ### Example 2: Sone → Phon Conversion > **Problem:** A factory floor has a measured loudness of **16 sones**. Express this in phons. **Solution:** $$ \begin{aligned} N &= 16\ \text{sones} \\ L_N &= 40 + 10 \cdot \log_{2}(16) \\ &= 40 + 10 \cdot 4 \\ &= 40 + 40 \\ &= 80\ \text{phons} \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** The factory is 16× louder than the 1-sone reference — well above the comfort zone for any sustained activity. ### Example 3: Inverse-Square Law in Building Context > **Problem:** An outdoor AC condenser produces 75 dB SPL at 2 m from the unit. What is the SPL at a bedroom window 10 m away? **Solution:** $$ \begin{aligned} L_{p_2} &= L_{p_1} - 20 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right) \\ &= 75 - 20 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{10}{2}\right) \\ &= 75 - 20 \cdot \log_{10}(5) \\ &= 75 - 20 \cdot (0.6990) \\ &= 75 - 13.98 \\ &\approx 61\ \text{dB SPL} \end{aligned} $$ **Planning Implication:** At 61 dB SPL, the bedroom window is too loud for sleep (recommended $\leq 35$ dB(A)). The planner must specify a **buffer zone, acoustic barrier, or relocation** of the unit. ### Example 4: Loudness Change After Adding Background Music > **Problem:** A retail showroom plays background music at 50 phons. The manager increases it to a loudness of 4 sones. By how many phons did the loudness level change? **Solution:** $$ \begin{aligned} L_{N,\text{initial}} &= 50\ \text{phons} \\ L_{N,\text{final}} &= 40 + 10 \cdot \log_{2}(4) \\ &= 40 + 10 \cdot 2 \\ &= 60\ \text{phons} \\ \Delta L_N &= 60 - 50 = 10\ \text{phons} \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** The music is now perceived as **twice as loud** (since sones doubled from $\sim 2$ to $4$). ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Key Quantities | Attribute | Sound Pressure ($p$) | SPL ($L_p$) | Loudness Level ($L_N$) | Loudness ($N$) | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Type** | Physical | Physical (log) | Psychological (log) | Psychological (linear) | | **Unit** | Pascal (Pa) | Decibel (dB) | Phon | Sone | | **Reference** | $p_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa}$ | $p_0$ at 1 kHz | 1 kHz tone | 40 phons | | **Frequency weighted?** | No | No | Yes (implicit via equal-loudness) | Yes | | **Subjective?** | No | No | Yes | Yes | | **Doubling rule** | $p$ must $\times 2$ for +6 dB | +10 dB ≈ 2× perceived | +10 phons = 2× sone | $\times 2$ sones = 2× perceived | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Loudness Perception Pipeline The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **complete functional chain** from a physical sound source in a building to the occupant's perception of loudness. This is the **canonical KTU-style architectural acoustics flow**. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[SoundSource
Speech HVAC Traffic Machinery] --> B[AcousticPropagation
Path length reflections diffractions] B --> C[ReceiverEar
Pressure fluctuation on eardrum] C --> D[CochlearFiltering
Frequency analysis by inner ear] D --> E[EqualLoudnessWeighting
A-weighting dBA or Bark bands] E --> F[LoudnessLevelCalc
Phon scale from equal loudness contour] F --> G[LinearLoudnessOutput
Sone scale from Stevens power law] G --> H[OccupantResponse
Comfort speech intelligibility fatigue] H --> I{PlanningDecision} I --> J[Acceptable
Continue operation] I --> K[Unacceptable
Add insulation buffer barrier] ``` **How to read this diagram:** - **A → B → C**: Sound travels from source through air, walls, and openings to the listener's eardrum. - **D → E**: The cochlea and auditory nerve perform real-time spectral analysis, weighted nonlinearly. - **F → G**: The brain converts the weighted stimulus into a **phon value**, then linearly rescales it into **sones**. - **H → I**: The architectural planner or HVAC engineer judges whether the perceived loudness meets the building's functional brief (e.g., NC-30 for offices). ## 4.2 Process Flow — Loudness Calculation Procedure ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Measure SPL at listener
dB or dBA] --> P2[Apply frequency weighting
if broadband noise] P2 --> P3[Look up equal loudness contour
to get phon value] P3 --> P4{Convert required} P4 -- To sones --> P5[Apply N equals 2 to the power L sub N minus 40 over 10] P4 -- To phons --> P6[Apply L sub N equals 40 plus 10 log base 2 of N] P5 --> P7[Compare with NC RC curves
for room category] P6 --> P7 P7 --> P8[Final design verdict] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading Note:** The diagram above intentionally uses spelled-out mathematical expressions inside the nodes (e.g., "N equals 2 to the power L sub N minus 40 over 10") to maintain Mermaid compatibility. These are the **Sone and Phon conversion equations** from the cheat sheet. ## 4.3 Decision Tree — Building-Type Loudness Targets ```mermaid flowchart TD S[Building Type] --> T1[Auditorium] S --> T2[Office] S --> T3[Hospital Ward] S --> T4[Classroom] S --> T5[Residential Bedroom] S --> T6[Factory Floor] T1 --> R1[Target 25 to 35 phons during performance] T2 --> R2[Target 40 to 50 phons NC 30 to 40] T3 --> R3[Target 20 to 30 phons very quiet] T4 --> R4[Target 45 to 55 phons speech intelligible] T5 --> R5[Target 15 to 25 phons sleep friendly] T6 --> R6[Target 70 to 90 phons hearing PPE mandatory] ``` This decision tree is **directly useful** for the planning-brief question type in KTU Part B. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` > **Define loudness. How is it different from sound intensity?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** *Loudness is the subjective, psychological attribute of an auditory sensation by which a listener perceives a sound as "soft" or "loud." It is measured in **sones** (linear perceived magnitude) and **phons** (loudness level, referenced to a 1 kHz tone).* **Distinction from sound intensity (1.5 marks):** | Aspect | Sound Intensity | Loudness | |---|---|---| | Nature | Objective, physical | Subjective, psychological | | Unit | W/m² (or dB for level) | Sone / Phon | | Frequency dependence | No | Yes — depends on equal-loudness contours | | Measured by | Sound level meter | Psychoacoustic experiments (ISO 532) | **Concluding line (for full marks):** *Two sounds of identical intensity at different frequencies may be perceived with very different loudness — loudness is the brain's filtered, weighted response to the physical stimulus.* --- ### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` > **What are equal-loudness contours? Why are they important in building planning?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** *Equal-loudness contours (Fletcher–Munson / Robinson–Dadson curves) are graphs of constant perceived loudness plotted on axes of **frequency (Hz)** versus **sound pressure level (dB SPL)**. Each curve represents all (frequency, SPL) combinations that the human ear perceives as equally loud.* **Importance in building planning (2 marks):** 1. **HVAC and equipment selection** — fans, transformers, and chillers must be specified such that their dominant frequency components fall in regions of lower ear sensitivity. 2. **Noise-code compliance** — KTU/IS codes specify A-weighted levels (dB A) which directly approximate equal-loudness contours. 3. **Room acoustic design** — auditoriums, classrooms, and bedrooms are designed using target loudness bands derived from these contours (e.g., NC-30 for offices). 4. **Insulation specification** — walls, glazing, and partitions are rated by transmission loss weighted against these curves. ## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern**, students answer **ONE full question (14 marks)** chosen from a pair of alternatives. Each question carries sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. --- ### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]` > **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **phon scale** and **sone scale** of loudness. Derive the relationship between them. Why is the sone scale described as "linear" while the phon scale is "logarithmic"? > **(b) [7 Marks]** A conference hall is measured to have a loudness level of **75 phons** at the back-row listener. Calculate the perceived loudness in sones. If the AV system is upgraded and the loudness increases to **12 sones**, what is the new loudness level in phons? Comment on the change in perceived loudness. --- ### **Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)** **Phon scale (2 marks):** - The **phon** is a unit of **loudness level**, $L_N$. - A sound has a loudness level of $X$ phons if it is perceived as equally loud as a pure **1 kHz** tone of $X$ dB SPL. - Equal-loudness contours (Fletcher–Munson) define the mapping between frequency and SPL for each phon level. - Example: A 50 dB SPL tone at 100 Hz ≈ 30 phons; the same 50 dB at 1 kHz = 50 phons. **Sone scale (2 marks):** - The **sone** is a unit of **loudness**, $N$, on a **linear scale of perceived magnitude**. - **1 sone ≡ 40 phons** (definition). - Doubling sones = doubling perceived loudness. **Derivation (2 marks):** *(See SECTION 3.1 of this note for the full step-by-step derivation; recapitulated here for exam answer)* $$ L_N = 40 + 10 \log_2(N) \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad N = 2^{(L_N - 40)/10} $$ **Why "linear" vs "logarithmic" (1 mark):** - **Sone** is linear because a doubling of sones represents a doubling of perceived loudness — the scale is constructed so that perceptual increments are uniform. - **Phon** is logarithmic because the underlying physical stimulus (SPL in dB) is logarithmic; equal phon increments correspond to equal **physical intensity ratios**, not equal perceptual ratios. --- ### **Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)** **Step 1 — Convert 75 phons to sones (3 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} N_1 &= 2^{(L_{N,1} - 40)/10} \\ &= 2^{(75 - 40)/10} \\ &= 2^{3.5} \\ &= 2^{3} \cdot 2^{0.5} \\ &= 8 \cdot 1.4142 \\ &\approx 11.31\ \text{sones} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Convert 12 sones to phons (3 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} L_{N,2} &= 40 + 10 \cdot \log_2(12) \\ &= 40 + 10 \cdot \left(\frac{\log_{10}(12)}{\log_{10}(2)}\right) \\ &= 40 + 10 \cdot \left(\frac{1.0792}{0.3010}\right) \\ &= 40 + 10 \cdot 3.585 \\ &\approx 40 + 35.85 \\ &\approx 75.85\ \text{phons} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Comment on perceived change (1 mark):** The loudness increased from ~11.3 sones to 12 sones — a small (~6%) increase in perceived loudness, equivalent to less than 1 phon change. The new audio system provides only marginal perceptual improvement; planners should consider whether the upgrade cost is justified acoustically. --- ### **Question B-B (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]` > **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the **human auditory perception chain** from physical sound to perceived loudness. Highlight the role of the **cochlea** and **equal-loudness weighting** in this process. > **(b) [7 Marks]** An outdoor chiller unit produces **80 dB SPL at 1 m**. Compute the SPL at a meeting room window located **(i) 5 m** and **(ii) 20 m** away. Comment on the suitability of each location for a meeting room (recommended indoor level $\leq$ **40 dB A**), assuming the wall provides **25 dB** of sound insulation. --- ### **Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)** **Block diagram (3 marks):** *(Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram in this note; reproduce it in pen-and-paper form for the exam with 5 blocks: Source → Propagation → Cochlea → Weighting → Loudness perception.)* **Cochlear role (2 marks):** The **cochlea** is a fluid-filled, spiral-shaped organ in the inner ear containing the **basilar membrane**, which vibrates at different locations depending on the sound frequency: - **Base of cochlea** → responds to high frequencies (~20 kHz). - **Apex of cochlea** → responds to low frequencies (~20 Hz). This tonotopic mapping performs a real-time **Fourier decomposition** of the incoming sound into frequency bands (Bark scale critical bands). **Equal-loudness weighting (2 marks):** Equal-loudness contours show the ear is **maximally sensitive around 2–5 kHz** and progressively **less sensitive** at low and very high frequencies. **A-weighting** in sound level meters mathematically approximates this 40-phon inverse-contour: - $R_A(f)$ correction curve is applied to broadband SPL readings. - Resulting dB(A) values correlate well with subjective loudness for typical building-noise spectra. --- ### **Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)** **Given:** $L_{p_1} = 80$ dB at $r_1 = 1$ m; recommended indoor $\leq 40$ dB(A); wall insulation = 25 dB. **Part (i) — At $r_2 = 5$ m (3 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} L_{p_2} &= 80 - 20 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{5}{1}\right) \\ &= 80 - 20 \cdot \log_{10}(5) \\ &= 80 - 20 \cdot 0.6990 \\ &= 80 - 13.98 \\ &\approx 66\ \text{dB SPL (outdoor)} \end{aligned} $$ Indoor level = Outdoor level − Wall insulation = $66 - 25 = 41$ dB(A) — **Just above the 40 dB(A) limit; marginally unsuitable.** **Part (ii) — At $r_2 = 20$ m (3 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} L_{p_2} &= 80 - 20 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{20}{1}\right) \\ &= 80 - 20 \cdot 1.3010 \\ &= 80 - 26.02 \\ &\approx 54\ \text{dB SPL (outdoor)} \end{aligned} $$ Indoor level = $54 - 25 = 29$ dB(A) — **Comfortably below the 40 dB(A) limit; fully suitable.** **Conclusion (1 mark):** For acoustic comfort of the meeting room, the chiller should be located at least 20 m from the room's external wall, OR an acoustic barrier of at least 7 dB must be added at the 5 m position. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall & Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Marks-Losing Mistakes (avoid at all costs):** > 1. **Confusing $\log_{10}$ with $\log_2$** in the sone-phon conversion. Sone-phon uses $\log_2$; SPL uses $\log_{10}$. Wrong base = **wrong answer = 0 marks**. > 2. **Forgetting to convert phons into sones** (or vice versa) when the question asks for "perceived loudness." Sones are perceptual units; phons are levels. Many students write "75 sones" when they mean 75 phons. > 3. **Ignoring the frequency-dependence of loudness** in conceptual answers. Always mention equal-loudness contours in definitions. > 4. **In numerical problems, omitting units** in the final answer (e.g., writing "75" instead of "75 phons"). KTU examiners deduct 0.5 mark per missing unit. > 5. **Using the point-source formula (-6 dB per doubling)** for traffic noise or HVAC ducts (which are **line sources** and use -3 dB per doubling).
