<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 5.1 PART A QUESTIONS (3 MARKS EACH)

### Question 1: Define Visual Task. Explain the factors affecting visual task requirements in building planning. [3 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

**Model Answer:**

A **visual task** is the specific visual work or activity performed by an occupant in a space (e.g., reading, drafting, surgery) that determines the daylight requirements of that space.

The **four factors** affecting visual task requirements are:

1. **Size of the critical detail:** Smaller details need more light for visibility.
2. **Contrast between detail and background:** Lower contrast demands higher illuminance.
3. **Luminance of the background:** Adequate background brightness enables better visual adaptation.
4. **Time available for seeing:** Shorter durations require higher light levels.

These are known as **Bouguer's Four Parameters of Visual Task**.

---

### Question 2: What is the Daylight Factor? State its recommended values for different types of buildings. [3 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

**Daylight Factor (DF)** is the ratio of indoor illuminance on the working plane to the outdoor illuminance under standard CIE overcast sky, expressed as a percentage.

$$DF = \dfrac{E_i}{E_o} \times 100 \quad (\%)$$

**Recommended DF values (NBC 2016 & IS 3646):**

| Building Type | Recommended DF |
|---|---|
| Residences | 1.0 – 2.0% |
| Classrooms, Offices | 2.0 – 5.0% |
| Drawing offices, Laboratories | 5.0 – 7.0% |
| Hospitals (wards) | 1.5 – 2.5% |
| Workshops | 2.0 – 3.0% |

---

## 5.2 PART B QUESTIONS (14 MARKS — INTERNAL CHOICE)

### QUESTION A (14 MARKS): Complete Daylight Factor Design Problem

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, S6 B.Tech Civil]**

A proposed office room measures 8 m (length) × 6 m (width) × 3 m (height). It has two windows each of size 2.5 m × 1.5 m on the longer wall. The geometric factor θ = 0.25 and the average internal reflectance is 0.6. Assume an external reflected component (ERC) of 0.4% and the outdoor illuminance under overcast sky is 10,000 lux.

**Calculate:**
**(a)** The Sky Component (SC) and Internally Reflected Component (IRC). [7 Marks]
**(b)** The total Daylight Factor (DF) and the indoor working plane illuminance. Comment on whether the design meets the visual task requirements for office work. [7 Marks]

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — SC and IRC Calculation**

**Step 1: Sky Component (SC)**
Assume SC contribution per window is 1.5% (typical for a window with no external obstruction):
$$SC = 2 \times 1.5\% = 3.0\%$$

**Step 2: Total Internal Surface Area**
$$A_{total} = 2(LW) + 2(LH) + 2(WH)$$
$$A_{total} = 2(8 \times 6) + 2(8 \times 3) + 2(6 \times 3)$$
$$A_{total} = 96 + 48 + 36 = 180 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3: Total Window Area**
$$A_w = 2 \times (2.5 \times 1.5) = 7.5 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4: Apply BRS Formula for IRC**
$$IRC = \dfrac{A_w \cdot \rho \cdot \theta}{A_{total} \cdot (1 - \rho^2)} \times 100$$

$$IRC = \dfrac{7.5 \times 0.6 \times 0.25}{180 \times (1 - 0.36)} \times 100$$

$$IRC = \dfrac{1.125}{180 \times 0.64} \times 100 = \dfrac{1.125}{115.2} \times 100$$

$$IRC = 0.977\% \approx 0.98\%$$

**Part (b) — Total DF and Indoor Illuminance**

**Step 5: Total Daylight Factor**
$$DF = SC + ERC + IRC = 3.0 + 0.4 + 0.98 = 4.38\%$$

**Step 6: Indoor Working Plane Illuminance**
$$E_i = \dfrac{DF \times E_o}{100} = \dfrac{4.38 \times 10{,}000}{100} = 438 \text{ lux}$$

**Step 7: Comment on Visual Task Compliance**
For office work, the recommended illuminance is **300–500 lux** (Grade 3 visual task per IS 3646). The achieved 438 lux falls **within the acceptable range**, and the DF of 4.38% exceeds the minimum 2% requirement. Hence, **the design satisfies the visual task requirements**.

---

### QUESTION B (14 MARKS): Theory and Classification of Visual Tasks

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, S6 B.Tech Civil, Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the **IS 3646 classification of visual tasks** in detail with suitable examples and recommended illumination levels for each grade. [7 Marks]

**(b)** Discuss the **Bouguer's four parameters** governing visual performance. How do they influence the design of natural lighting in buildings? [7 Marks]

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — IS 3646 Classification (7 Marks)**

The Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) document **IS 3646 (Code of Practice for Interior Illumination)** classifies visual tasks into **six grades** based on the size of the critical detail and the contrast between detail and background:

1. **Grade 1 – Casual Seeing:** Movement of persons in corridors, lounges, and entrance halls. Critical detail is large (>10 mm) with high contrast. Recommended: **50–100 lux**.

2. **Grade 2 – Ordinary Tasks:** Reading printed matter, simple kitchen work, rough bench work. Detail size 5–10 mm, moderate contrast. Recommended: **100–200 lux**.

3. **Grade 3 – Moderately Difficult:** Reading normal size print, general office work, classroom chalkboard reading. Detail size 2–5 mm. Recommended: **200–300 lux**.

4. **Grade 4 – Difficult:** Reading poor contrast print, drafting, fine bench work, account checking. Detail size 1–2 mm. Recommended: **300–750 lux**.

5. **Grade 5 – Very Difficult:** Prolonged reading of poor contrast material, fine inspection work, watchmaking. Detail size <1 mm. Recommended: **750–1500 lux**.

6. **Grade 6 – Exacting:** Special tasks like colour matching in textile/printing, surgical operations, fine engraving. Requires **1500–3000 lux** and special lighting design.

**Part (b) — Bouguer's Four Parameters (7 Marks)**

**Pierre Bouguer** (1729) established that visual performance depends on four interacting parameters:

1. **Size of the Critical Detail (S):** Expressed in minutes of arc. As S decreases, the required illumination increases. For example, reading 2 mm print requires 200 lux, but reading 0.5 mm engraving requires 1000+ lux.

2. **Contrast (C):** Defined as $C = \dfrac{\vert L_b - L_o \vert}{L_b}$. A black line on white paper (C = 0.9) is easily seen at 100 lux; a grey line on grey paper (C = 0.2) needs 5× more light.

3. **Luminance of the Background (L_b):** The eye adapts to background brightness. Higher L_b allows the pupil to constrict, improving depth of field and acuity. Recommended L_b for offices: 50–150 cd/m².

4. **Time Available (t):** The eye needs minimum exposure time. Tasks requiring quick recognition (e.g., reading moving signage) need 2–3× more light than static tasks.

**Design Influence:** A building designer must first identify the **dominant visual task** in each room, then size the window or skylight to deliver the corresponding illuminance, while ensuring adequate contrast (through wall colours and finishes) and controlling glare.

---

## 5.3 KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in Visual Task Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the BRS formula denominator** $(1 - \rho^2)$: Many students write $A_{total}$ only and lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Not converting DF percentage to lux** correctly: Remember $E_i = (DF \times E_o) / 100$, not $E_i = DF \times E_o$.
> 3. **Confusing SC with DF:** SC is only one component. Total DF always = SC + ERC + IRC.
> 4. **Skipping units** in the final answer: Always write "lux" or "%" explicitly.
> 5. **Omitting the "comment" line** in numerical problems: KTU evaluators award 1–2 marks for interpreting the result against IS 3646 standards.
> 6. **Mixing up contrast definitions:** The photometric contrast $C = |L_b - L_o| / L_b$ is **not** the same as Weber's fraction used in psychophysics.

---

## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Visual Task = The activity** that determines daylight requirements; classify into 6 IS 3646 grades.
- **DF = (Ei / Eo) × 100 %** — the master formula; DF has three components: **SC + ERC + IRC**.
- **Bouguer's 4 Parameters:** **Size, Contrast, Luminance, Time** — all must be adequate for visual comfort.
- **Standard CIE Overcast Sky Eo = 10,000 lux** is the universal reference for DF calculations.
- **IRC BRS Formula:** $IRC = \dfrac{A_w \cdot \rho \cdot \theta}{A_{total} \cdot (1 - \rho^2)} \times 100$
- **Recommended DF:** 1% (residences) to 5%+ (classrooms/offices); >5% for fine task spaces.
- **Contrast formula:** $C = \dfrac{\vert L_b - L_o \vert}{L_b}$ — dimensionless, ranges from 0 to 1.
- **Window orientation matters:** North-facing windows give uniform diffuse light (best for visual tasks); south/east/west need shading devices.
- **NBC 2016 mandates minimum 300 lux** in office and educational spaces during working hours.
- **ECBC 2017 mandates** minimum 2% DF for 75% of regularly occupied floor area in commercial buildings.
- **GRIHA / IGBC** award credit points for sDA (spatial Daylight Autonomy) ≥ 55% with 300 lux for at least 50% of the year.
- **Greenhouse effect analogy:** Use to explain why large south-facing glass without shading fails the visual task despite high illuminance (excessive contrast and glare).
- **Units to memorize:** lux (illuminance), cd/m² (luminance), lumen (flux), minutes of arc (visual angle), % (daylight factor).
- **Glare Index (DGP):** For visual task comfort, Daylight Glare Probability should be < 0.35 in offices and < 0.20 in studios.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Units of Light
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET834 - FUNDAMENTALS OF BUILDING PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Units of Light

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Photometry vs. Radiometry — The Foundation

In **Building Planning**, lighting design is governed by the science of **Photometry** — the measurement of visible light as perceived by the human eye. Unlike radiometry (which measures total electromagnetic energy), photometry weights energy by the standard **photopic luminosity function V(λ)**, which represents the human eye's spectral sensitivity peaking at **555 nm (yellow-green light)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)**
> **Light** is the visible portion of the electromagnetic spectrum (wavelength range **380 nm to 780 nm**) that produces a visual sensation on the retina of the human eye. In building planning, light is quantified using standardized photometric units that account for both energy and human visual response.

### 1.2 The Seven Fundamental Photometric Quantities

The **International System of Units (SI)** recognizes **seven base quantities**, and from these, all photometric units are derived. For a B.Tech student of Building Planning, mastering the following five core quantities is non-negotiable:

| # | Photometric Quantity | SI Unit | Symbol |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Luminous Flux | **Lumen (lm)** | Φ or F |
| 2 | Luminous Intensity | **Candela (cd)** | I |
| 3 | Illuminance | **Lux (lx)** | E |
| 4 | Luminance | **Candela/m² (cd/m²)** | L |
| 5 | Luminous Efficacy | **Lumen/Watt (lm/W)** | η |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Water Hose Model"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy for B.Tech Students**
> Imagine a water hose spraying water onto a wall:
> - **Luminous Flux (Φ)** = Total water flowing out of the hose per second
> - **Luminous Intensity (I)** = Water flow *concentrated* in a specific direction (per steradian)
> - **Illuminance (E)** = How much water *hits* a unit area of the wall
> - **Luminance (L)** = How *bright* the wall itself appears to your eye
> - **Luminous Efficacy (η)** = How efficiently the pump converts electricity to water flow

This mental model will help you remember that light behaves like a *directional fluid* spreading from a source and falling on surfaces.

### 1.4 Historical Context — The Candela Standard

The **Candela** is one of the **seven SI base units** (defined since 1979). It was originally defined by the luminous intensity of a "standard candle" — hence its name. Modern definition (2019):

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modern SI Definition of Candela**
> One **candela (cd)** is the luminous intensity, in a given direction, of a source that emits monochromatic radiation of frequency **540 × 10¹² Hz** (corresponding to 555 nm) and has a radiant intensity in that direction of **1/683 W/sr**.

This means **1 watt of radiant power at 555 nm = 683 lumens** of luminous flux — a critical conversion constant for exam problems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Luminous Flux (Φ) — "The Total Light Output"

**Luminous flux** is the *total* amount of visible light energy emitted by a source per unit time, weighted by the eye's sensitivity curve V(λ).

$$\Phi_v = 683 \int_{380}^{780} \Phi_{e,\lambda} \cdot V(\lambda) \, d\lambda$$

where:
- $\Phi_v$ = Luminous flux (in **lumens**)
- $\Phi_{e,\lambda}$ = Spectral radiant flux (W/nm)
- $V(\lambda)$ = Photopic luminosity function (dimensionless, 0 to 1)
- $683$ = Maximum theoretical luminous efficacy at 555 nm (lm/W)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 683?** A 1-watt monochromatic source at 555 nm (peak eye sensitivity) produces exactly 683 lumens. This is the cornerstone of all photometric-radiometric conversions.

**Practical meaning:** A 100 W incandescent bulb emits ~1500 lm; an LED of similar brightness consumes only ~15 W. This comparison is fundamental to building energy audits.

### 2.2 Luminous Intensity (I) — "Directional Punch"

**Luminous intensity** is the luminous flux emitted *per unit solid angle* in a given direction. It tells you *how concentrated* the light is in one direction.

$$I = \frac{d\Phi}{d\omega}$$

where:
- $I$ = Luminous intensity (cd)
- $d\Phi$ = Luminous flux in the elementary cone (lm)
- $d\omega$ = Elementary solid angle (sr)

For an *isotropic* (uniformly radiating) source: $I = \Phi / (4\pi)$

### 2.3 Solid Angle (ω) — The "3D Angle"

A **solid angle** is the 3-dimensional analogue of a planar angle. Just as a planar angle measures the arc divided by radius, a solid angle measures the *spherical area* divided by radius squared.

$$\omega = \frac{A}{r^2}$$

where:
- $A$ = Area on the sphere (m²)
- $r$ = Radius of sphere (m)
- Unit: **Steradian (sr)**

For a full sphere: $\omega = 4\pi \approx 12.566$ sr
For a hemisphere: $\omega = 2\pi \approx 6.283$ sr

### 2.4 Illuminance (E) — "Light Falling on a Surface"

**Illuminance** is the *most important* quantity in building planning because it directly determines whether a room is *adequately lit for the activity*.

$$E = \frac{d\Phi}{dA}$$

where:
- $E$ = Illuminance (lx or lm/m²)
- $d\Phi$ = Luminous flux incident on area (lm)
- $dA$ = Surface area (m²)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight — Recommended Illuminance Levels (IS 3646 / NBC 2016)**
> - Office work / study: **300–500 lx**
> - Drawing tables: **500–750 lx**
> - Corridors / passages: **100–150 lx**
> - Residential living: **100–300 lx**
> - Kitchen: **200–300 lx**
> - Hospital operation theatre: **1000+ lx**

### 2.5 Luminance (L) — "How Bright a Surface Appears"

**Luminance** is what your eye actually *perceives* as brightness. It depends on the intensity emitted *and* the viewing angle.

$$L = \frac{I}{A \cdot \cos\theta}$$

where:
- $L$ = Luminance (cd/m²)
- $I$ = Luminous intensity in viewing direction (cd)
- $A$ = Projected area of the surface (m²)
- $\theta$ = Angle between the surface normal and viewing direction

Common units: **cd/m²** (also called **nit**), **foot-lambert (fL)** (1 fL ≈ 3.426 cd/m²), **stilb (sb)** = 1 cd/cm²

### 2.6 Luminous Efficacy (η) — "Efficiency Metric"

**Luminous efficacy** measures how well a light source converts electrical power into visible light.

$$\eta = \frac{\Phi}{P}$$

where:
- $\eta$ = Luminous efficacy (lm/W)
- $\Phi$ = Total luminous flux (lm)
- $P$ = Electrical power consumed (W)

**Typical Values for Building Design:**
| Source Type | Efficacy (lm/W) | Lifetime (hrs) |
|---|---|---|
| Incandescent bulb | 10–17 | 1,000 |
| Halogen | 15–25 | 2,000–4,000 |
| CFL | 50–70 | 8,000–10,000 |
| Fluorescent tube | 60–100 | 10,000–15,000 |
| **LED (current best)** | **100–200+** | **25,000–50,000** |

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master This Table — Every Formula Below is KTU Exam Gold**

| # | Quantity | Formula | SI Unit | Auxiliary Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Solid Angle | $\omega = A/r^2$ | **sr** | — | Full sphere: $4\pi$ sr |
| 2 | Luminous Flux | $\Phi = I \cdot \omega$ | **lm** | — | 1 lm = 1 cd·sr |
| 3 | Luminous Intensity | $I = \Phi/\omega$ | **cd** | — | SI base unit |
| 4 | Illuminance | $E = \Phi/A$ | **lx** | 1 fc = 10.76 lx | 1 lx = 1 lm/m² |
| 5 | Inverse Square Law | $E = I/d^2$ | lx | — | Point source only |
| 6 | Cosine Law | $E = (I \cos\theta)/d^2$ | lx | — | Oblique incidence |
| 7 | Luminance | $L = I/(A\cos\theta)$ | **cd/m²** | 1 nit = 1 cd/m² | Perceived brightness |
| 8 | Luminous Efficacy | $\eta = \Phi/P$ | **lm/W** | — | Efficiency rating |
| 9 | Total flux from source | $\Phi = 4\pi I$ (isotropic) | lm | — | Only if uniform |
| 10 | Average Illuminance | $E_{avg} = (N \cdot \Phi \cdot UF \cdot MF) / A$ | lx | — | UF = Utilization Factor, MF = Maintenance Factor |

> [!NOTE]
> **The 683 lm/W Conversion Constant**
> $1 \text{ W (at 555 nm)} = 683 \text{ lm}$
> This is THE most-tested conversion in KTU exams — memorize it!

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

In professional building planning, these units are used to:

1. **Design artificial lighting layouts** (number of fixtures, spacing, mounting height)
2. **Calculate daylight factor** for energy-efficient window sizing
3. **Perform glare analysis** (luminance ratios between task and surroundings)
4. **Conduct energy audits** (kWh per m² per year lighting load)
5. **Comply with NBC 2016 / IS 3646** standards for occupancies
6. **Lumen method calculations** for indoor illumination design

> [!NOTE]
> **Lumen Method (KTU Favorite):**
> $$E_{avg} = \frac{N \cdot \Phi \cdot \eta_{uf} \cdot \eta_{mf}}{A}$$
> where N = number of lamps, $\eta_{uf}$ = utilization factor, $\eta_{mf}$ = maintenance factor. This is the industry-standard formula for indoor lighting design and appears in nearly every KTU exam.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Inverse Square Law of Illuminance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This derivation is a KTU Exam Favorite — appears almost every semester**

**Statement:** The illuminance on a surface produced by a point source varies *inversely as the square of the distance* between the source and the surface.

**Assumptions:**
1. The light source is a **point source** (dimensions negligible compared to distance)
2. The medium is **homogeneous and transparent** (no absorption)
3. The light radiates **uniformly in all directions** (isotropic)

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

Consider a point source **S** emitting total luminous flux $\Phi$ uniformly in all directions. Construct an imaginary sphere of radius $d$ centered on the source.

**Step 1:** The total solid angle subtended by a complete sphere is $4\pi$ steradians.

$$\omega_{total} = 4\pi \text{ sr}$$

**Step 2:** Luminous intensity is flux per unit solid angle. For an isotropic source, intensity is the same in all directions:

$$I = \frac{\Phi_{total}}{\omega_{total}} = \frac{\Phi}{4\pi}$$

This means total flux:
$$\Phi = 4\pi I$$

**Step 3:** Now consider a small surface patch of area $dA$ on the sphere at distance $d$. The solid angle subtended by this patch at the source is:

$$d\omega = \frac{dA}{d^2}$$

**Step 4:** The flux passing through this small area is the intensity times the solid angle:

$$d\Phi = I \cdot d\omega = I \cdot \frac{dA}{d^2}$$

**Step 5:** By definition, illuminance is flux per unit area:

$$E = \frac{d\Phi}{dA} = \frac{I}{d^2}$$

**Step 6:** Rearranging, we obtain the **Inverse Square Law**:

$$\boxed{E = \frac{I}{d^2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification of units:** $E = I/d^2$ has units of $\text{cd}/\text{m}^2 = \text{lm}/(\text{sr} \cdot \text{m}^2)$. Since $1 \text{ lx} = 1 \text{ lm}/\text{m}^2$ and the steradians cancel for an aligned source, the equation is dimensionally consistent.

**Practical Consequence:** Doubling the distance from a lamp to your desk reduces the illuminance to **one-fourth** — this is why mounting height is critical in lighting design.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Lambert's Cosine Law of Illumination

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **When light falls *obliquely* on a surface, illuminance depends on the cosine of the angle of incidence.**

**Statement:** The illuminance on a surface is *maximum* when the light ray is *perpendicular* (normal) to the surface and *decreases* as the cosine of the angle between the ray and the normal.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

Consider a point source **S** illuminating a surface. Let the surface element $dA$ be oriented such that its **normal** makes an angle $\theta$ with the line joining S to the surface (i.e., the angle of incidence is $\theta$).

**Step 1:** The solid angle subtended by $dA$ at source distance $d$ is:

$$d\omega = \frac{dA \cdot \cos\theta}{d^2}$$

The factor $\cos\theta$ appears because we need the *projected* area perpendicular to the line of sight.

**Step 2:** The flux received by the surface:

$$d\Phi = I \cdot d\omega = I \cdot \frac{dA \cdot \cos\theta}{d^2}$$

**Step 3:** Illuminance on the actual (inclined) surface:

$$E = \frac{d\Phi}{dA} = \frac{I \cdot \cos\theta}{d^2}$$

**Step 4:** The final **Lambert's Cosine Law**:

$$\boxed{E = \frac{I \cdot \cos\theta}{d^2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Special Cases (must remember for KTU):**
> - $\theta = 0°$ (perpendicular): $E = I/d^2$ → **maximum** illuminance
> - $\theta = 60°$: $E = 0.5 \cdot (I/d^2)$ → half the maximum
> - $\theta = 90°$ (grazing): $E = 0$ → no light received

**Real-world application:** A wall surface oriented away from a window receives *less* light per unit area than a wall facing it. This is why building orientation (East/West/South facing) drastically changes interior illumination levels.

---

### 3.3 Numerical Solved Example — KTU Pattern

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example (Likely 7-mark KTU question pattern)**
>
> **Problem:** A lamp with luminous intensity **200 cd** in all directions is suspended **4 m** above a desk. Find:
> (a) The illuminance directly below the lamp on the desk.
> (b) The illuminance at a point on the desk **3 m horizontally** away from the point directly below the lamp.
>
> **Solution:**
>
> **Part (a):** The light falls perpendicular to the desk, so $\theta = 0°$ and $\cos\theta = 1$.
>
> Using the inverse square law:
> $$E_a = \frac{I}{d^2} = \frac{200}{(4)^2} = \frac{200}{16} = 12.5 \text{ lx}$$
>
> **Part (b):** The new distance and angle:
>
> $d' = \sqrt{4^2 + 3^2} = \sqrt{25} = 5 \text{ m}$
>
> $\cos\theta = \frac{4}{5} = 0.8$
>
> Using Lambert's Cosine Law:
> $$E_b = \frac{I \cdot \cos\theta}{(d')^2} = \frac{200 \times 0.8}{(5)^2} = \frac{160}{25} = 6.4 \text{ lx}$$
>
> **Conclusion:** Illuminance drops from 12.5 lx to 6.4 lx as we move 3 m laterally — about 49% reduction. This shows why **uniform illumination** in a room requires multiple light sources.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Law

| Scenario | Applicable Law | Formula | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Light from a point source falling *perpendicular* on a surface | Inverse Square Law only | $E = I/d^2$ | $\cos\theta = 1$ |
| Light from a point source falling *obliquely* | Combined (Cosine + Inverse Square) | $E = (I \cos\theta)/d^2$ | Both effects apply |
| Sunlight on building facades | Cosine Law dominant | $E = E_0 \cos\theta$ | Sun is very far, $d \to \infty$ |
| Linear source (fluorescent tube) | Different formula needed | $E = (I \cdot \cos\theta \cdot \sin\alpha)/h$ | Cannot use point source formula |
| Diffuse / extended source | Integration over source area | Complex | Source has finite size |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Concept Map — Photometric Quantities and Their Relationships

The following block diagram illustrates the **logical flow and interconnections** between the five fundamental photometric quantities. It serves as the master visual aid for the entire topic.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[RADIANT ENERGY in Watts] --> B[LUMINOUS FLUX in Lumens]
    B --> C[LUMINOUS INTENSITY in Candela]
    B --> D[ILLUMINANCE in Lux]
    C --> E[LUMINANCE in cd per sqm]
    C --> F[SOLID ANGLE in Steradian]
    D --> G[INVERSE SQUARE LAW]
    D --> H[LAMBERT COSINE LAW]
    F --> G
    F --> H
    B --> I[LUMINOUS EFFICACY in lm per W]
    A --> I
    G --> J[BUILDING DESIGN]
    H --> J
    D --> J
    E --> J
    I --> K[ENERGY AUDIT]
    J --> L[IS 3646 NBC 2016 COMPLIANCE]
    K --> L
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Start from **Radiant Energy** (physical watts) → converts to **Luminous Flux** (lumens, eye-weighted) → splits into the four directional/area concepts → all feed into practical **Building Design** and **Energy Audit** applications → culminating in **Standards Compliance**.

### 4.2 Geometric Diagram — Solid Angle Concept

The following Mermaid block renders a **functional geometric flow** showing how a point source projects a solid angle onto a spherical cap. This replaces a hand-drawn 3D figure.

```mermaid
graph LR
    P[Point Source S] --> Q[Imaginary Sphere radius d]
    Q --> R[Spherical Patch Area dA]
    R --> S2[Solid Angle dOmega = dA over d squared]
    S2 --> T[Flux through patch = I times dOmega]
    T --> U[Illuminance = I over d squared]
    U --> V1[If perpendicular cos theta = 1]
    U --> V2[If oblique multiply by cos theta]
```

### 4.3 Hierarchical Block Diagram — Application Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[PHOTOMETRIC UNITS] --> B1[FUNDAMENTAL QUANTITIES]
    A1 --> C1[DERIVED LAWS]
    A1 --> D1[DESIGN APPLICATIONS]
    B1 --> B2[Luminous Flux]
    B1 --> B3[Luminous Intensity]
    B1 --> B4[Illuminance]
    B1 --> B5[Luminance]
    B1 --> B6[Luminous Efficacy]
    C1 --> C2[Inverse Square Law]
    C1 --> C3[Cosine Law]
    C1 --> C4[Lumen Method]
    D1 --> D2[Indoor Lighting Design]
    D1 --> D3[Daylight Factor Analysis]
    D1 --> D4[Glare Control]
    D1 --> D5[Energy Efficiency]
    D2 --> E1[NBC 2016 Compliance]
    D3 --> E1
    D4 --> E1
    D5 --> E1
```

### 4.4 Comparative Table — Units Across Photometric Quantities (Visual Block)

```mermaid
graph TD
    Q1[LUMINOUS FLUX] --> U1[Unit: Lumen lm]
    Q1 --> R1[1 lm = 1 cd multiplied by sr]
    Q2[LUMINOUS INTENSITY] --> U2[Unit: Candela cd]
    Q2 --> R2[SI Base Unit since 1979]
    Q3[ILLUMINANCE] --> U3[Unit: Lux lx]
    Q3 --> R3[1 lx = 1 lm per sqm]
    Q4[LUMINANCE] --> U4[Unit: cd per sqm or Nit]
    Q4 --> R4[Defines perceived brightness]
    Q5[EFFICACY] --> U5[Unit: lm per W]
    Q5 --> R5[Quality metric for lamps]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Study Tip:** Whenever you see a "cd" value on a lamp datasheet, that's the **candela rating** in a specific direction. When you see "lumens" on a bulb package, that's the **total flux**. The conversion requires the beam angle: $\Phi = I \cdot \omega$ where $\omega$ is the solid angle of the beam cone.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the following photometric quantities and state their SI units:*
*(a) Luminous Flux*  *(b) Luminous Intensity*  *(c) Illuminance*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 per sub-part):**

**(a) Luminous Flux (Φ):** It is the total quantity of visible light energy emitted by a source per unit time, as perceived by the human eye. **SI Unit: Lumen (lm)**. Mathematically, $\Phi = I \cdot \omega$.

**(b) Luminous Intensity (I):** It is the luminous flux emitted by a source per unit solid angle in a given direction. **SI Unit: Candela (cd)**, which is one of the seven SI base units. Expressed as $I = d\Phi/d\omega$.

**(c) Illuminance (E):** It is the luminous flux incident per unit area of a surface. **SI Unit: Lux (lx)** where $1 \text{ lx} = 1 \text{ lm}/\text{m}^2$. Calculated as $E = \Phi/A$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*State and explain the **Inverse Square Law of Illuminance**. Mention any two limitations of this law.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Statement:** The illuminance produced by a point source of light on a surface is *inversely proportional to the square of the distance* between the source and the surface. Expressed as:

$$E \propto \frac{1}{d^2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad E = \frac{I}{d^2}$$

**Explanation (1.5 marks):** As the distance doubles, the same flux spreads over four times the spherical area, reducing illuminance to one-fourth. This is because light radiates spherically and the surface area of a sphere grows as $4\pi d^2$.

**Limitations (1.5 marks — any two):**
1. **Point source assumption** fails for extended sources (fluorescent tubes, large panels) where the source size is comparable to the distance.
2. **No absorption assumption** is unrealistic — in real atmospheres, dust, humidity, and pollutants absorb/scatter light.
3. **Uniform radiation assumption** fails for directional sources like spotlights or LED reflectors.

---

### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **As per KTU 2024 Scheme:** Part B questions carry 14 marks. You must answer EITHER Question A OR Question B. Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, OECET834, Module 2]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Derive the relationship between **Illuminance (E)**, **Luminous Intensity (I)**, and **distance (d)** for a point source. State the assumptions clearly and explain why this law is critical in determining **mounting height of lamps** in a building.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* A room of dimensions **10 m × 8 m × 4 m** (L × B × H) is to be illuminated to an average illuminance of **300 lux**. Using **20 luminaires** each with a luminous flux of **5000 lumens**, calculate the **Utilization Factor (UF)** and the **Maintenance Factor (MF)**. Take room reflection factors as standard. Comment on whether the design meets the **IS 3646 recommended level** for office spaces.

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

**Part (a) — Derivation of Inverse Square Law (7 marks):**

**Assumptions (1.5 marks):**
1. The light source is a point source.
2. The medium is homogeneous and transparent.
3. The source radiates uniformly in all directions (isotropic).

**Derivation Steps (4 marks):**
- Consider point source S emitting total flux $\Phi$ uniformly. (0.5 mark)
- Construct an imaginary sphere of radius $d$ around S. The total solid angle of a sphere is $4\pi$ sr. (0.5 mark)
- Luminous intensity: $I = \Phi / (4\pi)$ (0.5 mark)
- Flux through small patch $dA$: $d\Phi = I \cdot dA/d^2$ (1 mark)
- Illuminance: $E = d\Phi/dA = I/d^2$ (1 mark)
- Final boxed equation: $\boxed{E = I/d^2}$ (0.5 mark)

**Application to mounting height (1.5 marks):**
Since $E \propto 1/d^2$, mounting height drastically affects illuminance. A lamp at 4 m gives 1/16 the illuminance of the same lamp at 1 m. Hence, designers must balance:
- **Lower mounting height** → higher illuminance but poor uniformity and glare
- **Higher mounting height** → better uniformity but more lamps needed → higher cost
Optimum mounting height is determined using **Sail's formula** or **point-by-point method**.

**Part (b) — Lumen Method Calculation (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Room area: $A = 10 \times 8 = 80 \text{ m}^2$
- Required illuminance: $E_{avg} = 300 \text{ lx}$
- Number of lamps: $N = 20$
- Flux per lamp: $\Phi = 5000 \text{ lm}$
- Total flux: $N \cdot \Phi = 20 \times 5000 = 100{,}000 \text{ lm}$

**Lumen Method Formula:**

$$E_{avg} = \frac{N \cdot \Phi \cdot UF \cdot MF}{A}$$

**Rearranging for the unknown product $UF \cdot MF$:**

$$UF \cdot MF = \frac{E_{avg} \cdot A}{N \cdot \Phi} = \frac{300 \times 80}{100{,}000} = \frac{24{,}000}{100{,}000} = 0.24$$

**Assuming standard values:**
- Typical UF for office room with light-coloured walls and ceiling: **0.5**
- Therefore, $MF = 0.24 / 0.5 = 0.48$

**OR (alternate split):**
- If UF = 0.6 (highly efficient luminaire), then MF = 0.24/0.6 = 0.4
- If MF = 0.8 (good maintenance), then UF = 0.24/0.8 = 0.3

**Comment on IS 3646 compliance (1 mark):**
IS 3646 recommends **300–500 lx** for general office work. The design of 300 lx **meets the minimum requirement**, but designers typically target 400–500 lx for comfortable work. To improve, either increase the number of lamps or use higher-flux luminaires.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper, OECET834]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Define **Luminance** and **Luminous Efficacy**. A fluorescent tube is rated **40 W, 3200 lumens**. Calculate its efficacy and comment on its energy efficiency. Compare it with an LED bulb of **9 W producing 1100 lumens**.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Two lamps A and B are placed at the **ceiling level (height 3 m)** in a long corridor. Lamp A is directly above a point P on the floor, while Lamp B is **4 m away horizontally** from P. Both lamps have intensity **100 cd** in all directions. Using the **Inverse Square Law and Cosine Law**, find:
- (i) Illuminance at P due to Lamp A
- (ii) Illuminance at P due to Lamp B
- (iii) Total illuminance at P

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

**Part (a) — Luminance and Efficacy (7 marks):**

**Luminance (L) (1.5 marks):**
Luminance is the luminous intensity per unit projected area of a surface in a given direction. It is the photometric quantity that most closely corresponds to the *visual sensation of brightness* perceived by the human eye.
$$L = \frac{I}{A \cdot \cos\theta}$$
**Unit:** Candela per square metre ($\text{cd}/\text{m}^2$) — also called **nit**.

**Luminous Efficacy (η) (1.5 marks):**
Luminous efficacy is the ratio of total luminous flux emitted by a source to the electrical power consumed by it.
$$\eta = \frac{\Phi}{P}$$
**Unit:** Lumens per Watt (lm/W). It measures how efficiently a source converts electrical energy into visible light.

**Calculation for Fluorescent Tube (1.5 marks):**
$$\eta_{fluor} = \frac{3200 \text{ lm}}{40 \text{ W}} = 80 \text{ lm/W}$$

**Calculation for LED Bulb (1.5 marks):**
$$\eta_{LED} = \frac{1100 \text{ lm}}{9 \text{ W}} \approx 122.2 \text{ lm/W}$$

**Comment on Energy Efficiency (1 mark):**
The LED is approximately **1.53 times more efficient** than the fluorescent tube. For the same light output, the LED consumes significantly less power, making it the preferred choice per **ECBC (Energy Conservation Building Code)** norms and **green building rating systems** (GRIHA, LEED).

**Part (b) — Two-Lamp Illumination Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:** Height $h = 3$ m, Intensity $I_A = I_B = 100$ cd, Horizontal distance for B from P: $x = 4$ m.

**(i) Illuminance at P due to Lamp A (2 marks):**
Lamp A is directly above P, so light falls perpendicular ($\theta = 0°$, $\cos\theta = 1$), distance $d_A = 3$ m.
$$E_A = \frac{I_A \cos\theta}{d_A^2} = \frac{100 \times 1}{3^2} = \frac{100}{9} \approx 11.11 \text{ lx}$$

**(ii) Illuminance at P due to Lamp B (3 marks):**
Distance from Lamp B to P:
$$d_B = \sqrt{3^2 + 4^2} = \sqrt{25} = 5 \text{ m}$$
Angle of incidence: $\cos\theta = h/d_B = 3/5 = 0.6$
$$E_B = \frac{I_B \cos\theta}{d_B^2} = \frac{100 \times 0.6}{5^2} = \frac{60}{25} = 2.4 \text{ lx}$$

**(iii) Total illuminance at P (2 marks):**
$$E_{total} = E_A + E_B = 11.11 + 2.4 = 13.51 \text{ lx}$$

**Verification:** This is well below the IS 3646 recommendation of 100–150 lx for corridors, indicating that this lamp arrangement is **insufficient** and more lamps (or higher-intensity fixtures) are needed for compliance.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the cosine factor:** Many students write $E = I/d^2$ even for *oblique* incidence, losing 2–3 marks. Always check if the light is perpendicular to the surface.
>
> 2. **Wrong solid angle units:** A common error is using steradians for planar angles or vice versa. Remember: **radian** = planar angle, **steradian** = solid (3D) angle.
>
> 3. **Confusing Illuminance with Luminance:** Illuminance (lux) is *incident* light on a surface; Luminance ($\text{cd}/\text{m}^2$) is *emitted/reflected* light *from* a surface. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
>
> 4. **Unit conversion errors:** Converting foot-candles to lux or lumens per square foot — memorize: **1 fc = 10.76 lx**.
>
> 5. **Skipping assumptions in derivations:** The Inverse Square Law *requires* the point-source assumption. Without stating it, your derivation is incomplete and loses marks.
>
> 6. **Not mentioning the 683 lm/W constant:** In any radiometric-photometric conversion, this constant is mandatory.
>
> 7. **Confusing candlepower with candela:** "Candlepower" is an obsolete term; the correct SI term is **candela (cd)**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 — Units of Light**

- **Luminous Flux (Φ)** → **Lumen (lm)** → total light output of a source
- **Luminous Intensity (I)** → **Candela (cd)** → flux per solid angle; SI base unit
- **Illuminance (E)** → **Lux (lx = lm/m²)** → flux incident per unit area; **most important in building design**
- **Luminance (L)** → **cd/m² or Nit** → perceived brightness; depends on viewing angle
- **Luminous Efficacy (η)** → **lm/W** → efficiency of lamp; LEDs best (100–200+ lm/W)
- **Solid Angle (ω)** → **Steradian (sr)** → full sphere = **4π sr** ≈ 12.566 sr
- **Inverse Square Law:** $E = I/d^2$ (point source, perpendicular incidence) — **memorize derivation**
- **Cosine Law:** $E = (I \cos\theta)/d^2$ (oblique incidence) — **memorize derivation**
- **Conversion constant:** 1 W at 555 nm = **683 lm** (most-tested constant in KTU papers)
- **Lumen Method:** $E_{avg} = N \Phi \cdot UF \cdot MF / A$ — **standard design formula**
- **IS 3646 / NBC 2016 levels:** Office 300–500 lx, Drawing 500–750 lx, Corridor 100–150 lx
- **Unit equivalences:** 1 fc = 10.76 lx; 1 fL ≈ 3.426 cd/m²; 1 nit = 1 cd/m²
- **Sail's formula** for spacing-to-height ratio is used for uniform illumination layout
- **Point source assumption** is the biggest limitation of inverse square law
- **Cosine Law maximum** is at normal incidence ($\theta = 0°$); zero at grazing angle ($\theta = 90°$)
- **Seven SI Base Units** include candela — only photometric quantity with base-unit status
- **Modern LEDs** dominate modern building planning with efficacies 5–10× higher than incandescent
- **Glare control** uses luminance ratios (task vs. surroundings should be ≤ 3:1 per IS 3646)
- **Daylight Factor** is the ratio of indoor illuminance to outdoor illuminance under overcast sky — typically 1–5% for residential
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Light
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET834 - FUNDAMENTALS OF BUILDING PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Light

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of building planning, **Light** refers to the electromagnetic radiation in the visible spectrum (wavelength range of approximately **380 nm to 780 nm**) that enables human visual perception of the built environment. From an architectural and building services engineering perspective, lighting design is the discipline concerned with the provision of adequate, comfortable, and energy-efficient illumination through the integrated use of **natural daylighting** and **artificial (electrical) lighting** systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"Light, in building planning, is the controlled admission of natural and artificial illumination into a building interior to satisfy visual performance, visual comfort, safety, aesthetics, and energy conservation requirements of the occupants."*

The science of lighting in buildings rests on four foundational photometric quantities defined by the **International Commission on Illumination (CIE)** and adopted by the **Bureau of Indian Standards (IS 3646)**:

- **Luminous Flux (Φ):** The total quantity of light energy emitted per second by a source, measured in **lumens (lm)**.
- **Luminous Intensity (I):** The luminous flux emitted per unit solid angle in a given direction, measured in **candela (cd)**.
- **Illuminance / Illumination (E):** The luminous flux incident per unit area on a surface, measured in **lux (lx) = lm/m²**.
- **Luminance (L):** The luminous intensity per unit projected area of a surface in a given direction, measured in **cd/m²**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Water Through a Pipe" Analogy**
> Imagine a garden hose spraying water onto a flower bed. The total water flowing out per second is like **luminous flux**. The concentration of water hitting a specific patch of soil is like **illuminance** — the more focused the spray on a smaller area, the more "lit up" that patch becomes. The brightness you actually *see* when you look at a wall (or the wet soil) is like **luminance** — it depends not only on how much light hits the surface but also on how reflective the surface is.

A practical building analogy: think of a classroom window as the "hose" and the desk as the "flower bed." The **size, position, and orientation of the window** determine how much daylight reaches the desk, just as the **diameter, angle, and distance of the hose** determine how much water reaches the soil.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Constant / Standard Metric | Symbol | Value / Standard |
|----------------------------|--------|------------------|
| Speed of light in vacuum | $c$ | $2.998 \times 10^{8}\ \text{m/s}$ |
| Wavelength range of visible light | $\lambda$ | $380\text{–}780\ \text{nm}$ |
| Luminous efficacy of monochromatic 555 nm radiation | $K_m$ | $683\ \text{lm/W}$ |
| Standard outdoor illuminance (overcast day) | $E_{ext}$ | $5000\text{–}10000\ \text{lx}$ |
| Standard outdoor illuminance (clear noon) | $E_{ext}$ | $100000\ \text{lx}$ |
| Minimum illuminance for general office work (IS 3646) | $E_{min}$ | $300\ \text{lx}$ |
| Minimum illuminance for corridor / passage (IS 3646) | $E_{min}$ | $100\ \text{lx}$ |
| Minimum illuminance for detailed drawing work (IS 3646) | $E_{min}$ | $750\ \text{lx}$ |

---

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Questions on "Light" most often test (a) the difference between illumination and luminance, (b) the Daylight Factor concept, and (c) recommended lux levels from IS 3646. Always quote IS code numbers in your answer for full marks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Inverse Square Law of Illumination

This is the **fundamental law** governing point-source lighting and forms the basis of all artificial lighting calculations.

**Statement:** The illuminance on a surface is **inversely proportional to the square of the distance** between the surface and the point source of light, and **directly proportional to the cosine of the angle of incidence** of light on the surface (Lambert's cosine law component).

### 2.1.1 Logical Derivation Steps

- **Step 1 — Point source assumption:** A point source emits luminous flux uniformly in all directions, forming a spherical wavefront of radius $d$ centered on the source.
- **Step 2 — Flux distribution:** The total flux $\Phi$ spreads over the surface area of the sphere, $A = 4\pi d^{2}$. Thus, flux per unit area at distance $d$ is $\Phi / (4\pi d^{2})$.
- **Step 3 — Intensity relation:** Luminous intensity $I = \Phi / (4\pi)$, so illuminance on a surface **normal** to the incident ray is:

$$E_{n} = \frac{I}{d^{2}}$$

- **Step 4 — Oblique incidence (Lambert's Cosine Law):** If the light strikes the surface at angle $\theta$ from the normal, the projected area increases by factor $1/\cos\theta$, reducing illuminance to:

$$E = \frac{I \cos\theta}{d^{2}}$$

- **Step 5 — Engineering intuition:** Doubling the distance from a lamp reduces illuminance to **one-fourth** — this is why ceiling height is critical in lighting design.

## 2.2 Daylight Factor (DF) — The Core Daylighting Metric

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** The **Daylight Factor** is the ratio of illumination at a point on a horizontal work plane inside a building (due to daylight at that moment) to the simultaneous illumination on a horizontal plane outdoors, exposed to an unobstructed overcast sky, expressed as a **percentage**.

$$\text{Daylight Factor (DF)} = \frac{E_{\text{indoor}}}{E_{\text{outdoor}}} \times 100\ \%$$

### 2.2.1 The Three Components of Daylight Factor

The total DF is the sum of three physically distinct contributions:

| Component | Symbol | Description | Typical Contribution |
|-----------|--------|-------------|---------------------|
| Sky Component | SC | Light reaching the point directly from the visible sky | 60–80% |
| Externally Reflected Component | ERC | Light reflected from external surfaces (ground, opposing buildings) before entering | 10–20% |
| Internally Reflected Component | IRC | Light entering the room, reflecting off internal surfaces, then reaching the point | 10–30% |

$$\text{DF}_{\text{total}} = \text{SC} + \text{ERC} + \text{IRC}$$

### 2.2.2 Recommended Daylight Factor Values (IS 2440 & IS 3646)

| Building / Room Type | Recommended DF (%) |
|----------------------|--------------------|
| Kitchens, staircases | $0.5$ to $1.0$ |
| Living rooms, bedrooms | $1.0$ to $2.0$ |
| Classrooms, offices | $2.0$ to $5.0$ |
| Drawing offices, laboratories | $5.0$ to $10.0$ |

## 2.3 Lumen Method of Lighting Design (For Artificial Lighting)

Used to compute the **number of lamps** required to achieve a specified average illuminance in a room.

### 2.3.1 Step-by-Step Logic

- **Step 1:** Compute the room area $A$ and determine mounting height $h$ of the luminaire above the work plane.
- **Step 2:** Compute the **Room Index (k)** which characterises room proportions:

$$k = \frac{L \times W}{h \times (L + W)}$$

where $L$ = length, $W$ = width, $h$ = mounting height above work plane.

- **Step 3:** Determine the **Coefficient of Utilization (CU or $\eta$)** from the luminaire manufacturer's photometric data using room index $k$ and the room surface reflectances (ceiling $\rho_{c}$, wall $\rho_{w}$, floor $\rho_{f}$).
- **Step 4:** Apply the **Maintenance Factor (MF)** — also called Light Loss Factor — typically $0.7$ to $0.8$ for clean environments.
- **Step 5:** Apply the Lumen Formula:

$$N = \frac{E \times A}{\Phi \times \text{CU} \times \text{MF}}$$

where $N$ = number of lamps, $E$ = required illuminance (lux), $A$ = area (m²), $\Phi$ = luminous flux per lamp (lumens).

### 2.3.2 Reflectance Values for Common Surfaces

| Surface / Material | Reflectance ($\rho$) |
|--------------------|---------------------|
| White paint (new) | $0.80$ to $0.85$ |
| Light cream / off-white walls | $0.60$ to $0.75$ |
| Light grey walls | $0.40$ to $0.60$ |
| Medium wood / concrete | $0.20$ to $0.40$ |
| Dark carpet / dark wood | $0.10$ to $0.20$ |
| White ceiling | $0.70$ to $0.80$ |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Energy Code Compliance:** ECBC (Energy Conservation Building Code) of India mandates minimum DF of 1.5% to 2.5% for energy efficiency.
- **Green Building Ratings:** GRIHA and LEED-India award credits for daylight autonomy and views to the outside.
- **Occupant Health:** Adequate daylight reduces eye strain, improves circadian rhythm, and enhances productivity — quantified by WELL Building Standard.
- **Cost Optimisation:** Proper daylighting reduces artificial lighting load by 40–60% during daytime, lowering OPEX.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **must-memorise** formula list for KTU exams. Cover every formula in this table during revision.

| # | Formula | Meaning of Symbols | Application |
|---|---------|--------------------|-------------|
| 1 | $E = \dfrac{\Phi}{A}$ | $E$ = illuminance (lx), $\Phi$ = flux (lm), $A$ = area (m²) | Basic illuminance definition |
| 2 | $I = \dfrac{\Phi}{\omega}$ | $I$ = intensity (cd), $\omega$ = solid angle (sr) | Intensity definition |
| 3 | $E = \dfrac{I}{d^{2}}$ | $d$ = distance (m) | Inverse square law (normal) |
| 4 | $E = \dfrac{I \cos\theta}{d^{2}}$ | $\theta$ = incidence angle from normal | Inverse square law (oblique) |
| 5 | $\text{DF} = \dfrac{E_{\text{in}}}{E_{\text{out}}} \times 100$ | $E_{\text{in}}$, $E_{\text{out}}$ = indoor, outdoor illuminance | Daylight factor |
| 6 | $\text{DF} = \text{SC} + \text{ERC} + \text{IRC}$ | Three components | Daylight factor split |
| 7 | $k = \dfrac{L \times W}{h(L+W)}$ | $L,W$ = room dims, $h$ = mounting height | Room index |
| 8 | $N = \dfrac{E \cdot A}{\Phi \cdot \text{CU} \cdot \text{MF}}$ | $N$ = number of lamps | Lumen method |
| 9 | $\text{SVH Ratio} = \dfrac{\text{Window Area}}{\text{Floor Area}} \times 100$ | Ratio expressed as % | Ventilation-light check |
| 10 | $L = \dfrac{\rho \cdot E}{\pi}$ | $L$ = luminance, $\rho$ = reflectance, $E$ = illuminance | Lambertian reflection |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Inverse Square Law from First Principles

**Starting point:** Consider an isotropic point source $S$ that emits a total luminous flux $\Phi$ uniformly in all $4\pi$ steradians of solid angle.

**Step 1 — Express luminous intensity:**

$$I = \frac{\Phi}{4\pi}$$

*Textual explanation:* By definition, intensity is flux per unit solid angle. Since the source is isotropic, the total solid angle surrounding it is $4\pi$ steradians (the full sphere).

**Step 2 — Flux intercepted by a sphere of radius $d$:**

The sphere of radius $d$ has surface area $A = 4\pi d^{2}$. Since flux spreads uniformly, the flux density (flux per unit area) on this sphere is:

$$\frac{\Phi}{4\pi d^{2}} = \frac{I}{d^{2}}$$

*Textual explanation:* Total flux $\Phi$ is now "diluted" over a much larger area as $d$ grows. Halving $d$ quarters the area, quadrupling the flux density.

**Step 3 — This flux density is illuminance on a surface whose normal points at the source:**

$$E_{n} = \frac{I}{d^{2}} \quad \text{(lux)}$$

**Step 4 — Incorporating Lambert's Cosine Law for oblique incidence:**

If the surface is tilted by angle $\theta$ from the normal-to-ray direction, the **projected area** receiving the same flux increases by $1/\cos\theta$. Since illuminance is flux per unit (projected) area:

$$E = \frac{I \cos\theta}{d^{2}}$$

*Textual explanation:* When the light grazes a surface at a steep angle, the same beam "spreads" over a larger projected area, reducing the local illuminance.

**Step 5 — Final compact form:**

$$\boxed{E = \frac{I \cos\theta}{d^{2}}}$$

**Sanity check:** If $\theta = 0°$ (light perpendicular to surface), $\cos 0° = 1$, giving maximum illuminance. If $\theta = 90°$ (grazing), $\cos 90° = 0$, giving zero illuminance. ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Number of Lamps via Lumen Method

**Problem statement:** Design a lighting scheme for a drafting hall of size $12\ \text{m} \times 8\ \text{m} \times 3.5\ \text{m}$ (height). Use fluorescent tubes of $40\ \text{W}$ rating, each delivering $2400\ \text{lm}$, mounted $2.5\ \text{m}$ above the work plane. Required illuminance $E = 500\ \text{lx}$. Take CU = $0.50$ and MF = $0.80$.

**Step 1 — Compute floor area $A$:**

$$A = L \times W = 12 \times 8 = 96\ \text{m}^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Room Index $k$:**

$$k = \frac{L \times W}{h \times (L + W)} = \frac{12 \times 8}{2.5 \times (12 + 8)} = \frac{96}{2.5 \times 20} = \frac{96}{50} = 1.92$$

**Step 3 — Identify the required inputs:**

- $\Phi = 2400\ \text{lm}$ (per lamp)
- $E = 500\ \text{lx}$
- $\text{CU} = 0.50$
- $\text{MF} = 0.80$

**Step 4 — Apply the Lumen Formula:**

$$N = \frac{E \times A}{\Phi \times \text{CU} \times \text{MF}}$$

$$N = \frac{500 \times 96}{2400 \times 0.50 \times 0.80}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the denominator:**

$$2400 \times 0.50 \times 0.80 = 2400 \times 0.40 = 960$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the numerator:**

$$500 \times 96 = 48000$$

**Step 7 — Final division:**

$$N = \frac{48000}{960} = 50\ \text{lamps}$$

**Step 8 — Sanity check on spacing:**

Total tubes $= 50$. If arranged in rows across the $12\ \text{m}$ length with $2\ \text{m}$ spacing, we can fit $12/2 = 6$ tubes per row. Number of rows = $50/6 \approx 8.3$, so adopt $9$ rows $\times$ $6$ tubes $= 54$ tubes (slight over-design for safety margin). ✓

**Answer:** $50$ lamps required (round up to next practical arrangement: $54$).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Daylight Factor for a Point in a Room

**Scenario:** A point on a work plane $P$ is located at a horizontal distance of $4\ \text{m}$ from a vertical window of clear glazing. The window head height is $2.5\ \text{m}$ above the work plane; the sill is at $0.9\ \text{m}$. Outdoor illuminance is $E_{\text{out}} = 8000\ \text{lx}$ under overcast sky.

**Step 1 — Define the angular subtense of visible sky:**

The angle subtended at point $P$ by the sky patch visible through the window (in the vertical plane) is:

$$\alpha = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{H_{h}}{D}\right) - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{H_{s}}{D}\right)$$

where $H_{h}$ = head height above work plane, $H_{s}$ = sill height above work plane, $D$ = horizontal distance from window.

**Step 2 — Substitute the values:**

$$\alpha = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{2.5}{4}\right) - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.9}{4}\right)$$

$$\alpha = \tan^{-1}(0.625) - \tan^{-1}(0.225)$$

$$\alpha = 32.00° - 12.68° = 19.32°$$

**Step 3 — Sky luminance assumption (CIE Overcast Sky):**

For a CIE standard overcast sky, the sky luminance at angle $\gamma$ from the zenith is:

$$L_{\gamma} = L_{z}\cdot \frac{1 + 2\sin\gamma}{3}$$

For typical CIE sky with $L_{z} = 6000\ \text{cd/m}^{2}$ averaged, and approximate $\gamma = 45°$ (mid-sky), $\sin\gamma \approx 0.707$:

$$L_{\gamma} \approx 6000 \times \frac{1 + 1.414}{3} = 6000 \times 0.805 = 4830\ \text{cd/m}^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Indoor illuminance due to SC (simplified, using Waldram's diagram approximation):**

For a vertical window, the SC component is approximated by:

$$\text{SC} = \frac{L_{sky} \cdot \omega_{w}}{E_{out}} \times \pi$$

where $\omega_{w}$ is the solid angle subtended by the window. For a long window, use planar angle $\alpha$:

$$\omega_{w} \approx \alpha \times W_{w} / D$$

Let window width $W_{w} = 3\ \text{m}$, so $\omega_{w} = 19.32° \times (\pi/180) \times 3 / 4 = 0.253\ \text{sr}$ (approx).

**Step 5 — Compute SC:**

$$\text{SC} = \frac{4830 \times 0.253 \times \pi}{8000} \times 100\%$$

$$\text{SC} = \frac{4830 \times 0.795}{8000} \times 100\% = \frac{3840}{8000} \times 100\% \approx 48\%$$

**Step 6 — Add ERC and IRC (assumed):**

Assume $\text{ERC} = 5\%$ and $\text{IRC} = 2\%$ (light-coloured walls):

$$\text{DF} = \text{SC} + \text{ERC} + \text{IRC} = 48 + 5 + 2 = 55\%$$

**Step 7 — Sanity check:** DF of 55% is very high — the point is very close to the window. Per IS 3646, this is acceptable for detailed drawing work (5–10% minimum). For a 4 m depth, this is unusually bright, indicating good daylight penetration. ✓

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Quick Comparison Table

| Parameter | Natural Lighting | Artificial Lighting |
|-----------|------------------|---------------------|
| Source | Sun + Sky | Electric lamps (LED, FL, HID) |
| Availability | Daytime only, weather-dependent | 24×7, controllable |
| Heat gain | Significant (solar heat gain) | Small (except incandescent) |
| Colour quality | Excellent (full spectrum) | Varies (CRI 80–95 for good LEDs) |
| Glare control | Difficult (needs shading) | Easy (diffusers, optics) |
| Initial cost | Moderate (windows, shading) | Higher (fixtures, wiring) |
| Running cost | Zero | Recurring electricity |
| Energy use | Free (passive) | $0.5\text{–}2.0\ \text{W/m}^{2/lx}$ |
| Design metric | Daylight Factor (%) | Illuminance (lux) |
| Code reference | IS 2440, IS 1671, NBC 2016 | IS 3646 (Parts 1–3) |
| Best practice | Glare control, uniform DF $\geq$ 2% | Layered lighting, CU optimisation |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Lighting Design Decision Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Room Function] --> B{Available Daylight?}
    B -- Yes, Adequate --> C[Set Daylight Target DF greater than 2 percent]
    B -- No or Insufficient --> D[Plan Pure Artificial Lighting]
    C --> E[Design Window: Size, Position, Glazing]
    E --> F[Compute DF at Critical Point]
    F --> G{DF within IS 3646 Range?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Proceed to Artificial Supplement]
    G -- No --> I[Adjust Window Size or Add Clerestory]
    I --> F
    D --> J[Compute Required Illuminance E in lux]
    H --> J
    J --> K[Select Luminaire Type and CU]
    K --> L[Apply Lumen Method: N equals E times A over Phi times CU times MF]
    L --> M[Determine Layout and Spacing]
    M --> N[Verify Uniformity Ratio 0.7 to 0.8]
    N --> O[Finalise: Glare Check, Energy Check]
    O --> P[End: Issue Lighting Layout]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Components of Daylight Factor

```mermaid
graph LR
    SkyComp[SKY COMPONENT SC] --> SumFun[Total Daylight Factor]
    ExtRef[EXTERNALLY REFLECTED COMPONENT ERC] --> SumFun
    IntRef[INTERNALLY REFLECTED COMPONENT IRC] --> SumFun
    SumFun --> CriticalPoint[Critical Point on Work Plane]
    SkyComp -. 60 to 80 percent .-> SumFun
    ExtRef -. 10 to 20 percent .-> SumFun
    IntRef -. 10 to 30 percent .-> SumFun
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Inverse Square Law Visualisation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SourceA[Point Source Lamp at Distance d] --> Normal1[Normal Incidence: theta equals 0]
    SourceA --> Oblique1[Oblique Incidence: theta equals 45]
    SourceA --> Grazing1[Grazing Incidence: theta equals 80]
    Normal1 --> Eq1[E equals I over d squared]
    Oblique1 --> Eq2[E equals I cos 45 over d squared equals 0.707 I over d squared]
    Grazing1 --> Eq3[E equals I cos 80 over d squared equals 0.174 I over d squared]
    Eq1 --> Comparison[Distance Doubles: Illuminance drops to 25 percent]
    Eq2 --> Comparison
    Eq3 --> Comparison
```

## 4.4 Schematic Description: Room Cross-Section for Daylight Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Drawing:** A physical room cross-section showing the angular subtense of the sky patch is best drawn by hand on graph paper. The conceptual parameters are summarised below for reference during the exam.

| Parameter | Description | Typical Value |
|-----------|-------------|---------------|
| $D$ | Horizontal depth from window to point | $1.0\ \text{m}$ to $6.0\ \text{m}$ |
| $H_h$ | Head height of window above work plane | $2.0\ \text{m}$ to $2.5\ \text{m}$ |
| $H_s$ | Sill height above work plane | $0.8\ \text{m}$ to $1.0\ \text{m}$ |
| $\alpha$ | Angular subtense of visible sky | $15°$ to $30°$ |
| $\theta$ | Angle of incidence at work plane | $30°$ to $60°$ |

## 4.5 Mermaid Flowchart: Lumen Method Calculation Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Step 1: Compute Room Area A equals L times W] --> A2[Step 2: Compute Room Index k equals L W over h times L plus W]
    A2 --> A3[Step 3: Look up CU from Luminaire Table using k and Reflectances]
    A3 --> A4[Step 4: Apply Maintenance Factor MF typically 0.7 to 0.8]
    A4 --> A5[Step 5: Compute N equals E times A over Phi times CU times MF]
    A5 --> A6[Step 6: Round N up to whole number]
    A6 --> A7[Step 7: Determine Layout: Spacing to Mounting Height Ratio S/H less than 1.5]
    A7 --> A8[End: Final Number and Layout of Lamps]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Daylight Factor. State the recommended DF values for a classroom and a drawing office as per IS 3646. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** The Daylight Factor (DF) is the ratio of the illumination at a point on a horizontal work plane inside a room, due to daylight at that moment, to the simultaneous illumination on a horizontal plane outdoors, exposed to an unobstructed overcast sky. It is expressed as a **percentage**.

$$\text{DF} = \frac{E_{\text{indoor}}}{E_{\text{outdoor}}} \times 100\ \%$$

**Recommended values per IS 3646:**

- **Classroom:** DF $= 2.0\%$ to $5.0\%$
- **Drawing office:** DF $= 5.0\%$ to $10.0\%$ (requires very high illuminance for detailed work)

The DF comprises three components: **Sky Component (SC)**, **Externally Reflected Component (ERC)**, and **Internally Reflected Component (IRC)**.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between Illumination and Luminance. State their SI units. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Illumination (Illuminance) | Luminance |
|----------|----------------------------|-----------|
| Symbol | $E$ | $L$ |
| Definition | Luminous flux incident **per unit area** on a surface | Luminous intensity per unit **projected area** of a surface in a given direction |
| Physical meaning | How much light **falls** on a surface | How much light is **emitted or reflected** from a surface (brightness) |
| SI Unit | **Lux (lx)** = lm/m² | **Candela per square metre (cd/m²)** |
| Measured by | Lux meter / photometer at the surface | Luminance meter pointed at the surface |
| Example | 500 lx on a study desk | A white wall reflecting 200 cd/m² looks "bright" |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part B questions offer **internal choice**. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each part (a) carries 7 marks and part (b) carries 7 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** State and explain the **Inverse Square Law** of illumination. A lamp of luminous intensity $400\ \text{cd}$ is suspended at a height of $4\ \text{m}$ above a work plane. Find the illuminance on the work plane (i) directly below the lamp, and (ii) at a point $3\ \text{m}$ away horizontally. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the **Lumen Method** of artificial lighting design. A drawing hall of dimensions $20\ \text{m} \times 10\ \text{m} \times 4\ \text{m}$ (height) is to be illuminated to a level of $750\ \text{lx}$. Using $40\ \text{W}$ fluorescent tubes, each of $2400\ \text{lm}$, mounted at a height of $3\ \text{m}$ above the work plane, determine the number of tubes required. Take CU = $0.45$ and MF = $0.75$. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Question A (a):

**Statement of Inverse Square Law:**
*"The illuminance on a surface, due to a point source of light, is directly proportional to the luminous intensity of the source and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between the source and the surface."*

For oblique incidence, the cosine law is also included:

$$E = \frac{I \cos\theta}{d^{2}}$$

**(i) Directly below the lamp:**

Here, $\theta = 0°$ and $d = 4\ \text{m}$:

$$E_1 = \frac{I \cos 0°}{d^{2}} = \frac{400 \times 1}{(4)^{2}} = \frac{400}{16} = 25\ \text{lx}$$

**(ii) At point 3 m horizontally away:**

The slant distance $d = \sqrt{(3)^{2} + (4)^{2}} = \sqrt{9 + 16} = \sqrt{25} = 5\ \text{m}$

The angle $\theta$ with respect to the vertical (normal to horizontal work plane) is given by $\cos\theta = h/d = 4/5 = 0.8$:

$$E_2 = \frac{I \cos\theta}{d^{2}} = \frac{400 \times 0.8}{(5)^{2}} = \frac{320}{25} = 12.8\ \text{lx}$$

**Final Answers:** $E_1 = 25\ \text{lx}$; $E_2 = 12.8\ \text{lx}$

---

#### Model Solution for Question A (b):

**Lumen Method Explanation:**

The lumen method (also called the **Luminous Flux Method**) is used to calculate the number of lamps required to achieve a desired average illuminance level in a room. It accounts for room geometry (via **Room Index $k$**), luminaire efficiency (via **Coefficient of Utilization CU**), and depreciation (via **Maintenance Factor MF**). The standard formula is:

$$N = \frac{E \times A}{\Phi \times \text{CU} \times \text{MF}}$$

**Step 1 — Floor area:**

$$A = L \times W = 20 \times 10 = 200\ \text{m}^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Room Index $k$:**

$$k = \frac{L \times W}{h \times (L + W)} = \frac{20 \times 10}{3 \times (20 + 10)} = \frac{200}{3 \times 30} = \frac{200}{90} = 2.22$$

**Step 3 — Apply Lumen Formula:**

$$N = \frac{E \times A}{\Phi \times \text{CU} \times \text{MF}} = \frac{750 \times 200}{2400 \times 0.45 \times 0.75}$$

**Step 4 — Denominator evaluation:**

$$2400 \times 0.45 \times 0.75 = 2400 \times 0.3375 = 810$$

**Step 5 — Numerator evaluation:**

$$750 \times 200 = 150000$$

**Step 6 — Final division:**

$$N = \frac{150000}{810} = 185.18 \approx 186\ \text{lamps}$$

**Final Answer:** $\mathbf{186\ \text{fluorescent\ tubes\ required}}$ (round up to next whole number).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Alternative Set]`

**(a)** What is **Daylight Factor**? Explain the **three components** of daylight factor with the help of a neat sketch. State the recommended DF for various rooms as per IS 3646. (7 marks)

**(b)** Define the following terms with reference to lighting: (i) Luminous Flux, (ii) Luminous Intensity, (iii) Luminance, (iv) Glare, (v) Colour Rendering Index, (vi) Solid Angle, (vii) Maintenance Factor. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Question B (a):

**Definition (as above):** DF is the ratio of indoor illuminance at a point on the work plane to the outdoor horizontal illuminance under overcast sky, expressed as a percentage.

**Three Components:**

1. **Sky Component (SC):** Light reaching the reference point **directly** from the sky patch visible through the window. It is the dominant contributor (60–80% of total DF). It depends on the **angular height** of the window, the **glazing transmittance**, and the **distance of the point from the window**.

2. **Externally Reflected Component (ERC):** Light from the sky that first strikes **external surfaces** (ground, pavement, adjacent buildings) and is then reflected through the window into the room. It typically contributes 10–20% of DF. Light-coloured external paving increases ERC.

3. **Internally Reflected Component (IRC):** Light that has entered the room and then undergone **multiple reflections** off the internal surfaces (walls, ceiling, floor) before reaching the reference point. It depends on the **interior reflectance values** and **room geometry**, contributing 10–30% of DF.

**Mathematical sum:**

$$\text{DF} = \text{SC} + \text{ERC} + \text{IRC}$$

**Recommended DF values per IS 3646:**

| Room Type | Recommended DF (%) |
|-----------|--------------------|
| Staircases, kitchens | $0.5$ to $1.0$ |
| Living rooms, bedrooms | $1.0$ to $2.0$ |
| Classrooms, offices, hospital wards | $2.0$ to $5.0$ |
| Drawing offices, precision workshops | $5.0$ to $10.0$ |

---

#### Model Solution for Question B (b):

**(i) Luminous Flux ($\Phi$):** The total quantity of visible light energy emitted per second by a source. **Unit: lumen (lm).** It measures the total "amount of light" produced regardless of direction.

**(ii) Luminous Intensity ($I$):** The luminous flux emitted per unit solid angle in a specified direction. **Unit: candela (cd) = lm/sr.** It describes how **concentrated** the light is in a particular direction.

**(iii) Luminance ($L$):** The luminous intensity per unit projected area of a surface in a given direction. **Unit: cd/m² (also called "nit").** It is the physical correlate of perceived **brightness** — what the eye actually sees.

**(iv) Glare:** The sensation of discomfort or loss of visual performance caused by excessively bright light sources or large luminance contrasts within the field of view. Glare is reduced by diffusers, louvres, indirect lighting, and matte surface finishes.

**(v) Colour Rendering Index (CRI or $R_a$):** A measure (0–100) of how accurately a light source renders the colours of objects compared to a reference source (typically daylight or blackbody). **CRI $\geq$ 80** is required for office and residential spaces; **CRI $\geq$ 90** for art galleries, hospitals, and textile inspection.

**(vi) Solid Angle ($\omega$):** The two-dimensional angle in three-dimensional space, subtended at a point by a surface. Measured in **steradians (sr)**. The full sphere subtends $4\pi$ sr at its centre.

**(vii) Maintenance Factor (MF):** The ratio of illuminance on the work plane after a specified period of lamp use to the initial illuminance. It accounts for lamp lumen depreciation, luminaire dirt accumulation, and room surface soiling. **Typical value: $0.7$ to $0.8$.**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make — and where you lose marks:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing units:** Writing "illuminance" when you mean "luminance" (or vice versa). Always state both the **symbol** AND the **unit** in the definition.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting to express DF as a percentage:** Writing DF as a decimal (e.g., 0.05 instead of 5%) loses 1 mark. Always write the multiplication by 100 explicitly.
> 
> 3. **Wrong distance in Inverse Square Law:** Using **slant distance** $d$ in the numerator for normal incidence formula $E = I/d^{2}$ only when the surface **normal** points at the source. For horizontal work plane and ceiling lamp, you need to use $\cos\theta$ via $E = I\cos\theta/d^{2}$.
> 
> 4. **Lumen Method without showing Room Index computation:** Examiners expect the full step — $L$, $W$, $h$, then $k$ — even if the value of $k$ is not used explicitly in the formula. It demonstrates understanding.
> 
> 5. **Using outdated reflectance values:** Always quote IS 3646 (latest version) for the recommended illuminance table. Older editions had slightly different values.
> 
> 6. **Mixing natural and artificial lighting units:** Daylight is in DF (%), artificial lighting is in lux (lx). Do not convert between them.
> 
> 7. **Not stating assumptions:** In Daylight Factor problems, always state the assumed CIE overcast sky condition and the standard outdoor illuminance value.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute revision checklist for the topic "Light" — Module 2**

- **Four photometric quantities:** Flux (lm), Intensity (cd), Illuminance (lx), Luminance (cd/m²) — know the symbols, units, and definitions by heart.
- **Inverse Square Law:** $E = I \cos\theta / d^{2}$ — applicable to point sources only. Doubling distance quarters illuminance.
- **Lambert's Cosine Law:** Illuminance reduces by factor $\cos\theta$ when light strikes at angle $\theta$ from normal.
- **Daylight Factor (DF):** $\text{DF} = (E_{\text{in}} / E_{\text{out}}) \times 100\%$ — three components SC, ERC, IRC summing to total DF.
- **Recommended DF:** Staircase 0.5–1%, Living 1–2%, Classroom 2–5%, Drawing office 5–10% (IS 3646 / IS 2440).
- **Room Index $k$:** $k = LW / [h(L+W)]$ — used in CU lookup tables.
- **Lumen Method formula:** $N = E \cdot A / (\Phi \cdot \text{CU} \cdot \text{MF})$ — know the meaning of every symbol.
- **Maintenance Factor (MF):** Usually $0.7$ to $0.8$ for clean interiors.
- **Coefficient of Utilization (CU):** Depends on luminaire type, room index $k$, and surface reflectances ($\rho_c$, $\rho_w$, $\rho_f$).
- **Sky Component** is the largest contributor to DF (60–80%); light-coloured internal walls boost IRC.
- **CIE Overcast Sky** is the standard reference sky for DF calculations.
- **Glare control:** Use diffusers, indirect lighting, louvres, and matte finishes; avoid direct view of bright lamps.
- **CRI:** $\geq 80$ for offices, $\geq 90$ for hospitals/art galleries.
- **Energy codes:** ECBC mandates DF $\geq 1.5\%$ in office spaces for credit.
- **IS Codes to memorise:** IS 3646 (illumination values), IS 2440 (daylight calculations), NBC 2016 (Part 8 on lighting).
- **Conversion reminder:** $1\ \text{lux} = 1\ \text{lumen per square metre}$; $1\ \text{foot-candle} = 10.76\ \text{lux}$ (FPS to SI).
- **Sanity check for Inverse Square Law:** At $d = 0.5\ \text{m}$, illuminance is $4\times$ that at $d = 1\ \text{m}$.
- **Sanity check for Lumen Method:** Number of lamps always **rounded up**, never down — under-design is a safety issue.

---

> **END OF NOTES — Module 2, Topic: Light**

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Vision in Building Planning** refers to the deliberate and conscious design of architectural spaces to control, channel, and enhance the human visual experience—both from the exterior looking inward and from the interior looking outward. It is the planning principle that governs **what a person sees, from where, how far, and through what sequence** as they move through or around a building.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, *Vision and Buildings* is treated as a sub-domain of **Aesthetics in Building Design** and falls under the broader umbrella of **Principles of Architectural Composition**. It deals with the geometric, psychological, and physiological aspects of human sight as applied to spatial organization.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> Vision is one of the five classical senses that architects MUST primarily design *for*. Approximately **80% of all environmental information** received by the human brain is visual. A building that fails to handle vision properly is, by definition, a *visually dysfunctional* building.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine walking through a **museum gallery**. The architect has:
- Placed the paintings at a specific **height** (so your eyes naturally fall on them).
- Arranged them along a **straight line** (so your gaze flows from one to the next).
- Avoided putting a pillar or door in your **line of sight** (so nothing breaks the visual flow).
- Made the corridor narrow enough to **frame the artwork** (so peripheral distractions vanish).

That entire experience—the framed view, the guided eye, the absence of visual obstacles—is what *Vision and Buildings* means in planning terms. The architect has **engineered your sight-line**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Definition:**
> Vision in building planning = "Designing the path your eyes take inside and around a building, so that you see the *right* things, in the *right* order, from the *right* distance."

## 1.3 Key Parameters of Vision in Buildings

The three pillars that govern vision-based design are:

| Parameter | Description | Typical Planning Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Eye Level** | Height at which a standing human sees straight ahead | **1.5 m to 1.6 m** (standard) |
| **Visual Angle** | Cone of comfortable human vision without head/eye movement | **60° (vertical)** and **120° (horizontal)** |
| **Critical Distance** | Maximum distance at which facial expressions are recognizable | **~24 m to 30 m** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constant to Remember:**
> The **standard human eye level** is universally taken as **1.5 m (1500 mm)** above the finished floor level for all building planning calculations. This is the *Datum Line* for vision-based design.
