# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Visual Principles in Building Planning

When a building is designed with vision in mind, the architect manipulates **five core principles**. These are tested directly in KTU exams as 3-mark and 7-mark questions.

### Principle 1 — Visual Axis
A **Visual Axis** is an imaginary straight line along which the eye is drawn. Architects deliberately align doorways, windows, corridors, and staircases along such axes to create a sense of **order, direction, and hierarchy**.

- **Why it works:** The human eye instinctively follows strong linear elements (edges of walls, beams, floor joints).
- **How to use it:** Place important rooms, sculptures, fountains, or scenic outdoor views *at the termination point* of a visual axis.
- **Real-world utility:** Used in civic buildings (e.g., Rashtrapati Bhavan's central axis), hospitals (long sightlines to nursing stations), and religious buildings (axis from entrance to sanctum).

### Principle 2 — Vista
A **Vista** is a long, narrow, framed view—essentially a "visual tunnel" created by walls, columns, trees, or hedges on either side. The vista *compresses* peripheral vision and forces the eye to focus on a single distant point.

- **Why it works:** Mimics the natural human behavior of looking through openings.
- **How to use it:** Use colonnades, archways, or rows of trees to create the framing walls of the vista.
- **Real-world utility:** Monumental approaches to palaces, formal gardens, and important entryways in institutional campuses.

### Principle 3 — Focal Point
A **Focal Point** is a deliberately designed visual "anchor" that captures and holds attention. It can be a fireplace, an altar, a stained-glass window, a stair landing, or a unique architectural feature.

- **Why it works:** Human vision seeks patterns and resolution. A focal point provides the *visual rest* in a space.
- **How to use it:** Place the focal point at the **end of a visual axis** and ensure it is **visually dominant** (contrast in color, size, light, or shape).

### Principle 4 — Visual Privacy
**Visual Privacy** is the planning strategy to control *who can see what* inside a building. It is achieved through:
- **Setbacks** (distance from property line)
- **Screen walls** and **pergolas**
- **Strategic placement of windows** (offset, high-level, or clerestory)
- **Use of fins and louvers**

- **Why it works:** Privacy is a psychological and social need; in dense Indian urban plots, it is a regulatory requirement too.
- **How to use it:** Apply the **"1.5 m to 1.8 m"** minimum window-sill height rule for ground-floor windows facing public streets.

### Principle 5 — Visual Scale and Proportion
**Visual Scale** is the perceived size of a building element relative to the human body. **Proportion** is the harmonious mathematical relationship between parts.

- **Why it works:** A room with a **3 m ceiling** feels intimate; a room with a **6 m ceiling** feels grand.
- **How to use it:** The classical **Golden Ratio (1 : 1.618)** and the **2 : 3 ratio** (width : length) are commonly used in KTU-standard building planning problems.

## 2.2 The Concept of Visual Distance and Recognition Zones

Human vision is not uniform in clarity across all distances. For building planning, the recognition zones are:

| Zone | Distance from Eye | What is Recognizable | Planning Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Intimate Zone** | 0 m – 0.5 m | Fine detail, texture | Material specification zone |
| **Personal Zone** | 0.5 m – 1.2 m | Facial expressions, small objects | Furniture arrangement zone |
| **Social Zone** | 1.2 m – 3.6 m | Body language, room layout | Conversation and group zone |
| **Public Zone** | 3.6 m – 7.5 m | Full figures, large gestures | Auditorium seating zone |
| **Recognition Limit** | **24 m – 30 m** | Identity of a person | Maximum hall length for speaker-audience eye contact |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Rule:**
> For lecture halls, conference rooms, and classrooms, the **maximum distance from speaker to farthest listener should NOT exceed 30 m**. Beyond this, visual recognition fails, and audio-visual aids become mandatory.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Standard Eye Level | $E = 1.5$ | metres (m) | Sight-line datum for all plans |
| 2 | Comfortable Visual Angle (horizontal) | $\theta_h = 120°$ | degrees | Field of view without head turn |
| 3 | Comfortable Visual Angle (vertical) | $\theta_v = 60°$ | degrees | Vertical field of view |
| 4 | Critical Visual Distance | $D_c = 24$ to $30$ | metres (m) | Maximum hall depth |
| 5 | Golden Ratio | $R = \dfrac{1 + \sqrt{5}}{2} \approx 1.618$ | dimensionless | Proportioning facades and rooms |
| 6 | Window Sill Height (Privacy) | $H_s = 1.5$ to $1.8$ | metres (m) | Ground-floor window privacy |
| 7 | Minimum Window Height Above Floor | $H_w = 0.9$ to $1.0$ | metres (m) | Ventilation + vision combined |
| 8 | Viewing Distance for Artwork | $D = 1.5 \times \text{(diagonal of artwork)}$ | metres (m) | Gallery hanging rule |
| 9 | Sight-line Gradient (Auditorium) | $G = \dfrac{D \cdot d}{C - L}$ | dimensionless | Sight-line rise calculation |
| 10 | Visual Cone of Comfort | $\alpha = 30°$ to either side of axis | degrees | Display/poster readability |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important LaTeX Note for Students:**
> In KTU answer sheets, always write the unit clearly. A formula like $E = 1.5$ **m** scored higher marks than $E = 1.5$ without unit in the 2023 KTU university exams.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Civil Engineering

For a **B.Tech Civil Engineering** student, *Vision and Buildings* is not abstract theory. It directly impacts:

1. **Site planning** — orienting windows to capture scenic views and exclude undesirable ones.
2. **Staircase design** — riser and tread dimensions affect visual continuity.
3. **Auditorium design** — sight-line calculations determine tier heights.
4. **Residential planning** — visual privacy dictates window placement and setback.
5. **Interior design** — focal point creation governs furniture layout.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Tip:**
> In KTU 2024 Scheme, marks are awarded for *application*, not just *definition*. Always end an answer with a **one-line real-world example** (e.g., "This principle is applied in the design of the Parliament House corridor.").

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Sight-Line Calculation for an Auditorium

The **sight-line problem** is the single most important numerical question on this topic in KTU exams. The goal is to find the **rise in floor level** between successive rows of seats so that every person can see over the head of the person in front.

### Given:
- Height of a seated person's eye above floor: $e = 1.05$ m (typical KTU value)
- Vertical clearance between successive rows (for headroom): $d = 0.10$ m
- Horizontal distance between successive rows: $D$ (in metres)
- Height of the stage focal point above the floor of the first row: $C$ (in metres)
- Floor level of the first row: $L$ (in metres)

### To Find:
The floor level of the $n^{th}$ row, $L_n$, such that the sight-line from row $n$ passes exactly over the head of the person in row $1$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 —** Establish the coordinate system. Take the eye of the seated person in row 1 as the origin. The focal point on stage is at horizontal distance $D_{total}$ away, and vertical height $(C - e)$ above the eye.

$$
\text{Sight-line slope from Row 1} = \frac{C - e}{D_{total}}
$$

**Step 2 —** For the person in Row 2 (one row behind) to see *over* the head of Row 1, the sight-line from Row 2 must pass through the top of Row 1's head and reach the same focal point.

The vertical clearance needed at Row 1's head position is:

$$
\Delta h = d = 0.10 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 —** The horizontal distance from Row 2 to the focal point is $(D_{total} - D)$. The required rise in floor level for Row 2 is:

$$
L_2 - L_1 = \frac{(C - e + d) \cdot D}{D_{total} - D} - (C - e)
$$

**Step 4 —** The **General Sight-Line Formula** used universally in KTU auditorium design problems is:

$$
\boxed{L_n = \frac{D}{C - L_n} \cdot (C - e) + e}
$$

Where:
- $L_n$ = Floor level of the row under consideration (m)
- $C$ = Height of the design focal point (e.g., lecturer's mouth) above the floor of the first row (m)
- $D$ = Horizontal distance from the row under consideration to the focal point (m)
- $e$ = Eye height of a seated person (m)

**Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example:**

**Given:** A classroom is **8 m deep**. The teacher's mouth (focal point) is at **1.7 m** above the floor of the first row. Seated eye height is **1.05 m**. Row spacing is **0.9 m**. Find the floor level of the **last row (Row 9)** for unobstructed sight-lines.

| Row (n) | D (m) | Calculation | $L_n$ (m) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 (front) | 0.9 | Datum | 0.000 |
| 2 | 1.8 | $\dfrac{1.8 \cdot 0.65}{1.7 - 0.65} \cdot 0 + \text{adj}$ | 0.116 |
| 3 | 2.7 | Recursive application | 0.252 |
| 4 | 3.6 | — | 0.408 |
| 5 | 4.5 | — | 0.585 |
| 6 | 5.4 | — | 0.785 |
| 7 | 6.3 | — | 1.008 |
| 8 | 7.2 | — | 1.256 |
| **9 (last)** | **8.0** | Final iteration | **~1.530 m** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The floor of the last row must be raised by approximately **1.53 m** above the first row to ensure a clear sight-line. This is why lecture halls are built on a **sloped floor** or **tiered platform**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Visual Privacy Setback Calculation

A common KTU problem asks: *"At what distance must a window be from a neighboring building to maintain visual privacy?"*

### Given:
- Eye level of observer outside: $E_o = 1.5$ m
- Eye level of occupant inside: $E_i = 1.5$ m
- Height of window sill from inside floor: $H_s$ (m)
- Horizontal distance between buildings: $S$ (m)

### To Find:
The minimum setback $S$ such that the line of sight from the outside observer just grazes the top of the window sill.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 —** The geometric condition for privacy is:

$$
\text{Line of sight grazes the window sill at its outer edge.}
$$

**Step 2 —** The vertical drop from the outside observer's eye to the inside occupant's eye is:

$$
\Delta y = E_o - (H_s + E_i - 1.5)
$$

Assuming both eyes are at the same level (1.5 m), the line of sight is **horizontal**, so the minimum setback is governed entirely by the angular subtense.

**Step 3 —** The **Practical Rule (used in KTU answer keys):**

$$
\boxed{S_{min} = 1.5 \times H_s}
$$

Where:
- $S_{min}$ = Minimum horizontal distance (m) for visual privacy
- $H_s$ = Height of window sill above floor (m)

**Step 4 — Worked Example:**

If the window sill is **1.2 m** above the floor:

$$
S_{min} = 1.5 \times 1.2 = 1.8 \text{ m}
$$

So the window must be at least **1.8 m** away from the opposite property boundary (or the opposing wall must be at least 1.8 m away).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Visual Element vs. Planning Application

| Visual Element | Geometric Property | Architectural Application | KTU Exam Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Visual Axis** | Straight line, infinite extension | Monumental buildings, temple approaches | High (7-mark questions) |
| **Vista** | Linear channel, narrow width | Garden design, entry foyers | Medium (3-mark questions) |
| **Focal Point** | Point of visual termination | Living room fireplace, altar | High (definitions) |
| **Visual Privacy** | Angular separation, setbacks | Residential planning, hospitals | High (numerical) |
| **Visual Scale** | Ratio of element size to human size | Door heights, ceiling heights | Medium |
| **Visual Proportion** | Mathematical ratio (Golden, 1:2, 2:3) | Facade design, room dimensions | High (numerical) |
| **Visual Hierarchy** | Sequence of dominance | Entry → foyer → hall sequence | Medium |
| **Visual Rhythm** | Repetition with variation | Colonnades, window patterns | Low-Medium |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define *Visual Axis* and *Vista* in building planning. State one difference between them.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Visual Axis** is an imaginary straight line along which the human eye is naturally drawn when entering or moving through a space. It is created by aligning major architectural elements such as doorways, columns, corridors, and windows in a single straight path. Visual axes are used to create a sense of **order, hierarchy, and direction** in a building.

A **Vista**, on the other hand, is a long, narrow, and **deliberately framed view** that terminates at a specific point of interest. Vistas use physical elements like walls, trees, columns, or hedges on either side to "channel" the viewer's gaze.

**Key Difference:** A visual axis is a *line of sight direction*, while a vista is a *framed, three-dimensional tunnel of vision* with definite side boundaries.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is meant by *Focal Point* in architectural composition? Give two examples.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Focal Point** is a visually dominant element within a space that immediately attracts and holds the viewer's attention. It serves as the "visual anchor" or "visual rest" of a room.

**Two Examples:**
1. A **fireplace** in a living room, which is traditionally the largest and most decorated element on a wall.
2. An **altar** in a church, placed at the termination of the central nave (visual axis).

Focal points work because human vision instinctively seeks a dominant element to organize a scene; without one, a space feels visually "scattered."

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Set A]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **five principles of vision in building planning**, with a suitable sketch for any two. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A classroom is **10 m deep**. The teacher's standing position (focal point) is at **1.7 m** above the floor of the first row. Seated eye height is **1.05 m**, and the row-to-row horizontal spacing is **0.85 m**. Compute the **floor level rise required at the last row** for an unobstructed sight-line. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

The five principles of vision in building planning are:

1. **Visual Axis** — An imaginary line along which the eye is drawn. Created by aligning doorways, corridors, and windows in a straight path. *Example:* The central corridor of a government building aligned from the main gate to the rear lawn.

2. **Vista** — A long, narrow, framed view terminated at a focal point. Created by colonnades, archways, or hedges. *Example:* The approach to the India Gate in New Delhi.

3. **Focal Point** — The visually dominant element that anchors a space. *Example:* A large painting on a wall, an altar, or a chandelier.

4. **Visual Privacy** — The control of sight-lines to ensure occupants are not undesirably observed. Achieved by setbacks, high sill heights (1.5–1.8 m), screen walls, and fins. *Example:* Ground-floor windows of urban homes facing the street.

5. **Visual Scale and Proportion** — The tuning of building dimensions to human perception. *Example:* A residential room with a ceiling height of 3 m feels intimate; a cathedral with 15 m height feels grand.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** Sketches need not be artistic. A simple plan view showing two aligned doorways with a straight line between them is sufficient for a 7-mark answer.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:**
- Total depth $D_{total} = 10$ m
- Focal point height $C = 1.7$ m above first row floor
- Seated eye height $e = 1.05$ m
- Row spacing $D = 0.85$ m
- Number of rows $n$ such that total depth = 10 m → Row positions at 0.85, 1.70, 2.55, ..., 10.20 m
- Last row position = 10.20 m (Row 12)

**Step 1 —** Apply the Sight-Line Formula:

$$
L_n = e + \frac{(C - e) \cdot D}{D_{total} - D_n}
$$

**Step 2 —** Compute the sight-line rise at the last row where $D_n = 10.2$ m, but the reference distance is to the focal point. Using the iterative form:

$$
L_1 = 0 \text{ (datum)}
$$
$$
L_n = L_{n-1} + \frac{(C - e) \cdot D}{D_{total} - D_n}
$$

**Step 3 — Tabulated calculation:**

| Row | $D_n$ (m) | Rise (m) | $L_n$ (m) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.85 | 0.000 | 0.000 |
| 2 | 1.70 | 0.066 | 0.066 |
| 3 | 2.55 | 0.071 | 0.137 |
| 4 | 3.40 | 0.077 | 0.214 |
| 5 | 4.25 | 0.084 | 0.298 |
| 6 | 5.10 | 0.092 | 0.390 |
| 7 | 5.95 | 0.102 | 0.492 |
| 8 | 6.80 | 0.113 | 0.605 |
| 9 | 7.65 | 0.127 | 0.732 |
| 10 | 8.50 | 0.144 | 0.876 |
| 11 | 9.35 | 0.165 | 1.041 |
| **12** | **10.20** | **0.193** | **1.234** |

**Result:** The floor of the last row must be raised by approximately **1.23 m** above the first row to ensure a clear sight-line to the teacher's mouth.

> [!NOTE]
> **Approximation Note:** In KTU exams, the iterative method above is preferred over a closed-form equation because it scores more partial marks at each step.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Set B]`

**(a)** Discuss the role of **visual privacy** in residential building planning. List **four planning strategies** used to achieve it. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A ground-floor bedroom window has its sill at **1.2 m** above the floor. The opposite building is at a horizontal distance of **2.0 m**. Calculate the **minimum additional setback** required (if any) to maintain visual privacy, given a standard eye level of **1.5 m**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Visual Privacy** in residential building planning refers to the deliberate control of external sight-lines so that occupants are not inadvertently observed by neighbours, passers-by, or the public. It is both a **psychological need** (sense of personal space) and a **regulatory requirement** under most municipal building bye-laws in India.

**Importance:**
- Ensures dignity and comfort of occupants.
- Prevents social friction in dense residential layouts.
- Enhances the perceived value of the property.

**Four Planning Strategies:**

1. **Adequate Setbacks** — Maintaining prescribed distance between buildings (typically 1.5 m to 3.0 m for side setbacks in Kerala).

2. **High Window Sill Heights** — Ground-floor windows facing streets should have sills at **1.5 m to 1.8 m** above floor level to prevent direct eye-level viewing from outside.

3. **Use of Screen Walls and Pergolas** — Solid compound walls (1.8 m to 2.1 m high) and pergolas block external views while allowing light and air.

4. **Strategic Window Placement** — Offsetting windows, using clerestory windows (high-level), or placing windows only on the inner courtyard side.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:**
- Window sill height, $H_s = 1.2$ m
- Current horizontal distance, $S_{current} = 2.0$ m
- Standard eye level, $E = 1.5$ m

**Step 1 —** Apply the visual privacy setback formula:

$$
S_{min} = 1.5 \times H_s
$$

$$
S_{min} = 1.5 \times 1.2 = 1.8 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 —** Compare with the current distance:

$$
S_{current} = 2.0 \text{ m} > S_{min} = 1.8 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Conclusion:**

Since the current setback (**2.0 m**) is already **greater than** the minimum required (**1.8 m**), **no additional setback is needed**. The window already satisfies the visual privacy condition with a margin of **0.2 m**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT** confuse *visual privacy setback* with *fire safety setback* (NBC 2016 specifies 1.5 m minimum for non-fire-rated buildings). They are different rules.
> - **Do NOT** forget to state the **unit (m)** when writing the final answer. Marks are deducted for unit-less numerical answers.
> - **Do NOT** apply the 1.5 factor universally; the KTU-accepted empirical rule is specific to sill-height-based privacy. For façade-based privacy, the **30° angular rule** is used instead.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:**

- ☐ **Standard Eye Level** = **1.5 m** above finished floor (most-tested value).
- ☐ **Critical Visual Distance** = **24–30 m** (recognizing a face).
- ☐ **Comfortable Visual Angle** = **120° horizontal, 60° vertical**.
- ☐ **Five Principles of Vision** = Axis, Vista, Focal Point, Privacy, Scale & Proportion.
- ☐ **Golden Ratio** = $1 : 1.618$ (use it for facade proportioning).
- ☐ **Window Sill for Privacy** = **1.5–1.8 m** above floor.
- ☐ **Sight-line Formula** (Auditorium): $L_n = e + \dfrac{(C - e) \cdot D}{D_{total} - D_n}$.
- ☐ **Visual Privacy Setback** = $S_{min} = 1.5 \times H_s$.
- ☐ **Hall length** should not exceed **30 m** for unaided visual communication.
- ☐ **Always write units** (m, mm, °) in numerical answers.
- ☐ **Always close with a real-world example** in 7-mark and 14-mark answers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-Line Definitions to Memorize (Most Expected 3-Mark Questions):**
> - **Visual Axis:** "An imaginary line along which the eye is drawn in a building."
> - **Vista:** "A long, narrow, framed view terminated at a focal point."
> - **Focal Point:** "A visually dominant element that anchors a space."
> - **Visual Privacy:** "The planning control of external sight-lines to protect occupant dignity."
> - **Visual Proportion:** "The harmonious mathematical relationship between building parts."

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Standards of Lighting and Visual Comfort – The Sky as a Source of Light
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET834 - FUNDAMENTALS OF BUILDING PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Standards of Lighting and Visual Comfort – The Sky as a Source of Light

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** This topic sits at the intersection of *Building Services* and *Climate-Responsive Design* — it directly informs fenestration design, room depth planning, and the building's energy footprint (daylight vs. electric load). As per the KTU OECET834 Module 2 outcome mapping, students must demonstrate the ability to evaluate interior luminous environments against national and international standards.

### 1.1 Formal Definition — Standards of Lighting

**Standards of Lighting** are codified quantitative guidelines — published by bodies such as the **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)**, the **National Building Code of India (NBC 2016)**, the **Illuminating Engineering Society (IES)**, and the **International Commission on Illumination (CIE)** — that prescribe recommended **illuminance levels (lux)**, **luminance distributions**, **glare limits**, and **colour rendering indices** for various visual tasks performed inside built environments.

The two principal Indian references for building planning are:

- **IS 3646 (Part 1 & 2):** Code of practice for interior illumination.
- **SP 32 (Handbook on Functional Requirements of Buildings):** Summarizes recommended lux levels per task type (rough bench work → 150 lux; office drafting → 750 lux; fine bench/assembly work → 1000 lux).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction for KTU:** "Lighting Standards" do **not** only prescribe *how much* light — they prescribe *how uniformly*, *from which direction*, *with what glare control*, and *with what spectral quality* light must be delivered. Visual comfort is the qualitative partner of quantitative illuminance.

### 1.2 Formal Definition — Visual Comfort

**Visual Comfort** is the subjective condition of the human visual system under which an occupant can perform required visual tasks *efficiently, accurately, and without experiencing visual fatigue, strain, or discomfort* over a sustained period. It is a function of four interacting parameters:

1. **Illuminance level (E)** — measured in **lux (lx) = lm/m²**
2. **Luminance distribution (L)** — measured in **cd/m²**
3. **Glare control** — quantified by the **Glare Index (GI)** or **Unified Glare Rating (UGR)**
4. **Colour rendering and colour temperature** — quantified by **CRI (Ra)** and **CCT (Kelvin)**

### 1.3 Formal Definition — The Sky as a Source of Light

**The Sky as a Source of Light** refers to the exploitation of **natural daylight** — solar radiation scattered and diffused by the earth's atmosphere — as the primary illuminant for interior spaces during daytime. The sky is not a point source (like the sun) but an **extended, hemi-spherical, luminance-distributed source** whose brightness varies with **zenith angle, atmospheric turbidity, cloud cover, and solar altitude**.

> [!NOTE]
> **CIE Sky Models (KTU Favourite):**
> - **CIE Standard Overcast Sky:** A theoretical model where zenith luminance is **3 times** the horizon luminance — used for worst-case daylighting design.
> - **CIE Standard Clear Sky:** A model with high horizon luminance and bright circumsolar region — used for glare and shading studies.

### 1.4 The Intuitive Analogy — "The Sky as a Giant Diffuser"

> [!TIP]
> **Imagine the sky as a frosted glass lampshade the size of a continent.**
> The sun is a 150-million-kilometre-distant *bulb* that is far too intense to look at directly. The earth's atmosphere — molecules of nitrogen, oxygen, water vapour, dust, and cloud droplets — acts like the **frosted glass**, scattering (redirecting) this raw solar energy in **every direction** simultaneously.
>
> When a building architect places a window, they are essentially **drilling a hole into this enormous diffuser** to pipe soft, omnidirectional, colour-balanced daylight deep into the room. The brightness gradient of this diffuser (brightest at the zenith, dimmest at the horizon on an overcast day) is what determines how deeply light penetrates the room and how it falls on the work plane.

This is why a north-facing window (in the northern hemisphere) receives **only sky light — no direct sun** — making it the architect's natural choice for studios, galleries, and offices requiring glare-free, stable illumination.

### 1.5 Why This Topic Matters in Kerala's Building Context

Kerala lies between **8°18′ N and 12°48′ N** — squarely in the **tropical monsoon belt**. The implications for daylighting design are profound:

- **High solar altitude angles** (sun nearly overhead in summer) → small window reveals still admit significant light.
- **Persistent cloud cover during the monsoon (June–September)** → the **CIE Overcast Sky** is the *design-controlling condition* for ~6 months of the year.
- **High humidity haze** → increased atmospheric scattering, softer shadows, but reduced contrast.
- **Equatorial glare risk** → careful shading is mandatory to prevent visual discomfort.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Local Context Callout:** The Kerala State Building Rules (KSBR) and the Energy Conservation Building Code (ECBC) **supersedes** generic IS recommendations for state-specific compliance — students must know **both** the national standard and the local climatic adaptation.

---

### 1.6 Glossary of Mandatory Technical Terms

| Term | Symbol | Unit | Plain Meaning |
|------|--------|------|---------------|
| Luminous Flux | $\Phi$ | **lumen (lm)** | Total "amount of light" emitted by a source |
| Luminous Intensity | $I$ | **candela (cd)** | Flux per unit solid angle |
| Illuminance | $E$ | **lux (lx = lm/m²)** | Flux falling on a surface |
| Luminance | $L$ | **cd/m²** | Brightness as perceived by the eye |
| Daylight Factor | $DF$ | **%** | Ratio of indoor to outdoor illuminance |
| Glare Index | $GI$ | dimensionless | Numerical glare discomfort metric |
| Luminous Efficacy | $\eta$ | **lm/W** | Light output per unit electrical power |
| Zenith Angle | $\theta_z$ | degrees | Angle from vertical to a sky point |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Architecture of the Visual Comfort System — Six Pillars

Visual comfort is **not** a single variable; it emerges from the **synchronous satisfaction** of six inter-locking design criteria. KTU examiners routinely test whether a student understands these as a *system* rather than as a checklist.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Six Pillars of Visual Comfort (Kruithof Curve Inspired):**
> 1. **Adequate Illuminance** — enough light for the task (lux ≥ recommended value).
> 2. **Uniformity** — ratio of minimum to average illuminance ≥ **0.7** (office), ≥ **0.8** (classroom).
> 3. **Controlled Glare** — Direct Glare Index, Discomfort Glare Rating, or UGR within limits.
> 4. **Appropriate Directionality** — light vector reveals form, texture, and avoids flat "shadowless" appearance.
> 5. **Correct Colour Appearance** — CCT and CRI matched to task (e.g., Ra ≥ 80 for offices).
> 6. **Absence of Flicker / Stroboscopic Effect** — particularly important with modern LED drivers.

### 2.2 Why the Sky is a Special Source of Light

The sky is fundamentally different from any artificial source, and understanding this difference is the **core conceptual unlock** for this topic.

- **Spatial Extent:** A 2π steradian hemi-sphere — *not a point*. Light arrives from many directions simultaneously, producing **soft shadows** (or no shadows on a fully overcast day).
- **Spectral Quality:** The sky delivers a **correlated colour temperature (CCT)** of approximately **5500 K – 10000 K** — close to "daylight white," which the human visual system is *evolutionarily optimized* for.
- **Dynamic Range:** The luminance ratio between sky and ground can exceed **1000:1** in clear-sky midday conditions — a primary cause of visual discomfort near unshaded windows.
- **Time Variance:** The sky's luminance distribution changes every minute of the day — a factor the **Daylight Factor** concept conveniently *averages out* for design purposes.

### 2.3 The CIE Standard Overcast Sky — Luminance Distribution Equation

The **CIE Standard Overcast Sky** is the universal worst-case reference model. Its luminance at any point is a function only of its **angle of elevation** $\theta$ measured from the horizon. The governing equation is:

$$L_\theta = L_z \cdot \frac{1 + 2 \sin \theta}{3}$$

Where:
- $L_\theta$ = Luminance (cd/m²) of the sky at angle $\theta$ above the horizon
- $L_z$ = Zenith luminance (cd/m²) — the **maximum** sky luminance
- $\theta$ = Angle of elevation of the sky element from the horizon (0° at horizon, 90° at zenith)

**Boundary behaviour of this equation:**

| Sky Position | $\sin \theta$ | $L_\theta / L_z$ | Interpretation |
|--------------|---------------|------------------|----------------|
| Horizon | $\sin 0° = 0$ | $1/3$ | Dimmest region — about 33% of zenith |
| 30° elevation | $\sin 30° = 0.5$ | $2/3$ | 67% of zenith |
| 60° elevation | $\sin 60° = 0.866$ | $0.911$ | ~91% of zenith |
| Zenith | $\sin 90° = 1$ | $1.0$ | Brightest region |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Meaning:** The ratio of horizon to zenith luminance is always exactly **1:3** in the CIE overcast model. This is why **high-level windows (clerestories)** admit more light per unit area than low-level windows — the sky above is intrinsically three times brighter than the sky on the horizon.

### 2.4 The Daylight Factor (DF) — Master Equation

The **Daylight Factor** is the most important daylighting design metric. It expresses the indoor illuminance as a *percentage* of the simultaneous outdoor illuminance on a horizontal plane, under the **CIE Standard Overcast Sky** condition.

$$DF = \frac{E_{in}}{E_{out}} \times 100 \quad [\%]$$

The Daylight Factor is the **sum of three physical components** — and understanding the *physical origin* of each is what differentiates a topper from an average student.

$$DF = SC + ERC + IRC$$

| Component | Symbol | Physical Origin | Typical Magnitude |
|-----------|--------|-----------------|-------------------|
| **Sky Component** | $SC$ | Direct light from the visible sky patch entering the window | 0.1% – 10% |
| **Externally Reflected Component** | $ERC$ | Sunlight/sky light reflected off the ground, opposite buildings, vegetation | 0.1% – 3% |
| **Internally Reflected Component** | $IRC$ | Light that has entered the room, bounced off internal surfaces, and reached the point again | 0.5% – 5% |

The IRC is given by the **split-flux formula**:

$$IRC = \frac{A_w \cdot \tau \cdot \rho_{int}}{A_{total} \cdot (1 - \rho_{int})}$$

Where:
- $A_w$ = Net glazed area of the window (m²)
- $\tau$ = Transmittance of the glazing
- $\rho_{int}$ = Area-weighted average internal reflectance
- $A_{total}$ = Total internal surface area (walls + ceiling + floor)

### 2.5 Recommended Daylight Factors — IS 3646 / NBC 2016

| Building / Task Type | Recommended Daylight Factor |
|----------------------|------------------------------|
| Residences (general living) | **1.0% – 2.0%** |
| Classrooms (reading & writing) | **2.0% – 3.5%** |
| Drawing offices / Studios | **3.5% – 5.0%** |
| Hospital wards (daytime) | **1.5% – 2.5%** |
| Industrial fine work | **5.0% – 10.0%** |
| Galleries, museums | **1.0% – 2.0%** (UV-filtered) |
| Staircases, corridors | **0.5% – 1.0%** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examination Pitfall:** A common KTU student error is to assume a *higher* DF is always better. This is **wrong**. Over-illumination causes **glare, heat gain, fading of materials, and visual discomfort** — design must meet, *not exceed*, the recommended band.

### 2.6 Glare Quantification — The Glare Index (GI)

The **Glare Index** as defined by the **IES Glare Index system** (used in IS 3646 Part 2):

$$GI = 10 \log_{10} \left(0.478 \sum \frac{L_s^{1.6} \cdot \omega_s^{0.8}}{L_b \cdot P^{1.6}} \right)$$

| GI Range | Subjective Discomfort |
|----------|-----------------------|
| 10 – 16 | Noticeable |
| 16 – 19 | Just Acceptable (office limit per IS 3646) |
| 19 – 22 | Uncomfortable |
| 22 – 25 | Just Uncomfortable |
| > 25 | Intolerable |

> [!NOTE]
> **Modern Replacement:** The **Unified Glare Rating (UGR)** has replaced GI in most international standards. KTU may use either — students should know the boundary value **UGR ≤ 19** for office work.

### 2.7 Engineering Utility of This System

| Domain | Real-World Application |
|--------|------------------------|
| **Sustainable Architecture** | Daylight harvesting reduces electrical lighting load by 40–70% |
| **Green Building Certification (GRIHA, LEED, IGBC)** | Mandatory Daylight Autonomy (DA) ≥ 50% for credit |
| **Energy Code Compliance (ECBC 2017, Kerala ECBC)** | Window-to-Wall Ratio (WWR) limits are tied to DF bands |
| **Healthcare Design** | Patient recovery rates measurably higher under adequate daylight |
| **Educational Building Design** | Standardized test performance improves by 7–15% with proper daylight |
| **Museum & Conservation** | UV + IR filtering essential to prevent artefact damage |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Rule:** Marks are awarded for **every explicit logical step**. A naked final answer with no derivation = **maximum 40% marks**. Show *all* intermediate substitutions and unit consistency checks.

### 3.1 Derivation — Luminance Distribution of the CIE Standard Overcast Sky

We begin with the **CIE definition** that the gradient of luminance from horizon to zenith follows a specific empirical function. The governing equation (re-stated for clarity):

$$L_\theta = L_z \cdot \frac{1 + 2 \sin \theta}{3}$$

**Derivation of the boundary ratio (zenith : horizon):**

Step 1: Substitute $\theta = 90°$ (zenith condition)

$$L_{90°} = L_z \cdot \frac{1 + 2 \sin 90°}{3} = L_z \cdot \frac{1 + 2(1)}{3} = L_z \cdot \frac{3}{3} = L_z$$

Step 2: Substitute $\theta = 0°$ (horizon condition)

$$L_{0°} = L_z \cdot \frac{1 + 2 \sin 0°}{3} = L_z \cdot \frac{1 + 2(0)}{3} = L_z \cdot \frac{1}{3}$$

Step 3: Form the ratio

$$\frac{L_{horizon}}{L_{zenith}} = \frac{L_z / 3}{L_z} = \frac{1}{3}$$

**Conclusion:** The horizon luminance is exactly **one-third** of the zenith luminance under the CIE Standard Overcast Sky model. This is a **3:1 brightness ratio** — a key design determinant for fenestration placement.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem — Daylight Factor Calculation

> **[KTU University Exam — Sample Problem, Dec 2024 Style]**
>
> *A reading room of plan dimensions 8 m × 6 m and ceiling height 3.2 m is to be designed. The room has one external window of size 3.0 m × 1.5 m with single-glazed clear glass ($\tau = 0.85$). The area-weighted average internal reflectance is $\rho_{int} = 0.55$. The room floor is at ground level. If the average Sky Component at the centre of the room is 1.8% and the average Externally Reflected Component is 0.4%, calculate:*
> *(a) The Daylight Factor at the centre of the room.*
> *(b) The Internally Reflected Component using the split-flux formula.*
> *(c) The total DF and a comment on whether the room satisfies the IS 3646 recommendation for reading rooms.*

#### Solution (a) — Daylight Factor at the Centre

Step 1: Identify the formula

$$DF = SC + ERC + IRC$$

Step 2: Substitute the given values (we will compute IRC in part b)

$$DF_{center} = SC + ERC + IRC$$
$$DF_{center} = 1.8\% + 0.4\% + IRC$$

#### Solution (b) — Internally Reflected Component

Step 1: State the split-flux formula

$$IRC = \frac{A_w \cdot \tau \cdot \rho_{int}}{A_{total} \cdot (1 - \rho_{int})}$$

Step 2: Calculate the window area

$$A_w = 3.0 \, \text{m} \times 1.5 \, \text{m} = 4.5 \, \text{m}^2$$

Step 3: Calculate the total internal surface area
- Two long walls: $2 \times (8 \times 3.2) = 51.2 \, \text{m}^2$
- Two short walls: $2 \times (6 \times 3.2) = 38.4 \, \text{m}^2$
- Ceiling: $8 \times 6 = 48.0 \, \text{m}^2$
- Floor: $8 \times 6 = 48.0 \, \text{m}^2$

$$A_{total} = 51.2 + 38.4 + 48.0 + 48.0 = 185.6 \, \text{m}^2$$

Step 4: Substitute into the formula

$$IRC = \frac{4.5 \times 0.85 \times 0.55}{185.6 \times (1 - 0.55)}$$

Step 5: Evaluate the numerator

$$4.5 \times 0.85 = 3.825$$
$$3.825 \times 0.55 = 2.10375$$

Step 6: Evaluate the denominator

$$1 - 0.55 = 0.45$$
$$185.6 \times 0.45 = 83.52$$

Step 7: Compute the ratio and convert to percentage

$$IRC = \frac{2.10375}{83.52} = 0.02519$$
$$IRC_{\%} = 0.02519 \times 100 = 2.52\%$$

#### Solution (c) — Total DF and Compliance Check

Step 1: Sum the components

$$DF_{total} = 1.8\% + 0.4\% + 2.52\% = 4.72\%$$

Step 2: Compare to IS 3646 standard for reading rooms

$$\text{Recommended DF range} = 2.0\% \, \text{to} \, 3.5\%$$

Step 3: Verdict

$$\text{Computed DF} = 4.72\% > 3.5\% \text{ (upper recommended limit)}$$

**Conclusion:** The room is **over-lit** by approximately **35%** above the upper recommended limit. The design must be revised by either:
- Reducing the window area,
- Using a lower-transmittance glazing ($\tau = 0.65$ tinted glass), or
- Introducing external shading devices (chajjas, louvers).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Reporting DF = 4.72% and calling the design "satisfactory" because "it is greater than 2.0%." This loses **3 out of 7 marks** in part (c) because IS 3646 specifies a **band**, not a minimum. Always quote the *range*.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Daylight Design Decision Map

| Design Decision | Parameter that Changes | Impact on DF | Mitigation |
|-----------------|------------------------|--------------|------------|
| Increase window size | $A_w \uparrow$ | SC $\uparrow$ and IRC $\uparrow$ | Add external shading or use low-$\tau$ glazing |
| Repaint walls lighter | $\rho_{int} \uparrow$ | IRC $\uparrow$ (multiplicatively) | None needed — this is a low-cost improvement |
| Increase room depth | $A_{total} \uparrow$ | IRC $\downarrow$ at the back | Add clerestory or light shelf |
| Add external chajja | Obstructs sky | SC $\downarrow$ | Use a 45° angled chajja sized to block summer sun but admit winter sky |
| Add internal partitions | $A_{total}\uparrow$ artificially | IRC changes | Re-evaluate for the subdivided space |
| Use double-glazed low-e glass | $\tau \downarrow$ | SC $\downarrow$ and IRC $\downarrow$ | Compensate by increasing $A_w$ |

---

### 3.4 Numerical Problem — Sky Luminance Gradient

> **[KTU Internal Assessment — Module 2, Sample]**
>
> *Under a CIE Standard Overcast Sky, the zenith luminance is measured to be 9000 cd/m². Calculate the sky luminance at:*
> *(i) A point 30° above the horizon*
> *(ii) A point 60° above the horizon*
> *(iii) Comment on the architectural significance of the gradient.*

#### Solution

**Part (i):** Substitute $\theta = 30°$, $L_z = 9000 \, \text{cd/m}^2$

$$L_{30°} = 9000 \times \frac{1 + 2 \sin 30°}{3}$$
$$L_{30°} = 9000 \times \frac{1 + 2(0.5)}{3}$$
$$L_{30°} = 9000 \times \frac{1 + 1}{3} = 9000 \times \frac{2}{3} = 6000 \, \text{cd/m}^2$$

**Part (ii):** Substitute $\theta = 60°$

$$L_{60°} = 9000 \times \frac{1 + 2 \sin 60°}{3}$$
$$L_{60°} = 9000 \times \frac{1 + 2(0.8660)}{3}$$
$$L_{60°} = 9000 \times \frac{1 + 1.7321}{3} = 9000 \times \frac{2.7321}{3}$$
$$L_{60°} = 9000 \times 0.9107 = 8196.3 \, \text{cd/m}^2$$

**Part (iii) — Architectural Significance:**
The luminance at 60° elevation is **37% brighter** than at 30°. Therefore, **high-level openings** (clerestories, fanlights, ridge ventilators) admit **substantially more daylight per unit area** than sills at standard sill height (~0.9 m). In tropical Kerala buildings, this justifies the traditional use of **sloped roofs with high-level openings** to admit deep room daylight without exposing the work plane to direct glare.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — The Visual Comfort System

This diagram maps the complete interaction between sky, building envelope, and visual perception. It is rendered as a hierarchical flow (replacing a physical 3-D drawing) to comply with the Mermaid Compilation Safeguards.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Source: Sun and Sky Hemi-sphere] --> B1[Atmospheric Scattering Layer]
    B1 --> C1[CIE Sky Model Selected]
    C1 --> D1[Overcast Sky L Distribution]
    C1 --> D2[Clear Sky L Distribution]
    D1 --> E1[Exterior Horizontal Illuminance Eout]
    D2 --> E1
    E1 --> F1[Building Envelope - Window Assembly]
    F1 --> G1[Glazing Transmittance Tau]
    F1 --> G2[Frame and Obstruction Factor]
    G1 --> H1[Indoor Illuminance Ein - Work Plane]
    G2 --> H1
    H1 --> I1[Daylight Factor DF Computation]
    I1 --> J1[SC Sky Component]
    I1 --> J2[ERC External Reflection]
    I1 --> J3[IRC Internal Reflection]
    J1 --> K1[Visual Comfort Verification]
    J2 --> K1
    J3 --> K1
    K1 --> L1[IS 3646 Compliance Check]
    L1 --> M1{DF within Recommended Band?}
    M1 -- Yes --> N1[Design Accepted - Visual Comfort Achieved]
    M1 -- No --> N2[Revise Window Area or Glazing]
    N2 --> F1
    K1 --> O1[Glare Index GI Computation]
    O1 --> P1{GI within Acceptable Limit?}
    P1 -- Yes --> N1
    P1 -- No --> Q1[Add Shading Device or Louver]
    Q1 --> F1
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — The Three Components of Daylight Factor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Daylight Factor DF] --> B[SC Sky Component]
    A --> C[ERC Externally Reflected Component]
    A --> D[IRC Internally Reflected Component]
    B --> B1[Direct sky light through window]
    C --> C1[Ground reflected light]
    C --> C2[Opposite building reflection]
    D --> D1[Light bouncing off internal walls]
    D --> D2[Light bouncing off ceiling]
    D --> D3[Light bouncing off floor]
```

### 4.3 Sky Luminance Gradient — Vertical Section Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Zenith - Lz Maximum Brightness] --> B[60 deg elevation - 0.91 Lz]
    B --> C[30 deg elevation - 0.67 Lz]
    C --> D[Horizon - 0.33 Lz - Minimum Brightness]
    E[Architectural Implication] --> F[High Level Windows receive 3x more light]
    E --> G[Low level windows receive 1x base light]
    D --> F
    D --> G
```

### 4.4 Window-to-Room Light Penetration Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Window Sill at 0.9 m] --> B[Sky Component Penetration Depth]
    B --> B1[Up to 2.5 x window head height for full DF]
    B --> B2[Beyond 2.5 x - IRC dominant]
    C[Clerestory Window at 2.5 m] --> D[Deep Room Penetration]
    D --> D1[Used in galleries and lecture halls]
    D --> D2[Reduces glare at work plane]
    E[Light Shelf at 2.2 m] --> F[Reflects light to ceiling]
    F --> F1[Indirect illumination of room depth]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Diagram Convention Used:** Rectangular nodes represent functional stages; diamond-shaped nodes represent decision gates; arrows represent data or material flow. This is the standard Mermaid syntax adopted by KTU digital answer sheets.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**
Define **Daylight Factor**. List its three components and state the recommended Daylight Factor range for a residential living room as per **IS 3646**.

**Model Answer:**

**Daylight Factor (DF)** is the ratio of the illuminance on the work plane inside a building to the simultaneous illuminance on a horizontal plane outside the building, expressed as a percentage, under the **CIE Standard Overcast Sky** condition.

$$DF = \frac{E_{in}}{E_{out}} \times 100 \quad [\%]$$

The three components are:
1. **Sky Component (SC)** — direct sky light entering through the window.
2. **Externally Reflected Component (ERC)** — light reflected from the ground or surrounding surfaces.
3. **Internally Reflected Component (IRC)** — light reflected from internal room surfaces.

**Recommended DF for a residential living room** as per IS 3646: **1.0% to 2.0%**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]**
Explain why **the sky is considered a diffuser** rather than a point source of light. What is the zenith-to-horizon luminance ratio under the **CIE Standard Overcast Sky**?

**Model Answer:**

The sky is considered a **diffuser** because the earth's atmosphere — through the combined action of **Rayleigh scattering** (by gas molecules), **Mie scattering** (by aerosols and dust), and **diffuse reflection** (by cloud droplets) — redirects incoming solar radiation in **all directions** across a 2π steradian hemi-spherical surface. Unlike a point source (which emits light from a single, geometrically small location), the sky emits light from an *extended* luminous surface with a **non-uniform luminance distribution**.

Under the **CIE Standard Overcast Sky**, the luminance varies with elevation angle $\theta$ according to:

$$L_\theta = L_z \cdot \frac{1 + 2 \sin \theta}{3}$$

The **zenith-to-horizon luminance ratio is 3:1** — i.e., the zenith is three times brighter than the horizon. This is why **high-level fenestration** is architecturally more efficient than equivalent low-level openings.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module Choice Convention:** Each 14-mark question typically carries sub-parts of **7 + 7 marks**. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model]*

**(a)** Explain the **CIE Standard Overcast Sky model** in detail. Derive the zenith-to-horizon luminance ratio from first principles. Discuss the architectural significance of this 3:1 ratio in the design of fenestration for tropical buildings like those in Kerala. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A classroom of plan 10 m × 7 m and clear height 3.5 m has two windows of size 2.4 m × 1.5 m each on the longer wall, fitted with 6 mm clear float glass ($\tau = 0.78$). The internal surface reflectance values are: ceiling 0.75, walls 0.55, floor 0.25. The average Sky Component at the centre of the classroom is 2.6% and the average Externally Reflected Component is 0.5%. Calculate:
- (i) The Internally Reflected Component. **(3 marks)**
- (ii) The total Daylight Factor at the centre. **(1 mark)**
- (iii) State whether the design satisfies IS 3646 for a classroom. If not, suggest two specific design corrections. **(3 marks)**

---

#### **Question A — Model Solution**

**Part (a) — CIE Standard Overcast Sky & Architectural Significance (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Definition of the model (2 marks)**
The CIE (Commission Internationale de l'Éclairage) Standard Overcast Sky is a theoretical, deterministic model that describes the luminance distribution of a completely overcast sky as a function **only of the angle of elevation** above the horizon. It is the **worst-case design condition** used universally for daylighting calculations because:
- It corresponds to the lowest outdoor illuminance that occurs in temperate and tropical climates.
- It is mathematically convenient (only one variable: $\theta$).
- It is conservative — a design that works under overcast will work under all clearer sky conditions.

**Step 2: Governing equation (1 mark)**

$$L_\theta = L_z \cdot \frac{1 + 2 \sin \theta}{3}$$

**Step 3: Derivation of the 3:1 ratio (2 marks)**
At zenith ($\theta = 90°$): $L_{zenith} = L_z$
At horizon ($\theta = 0°$): $L_{horizon} = L_z / 3$
Therefore: $L_{zenith} : L_{horizon} = 3 : 1$

**Step 4: Architectural significance for Kerala (2 marks)**
In Kerala, the overcast sky persists for ~6 months (monsoon). A north-facing clerestory window admits **3× the light** of an equivalent south-facing sill window. This justifies the **traditional sloped-roof + high-gable-vent** typology of Kerala classrooms, which uses the bright zenith sky to push daylight deep into the room without creating glare on the work plane.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Calculate window area (0.5 mark)**
$$A_w = 2 \times (2.4 \times 1.5) = 2 \times 3.6 = 7.2 \, \text{m}^2$$

**Step 2: Calculate weighted average internal reflectance (1 mark)**
- Ceiling area: $10 \times 7 = 70 \, \text{m}^2$ → $70 \times 0.75 = 52.5$
- Two long walls: $2 \times (10 \times 3.5) = 70 \, \text{m}^2$ → $70 \times 0.55 = 38.5$
- Two short walls: $2 \times (7 \times 3.5) = 49 \, \text{m}^2$ → $49 \times 0.55 = 26.95$
- Floor area: $10 \times 7 = 70 \, \text{m}^2$ → $70 \times 0.25 = 17.5$

$$A_{total} = 70 + 70 + 49 + 70 = 259 \, \text{m}^2$$
$$\text{Sum of } (\rho \times A) = 52.5 + 38.5 + 26.95 + 17.5 = 135.45$$

$$\rho_{int} = \frac{135.45}{259} = 0.523 \approx 0.52$$

**Step 3: Apply the split-flux IRC formula (1.5 marks)**

$$IRC = \frac{A_w \cdot \tau \cdot \rho_{int}}{A_{total} \cdot (1 - \rho_{int})}$$
$$IRC = \frac{7.2 \times 0.78 \times 0.52}{259 \times (1 - 0.52)}$$
$$IRC = \frac{2.9203}{259 \times 0.48} = \frac{2.9203}{124.32}$$
$$IRC = 0.02349 = 2.35\%$$

**Step 4: Total Daylight Factor (1 mark)**

$$DF_{total} = SC + ERC + IRC = 2.6\% + 0.5\% + 2.35\% = 5.45\%$$

**Step 5: Compliance check and corrections (3 marks)**

IS 3646 recommends a **DF range of 2.0% – 3.5%** for classrooms.

**Computed DF = 5.45%** → **exceeds the upper limit by ~56%**. Design **does NOT satisfy** the standard.

**Two specific design corrections:**
1. **Reduce window area** from $7.2 \, \text{m}^2$ to approximately $4.5 \, \text{m}^2$ (use a single window of 3 m × 1.5 m instead of two). This reduces both SC and ERC proportionally.
2. **Replace clear glass with tinted or low-e double glazing** ($\tau$ drops from 0.78 to ~0.55), reducing both direct transmission and reflected components by ~30%.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **1 mark lost** if student does **not compute $\rho_{int}$ as an area-weighted average** (some students incorrectly use the simple arithmetic mean of 0.55, 0.75, 0.25 = 0.52 — coincidentally similar here but methodologically wrong).
> - **1 mark lost** if student forgets to express IRC as a **percentage** (writes 0.0235 instead of 2.35%).
> - **2 marks lost** if the verdict in part (b)(iii) does **not** state the design *fails* and does *not* give a **quantified** correction.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model]*

**(a)** Explain the **concept of Visual Comfort**. Discuss the four primary factors that determine visual comfort in an interior space. State the recommended illuminance level (lux) and glare index limit for an office workspace as per **IS 3646**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the role of the **sky as a source of light** in building design. Compare the **CIE Standard Overcast Sky** and the **CIE Standard Clear Sky** models, highlighting the conditions under which each is used for design. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Question B — Model Solution**

**Part (a) — Visual Comfort (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Definition (2 marks)**
**Visual Comfort** is the state of the visual environment in which an occupant can perform required visual tasks with **minimum visual fatigue and maximum efficiency** over a sustained duration. It is a *qualitative* human response to a *quantitative* physical stimulus.

**Step 2: Four primary factors (4 marks)**
1. **Illuminance level ($E$ in lux):** Adequate quantity of light. For offices, IS 3646 recommends **300 – 500 lux** for general work, **750 lux** for detailed drafting.
2. **Luminance distribution ($L$ in cd/m²):** Balanced brightness across the field of view; maximum luminance ratio of **40:1** between task and immediate surround.
3. **Glare control:** Direct or reflected glare should not exceed the discomfort threshold. The **Glare Index limit for offices is GI ≤ 16 (Just Acceptable)**; for detailed work UGR ≤ 19.
4. **Colour rendering and colour temperature:** A **CRI (Ra) ≥ 80** is required for office work; CCT in the cool-white range (4000 K – 5000 K) is preferred for alertness.

**Step 3: IS 3646 recommended values (1 mark)**
- Illuminance: **300 – 500 lux** for general office work.
- Glare Index: **≤ 16** for routine office, **≤ 13** for VDT/PC intensive workstations.

---

**Part (b) — Sky as Source & CIE Model Comparison (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Role of the sky in building design (3 marks)**
The sky delivers **free, sustainable, full-spectrum daylight** to interiors. Architectural exploitation of sky light reduces electrical lighting energy by **40%–70%** and improves occupant well-being. Building elements — windows, clerestories, light shelves, courtyards, atriums, sawtooth roofs — are designed as *coupling devices* between the sky hemi-sphere and the work plane.

**Step 2: CIE Standard Overcast Sky (2 marks)**
- **Luminance distribution:** L is a function only of elevation angle.
- **Luminance gradient:** Zenith : Horizon = 3:1.
- **Use:** Daylight Factor calculations, sizing of windows, ensuring minimum illuminance under worst-case conditions.

**Step 3: CIE Standard Clear Sky (2 marks)**
- **Luminance distribution:** Bright circumsolar region, deep blue zenith, low horizon.
- **Luminance gradient:** Inverse of overcast (horizon may be brighter than zenith at low solar altitudes).
- **Use:** Glare analysis, sizing of overhangs and louvers, solar heat gain studies, design of sun-control devices.

| Parameter | CIE Overcast Sky | CIE Clear Sky |
|-----------|------------------|---------------|
| Used for | DF calculations | Shading design |
| Worst case | Diffuse / uniform | Direct sun + glare |
| Governing variable | Elevation angle $\theta$ | Solar position + turbidity |
| Horizon : Zenith ratio | 1 : 3 | Variable (often inverse) |
| Kerala applicability | Monsoon design (Jun–Sep) | Dry season design (Oct–May) |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning Summary

> [!WARNING]
> **High-Frequency Mark Deductions on This Topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert** the Daylight Factor ratio to a **percentage** (writing 0.0472 instead of 4.72%) → −1 to −2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing units** of luminance (cd/m²) with illuminance (lux) in the same equation → −2 marks.
> 3. **Skipping the area-weighted average** for $\rho_{int}$ calculation → −1 mark.
> 4. **Stating "DF is good because it is high"** without comparing against the IS 3646 *range* → −2 to −3 marks.
> 5. **Confusing** Daylight Factor (DF) with **Daylight Autonomy (DA)** — they are different metrics.
> 6. **Omitting the CIE assumption** in numerical problems — the DF formula is valid *only* under CIE Standard Overcast Sky.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 — Lighting Standards & Visual Comfort**

- [x] **Lighting standards** are quantitative + qualitative prescriptions published by BIS (IS 3646), NBC, and CIE.
- [x] **Visual comfort** rests on **4 pillars**: adequate illuminance, balanced luminance, glare control, and good colour rendering.
- [x] The **sky is a hemi-spherical diffuser**, not a point source — it scatters sunlight via Rayleigh + Mie mechanisms.
- [x] **CIE Standard Overcast Sky** is the worst-case design sky; **CIE Clear Sky** is for glare and shading design.
- [x] **Luminance equation:** $L_\theta = L_z \cdot (1 + 2 \sin \theta)/3$ → **zenith is 3× brighter than horizon**.
- [x] **Daylight Factor (DF)** = ratio of indoor to outdoor illuminance, expressed in **%**, under CIE Overcast Sky.
- [x] **DF = SC + ERC + IRC** — these are the three physical components.
- [x] **IRC split-flux formula:** $IRC = (A_w \cdot \tau \cdot \rho_{int}) / (A_{total} \cdot (1 - \rho_{int}))$.
- [x] **IS 3646 recommended DF** for residences: **1.0–2.0%**; classrooms: **2.0–3.5%**; fine work: **5.0–10.0%**.
- [x] **Glare Index limit** for offices (IS 3646): **GI ≤ 16** (Just Acceptable).
- [x] **High-level windows** (clerestories) admit **3× more light** than low-level windows of the same area (because zenith is 3× horizon).
- [x] In **Kerala's tropical climate**, the overcast sky controls daylight design for ~6 months/year.
- [x] **Excessive DF** is as bad as inadequate DF — it causes glare, heat gain, and fading.
- [x] **Green building codes (GRIHA, LEED, ECBC)** use **Daylight Autonomy (DA ≥ 50%)** as a sustainability metric.
- [x] **Recommended illuminance levels:** Rough work 150 lux | Office 300–500 lux | Drafting 750 lux | Fine assembly 1000 lux.
- [x] **Uniformity ratio** $U = E_{min} / E_{avg} \geq 0.7$ for offices; $\geq 0.8$ for classrooms.

---

**End of Module 2 Notes — Standards of Lighting and Visual Comfort – The Sky as a Source of Light**
**Course:** OECET834 — Fundamentals of Building Planning | **Scheme:** KTU 2024 (NEP 2020 Aligned)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Daylight Factor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET834 - FUNDAMENTALS OF BUILDING PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Daylight Factor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Daylight Factor (DF):** The **Daylight Factor** at a point on a horizontal working plane inside a room is defined as the **ratio of the illumination (E) at that point on the working plane due to daylight received directly or indirectly from the open sky to the simultaneous illumination on a horizontal surface exposed to the open sky**, expressed as a **percentage (%)**.

Mathematically, the most fundamental expression is:

$$DF = \left(\frac{E_{\text{inside}}}{E_{\text{outside}}}\right) \times 100$$

Where:
- $E_{\text{inside}}$ = Total illumination (in **lux**) received on the working plane inside the room from daylight sources.
- $E_{\text{outside}}$ = Illumination (in **lux**) received at the same moment on a horizontal surface fully exposed to the open unobstructed sky.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Cloudy Daylight Camera":**
> Imagine you are standing inside a closed room on a heavily clouded, overcast day. Outside, the sky is uniformly bright (no harsh sunbeams). Hold a light meter inside the room on your desk and another one outside on the roof at the same instant. If the indoor meter reads **50 lux** and the outdoor meter reads **1000 lux**, then the daylight factor is **5%**. This 5% essentially tells the architect: *"Under any overcast sky condition, this room will receive exactly 5% of the light energy that falls on an open rooftop."* Because the ratio is constant under CIE Standard Overcast Sky conditions, it becomes a **universal design metric** — independent of time, season, or weather.

### 1.3 Classification of Daylight Factor Components

The total daylight factor at any internal point is the algebraic sum of three independent components:

| Component | Symbol | Source of Light |
|---|---|---|
| **Sky Component** | **SC** | Direct light from the visible portion of the sky (through the window aperture) |
| **Externally Reflected Component** | **ERC** | Light reflected from opposing external surfaces (e.g., ground, opposite buildings) into the room |
| **Internally Reflected Component** | **IRC** | Light that enters the room, hits internal surfaces (walls, ceiling, floor), and is reflected to the working plane |

Hence the master summation:

$$DF_{total} = SC + ERC + IRC$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Always remember that **SC + ERC** is collectively called the **"Direct Component"** of daylight, while **IRC** is the **"Reflected Component."** Examiners often give 2-mark sub-questions on identifying these three components.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics and Recommended Values (BIS / IS 3646)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)** under **IS 3646 (Part I): 1992** — *"Code of Practice for Artificial Illumination and Daylighting of Buildings"* — prescribes the minimum recommended daylight factors for various occupancy types in India.

| Type of Building / Room | Recommended DF (%) |
|---|---|
| **Living rooms (Residential)** | **1.0 – 2.0 %** |
| **Kitchens (Residential)** | **1.5 – 2.0 %** |
| **Classrooms (Schools)** | **2.0 – 3.0 %** |
| **Offices / Drawing Rooms** | **2.5 – 5.0 %** |
| **Laboratories** | **3.0 – 5.0 %** |
| **Hospital Wards** | **1.5 – 2.0 %** |
| **Staircases, Corridors** | **0.5 – 1.0 %** |
| **Factories (General work)** | **2.0 – 5.0 %** |
| **Factories (Fine work)** | **5.0 – 10.0 %** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Component-Wise Theoretical Breakdown

#### 2.1.1 Sky Component (SC)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** The Sky Component is the ratio (in %) of the illumination at a point on the working plane **received directly from the visible portion of the sky** through the window opening, to the simultaneous outdoor horizontal illumination under a CIE Standard Overcast Sky.

**Why it matters:** SC is the **dominant contributor** to total daylight in most rooms. It depends on:
- **Window area** ($A_w$)
- **Window position** (height of sill, head, and side reveals)
- **Depth of room** from the window wall
- **Glazing transmission factor** ($\tau$)

**Mathematical Expression (Walsh's Formula-based derivation):**

$$SC = \frac{\pi \cdot A_w \cdot \tau \cdot \theta}{2 \cdot A \cdot (1 - R^2)}$$

Where the simplified form commonly used in KTU problems is:

$$SC = \frac{L_s \cdot A_w \cdot \tau}{A \cdot (1 - R^2)}$$

> [!TIP]
> For initial KTU board-level numericals, students are **not expected** to derive Walsh's formula from first principles. Instead, they use **standard pre-calculated Sky Component tables** (Walsh's Tables) that are typically supplied in the question paper itself. Focus on the **applied calculation**, not the source derivation.

#### 2.1.2 Externally Reflected Component (ERC)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** ERC is the ratio of illumination at a point on the working plane **due to light reflected from external obstructing surfaces** (such as the ground, opposite buildings, courtyards) into the room, to the simultaneous outdoor illumination.

**Why it matters:** In dense urban Indian contexts (e.g., Ernakulam, Thrissur, Kozhikode town centers), buildings are close together, so ERC becomes highly significant.

**Simplified Expression:**

$$ERC = \frac{R_g \cdot W \cdot A_w \cdot \theta}{A \cdot (1 - R^2)} \cdot C$$

Where:
- $R_g$ = Reflection factor of the external ground/obstruction (typically **0.1 to 0.3**)
- $W$ = Window width
- $\theta$ = Visible sky angle from the reference point
- $C$ = Correction factor (accounts for obstructions)

#### 2.1.3 Internally Reflected Component (IRC)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** IRC is the ratio of illumination at a point on the working plane due to **multiple internal reflections** from room surfaces (walls, ceiling, floor) after light has entered the room, to the simultaneous outdoor illumination.

**Why it matters:** IRC improves the **uniformity** of daylight distribution deep inside the room — it "rescues" points far from the window.

**Lyon's Formula (Standard KTU Form):**

$$IRC = \frac{A_w \cdot \tau \cdot R_c}{A \cdot (1 - R)} \cdot \left(\frac{1}{1 - R_{wc} \cdot R_{fc}}\right)$$

A more practical simplified form commonly tested:

$$IRC = \frac{A_w}{A_{total}} \cdot \tau \cdot R_{avg} \cdot K$$

Where:
- $A_w$ = Window area
- $A_{total}$ = Total internal surface area (walls + ceiling + floor)
- $R_{avg}$ = Average internal reflection coefficient
- $K$ = Factor accounting for internal geometry

#### 2.1.4 Average Internal Reflection Coefficient ($R$)

The weighted average reflection factor of all room surfaces:

$$R = \frac{\sum A_i \cdot R_i}{\sum A_i}$$

Where $A_i$ and $R_i$ are the area and reflection factor of the $i^{th}$ internal surface respectively.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is your **rapid-revision armoury** for KTU University Exams. Memorize the boundary conditions and units.

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Basic Daylight Factor** | $DF = \dfrac{E_{in}}{E_{out}} \times 100$ | Dimensionless; expressed in **%** |
| 2 | **Total DF** | $DF_{total} = SC + ERC + IRC$ | All components in **%** |
| 3 | **Sky Component (Walsh)** | $SC = \dfrac{L_s \cdot A_w \cdot \tau}{A \cdot (1 - R^2)}$ | $L_s$ = luminance of sky; $\tau$ = glass transmission |
| 4 | **Externally Reflected Component** | $ERC = \dfrac{R_g \cdot A_w \cdot \theta}{A \cdot (1 - R^2)}$ | $R_g$ for ground: **0.1 to 0.3** |
| 5 | **Internal Reflected Component (Lyon)** | $IRC = \dfrac{A_w \cdot R_{avg} \cdot K}{A_s}$ | $K$ is geometry factor |
| 6 | **Average Reflection Factor** | $R = \dfrac{\sum A_i R_i}{\sum A_i}$ | $0 \leq R \leq 1$ (typically 0.4–0.7) |
| 7 | **Critical Daylight Factor (CDF)** | $CDF$ = DF at worst-lit point | Must meet **IS 3646** minimums |
| 8 | **Glazing Transmission $\tau$** | Clear glass: **0.85–0.90**; Frosted: **0.70–0.75** | Dimensionless |
| 9 | **Minimum DF (Classroom)** | $\geq 2\%$ | Per **IS 3646** |
| 10 | **Minimum DF (Office)** | $\geq 2.5\%$ | Per **IS 3646** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often forget that DF is a **percentage** and present answers in decimals. Always multiply by 100. Also, never confuse **Lumen ($\text{lm}$)** with **Lux ($\text{lx}$)** — illumination E is measured in **lux**.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The daylight factor methodology is the cornerstone of:

- **Green Building Certification (IGBC, GRIHA):** Daylight autonomy and DF are scored parameters.
- **Energy-Efficient Design:** Reduces artificial lighting load → **lower electricity bills** and **lower HVAC load** (less heat from lamps).
- **Visual Comfort (Indoor Environmental Quality):** Adequate DF prevents eye-strain, supports circadian rhythms.
- **Code Compliance:** Mandatory under **National Building Code of India (NBC 2016)** Part 8 Section 1 for lighting and ventilation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Linking the Three Components into a Unified Expression

We start from the definition of total illumination inside a room on the working plane:

$$E_{in,total} = E_{SC} + E_{ERC} + E_{IRC}$$

Where $E_{SC}$, $E_{ERC}$, and $E_{IRC}$ are the illuminations contributed by sky, external reflection, and internal reflection respectively.

**Step 1:** Divide every term by the outdoor horizontal illumination $E_{out}$:

$$\frac{E_{in,total}}{E_{out}} = \frac{E_{SC}}{E_{out}} + \frac{E_{ERC}}{E_{out}} + \frac{E_{IRC}}{E_{out}}$$

**Step 2:** Recognize that the left-hand side is, by definition, the Total Daylight Factor divided by 100 (since DF is in %):

$$\frac{DF_{total}}{100} = \frac{SC}{100} + \frac{ERC}{100} + \frac{IRC}{100}$$

**Step 3:** Multiply the entire equation by 100:

$$DF_{total} = SC + ERC + IRC$$

This proves the **additive nature** of the three daylight components — a foundational identity tested in KTU exams.

### 3.2 Detailed Derivation of the Sky Component (SC)

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation follows **Walsh's geometric approach** (commonly referenced in IS 3646 commentary and in architectural physics textbooks).

**Step 1 — Visualizing the Geometry:**

Consider a point P on the working plane at a depth $d$ from the window wall. The window has area $A_w$, and the point P sees a portion of the sky through a **solid angle** $\omega$ subtended by the window opening at P.

**Step 2 — Luminous Flux Received from the Sky:**

Under a **CIE Standard Overcast Sky**, the luminance $L_s$ of the sky is assumed uniform. The luminous flux $\Phi$ entering the room from the sky through the window and reaching point P is:

$$\Phi_{SC} = L_s \cdot A_w \cdot \tau \cdot \cos\theta$$

Where:
- $L_s$ = Luminance of the sky in $\text{cd/m}^2$
- $A_w$ = Window area in $\text{m}^2$
- $\tau$ = Glazing transmission factor
- $\theta$ = Angle of incidence of sky light (for vertical window, $\cos\theta = $ projection factor)

**Step 3 — Illumination at Point P:**

The illumination $E_{SC}$ at P is the flux distributed over the equivalent receiving area. For a horizontal working plane, the effective area is enlarged by a factor of $2(1 - R^2)$ accounting for the room's inter-reflection geometry (a derived constant from room theory). Thus:

$$E_{SC} = \frac{L_s \cdot A_w \cdot \tau}{2 A (1 - R^2)}$$

Where $A$ is the total internal room surface area (walls + ceiling + floor).

**Step 4 — Convert to Sky Component in Percentage:**

$$SC = \frac{E_{SC}}{E_{out}} \times 100 = \frac{L_s \cdot A_w \cdot \tau}{2 A (1 - R^2) E_{out}} \times 100$$

> [!TIP]
> Under the CIE Standard Overcast Sky model, the ratio $L_s / E_{out}$ is a **standardized constant** (often taken as 0.318 for Indian conditions per IS 3646). This is why Walsh's pre-calculated tables exist — you do not compute $L_s$ from scratch in the exam.

**Step 5 — Simplified Final Form Used in KTU Problems:**

$$SC (\%) = \frac{L_s \cdot A_w \cdot \tau}{2 A (1 - R^2)} \times \frac{100}{E_{out}}$$

In many exam problems, the outdoor illumination $E_{out}$ is given directly, and a pre-calculated factor (Walsh's factor $W_f$) is provided. The exam form then reduces to:

$$SC = W_f \times A_w \times \tau \times \text{(angular factor)}$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Study of the Three Components

| Parameter | Sky Component (SC) | External Reflected Component (ERC) | Internal Reflected Component (IRC) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Source** | Direct visible sky | Reflected from outside obstructions | Reflected from inside surfaces |
| **Dominant in** | Well-daylit rooms near window | Dense urban settings | Deep interior, poorly daylit zones |
| **Magnitude (typical)** | 0.5 – 2.5% | 0.1 – 1.0% | 0.5 – 3.0% |
| **Depends on** | Window area, glazing | External surface reflectance | Wall/ceiling/floor reflectance |
| **Improves by** | Larger windows, higher sills, light wells | Light-colored external paving | Light interior paint, white ceiling |
| **Formula origin** | Walsh (1946) | Lynes / Hopkinson | Lyon (1950s) |
| **Typical KTU weightage** | 7 marks | 3–4 marks | 7 marks |

### 3.4 Worked Example — Daylight Factor Calculation (KTU-style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A classroom has the following data:
> - Floor area = $50 \text{ m}^2$ (length = 10 m, width = 5 m)
> - Ceiling height = 3.5 m
> - One window of size $2 \text{ m} \times 1.5 \text{ m} = 3 \text{ m}^2$
> - Glazing transmission factor $\tau = 0.85$
> - Sky luminance $L_s = 2500 \text{ cd/m}^2$
> - Outdoor illumination $E_{out} = 10,000 \text{ lux}$
> - Average internal reflection factor $R = 0.5$
> - External ground reflection $R_g = 0.2$
> - Geometric factor $K$ for IRC = 0.6
>
> Calculate the **Sky Component (SC)** and **Internally Reflected Component (IRC)**. Check whether the room meets IS 3646 norms for a classroom (DF ≥ 2%).

**Step 1 — Compute Total Internal Surface Area $A$:**

The room has 4 walls, 1 ceiling, 1 floor.

- Floor area = $10 \times 5 = 50 \text{ m}^2$
- Ceiling area = $10 \times 5 = 50 \text{ m}^2$
- Two long walls ($10 \text{ m} \times 3.5 \text{ m}$) = $2 \times 35 = 70 \text{ m}^2$
- Two short walls ($5 \text{ m} \times 3.5 \text{ m}$) = $2 \times 17.5 = 35 \text{ m}^2$

$$A = 50 + 50 + 70 + 35 = 205 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute $(1 - R^2)$:**

$$1 - R^2 = 1 - (0.5)^2 = 1 - 0.25 = 0.75$$

**Step 3 — Compute Sky Component (SC):**

Using the formula:

$$SC = \frac{L_s \cdot A_w \cdot \tau}{2 \cdot A \cdot (1 - R^2)} \times \frac{100}{E_{out}}$$

Substituting:

$$SC = \frac{2500 \times 3 \times 0.85}{2 \times 205 \times 0.75} \times \frac{100}{10{,}000}$$

Compute the numerator:

$$2500 \times 3 \times 0.85 = 6375$$

Compute the denominator (without $E_{out}$):

$$2 \times 205 \times 0.75 = 307.5$$

So:

$$SC = \frac{6375}{307.5} \times \frac{100}{10{,}000}$$

$$SC = 20.73 \times 0.01 = 0.2073\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Important Note on this result:** A SC of 0.21% is far below the classroom requirement. This indicates the room is **critically under-daylit** from sky alone, and the design must rely on IRC and possibly a larger window. Real KTU problems may give higher $L_s$ values or larger windows so the result lands in the 2–5% range. Always cross-check with the IS 3646 standard.

**Step 4 — Compute IRC (using simplified Lyon form):**

$$IRC = \frac{A_w \cdot \tau \cdot R_{avg} \cdot K}{A} \times 100$$

(Note: the constant factor 2 from the SC formula is absorbed into $K$ in the simplified form)

$$IRC = \frac{3 \times 0.85 \times 0.5 \times 0.6}{205} \times 100$$

Compute the numerator:

$$3 \times 0.85 \times 0.5 \times 0.6 = 0.765$$

So:

$$IRC = \frac{0.765}{205} \times 100 = 0.3732\%$$

**Step 5 — Compute Total DF (ignoring ERC for simplicity):**

$$DF_{total} = SC + IRC = 0.2073 + 0.3732 = 0.5805\%$$

**Step 6 — Compare with IS 3646 Standard:**

- **Required for classroom:** DF ≥ **2.0%**
- **Achieved:** **0.58%**
- **Conclusion:** The room is **inadequate**. The architect must either:
  1. Increase window area (e.g., $A_w$ from 3 m² to ~10 m²)
  2. Use clerestory windows or light shelves
  3. Increase ceiling/wall reflectance by using white paint ($R$ from 0.5 to 0.7)
  4. Add light-colored external paving to boost ERC

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part B questions on Daylight Factor usually provide a partially completed table of geometric factors and ask you to compute **DF at a specific point** (often the **worst-lit point** = farthest corner from window) using the formula $DF = \dfrac{E_{in}}{E_{out}} \times 100$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Flow Diagram — Components of Daylight Factor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Open Sky Luminance Ls"] --> B["Window Aperture Aw"]
    B --> C{"Light Entry Decision"}
    C --> D["Direct Sky Transmission"]
    D --> E["Sky Component SC"]
    A2["External Surfaces Ground Walls"] --> F["Light Reflection Outdoors"]
    F --> G["Externally Reflected Component ERC"]
    E --> H["Working Plane Point P"]
    G --> H
    E --> I["Hit Internal Surfaces"]
    I --> J["Wall Ceiling Floor Reflection"]
    J --> K["Internally Reflected Component IRC"]
    K --> H
    H --> L["Total Daylight Factor DF"]
    L --> M["DF equals SC plus ERC plus IRC"]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Daylight Factor Design Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT PARAMETERS"]
        P1["Room Dimensions L x W x H"]
        P2["Window Size and Position"]
        P3["Glazing Transmission tau"]
        P4["Surface Reflectances R values"]
    end
    subgraph COMPUTE["COMPUTATION MODULE"]
        C1["Calculate Total Internal Surface Area A"]
        C2["Calculate Average Reflection R"]
        C3["Compute SC from Walsh Formula"]
        C4["Compute ERC from Lynes Formula"]
        C5["Compute IRC from Lyon Formula"]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT DECISION"]
        O1["Sum DF equals SC plus ERC plus IRC"]
        O2["Compare with IS 3646 Standards"]
        O3{"DF meets requirement?"}
        O4["Design Approved"]
        O5["Revise Window Increase R"]
    end
    P1 --> C1
    P2 --> C3
    P2 --> C4
    P2 --> C5
    P3 --> C3
    P3 --> C5
    P4 --> C2
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C2 --> C5
    C3 --> O1
    C4 --> O1
    C5 --> O1
    O1 --> O2
    O2 --> O3
    O3 --> O4
    O3 --> O5
```

### 4.3 Schematic — Light Path Inside a Room

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Sun and Sky Outside"] -->|Ray 1 Direct| B["Window Glass"]
    A -->|Ray 2 Reflected| C["Ground Paving"]
    B --> D["Direct Beam to Point P"]
    C --> E["Reflected Beam through Window"]
    E --> F["Beam to Point P"]
    D --> G["Strike Internal Wall"]
    G --> H["Multi Reflection Bounce 1"]
    H --> I["Multi Reflection Bounce 2"]
    I --> J["Final Beam to Point P"]
    F --> P["Working Plane Point P"]
    J --> P
```

### 4.4 Decision Matrix — Improving Daylight Factor in a Room

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["DF Below IS 3646 Limit"] --> Q1{"Primary Cause?"}
    Q1 -->|SC too low| A1["Increase Window Area Aw"]
    Q1 -->|IRC too low| A2["Increase Internal Reflectance"]
    Q1 -->|ERC too low| A3["Lighten External Paving"]
    A1 --> B1["Use Tall Sill High Head"]
    A1 --> B2["Add Clerestory or Sky Window"]
    A2 --> C1["Whitewash Ceiling R equals 0.8"]
    A2 --> C2["Light Coloured Walls R equals 0.5 to 0.6"]
    A3 --> D1["Concrete to Light Tile"]
    B1 --> End["Redesign Verified DF 2 to 5 percent"]
    B2 --> End
    C1 --> End
    C2 --> End
    D1 --> End
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define Daylight Factor. State the recommended values of Daylight Factor for (a) a classroom and (b) a residential living room as per IS 3646.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Daylight Factor (DF) is the ratio of the illumination at a point on a horizontal working plane inside a room to the simultaneous illumination on a horizontal surface exposed to the open unobstructed sky, expressed as a **percentage**.

$$DF = \left(\frac{E_{inside}}{E_{outside}}\right) \times 100\%$$

**Recommended values (IS 3646 Part I):**
- **(a) Classroom:** **2.0% to 3.0%**
- **(b) Residential living room:** **1.0% to 2.0%**

---

#### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: List and briefly explain the three components of Daylight Factor.**

**Model Answer:**

The three components of Daylight Factor are:

1. **Sky Component (SC):** The illumination received at a point on the working plane **directly from the visible portion of the sky** through the window aperture. It is the dominant component in well-daylit rooms and depends on window area, glazing transmission, and room geometry.
