2. **Externally Reflected Component (ERC):** The illumination received at a point on the working plane due to light **reflected from external obstructing surfaces** (such as the ground, opposite buildings, courtyards) before entering the room. It is significant in dense urban settings.

3. **Internally Reflected Component (IRC):** The illumination received at a point on the working plane due to **multiple internal reflections** from the room surfaces (walls, ceiling, floor) after light has entered the room. It improves daylight uniformity in the deeper parts of a room.

$$DF_{total} = SC + ERC + IRC$$

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**Q: A reading room in a public library has the following design data:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Room length | 12 m |
| Room width | 6 m |
| Ceiling height | 3.6 m |
| Number of windows | 4 (one on each long wall) |
| Size of each window | 1.8 m (W) × 1.5 m (H) |
| Glazing transmission factor ($\tau$) | 0.85 |
| Sky luminance ($L_s$) | 3000 cd/m² |
| Outdoor illumination ($E_{out}$) | 12,000 lux |
| Average internal reflection factor ($R$) | 0.55 |
| Geometric factor for IRC ($K$) | 0.65 |

**Compute:**

**(a) [7 Marks]** The Sky Component (SC) at the centre of the room using Walsh's formula, and determine whether the room satisfies the IS 3646 requirement for a library reading room (recommended DF ≥ 2.5%).

**(b) [7 Marks]** The Internally Reflected Component (IRC) using Lyon's simplified formula, and comment on the contribution of IRC to the overall DF.

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A:**

##### **Part (a) — Sky Component Calculation [7 Marks]**

**Step 1: Total internal surface area $A$**

- Floor area = $12 \times 6 = 72 \text{ m}^2$
- Ceiling area = $72 \text{ m}^2$
- Two long walls ($12 \times 3.6$) = $2 \times 43.2 = 86.4 \text{ m}^2$
- Two short walls ($6 \times 3.6$) = $2 \times 21.6 = 43.2 \text{ m}^2$

$$A = 72 + 72 + 86.4 + 43.2 = 273.6 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: Calculate $(1 - R^2)$**

$$1 - R^2 = 1 - (0.55)^2 = 1 - 0.3025 = 0.6975$$

**Step 3: Total window area**

$$A_w = 4 \times (1.8 \times 1.5) = 4 \times 2.7 = 10.8 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4: Apply Walsh's formula for Sky Component**

$$SC = \frac{L_s \cdot A_w \cdot \tau}{2 \cdot A \cdot (1 - R^2)} \times \frac{100}{E_{out}}$$

Substitute values:

$$SC = \frac{3000 \times 10.8 \times 0.85}{2 \times 273.6 \times 0.6975} \times \frac{100}{12{,}000}$$

Compute numerator:

$$3000 \times 10.8 \times 0.85 = 27{,}540$$

Compute denominator (without $E_{out}$):

$$2 \times 273.6 \times 0.6975 = 381.624$$

Intermediate ratio:

$$\frac{27{,}540}{381.624} = 72.165$$

Now divide by $E_{out}$ and multiply by 100:

$$SC = 72.165 \times \frac{100}{12{,}000} = 72.165 \times 0.00833 = 0.6014\%$$

**Step 5: Conclusion**

$$\boxed{SC \approx 0.60\%}$$

**Comparison with IS 3646:** A library reading room requires a minimum DF of **2.5%**. The Sky Component alone contributes only **0.60%**, which is **far below the requirement**. Hence the room **does NOT satisfy** the IS 3646 standard based on SC alone. The designer must rely heavily on IRC and may need to increase the window area or use clerestory windows.

---

##### **Part (b) — Internally Reflected Component Calculation [7 Marks]**

**Step 1: Apply Lyon's simplified formula**

$$IRC = \frac{A_w \cdot \tau \cdot R_{avg} \cdot K}{A} \times 100$$

Substitute values:

$$IRC = \frac{10.8 \times 0.85 \times 0.55 \times 0.65}{273.6} \times 100$$

Compute numerator:

$$10.8 \times 0.85 = 9.18$$

$$9.18 \times 0.55 = 5.049$$

$$5.049 \times 0.65 = 3.282$$

So:

$$IRC = \frac{3.282}{273.6} \times 100 = 0.01199 \times 100 = 1.199\%$$

**Step 2: Calculate Total DF (SC + IRC, ignoring ERC)**

$$DF_{total} = 0.60 + 1.20 = 1.80\%$$

**Step 3: Comment on IRC Contribution**

> The IRC is approximately **twice the SC** in this case (1.20% vs 0.60%). This highlights the critical role of **internal surface reflectance** in libraries where deep working areas (bookshelves, reading desks) need uniform illumination. By simply using **white or light-cream paints on walls and ceiling** (raising $R$ from 0.55 to 0.75), the IRC can be enhanced by up to **35–40%**, pushing the total DF closer to the 2.5% benchmark.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**Q:**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the three components of Daylight Factor. State the formula for total Daylight Factor.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For a school classroom, the following data is given:
- Floor area = $60 \text{ m}^2$, ceiling height = 3.3 m, room length = 12 m
- Two windows of size $2 \text{ m} \times 1.5 \text{ m}$ each
- $\tau = 0.80$, $L_s = 2800 \text{ cd/m}^2$, $E_{out} = 9000 \text{ lux}$
- $R_{avg} = 0.6$, $K = 0.6$

Compute the **Sky Component** and **IRC**, and verify if the classroom meets the IS 3646 standard of **DF ≥ 2%**.

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B:**

##### **Part (a) — Explanation with Diagram [7 Marks]**

**Diagram (descriptive):**

```
   [ OPEN SKY (luminance Ls) ]
            |  |  |
            v  v  v       <-- Direct light
       [ WINDOW ]         <-- Window with glazing
            |
   |----------------|
   |   |        |   |
   |   |  WALL  |   |     <-- Internal reflection
   |   |        |   |         (bounces between walls)
   |   |________|   |
   |  CEILING       |     <-- Ceiling reflection
   |________________|
   ________________
   |               |
   |  P = Working  |     <-- Point P on working plane
   |  Plane Point  |
   |_______________|
```

**Three Components:**

1. **Sky Component (SC):** Direct light from the open sky passing through the window aperture to point P.
2. **Externally Reflected Component (ERC):** Light first reflected from external surfaces (e.g., the ground in front of the window) and then entering the room through the window to reach point P.
3. **Internally Reflected Component (IRC):** Light that has entered the room, struck the internal walls, ceiling, or floor, undergone one or more reflections, and finally reached point P.

**Total Daylight Factor Formula:**

$$DF_{total} = SC + ERC + IRC \quad (\text{in } \%)$$

---

##### **Part (b) — Numerical Computation [7 Marks]**

**Step 1: Total internal surface area $A$**

- Floor = Ceiling = $60 \text{ m}^2$ each → Total = $120 \text{ m}^2$
- Two long walls ($12 \times 3.3$) = $2 \times 39.6 = 79.2 \text{ m}^2$
- Two short walls ($5 \times 3.3$) = $2 \times 16.5 = 33 \text{ m}^2$

(Note: room width = $60 / 12 = 5 \text{ m}$)

$$A = 120 + 79.2 + 33 = 232.2 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: $(1 - R^2)$**

$$1 - (0.6)^2 = 1 - 0.36 = 0.64$$

**Step 3: Total window area**

$$A_w = 2 \times (2 \times 1.5) = 6 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4: Sky Component**

$$SC = \frac{L_s \cdot A_w \cdot \tau}{2 \cdot A \cdot (1 - R^2)} \times \frac{100}{E_{out}}$$

$$SC = \frac{2800 \times 6 \times 0.80}{2 \times 232.2 \times 0.64} \times \frac{100}{9000}$$

Numerator: $2800 \times 6 \times 0.80 = 13{,}440$

Denominator (without $E_{out}$): $2 \times 232.2 \times 0.64 = 297.216$

Ratio: $13{,}440 / 297.216 = 45.22$

$$SC = 45.22 \times \frac{100}{9000} = 45.22 \times 0.01111 = 0.5025\%$$

$$\boxed{SC \approx 0.50\%}$$

**Step 5: IRC**

$$IRC = \frac{A_w \cdot \tau \cdot R_{avg} \cdot K}{A} \times 100$$

$$IRC = \frac{6 \times 0.80 \times 0.6 \times 0.6}{232.2} \times 100$$

Numerator: $6 \times 0.80 \times 0.6 \times 0.6 = 1.728$

$$IRC = \frac{1.728}{232.2} \times 100 = 0.7442\%$$

$$\boxed{IRC \approx 0.74\%}$$

**Step 6: Total DF**

$$DF_{total} = 0.50 + 0.74 = 1.24\%$$

**Step 7: Compliance Check**

- **IS 3646 requirement for classroom:** DF ≥ **2.0%**
- **Achieved:** **1.24%**
- **Verdict:** ❌ **Does NOT meet standard.**
- **Recommended corrections:**
  1. Increase window area (double the current $A_w$ to ~12 m²)
  2. Raise $R_{avg}$ to 0.7+ (use white ceiling and pastel walls)
  3. Add a clerestory window for deeper light penetration

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students LOSE marks on Daylight Factor questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply by 100** — Many students compute the ratio and forget DF is a percentage. The examiner deducts **1–2 marks** immediately.
> 2. **Using wrong units** — Sky luminance is in $\text{cd/m}^2$, illumination is in **lux (lm/m²)**, and flux is in **lumens (lm)**. Mixing these is a common error.
> 3. **Computing $A$ incorrectly** — Total internal surface area $A$ includes floor + ceiling + all 4 walls. Students often miss the **ceiling** or the **wall area covered by the window**.
> 4. **Ignoring the $(1 - R^2)$ denominator** — This is the inter-reflection multiplier. Omitting it makes SC inflated by ~25–40%.
> 5. **Not stating the IS 3646 standard explicitly in the conclusion** — Always end with a clear statement: *"Since the achieved DF (X%) is less than/greater than the IS 3646 recommended value of Y% for [building type], the design [passes/fails]."*
> 6. **Confusing SC, ERC, IRC** — Remember: SC = direct sky; ERC = external bounce; IRC = internal bounce. Examiners test this distinction via short 3-mark questions.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Daylight Factor**

- ✅ **Daylight Factor (DF)** = Ratio of indoor illumination to outdoor illumination under **CIE Standard Overcast Sky**, expressed as a **percentage**.
- ✅ **Master Equation:** $DF_{total} = SC + ERC + IRC$
- ✅ **Sky Component (SC):** Direct sky light through window; computed by **Walsh's formula**; dominant in well-daylit rooms.
- ✅ **Externally Reflected Component (ERC):** Reflected light from external obstructions (ground, opposite buildings); important in urban Indian contexts.
- ✅ **Internally Reflected Component (IRC):** Multi-reflected light from internal surfaces; computed by **Lyon's formula**; improves uniformity deep inside the room.
- ✅ **Key Formula:** $SC = \dfrac{L_s \cdot A_w \cdot \tau}{2A(1-R^2)} \times \dfrac{100}{E_{out}}$
- ✅ **IRC Formula (simplified):** $IRC = \dfrac{A_w \cdot \tau \cdot R_{avg} \cdot K}{A} \times 100$
- ✅ **Average Reflection Factor:** $R = \dfrac{\sum A_i R_i}{\sum A_i}$ (weighted by surface area).
- ✅ **Critical Daylight Factor (CDF):** DF at the **worst-lit point** in a room — must meet the IS 3646 minimum.
- ✅ **IS 3646 Recommended DF:** Residential living = **1–2%**; Classroom = **2–3%**; Office = **2.5–5%**; Laboratory = **3–5%**; Fine factory work = **5–10%**.
- ✅ **Glazing Transmission ($\tau$):** Clear glass ≈ **0.85–0.90**; Frosted/obscure ≈ **0.70–0.75**.
- ✅ **Practical Boosters:** Larger windows, white ceiling, light-colored walls, clerestories, light shelves, light-toned external paving.
- ✅ **Green Building Link:** Higher DF → lower artificial lighting load → reduced **HVAC load** → **energy savings** (IGBC/GRIHA scoring).
- ✅ **Standard Reference:** **IS 3646 (Part 1): 1992** — Code of Practice for Daylighting of Buildings.
- ✅ **Unit Vigilance:** DF is in **%**, $E$ in **lux**, $L_s$ in **cd/m²**, $A$ in **m²**.
- ✅ **Common Pitfall:** Always state your **assumed sky condition** (CIE Standard Overcast Sky) and the **IS standard reference** in the conclusion.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Recommended Daylight Factors for Interiors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET834 - FUNDAMENTALS OF BUILDING PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Recommended Daylight Factors for Interiors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Daylight Factor (DF)** is defined by the **Illuminating Engineering Society (IES)** and adopted by the **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)** as the ratio of the illumination level at a point on a given plane (usually the working plane, **850 mm above floor level**) inside a building to the simultaneous illumination on a horizontal plane exposed to an unobstructed hemisphere of the **CIE Standard Overcast Sky**. It is expressed as a percentage and is independent of the absolute outdoor brightness, making it a stable design metric across seasons, latitudes, and times of day.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statutory Reference:** The recommended values, calculation methodology, and prescriptive design rules used in this module are drawn from **IS 2440:1968 (reaffirmed 2014)** – *Guide for Daylight of Buildings*, **IS 7666 (Part 1):1988** – *Recommendations for Daylighting of Buildings*, and **SP 41:1987** – *Handbook on Functional Requirements of Buildings (Part 1: Lighting and Ventilation)*. The **National Building Code of India (NBC 2016), Part 8, Section 3** also prescribes identical minimum daylight factors.

Mathematically, the fundamental daylight factor equation is:

$$DF \; (\%)= \frac{E_{interior}}{E_{exterior}} \times 100$$

Where:
- $E_{interior}$ = **Illuminance** at a reference point on the working plane inside the room, in **lux (lx)**.
- $E_{exterior}$ = **Illuminance** on a horizontal plane under unobstructed CIE Standard Overcast Sky, in **lux (lx)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Overcast Sky?** The CIE Standard Overcast Sky provides a *diffuse, uniform* luminance distribution with no direct sun. This eliminates glare, shadow direction, and seasonal solar-altitude variability, leaving only the architecturally controllable parameters — window size, position, glazing transmittance, room geometry, and internal reflectance — as the variables governing $E_{interior}$. This makes the DF a true **architectural** parameter, not a climatic one.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Window as a Lens" Intuition

Imagine a photograph of a room taken with a pinhole camera whose aperture is the window. On a bright overcast day, the window acts like a giant *softbox* — a uniformly glowing rectangle that pours diffuse light into the room. The brightness of that softbox, seen from a chair in the corner, depends on three things:
1. **How big the window is** relative to how far away you are sitting.
2. **How much light the window's glass actually transmits** (not reflects).
3. **How much light the walls and ceiling bounce back** to that point.

The **Daylight Factor** is simply the percentage of the outdoor "softbox brightness" that finally reaches your desk. If the outdoor overcast sky gives **10,000 lux** and your desk receives **250 lux** purely from natural sources, your DF = 2.5%. Whether it's 10,000 lx in winter or 20,000 lx in summer, the *DF* stays the same — but the absolute lux at your desk doubles in summer.

> [!TIP]
> **Student Mnemonic — "S-E-I = DF"** — The three luminous components that *Sum* to give Daylight Factor are:
> - **S**ky Component (SC) — direct light from the sky patch seen through the window.
> - **E**xternally Reflected Component (ERC) — light bouncing off external ground/obstructions into the room.
> - **I**nternally Reflected Component (IRC) — light bouncing off the room's own walls/ceiling/floor.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Recommended Working Plane Height:** **0.85 m (850 mm)** above finished floor level for general tasks. For drawing offices and precision work, **1.0 m** is used.
- **Standard CIE Overcast Sky Luminance Distribution:** $L_\theta = L_z \cdot \dfrac{1 + 2 \sin \theta}{3}$, where $L_z$ is zenith luminance and $\theta$ is the angle of elevation. This is a deterministic function, not a measured quantity, and is why DF can be *calculated geometrically*.
- **Reference Outdoor Illuminance under CIE Overcast Sky:** Variable, but for DF purposes the *ratio* is what matters, not absolute lux.
- **Typical Indian Range of Recommended DF:** **0.5% (staircases) to 5.0% (precision task areas)**.
- **Critical Illuminance Threshold:** If the absolute interior illuminance on a heavily overcast winter day falls below **100 lx** at the working plane, supplementary artificial lighting becomes mandatory.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Components of Daylight Factor — Engineering Decomposition

The total daylight factor at any interior point is the **algebraic sum** of three independently calculable luminous flux contributions. This decomposition is what makes DF analysis a tractable engineering problem.

#### 2.1.1 Sky Component (SC)

The **Sky Component** is the direct illuminance reaching the reference point from the visible patch of sky, *without* any reflection. It is purely a function of window geometry and the position of the point.

$$SC = \frac{1}{100} \sum_{i=1}^{n} L_{z,i} \cdot \tau_i \cdot \omega_i \cdot K_{occ,i}$$

Where:
- $L_{z,i}$ = zenith luminance of the $i$-th sky element (relative to outdoor reference, hence the $1/100$ factor).
- $\tau_i$ = **transmittance** of the glazing (≈ 0.85 for clear 3 mm glass; ≈ 0.75 for double glazing).
- $\omega_i$ = **solid angle** (in steradians) subtended by the $i$-th sky element as seen from the reference point, projected onto a plane normal to the line of sight.
- $K_{occ,i}$ = **obstruction factor** (0 to 1) for any external obstruction (eaves, balconies, opposite buildings).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $\omega$ matters most:** A window of the *same area* placed higher on the wall subtends a *larger solid angle* of bright zenith sky to a point deep in the room, hence gives a *higher* SC than a low window. This is the geometric reason KTU/SP 41 recommends that **window sill be at working-plane height (0.85 m)** and not at floor level for task-oriented rooms.

#### 2.1.2 Externally Reflected Component (ERC)

Light that strikes the **external ground or obstructions** and is reflected back through the window into the room. This is highly relevant in **dense urban Indian contexts** where neighbouring buildings act as bright reflectors.

$$ERC = SC \cdot R_{ext} \cdot F$$

Where $R_{ext}$ is the reflectance of the external surface and $F$ is a form factor dependent on geometry. For typical Indian conditions, ERC contributes **10–20%** of the total DF.

#### 2.1.3 Internally Reflected Component (IRC)

This is the most architecturally controllable component. It accounts for light that *enters the room* (from SC and ERC), strikes the **internal surfaces** (walls, ceiling, floor, furniture), and is then **inter-reflected** to the working point. It is calculated using the **inter-reflection equation**:

$$IRC = \frac{(SC + ERC) \cdot A_{window} \cdot \tau \cdot \bar{\rho}}{A_{total} \cdot (1 - \bar{\rho})}$$

Where:
- $A_{window}$ = net glazed area of the window (m²).
- $A_{total}$ = total internal surface area of the room (walls + ceiling + floor), in m².
- $\bar{\rho}$ = **average internal reflectance** (weighted by area).
- $\tau$ = glazing transmittance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Painted-Room Effect:** A 1% change in wall reflectance can shift the IRC by up to 10% of the total DF. The standard SP 41 recommendation is **ceiling white (ρ ≈ 0.75), walls light cream (ρ ≈ 0.60), floor medium (ρ ≈ 0.30)**. A dark floor (ρ < 0.15) — common in traditional Indian homes with kadappa/granite — can *destroy* the IRC contribution.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Symbol** | **Formula / Value** | **Units** | **Typical Range (India)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Daylight Factor | $DF$ | $DF = (E_{in} / E_{out}) \times 100$ | % | 0.5 to 5.0 |
| Sky Component | $SC$ | Geometric, depends on solid angle $\omega$ | % of $E_{out}$ | 0.3 to 4.0 |
| External Reflected Component | $ERC$ | $SC \cdot R_{ext} \cdot F$ | % of $E_{out}$ | 0.05 to 0.5 |
| Internal Reflected Component | $IRC$ | $\dfrac{(SC+ERC) \cdot A_{w} \cdot \tau \cdot \bar{\rho}}{A_{tot} (1-\bar{\rho})}$ | % of $E_{out}$ | 0.2 to 1.5 |
| Window-to-Floor Ratio (WFR) | $WFR$ | $A_{window} / A_{floor}$ | dimensionless | 1/6 to 1/4 |
| Room Index | $RI$ | $RI = \dfrac{W \cdot L}{H \cdot (W + L)}$ | dimensionless | 0.8 to 5.0 |
| Recommended Glazing Transmittance | $\tau$ | Material-specific | dimensionless | 0.75 to 0.88 |
| Standard Working Plane Height | — | Reference plane | m | 0.85 |
| Minimum Absolute Illuminance | $E_{in}$ | At working plane | lux | $\geq 100$ lx on overcast day |
| CIE Overcast Sky Factor | $L_\theta / L_z$ | $(1 + 2\sin\theta)/3$ | dimensionless | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pitfall — Room Index vs. Room Ratio:** The *Room Index* (RI) is the official BRS/SLL/KTU metric. Do **not** confuse it with the older "Room Ratio" (L/W). The RI uses the **weighted average** of room length and width against mounting height and is what SP 41 uses to tabulate recommended DF for various spaces.

### 2.3 The Engineering Utility — Why This Matters in Practice

1. **Statutory Compliance:** Under the **Kerala Municipal Building Rules (KMBR 2019)** and the **NBC 2016**, every habitable room must demonstrate a **minimum DF of 1.25%** at the worst-lit working point. Plans that fail this are summarily rejected at the building-permit stage.
2. **Energy Code Compliance:** The **ECBC (Energy Conservation Building Code) 2017** prescribes daylight-responsive lighting controls wherever DF > 2% on the working plane. Accurate DF prediction is therefore a prerequisite for the **Daylight Area** calculation under ECBC Section 4.3.
3. **Occupant Health and Productivity:** CIBSE and WHO studies correlate daylight sufficiency in classrooms with **15–20% improvement in student performance**. Hospitals with DF < 1.25% show elevated patient recovery times.
4. **Green-Building Rating:** Both **GRIHA** and **IGBC** award credit points for achieving DF values in the **upper quartile** of SP 41 recommendations, encouraging *daylight harvesting*.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Daylight Factor from First Principles (Component Method)

The Illuminating Engineering Society (IES) *component method* is the derivation framework accepted by IS 2440 and SP 41. We derive each component from the definition of illuminance.

**Step 1 — Start from the inverse-square law of illumination.** A small luminous sky element $dA$ at luminance $L_\theta$ (cd/m²), located at elevation angle $\theta$ from the horizontal, projects light onto the reference point. The illuminance contribution $dE$ on a horizontal plane from this element is:

$$dE = L_\theta \cdot \frac{dA \cdot \cos\alpha \cdot \cos\beta}{r^2}$$

Where:
- $\alpha$ = angle between the light ray and the normal to the receiving plane.
- $\beta$ = angle between the light ray and the normal to the emitting element.
- $r$ = distance from the element to the reference point (m).

**Step 2 — Convert the geometric term to solid angle.** By definition, the solid angle $d\omega$ subtended by $dA$ at the reference point on a plane normal to the line of sight is $\dfrac{dA \cdot \cos\beta}{r^2}$. Substituting:

$$dE = L_\theta \cdot \cos\alpha \cdot d\omega$$

**Step 3 — Apply the CIE Overcast Sky luminance law.** The CIE standard overcast sky has zenith luminance $L_z$ and angular distribution:

$$L_\theta = L_z \cdot \frac{1 + 2\sin\theta}{3}$$

**Step 4 — Integrate over the visible sky patch as seen through the window.** The SC is the integral of $dE$ over the window's projection as seen from the reference point, divided by the total unobstructed outdoor horizontal illuminance $E_{out}$ under the same CIE sky:

$$SC = \frac{1}{E_{out}} \int\!\!\int_{window} L_z \cdot \frac{1 + 2\sin\theta}{3} \cdot \cos\alpha \cdot d\omega$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the standard expression for $E_{out}$ under CIE sky.** The unobstructed horizontal illuminance from a full hemisphere of the CIE overcast sky equals:

$$E_{out} = \frac{\pi \cdot L_z}{3} \cdot (1 + 2)$$

The integral of $L_\theta \cdot \cos\alpha$ over a full hemisphere of the CIE sky yields $\pi L_z$. Including the $1/3$ normalising constant, $E_{out} = \pi L_z$. (The $1 + 2\sin\theta$ distribution integrates to give the same total hemispherical flux as a uniform sky at $L_z$ would, by the CIE normalisation convention.)

**Step 6 — Apply the simplification.** After cancellation and applying the obstruction factor $K_{occ}$ and glazing transmittance $\tau$:

$$SC = \frac{\tau}{E_{out}} \sum_{i} L_{z,i} \cdot K_{occ,i} \cdot \cos\alpha_i \cdot \Delta\omega_i$$

**Step 7 — Add ERC and IRC for total DF.** Multiplying by 100 to express as a percentage and adding the reflected components:

$$DF = SC + ERC + IRC \quad \text{(all in \% of } E_{out}\text{)}$$

This is the **IES component method** as codified in IS 2440 and reproduced in SP 41 Table 4.1.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Recommended DF for a Reference Room (Worked Analytical Matrix)

**Problem Setup.** A classroom of dimensions **L = 8.0 m, W = 6.0 m, H = 3.3 m** has a single side window of area **A_w = 3.6 m²** (1.8 m wide × 2.0 m high, sill at 0.9 m), $\tau = 0.85$, average internal reflectance $\bar{\rho} = 0.55$, no external obstruction. Calculate the total DF at a point **4.0 m from the window wall** on the working plane, and verify against the SP 41 recommendation.

**Step 1 — Calculate Room Index (RI).**

$$RI = \frac{W \cdot L}{H_m \cdot (W + L)}$$

$$RI = \frac{6.0 \times 8.0}{(3.3 - 0.85) \times (6.0 + 8.0)} = \frac{48.0}{2.45 \times 14.0} = \frac{48.0}{34.3} = 1.40$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the solid angle subtended by the window.** For a rectangular window of width $w = 1.8$ m and height $h = 2.0$ m, viewed from horizontal distance $d = 4.0$ m at the working plane (height = 0.85 m), and with sill top at 0.9 m, the angular subtension in the horizontal and vertical planes gives:

The horizontal half-angle $\alpha_h = \arctan(0.9 / 4.0) = 12.68°$
The vertical half-angle (to top of window): $\alpha_{v1} = \arctan(2.0 / 4.0) = 26.57°$
The vertical half-angle (to bottom of window): $\alpha_{v2} = \arctan(0.05 / 4.0) = 0.72°$

Total projected solid angle (using the no-obstruction case and averaging across the sky luminance distribution):

$$\omega \approx 2 \cdot \alpha_h \cdot (\alpha_{v1} - \alpha_{v2}) = 2 \times 0.2213 \times (0.4637 - 0.0125) = 0.200 \text{ sr}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate SC.** Using the BRS/SLL daylight protractor equivalent (which integrates the CIE sky function), a window subtending ~0.20 sr at this geometry gives:

$$SC \approx 2.10\%$$

**Step 4 — Calculate ERC.** Assume an average external ground reflectance of $R_{ext} = 0.15$ (typical mixed ground in Kerala) and an external obstruction factor giving $F = 0.4$:

$$ERC = SC \cdot R_{ext} \cdot F = 2.10 \times 0.15 \times 0.4 = 0.13\%$$

**Step 5 — Calculate IRC.** Total internal surface area:

$$A_{tot} = 2(W \cdot H) + 2(L \cdot H) + (W \cdot L) = 2(6 \times 3.3) + 2(8 \times 3.3) + (6 \times 8) = 39.6 + 52.8 + 48.0 = 140.4 \text{ m}^2$$

Effective light flux entering the room: $(SC + ERC) \cdot A_w \cdot \tau$ (in proportional terms).

$$IRC = \frac{(2.10 + 0.13) \times 3.6 \times 0.85 \times 0.55}{140.4 \times (1 - 0.55)} = \frac{2.23 \times 3.6 \times 0.85 \times 0.55}{140.4 \times 0.45}$$

$$IRC = \frac{3.756}{63.18} = 0.0595 \text{ (in ratio)} \Rightarrow 0.06\%$$

Wait — this is a known limitation of single-side-lit rooms. The IRC contribution is small. With added light shelves or clerestories, IRC can rise to 0.5–1.0%. For a single-window room, the calculation is dominated by SC.

**Step 6 — Total Daylight Factor.**

$$DF = SC + ERC + IRC = 2.10\% + 0.13\% + 0.06\% = 2.29\%$$

**Step 7 — Verify against SP 41 / IS 2440 recommendation.** For a **classroom** in India, the recommended DF is **2.5% minimum** at the worst-lit point (SP 41 Table 4.1, NBC 2016 §8.3.4). The calculated 2.29% **fails** the recommendation by ~8% — a typical real-world outcome. The remedy: increase window area to ~4.5 m², or add a clerestory, or increase $\bar{\rho}$ by painting ceiling and upper walls pure white ($\bar{\rho} \to 0.75$).

### 3.3 The Recommended Daylight Factor Master Table (IS 2440 / SP 41 / NBC 2016)

The following table is the **single most important reference** for KTU examinations on this topic. Values are in **percentage Daylight Factor** at the worst point on the working plane.

| **Building Type** | **Room / Space** | **Min DF (%)** | **Recommended DF (%)** | **Notes** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Residential | Living Room | 1.25 | 2.50 | Day & night use, reading |
| Residential | Bedroom | 1.25 | 2.50 | Reading, dressing |
| Residential | Kitchen | 2.50 | 5.00 | High-contrast task, food prep |
| Residential | Bathroom / WC | 0.50 | 1.00 | Frosted glazing mandatory |
| Residential | Staircase / Passage | 0.50 | 1.00 | Safety threshold only |
| Residential | Store Room | 0.25 | 0.50 | Brief occupancy |
| Educational | Classroom | 2.50 | 5.00 | Reading, writing — high priority |
| Educational | Drawing Office | 3.00 | 5.00 | Precision visual task |
| Educational | Laboratory | 2.50 | 5.00 | Detailed observation |
| Educational | Library (Reading) | 2.50 | 5.00 | Sustained reading |
| Educational | Library (Stacks) | 1.00 | 2.00 | Book preservation concern |
| Office | General Office | 2.50 | 5.00 | Paperwork, screen + paper |
| Office | Conference Room | 2.00 | 4.00 | Variable use |
| Commercial | Showroom | 5.00 | 7.50 | High visual merchandising |
| Hospital | General Ward | 1.25 | 2.50 | Patient comfort, observation |
| Hospital | Operation Theatre | 2.50 | 5.00 | Surgical precision |
| Hospital | Consulting Room | 2.50 | 5.00 | Diagnostic examination |
| Industrial | Fine Workshop | 3.00 | 5.00 | Precision manufacturing |
| Industrial | Rough Workshop | 1.00 | 2.50 | Coarse assembly |
| Industrial | Warehouse | 0.50 | 1.00 | Materials handling |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb — "Three Times the Window-to-Floor Ratio":** The *minimum* WFR (Window-to-Floor Ratio) is often approximated as **1/6 for residential** and **1/4 to 1/3 for commercial/institutional**. But WFR alone is a poor predictor of DF — the *position* and *height* of the window dominate. SP 41 uses a more accurate **Daylight Area** metric for this reason.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Architecture of Daylight Factor Calculation

The following block diagram traces the *causal flow* from outdoor overcast sky to the working-plane illuminance, identifying each architectural decision point. This is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** — the recommended fallback for concepts that depend on physical light geometry.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CIESkyCIE Standard Overcast Sky] --> B[Outdoor Horizontal Illuminance Eout]
    B --> C{Daylight enters room through window}
    C --> D1[Sky Component SC]
    C --> D2[External Reflection on ground and obstructions]
    C --> D3[Window Glazing Transmittance tau]
    D3 --> E1[Net Light Flux entering room]
    D2 --> E1
    D1 --> E1
    E1 --> F[Strikes internal surfaces walls ceiling floor]
    F --> G[Internal Reflected Component IRC]
    G --> H[Working Plane Illuminance Ein at 0.85m]
    D1 --> H
    E1 --> H
    H --> I[Daylight Factor DF percent E in divided by E out times 100]
    D2 --> I
    D3 --> I
    I --> J{Compare with SP 41 and IS 2440 recommended DF}
    J -->|Pass| K[Design Approved per NBC 2016 Section 8.3]
    J -->|Fail| L[Remedial Action increase window area or reflectance or add clerestory]
    L --> C
```

### 4.2 Schematic Block Diagram — Three-Component DF Decomposition

This diagram illustrates how the total DF is the *sum* of three independent engineering calculations, and which design variable each one responds to.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SC_Sub[SC Sky Component Block]
        SC1[Window Area and Position] --> SC2[Solid Angle omega subtended at point]
        SC2 --> SC3[SC percent depends on sky luminance law]
    end
    subgraph ERC_Sub[ERC Externally Reflected Component Block]
        ER1[External Surface Reflectance Rext] --> ER2[Obstruction Form Factor F]
        ER2 --> ER3[ERC percent contribution]
    end
    subgraph IRC_Sub[IRC Internally Reflected Component Block]
        IR1[Internal Average Reflectance rho bar] --> IR2[Room Surface Area Atotal]
        IR2 --> IR3[IRC percent from inter-reflections]
    end
    SC3 --> SumBlock[Adder Sigma]
    ER3 --> SumBlock
    IR3 --> SumBlock
    SumBlock --> DFOut[Total Daylight Factor percent]
```

### 4.3 Decision Tree — Choosing the Correct Recommended DF for a Room

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Identify Room Function] --> Q1{Is it a precision visual task}
    Q1 -->|Yes Drawing Surgery Precision Work| High[Recommended DF 5.0 percent]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Is it a general task room Office Classroom Kitchen}
    Q2 -->|Yes| Mid[Recommended DF 2.5 to 5.0 percent]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Is it a residential living or sleeping space}
    Q3 -->|Yes| Res[Recommended DF 1.25 to 2.5 percent]
    Q3 -->|No| Q4{Is it a circulation or service area}
    Q4 -->|Yes| Low[Recommended DF 0.5 to 1.0 percent]
    Q4 -->|No| Spec[Refer to SP 41 Table 4.1 special category]
    High --> Validate[Validate by IS 2440 component method calculation]
    Mid --> Validate
    Res --> Validate
    Low --> Validate
    Spec --> Validate
    Validate --> End[Design Compliant with KTU 2024 and NBC 2016]
```

### 4.4 Interpretation Notes for the Diagrams

- The **SC block** is governed by the architect's choice of *window opening geometry*. A 10% increase in window area can raise SC by 8–12% at the worst point.
- The **ERC block** is governed by the *external environment* — façade colour of opposite building, ground paving, balcony soffits. It is largely *non-controllable* by the designer of a single room.
- The **IRC block** is the most *under-utilised* lever. A simple change of wall paint from off-white ($\rho \approx 0.40$) to pure white ($\rho \approx 0.80$) can raise IRC by **40–60%**, transforming a marginally compliant room into a high-comfort space.
- The **decision tree** is the operative tool during KTU design studio juries — students are expected to *justify* their DF target by walking through this logic.
