## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define **Daylight Factor**. State the standard reference sky condition used in its measurement and the recommended working plane height as per **IS 2440**.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Daylight Factor (DF)** is defined as the ratio of the illuminance at a point on the working plane inside a building to the simultaneous outdoor illuminance on a horizontal plane under unobstructed sky, expressed as a percentage. **(1.5 marks)**
> 
> $$DF = \frac{E_{interior}}{E_{exterior}} \times 100\%$$
> 
> The standard reference sky condition is the **CIE Standard Overcast Sky**, which provides a uniform diffuse luminance distribution without direct sunlight. **(1 mark)**
> 
> The standard working plane height prescribed by **IS 2440** is **0.85 m (850 mm) above finished floor level**. **(0.5 mark)**

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** List the **three components of daylight factor** as per the IES Component Method, and identify which component is most influenced by the *internal paint reflectance* of the room.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> The three components of daylight factor as per the IES Component Method are: **(1.5 marks)**
> 1. **Sky Component (SC)** — direct light from the visible sky patch through the window.
> 2. **Externally Reflected Component (ERC)** — light reflected from external surfaces into the room.
> 3. **Internally Reflected Component (IRC)** — light that enters the room and is inter-reflected from internal walls, ceiling, and floor before reaching the working point.
> 
> The component most influenced by the *internal paint reflectance* is the **Internally Reflected Component (IRC)**. **(1.5 marks)** A higher average internal reflectance $\bar{\rho}$ increases the IRC contribution roughly as $\bar{\rho} / (1 - \bar{\rho})$, so dark interior finishes can drastically reduce DF even with a large window.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]*

**(a)** Explain in detail the **IS 2440 / SP 41 procedure** for calculating the Daylight Factor of a room using the **IES Component Method**. List the formulae for SC, ERC, and IRC. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Tabulate the **recommended Daylight Factor values** for the following spaces as per **SP 41** and **NBC 2016**: Living Room, Kitchen, Classroom, Hospital General Ward, Drawing Office, and Staircase. Briefly comment on why the values vary so widely. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — IES Component Method Procedure (7 Marks):**

The IES Component Method, codified in **IS 2440:1968** and reproduced in **SP 41:1987**, computes the Daylight Factor as the **algebraic sum** of three independently calculated components. The procedure is as follows: **(1 mark for procedure overview)**

**Step 1 — Calculate the Sky Component (SC).** The SC is the ratio (in %) of the direct sky illuminance reaching the reference point to the total unobstructed outdoor horizontal illuminance under the CIE Standard Overcast Sky. It is calculated from the solid angle subtended by the window at the reference point, integrated over the CIE sky luminance function: **(2 marks)**

$$SC = \frac{1}{E_{out}} \int\!\!\int_{window} L_z \cdot \frac{1+2\sin\theta}{3} \cdot \tau \cdot K_{occ} \cdot \cos\alpha \cdot d\omega \times 100\%$$

In practice, this is read from a **BRS/SLL Daylight Protractor and Protractor Diagram**, or computed using a software tool (e.g., Radiance, ClimateStudio) with the CIE sky model. Glazing transmittance $\tau$ and obstruction factor $K_{occ}$ are applied as correction factors.

**Step 2 — Calculate the Externally Reflected Component (ERC).** The ERC accounts for daylight reflected from ground and external obstructions back into the window. It is approximately: **(2 marks)**

$$ERC = SC \cdot R_{ext} \cdot F$$

where $R_{ext}$ is the external surface reflectance (typical 0.10 for tarmac, 0.20 for concrete, 0.30 for light paving) and $F$ is a geometric form factor (0.2–0.5 typical). For heavily obstructed urban sites, ERC may be tabulated directly from SP 41 Annex C.

**Step 3 — Calculate the Internally Reflected Component (IRC).** The IRC is the most formula-intensive component, derived from the inter-reflection equation for a room treated as an integrating cavity: **(1.5 marks)**

$$IRC = \frac{(SC + ERC) \cdot A_{window} \cdot \tau \cdot \bar{\rho}}{A_{total} \cdot (1 - \bar{\rho})}$$

where $A_{window}$ is the net glazed area, $A_{total}$ is the total internal surface area (walls + ceiling + floor), and $\bar{\rho}$ is the area-weighted average internal reflectance.

**Step 4 — Sum the components:** **(0.5 mark)**

$$DF = SC + ERC + IRC$$

---

**(b) — Recommended DF Table (7 Marks):**

| **Space Type** | **Min DF (%)** | **Recommended DF (%)** | **Justification** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Living Room | 1.25 | 2.50 | Sustained reading, family activity, requires moderate ambient + task light. |
| Kitchen | 2.50 | 5.00 | High-contrast task (cutting, reading recipes), safety-critical, often occupied during low-external-light hours. |
| Classroom | 2.50 | 5.00 | Reading, writing, board visibility; child visual development; SP 41 and ECBC prescribe upper end. |
| Hospital General Ward | 1.25 | 2.50 | Patient comfort, recovery, observation; not a precision task. |
| Drawing Office | 3.00 | 5.00 | Highest precision sustained task among routine spaces; high contrast detection. |
| Staircase | 0.50 | 1.00 | Safety threshold for step identification only; brief occupancy. |

**(1.5 marks for the values, 0.5 mark for the table structure.)**

**Comment on variation (1 mark):** The recommended DF varies from **0.5% (staircase)** to **5.0% (precision task areas)** — a **10-fold** range — because the *visual task demand* and *occupancy duration* vary drastically. Precision and high-contrast tasks (drawing offices, kitchens, classrooms) require the upper range to support sustained visual acuity, while transit spaces (staircases, passages) only need sufficient light for safety. The Indian Standard deliberately distinguishes between **minimum acceptable** (legally compliant) and **recommended** (best-practice) values, allowing designers some flexibility for context.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]*

**(a)** A study room of dimensions **5.0 m × 4.0 m × 3.0 m (L × W × H)** has a single side window of size **1.5 m × 1.5 m** with sill at **0.9 m**. The glazing transmittance $\tau$ = 0.82, average internal reflectance $\bar{\rho}$ = 0.60, and external obstruction factor $K_{occ}$ = 0.85. The window is positioned centrally on the longer wall. Estimate the total **Daylight Factor** at a point **3.0 m from the window wall** on the working plane, using the component method. Assume $R_{ext} = 0.15$ and form factor $F = 0.35$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss **four key design factors** that influence the Daylight Factor of a room, with specific reference to Indian climatic conditions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Numerical DF Calculation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Room Index (RI) for mounting height $H_m = 3.0 - 0.85 = 2.15$ m:** **(0.5 mark)**

$$RI = \frac{4.0 \times 5.0}{2.15 \times (4.0 + 5.0)} = \frac{20.0}{19.35} = 1.03$$

**Step 2 — Solid angle subtended by window at the point.** For a window 1.5 m × 1.5 m, viewed from 3.0 m horizontal distance, sill at 0.9 m, working plane at 0.85 m (so window bottom is 0.05 m above the eye level): **(1 mark)**

Horizontal half-angle: $\arctan(0.75/3.0) = 14.04° = 0.245$ rad
Vertical half-angle to top: $\arctan(1.5/3.0) = 26.57° = 0.464$ rad
Vertical half-angle to bottom: $\arctan(0.05/3.0) = 0.95° = 0.017$ rad

Projected solid angle approximation: $\omega \approx 2 \times 0.245 \times (0.464 - 0.017) = 0.219$ sr

**Step 3 — SC calculation using CIE sky integration factor.** For a window subtending 0.219 sr at this geometry under CIE overcast sky, the standard protractor-equivalent value (incorporating the $1/3$ sky constant and projection factors): **(1.5 marks)**

$$SC_{gross} \approx 2.40\%$$

Apply correction factors:

$$SC = SC_{gross} \cdot \tau \cdot K_{occ} = 2.40 \times 0.82 \times 0.85 = 1.67\%$$

**Step 4 — ERC calculation:** **(1 mark)**

$$ERC = SC \cdot R_{ext} \cdot F = 1.67 \times 0.15 \times 0.35 = 0.088\% \approx 0.09\%$$

**Step 5 — IRC calculation:** **(2 marks)**

$$A_{total} = 2(W \cdot H) + 2(L \cdot H) + (W \cdot L)$$
$$= 2(4.0 \times 3.0) + 2(5.0 \times 3.0) + (4.0 \times 5.0)$$
$$= 24.0 + 30.0 + 20.0 = 74.0 \text{ m}^2$$
$$A_{window} = 1.5 \times 1.5 = 2.25 \text{ m}^2$$
$$IRC = \frac{(1.67 + 0.09) \times 2.25 \times 0.82 \times 0.60}{74.0 \times (1 - 0.60)}$$
$$IRC = \frac{1.76 \times 2.25 \times 0.82 \times 0.60}{74.0 \times 0.40} = \frac{1.949}{29.6} = 0.066 \text{ i.e. } 0.07\%$$

**Step 6 — Total DF:** **(1 mark)**

$$DF_{total} = 1.67\% + 0.09\% + 0.07\% = 1.83\%$$

**Interpretation:** The calculated DF of **1.83%** meets the SP 41 recommendation for a *living room* (≥ 1.25%) but **fails** the *study room / office* requirement of 2.5%. The designer should either increase the window to **~2.0 m × 1.5 m** or add a clerestory or light shelf to boost SC by ~50%.

---

**(b) — Four Key Design Factors Influencing DF (7 Marks):**

**(1) Window Size and Position (2 marks):** The window area, sill height, and head height all govern the *solid angle* subtended at the working point. In Indian practice, a sill at **0.85–0.9 m** (working-plane level) maximises SC for desk-based tasks. Windows placed too high reduce SC; windows placed too low cause glare and floor-wastage. The **Window-to-Floor Ratio (WFR)** of at least **1/6** is a common rule, but for task rooms in Indian offices a WFR of **1/4** is preferred.

**(2) Glazing Transmittance and Type (1.5 marks):** Clear 3 mm glass ($\tau \approx 0.85$) is the KTU default. In hot climates of Kerala, tinted or low-E glass is often used to control solar heat gain, but this *reduces DF*. The design trade-off: use **spectrally selective low-E glazing** that transmits visible light ($\tau_{vis} \approx 0.70$) while blocking infrared, or use **external shading devices** (chhajjas, louvers) that block direct sun but allow diffuse skylight.

**(3) Internal Reflectance of Room Finishes (2 marks):** As derived above, IRC scales as $\bar{\rho}/(1-\bar{\rho})$. In India, traditional homes with dark kadappa/granite floors ($\rho \approx 0.10$) and oil-painted walls ($\rho \approx 0.30$) suffer very low IRC. SP 41 recommends ceiling reflectance $\geq 0.75$ and wall reflectance $\geq 0.60$. This is especially critical in **tropical high-rainfall regions** where mould-resistant light-coloured paints (like distemper or acrylic emulsion) are both practical and DF-favourable.

**(4) External Obstruction and Orientation (1.5 marks):** The obstruction factor $K_{occ}$ captures the effect of opposite buildings, balconies, eaves, and trees. In dense Indian urban fabric (e.g., old city cores of Kochi, Thrissur, Thiruvananthapuram), $K_{occ}$ can be as low as **0.5–0.7**, halving the SC. Orientation matters because the *CIE overcast sky* is brighter at the zenith — north-facing windows (in the Northern Hemisphere) get more *zenith-dominated* light, while south-facing windows receive light from a *lower elevation* sky, which is less luminous. For Kerala, where the sun is predominantly overhead, **south and north** orientations give similar DF, while **east/west** facades suffer from low-angle direct sun (excluded from the CIE sky model) and glare.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply by 100** — Writing $DF = E_{in}/E_{out}$ without the percentage conversion. KTU penalises this. Always state the *unit* (%).
> 
> 2. **Confusing Daylight Factor with Daylight Area** — DF is a *ratio*; Daylight Area is a *spatial extent* (the floor area where DF meets a threshold). They are related but not interchangeable.
> 
> 3. **Using absolute lux in place of DF** — A common error is writing "the room has 250 lx" and claiming compliance. KTU's standard metric is **DF in %**, not absolute lux, because DF is a *design* parameter independent of the weather.
> 
> 4. **Omitting the working plane height** — IS 2440's 0.85 m reference is mandatory to state. Many students omit this and lose half a mark.
> 
> 5. **Wrong CIE sky reference** — Some students use "blue sky" or "clear sky" models (e.g., CIE Clear Sky with direct sun). For DF under IS 2440 / SP 41, the reference is **CIE Standard Overcast Sky** *only*.
> 
> 6. **Ignoring $\tau$ and $K_{occ}$** — The un-illuminated SC is purely geometric; the *real* SC must always include the transmittance and obstruction corrections. Skipping these is a recurring 1-mark penalty.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Recommended Daylight Factors for Interiors**

- ☐ **Definition:** $DF = (E_{in}/E_{out}) \times 100\%$, measured under **CIE Standard Overcast Sky** at the **working plane (0.85 m)**.
- ☐ **Three Components:** **SC** (sky), **ERC** (external reflection), **IRC** (internal inter-reflection). $DF = SC + ERC + IRC$.
- ☐ **Window-to-Floor Ratio (WFR):** Min **1/6 residential**, **1/4 commercial/institutional** — but WFR alone is a *weak* predictor; the **solid angle** of the window at the working point is what governs SC.
- ☐ **Sill Height:** Always at or just above working plane (0.85–0.9 m) for desk-based task rooms.
- ☐ **Internal Reflectance:** Ceiling ≥ 0.75, Walls ≥ 0.60, Floor ≥ 0.30 (SP 41).
- ☐ **Glazing Transmittance ($\tau$):** Clear 3 mm glass ≈ 0.85; tinted ≈ 0.50–0.70; double-glazed ≈ 0.75.
- ☐ **Key Standard Values to Memorise:**
  - *Living Room:* 1.25% min, 2.5% recommended.
  - *Bedroom:* 1.25% min, 2.5% recommended.
  - *Kitchen:* 2.5% min, 5.0% recommended.
  - *Classroom:* 2.5% min, 5.0% recommended.
  - *Drawing Office:* 3.0% min, 5.0% recommended.
  - *Hospital Ward:* 1.25% min, 2.5% recommended.
  - *Staircase:* 0.5% min, 1.0% recommended.
  - *Bathroom:* 0.5% min, 1.0% recommended (frosted glazing).
- ☐ **Statutory Basis:** IS 2440:1968, IS 7666 (Part 1):1988, SP 41:1987, NBC 2016 §8.3, KMBR 2019.
- ☐ **External Obstruction Factor $K_{occ}$:** Always ≤ 1.0; reduces SC in proportion. Urban Indian sites often 0.6–0.8.
- ☐ **IRC Formula:** $IRC = \dfrac{(SC+ERC) \cdot A_w \cdot \tau \cdot \bar{\rho}}{A_{total} \cdot (1-\bar{\rho})}$ — dark interiors devastate IRC.
- ☐ **ECBC Linkage:** Daylight-responsive lighting controls triggered when DF > 2% on working plane.
- ☐ **Green-Building Linkage:** GRIHA / IGBC award credits for upper-quartile DF performance.

> **Final High-Yield Mnemonic — "DARK ROOMS":**
> **D**efine DF as ratio × 100% under **C**IE overcast sky, **A**pply component method **S**C+ERC+IRC, **R**ecommended values range 0.5–5.0%, **K**ey standard is IS 2440, **R**oom index governs, **O**bstruction factor critical in urban India, **O**rient façade to maximise zenith sky, **M**in working plane 0.85 m, **S**ill at working plane height, **[Kerala Municipal Building Rules (KMBR 2019) compliance required at 1.25% minimum]**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Factors Affecting Thermal Comfort
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET834 - FUNDAMENTALS OF BUILDING PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Factors Affecting Thermal Comfort

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermal Comfort** is formally defined as *"that condition of mind which expresses satisfaction with the thermal environment"* — as established by **ASHRAE Standard 55** and adopted under the **ISHRAE (Indian Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air Conditioning Engineers)** guidelines referenced in KTU's built-environment modules.

In engineering terms, it is the condition in which a building occupant **neither feels too warm nor too cold**, and the body's thermoregulatory system is in **thermal equilibrium** with the surrounding environment — meaning the net heat stored in the body equals **zero (S = 0)**.

The **six primary factors** governing thermal comfort are categorized into two groups:

| Category | Factors | Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| **Environmental** | Air Temperature, Mean Radiant Temperature, Air Velocity, Humidity | $t_a,\ t_r,\ v_a,\ p_a$ |
| **Personal** | Metabolic Rate, Clothing Insulation | $M,\ I_{cl}$ |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Goldilocks Zone" of the Body

> [!NOTE]
> Think of your body as a **continuously running low-power heater (~100 W)** that MUST constantly dump its waste heat into the room. If the room is too cold, the heater can't unload heat → you shiver. If the room is too hot, the heater can't get rid of heat → you sweat. **Thermal comfort is the "Goldilocks Zone"** where the room's heat-extraction ability exactly matches your body's heat-production rate.

A real-world example: imagine sitting in a **parked car in summer**. Even though the air temperature may be 35 °C, the dashboard (radiant surface) can reach 70 °C — your skin absorbs radiant heat *and* struggles to evaporate sweat in humid air. This is **thermal discomfort caused by combined factors**, not temperature alone.

## 1.3 Why Thermal Comfort Matters in Building Planning

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Viewpoint:** Thermal comfort is *the* most frequently asked concept in Module 3 because it directly integrates **building orientation, wall material selection, window sizing, ventilation design, and HVAC sizing**. Every decision a planner makes — from choosing cavity-wall construction to designing sun-shades — is ultimately judged by how it influences the six thermal comfort parameters.

**Key Standard Metrics** the KTU examiner expects students to memorize:
- **1 met** = **58.2 W/m²** (unit of metabolic heat production)
- **1 clo** = **0.155 m²·K/W** (unit of clothing insulation)
- **PMV Scale Range**: −3 (Cold) to +3 (Hot), with **0 = Neutral**
- **PPD** at PMV = 0 is **never zero**; minimum dissatisfaction = **5%**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Primary Factors — Structured Analysis

### Factor 1: Air Temperature ($t_a$)

- The **dry-bulb temperature** of the air surrounding the occupant.
- Measured in **°C** or **K**.
- **Why it matters:** Governs the **convective heat transfer** between skin and air. Every 1 °C change in air temperature shifts PMV by roughly **±0.2 to ±0.3** units.
- **Practical design tip:** In tropical Kerala, recommended indoor $t_a$ = **23 °C to 26 °C** with ceiling fans; **24 °C to 27 °C** with natural ventilation (per NBC 2016).

### Factor 2: Mean Radiant Temperature ($t_r$ or MRT)

- The **uniform temperature of an imaginary enclosure** in which the radiant heat transfer from the human body equals the radiant heat transfer in the actual non-uniform enclosure.
- **Why it matters:** A cold wall in winter can make you feel cold even if $t_a$ is high — the body radiates heat *to* the wall. Conversely, a sunlit glass façade can pump MRT up by **5–10 °C** above $t_a$.
- **Real-world impact:** Designing **high-SRI (Solar Reflectance Index) paints** and **insulated cavity walls** is fundamentally an MRT-management strategy.

### Factor 3: Air Velocity ($v_a$)

- The relative speed of air movement across the occupant's body.
- Measured in **m/s**.
- **Why it matters:** Higher air velocity increases **convective cooling** and **evaporative cooling** from sweat. The "cooling effect" of $v_a$ is non-linear — it depends on whether the occupant is sweating.
- **Design rule:** Air velocity in naturally ventilated buildings should be **0.05 to 0.5 m/s**; AC buildings typically **< 0.15 m/s**.

### Factor 4: Humidity / Water Vapour Pressure ($p_a$)

- Expressed either as **Relative Humidity (%)** or **Partial Pressure of water vapour ($p_a$ in Pa)**.
- **Why it matters:** Humidity governs **evaporative cooling efficiency**. In humid Kerala (RH often 70–90%), sweat evaporates poorly → high discomfort even at moderate $t_a$.
- **Design benchmark:** Optimal indoor RH = **40% to 60%**.

### Factor 5: Metabolic Rate ($M$)

- Rate at which the body produces heat from internal chemical processes.
- Measured in **met** or **W/m²**.
- **Why it matters:** A sleeping person produces ~70 W; a running athlete produces ~700 W. A building designed for sedentary office use (M ≈ 1.2 met) will be miserable for a dance studio (M ≈ 3.0 met).
- **Reference values for building planning:**

| Activity | Metabolic Rate (met) | Metabolic Rate (W/m²) |
|---|---|---|
| Sleeping | 0.7 | 40.7 |
| Seated, reading | 1.0 | 58.2 |
| Office work | 1.2 | 69.8 |
| Walking (3.2 km/h) | 2.0 | 116.4 |
| Heavy labour | 3.0 – 4.0 | 175 – 233 |

### Factor 6: Clothing Insulation ($I_{cl}$)

- Thermal resistance provided by the occupant's clothing ensemble.
- Measured in **clo** or **m²·K/W**.
- **Why it matters:** Clothing acts as a **blanket around the body**, reducing both convective and evaporative heat loss. A winter sweater in an AC room can be as uncomfortable as a summer t-shirt in winter.
- **Reference values:**

| Garment | $I_{cl}$ (clo) | $I_{cl}$ (m²·K/W) |
|---|---|---|
| Naked | 0.0 | 0.000 |
| Light summer wear | 0.3 – 0.5 | 0.047 – 0.078 |
| Standard office wear | 0.7 – 1.0 | 0.109 – 0.155 |
| Heavy winter suit | 1.5 | 0.233 |

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Hack:** Memorize this table. In Part B questions, examiners frequently award marks for correctly writing the **full heat balance equation** and identifying each term.

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning of Terms | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $S = M - W - (C + R + E) - (C_{res} + E_{res})$ | Heat balance of human body | W/m² |
| 2 | $C = f_{cl} \cdot h_c \cdot (t_{cl} - t_a)$ | Convective heat loss from skin | W/m² |
| 3 | $R = 3.96 \times 10^{-8} \cdot f_{cl} \cdot [(t_{cl} + 273)^{4} - (\bar{t}_{r} + 273)^{4}]$ | Radiative heat loss from skin | W/m² |
| 4 | $E = 3.05 \times 10^{-3} + 1.7 \times 10^{-5} \cdot M \cdot (p_{a,s} - p_a)$ | Evaporative heat loss from skin | W/m² |
| 5 | $C_{res} = 1.4 \times 10^{-3} \cdot M \cdot (t_{ex} - t_a)$ | Convective respiratory loss | W/m² |
| 6 | $E_{res} = 1.63 \times 10^{-5} \cdot M \cdot (p_{a,ex} - p_a)$ | Evaporative respiratory loss | W/m² |
| 7 | $PMV = [0.303 \cdot e^{-0.036\,M} + 0.028] \cdot L$ | Fanger's Predicted Mean Vote | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $PPD = 100 - 95 \cdot e^{-0.03353 \cdot PMV^{4} - 0.2179 \cdot PMV^{2}}$ | Predicted % Dissatisfied | % |
| 9 | $1\ met = 58.2\ W/m^{2}$ | Metabolic unit conversion | — |
| 10 | $1\ clo = 0.155\ m^{2}\!\cdot\!K/W$ | Clothing unit conversion | — |
| 11 | $t_{cl} = 35.7 - 0.028 \cdot (M - W) - I_{cl} \cdot [3.96 \times 10^{-8} \cdot f_{cl} \cdot ((t_{cl}+273)^{4} - (\bar{t}_{r}+273)^{4}) + f_{cl} \cdot h_c \cdot (t_{cl} - t_a)]$ | Clothing surface temp (iterative) | °C |
| 12 | $h_c = \max(2.38 \cdot \vert t_{cl} - t_a \vert^{0.25},\ 12.1 \cdot \sqrt{v_a})$ | Convective heat transfer coefficient | W/(m²·K) |

### Notes on Notation Used
- $M$ = metabolic rate (W/m²); $W$ = external work (W/m²); $S$ = heat storage.
- $f_{cl}$ = clothing area factor (dimensionless); $h_c$ = convective coefficient.
- $t_{cl}$ = clothing surface temperature (°C); $\bar{t}_{r}$ = mean radiant temperature (°C).
- $p_a$ = ambient water vapour partial pressure (Pa); $p_{a,s}$ = saturated vapour pressure at skin (Pa).
- $L$ = thermal load on the body = internal heat production − heat loss to environment (W/m²).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> These six factors directly drive **three major design decisions** in any building project:

1. **HVAC Sizing:** Engineers calculate cooling loads using $t_a$, $t_r$, RH, and $v_a$. An auditorium with 500 occupants (high $M$, high $v_a$ demand) requires very different AC capacity than a reading library.
2. **Passive Design Choices:** Window orientation, wall thermal mass (for MRT control), and cross-ventilation design (for $v_a$) all manipulate the environmental factors without mechanical energy.
3. **Energy Codes (ECBC / ECBC+ in India):** Set maximum permissible $t_a$ ranges and minimum ventilation rates based on ASHRAE 55 / ISHRAE comfort bands.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Human Body Heat Balance Equation

The **fundamental thermal equilibrium** for the human body is a **control volume energy balance**. Consider the human body as a control volume. Apply the **First Law of Thermodynamics** for a steady-state, open system at uniform temperature.

### Step 1: Identify All Heat Inputs

The body generates heat internally through **metabolism** ($M$) and performs **external mechanical work** ($W$). The rate of usable heat retained inside the body is therefore:

$$
Q_{in} = M - W
$$

where $M$ is the total metabolic rate (W/m²) and $W$ is the portion of metabolic energy converted to external mechanical work rather than heat (W/m²).

### Step 2: Identify All Heat Outputs

The body loses heat to the environment via **six distinct pathways**:

1. **Convection from skin surface ($C$)** — driven by air temperature difference.
2. **Radiation from skin surface ($R$)** — driven by temperature difference between skin and surrounding surfaces.
3. **Evaporation of sweat from skin ($E$)** — phase change of water absorbs latent heat.
4. **Convection during respiration ($C_{res}$)** — warming inhaled air.
5. **Evaporation during respiration ($E_{res}$** — humidifying inhaled air.
6. **Conduction (minor, typically neglected)** — heat loss through feet in contact with cold floors.

### Step 3: Apply the Conservation of Energy

For thermal comfort at **steady state**, the net heat stored in the body $S = 0$. Therefore:

$$
\text{Heat In} = \text{Heat Out}
$$

$$
M - W = C + R + E + C_{res} + E_{res}
$$

### Step 4: Rearrange to Standard Form

Bringing all terms to the LHS gives the canonical **Fanger's heat balance equation**:

$$
M - W - C - R - E - C_{res} - E_{res} = 0
$$

Or, in terms of **heat storage $S$** (non-zero during transients):

$$
S = M - W - (C + R + E) - (C_{res} + E_{res})
$$

### Step 5: Substituting the Constitutive Equations

Substituting the standard empirical relations:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= f_{cl} \cdot h_c \cdot (t_{cl} - t_a) \\
R &= 3.96 \times 10^{-8} \cdot f_{cl} \cdot [(t_{cl} + 273)^{4} - (\bar{t}_{r} + 273)^{4}] \\
E &= 3.05 \times 10^{-3} + 1.7 \times 10^{-5} \cdot M \cdot (p_{a,s} - p_a) \\
C_{res} &= 1.4 \times 10^{-3} \cdot M \cdot (t_{ex} - t_a) \\
E_{res} &= 1.63 \times 10^{-5} \cdot M \cdot (p_{a,ex} - p_a)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on constants:** The radiative constant **3.96 × 10⁻⁸ W/(m²·K⁴)** equals the **Stefan-Boltzmann constant σ** multiplied by an effective emissivity factor of the clothed body (≈ 0.7 × 5.67 × 10⁻⁸).

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation of Fanger's PMV Equation

### Step 1: Define Thermal Load $L$

The thermal load on the body is defined as the **difference between internal heat generation and total heat loss to the environment**:

$$
L = (M - W) - [(C + R + E) + (C_{res} + E_{res})]
$$

- If $L > 0$: body is gaining heat → feeling **warm** (PMV positive).
- If $L < 0$: body is losing heat → feeling **cool** (PMV negative).
- If $L = 0$: thermal equilibrium → theoretically PMV = 0.

### Step 2: Empirical Calibration by P. O. Fanger (1970)

Fanger conducted **laboratory experiments** with over 1300 human subjects who voted on their thermal sensation on a 7-point ASHRAE scale. He fit an empirical regression linking $L$ to the **mean thermal sensation vote**.

### Step 3: The PMV Equation

The fitted relation, which the KTU syllabus expects verbatim, is:

$$
PMV = [0.303 \cdot e^{-0.036\,M} + 0.028] \cdot L
$$

where $M$ is in **W/m²** and $L$ is in **W/m²**.

### Step 4: PPD from PMV

Because individual perceptions vary, even at PMV = 0 some people are dissatisfied. The Predicted Percentage Dissatisfied is:

$$
PPD = 100 - 95 \cdot e^{-0.03353\,PMV^{4} - 0.2179\,PMV^{2}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight for KTU:** At PMV = 0, PPD = 5% — this is the famous **"5% dissatisfied" minimum**. The PPD-PMV curve is **parabolic** and symmetrical around PMV = 0. Examiners love asking: *"Why is the PPD never zero?"*

**Answer:** Because of **biological variability** — even in a perfectly controlled environment, ~5% of people will always feel slightly too warm or too cool.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A sedentary office worker (M = 1.2 met = 69.84 W/m²) in standard office wear (I_cl = 0.7 clo) sits in a room where $t_a$ = 25 °C, $\bar{t}_r$ = 26 °C, $v_a$ = 0.1 m/s, $p_a$ = 1500 Pa. Given $W$ = 0, $t_{cl}$ = 26.6 °C, $p_{a,s}$ = 2950 Pa, $h_c$ = 3.1 W/(m²·K), $f_{cl}$ = 1.15. Compute the PMV.

### Solution:

**Step 1: Compute $M - W$**

$$
M - W = 69.84 - 0 = 69.84 \text{ W/m}^2
$$

**Step 2: Compute C (convective loss)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= f_{cl} \cdot h_c \cdot (t_{cl} - t_a) \\
  &= 1.15 \cdot 3.1 \cdot (26.6 - 25) \\
  &= 1.15 \cdot 3.1 \cdot 1.6 \\
  &= 5.704 \text{ W/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute R (radiative loss)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= 3.96 \times 10^{-8} \cdot f_{cl} \cdot [(t_{cl} + 273)^{4} - (\bar{t}_{r} + 273)^{4}] \\
  &= 3.96 \times 10^{-8} \cdot 1.15 \cdot [(299.6)^{4} - (299)^{4}] \\
  &= 3.96 \times 10^{-8} \cdot 1.15 \cdot (8.06 \times 10^{9} - 8.01 \times 10^{9}) \\
  &\approx 4.55 \times 10^{-8} \cdot 5.0 \times 10^{7} \\
  &\approx 2.27 \text{ W/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute E (evaporative loss)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= 3.05 \times 10^{-3} + 1.7 \times 10^{-5} \cdot M \cdot (p_{a,s} - p_a) \\
  &= 3.05 \times 10^{-3} + 1.7 \times 10^{-5} \cdot 69.84 \cdot (2950 - 1500) \\
  &= 3.05 \times 10^{-3} + 1.7 \times 10^{-5} \cdot 69.84 \cdot 1450 \\
  &= 3.05 \times 10^{-3} + 1.721 \\
  &\approx 1.724 \text{ W/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Compute respiratory losses**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{res} &= 1.4 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 69.84 \cdot (35.7 - 25) \\
        &= 0.0978 \cdot 10^{-3} \cdot 10.7 \\
        &\approx 1.05 \text{ W/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{res} &= 1.63 \times 10^{-5} \cdot 69.84 \cdot (5630 - 1500) \\
        &= 1.63 \times 10^{-5} \cdot 69.84 \cdot 4130 \\
        &\approx 4.70 \text{ W/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Compute thermal load $L$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= (M - W) - (C + R + E + C_{res} + E_{res}) \\
  &= 69.84 - (5.704 + 2.27 + 1.724 + 1.05 + 4.70) \\
  &= 69.84 - 15.448 \\
  &\approx 54.39 \text{ W/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Compute PMV**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PMV &= [0.303 \cdot e^{-0.036 \cdot 69.84} + 0.028] \cdot 54.39 \\
    &= [0.303 \cdot e^{-2.514} + 0.028] \cdot 54.39 \\
    &= [0.303 \cdot 0.0809 + 0.028] \cdot 54.39 \\
    &= [0.0245 + 0.028] \cdot 54.39 \\
    &= 0.0525 \cdot 54.39 \\
    &\approx +2.86
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall Alert:** A positive PMV of +2.86 indicates a **HOT** condition. If your answer comes negative, double-check the sign convention — students often confuse whether L is "heat load gained" or "heat load lost." By definition, $L > 0$ means the body is gaining heat (feeling warm).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: Body Heat Balance Block Architecture

This diagram maps the **internal heat generation** and **six external heat loss pathways** as a closed-loop energy balance system. Each node represents a physical phenomenon; each arrow represents a heat flux (W/m²).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[Metabolic Heat M] --> W[External Work W]
    M --> S[Heat Storage S = 0 at Comfort]
    W --> S
    S --> C[Convective Loss from Skin C]
    S --> R[Radiative Loss from Skin R]
    S --> E[Evaporative Loss from Skin E]
    S --> Cres[Convective Respiratory Loss Cres]
    S --> Eres[Evaporative Respiratory Loss Eres]
    C --> env[To Environment]
    R --> env
    E --> env
    Cres --> env
    Eres --> env
    env -.Feedback.-> S
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Six Factors of Thermal Comfort — Functional Flow

This Mermaid diagram shows how the **six primary factors** feed into the **PMV-PPD model** to predict occupant thermal sensation. The output drives HVAC and passive design decisions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ENV[Environmental Factors]
        TA[Air Temperature ta]
        TR[Mean Radiant Temperature tr]
        VA[Air Velocity va]
        PA[Humidity pa]
    end

    subgraph PER[Personal Factors]
        META[Metabolic Rate M]
        CLO[Clothing Insulation Icl]
    end

    ENV --> FANGER[Fanger PMV-PPD Model]
    PER --> FANGER
    FANGER --> PMV[PMV Score minus 3 to plus 3]
    FANGER --> PPD[PPD Percent 0 to 100]
    PMV --> DESIGN[Building Design Decisions]
    PPD --> DESIGN
    DESIGN --> HVAC[HVAC Sizing]
    DESIGN --> PASSIVE[Passive Ventilation]
    DESIGN --> ENVELOPE[Wall and Window Thermal Design]
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Iterative PMV Calculation Flowchart

This flow shows the **iterative procedure** to solve for clothing surface temperature $t_{cl}$ before PMV can be calculated. This is a standard KTU 14-mark derivational question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Input ta, tr, va, pa, M, Icl] --> GUESS[Guess initial tcl equals 30 degrees C]
    GUESS --> CALC_HC[Compute hc from equation 12]
    CALC_HC --> CALC_TCL[Compute new tcl from equation 11]
    CALC_TCL --> CHECK{Has tcl converged within 0.001 C?}
    CHECK -- No --> CALC_HC
    CHECK -- Yes --> COMPUTE[Compute C, R, E, Cres, Eres]
    COMPUTE --> COMPUTE_L[Compute thermal load L]
    COMPUTE_L --> COMPUTE_PMV[Compute PMV from equation 7]
    COMPUTE_PMV --> COMPUTE_PPD[Compute PPD from equation 8]
    COMPUTE_PPD --> OUTPUT[Output PMV and PPD]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the iteration is needed:** The clothing surface temperature $t_{cl}$ appears on **both sides** of equation 11 — it depends on $h_c$, which itself depends on $t_{cl}$. Hence, an iterative (successive substitution) solver is required, typically converging in **5 to 8 iterations** for most realistic inputs.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

> **Q: Define the term "Thermal Comfort" as per ASHRAE Standard 55. List any four environmental factors affecting thermal comfort.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermal Comfort** is *"that condition of mind which expresses satisfaction with the thermal environment"* (ASHRAE 55).
>
> The **four environmental factors** are:
>
> 1. **Air Temperature ($t_a$)** — dry-bulb temperature of ambient air.
> 2. **Mean Radiant Temperature ($\bar{t}_r$)** — average temperature of surrounding surfaces as perceived radiatively.
> 3. **Air Velocity ($v_a$)** — relative speed of air motion at the occupant.
> 4. **Humidity / Water Vapour Pressure ($p_a$)** — moisture content of ambient air, usually expressed as Relative Humidity or partial pressure.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

> **Q: Define PMV and PPD. Why can PPD never be zero even under perfect conditions?**

**Model Answer:**

- **PMV (Predicted Mean Vote):** An index that predicts the **mean value** of thermal sensation votes of a large group of people on the ASHRAE 7-point scale, ranging from **−3 (Cold) to +3 (Hot)**, with **0 = Neutral**.
- **PPD (Predicted Percentage Dissatisfied):** An index that predicts the **percentage of thermally dissatisfied occupants** (those voting +3, +2, −2, or −3), derived empirically from PMV.
- **PPD can never be zero** because of **inter-individual physiological variability** — even in a perfectly neutral environment (PMV = 0), approximately **5% of people will always feel slightly too warm or too cool** due to differences in metabolism, age, sex, health, and acclimatization. The PPD–PMV relation is parabolic: $PPD \geq 5\%$ for all PMV.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Format:** Attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**.

---

### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Question]

> **(a)** Explain the **six primary factors** affecting thermal comfort with their units and suitable design ranges. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the **human body heat balance equation** starting from the First Law of Thermodynamics. Explain each term. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution for (a)

The **six primary factors** governing thermal comfort are:

**(i) Air Temperature ($t_a$) — Environmental**
- Unit: **°C or K**.
- Represents the dry-bulb temperature of the air surrounding the occupant.
- **Design range:** 20–27 °C for conditioned spaces; 23–30 °C for naturally ventilated tropical buildings.

**(ii) Mean Radiant Temperature ($\bar{t}_r$) — Environmental**
- Unit: **°C**.
- Uniform temperature of an imaginary enclosure whose radiant heat transfer with the body equals that of the actual non-uniform environment.
- **Design range:** Should be within **±2 °C** of $t_a$ for comfort.

**(iii) Air Velocity ($v_a$) — Environmental**
- Unit: **m/s**.
- Relative motion of air at the occupant's location.
- **Design range:** 0.05–0.5 m/s for natural ventilation; < 0.15 m/s for AC.

**(iv) Humidity / Vapour Pressure ($p_a$) — Environmental**
- Unit: **Pa** (or %RH).
- Moisture content of ambient air.
- **Design range:** 40–60% RH, equivalent to roughly 1000–2000 Pa at 25 °C.

**(v) Metabolic Rate ($M$) — Personal**
- Unit: **W/m²** or **met** (1 met = 58.2 W/m²).
- Rate of internal heat generation by the body.
- **Typical values:** Sleeping 0.7 met; Office 1.2 met; Walking 2.0 met.

**(vi) Clothing Insulation ($I_{cl}$) — Personal**
- Unit: **clo** or **m²·K/W** (1 clo = 0.155 m²·K/W).
- Thermal resistance of the clothing ensemble.
- **Typical values:** Summer indoor 0.5 clo; Winter indoor 1.0 clo; Heavy suit 1.5 clo.

#### ✅ Model Solution for (b)

Consider the human body as a **steady-state control volume**. Apply the **First Law of Thermodynamics**:

**Step 1:** Heat input = metabolic heat production less external work done.

$$
Q_{in} = M - W
$$

**Step 2:** Heat output occurs via **six pathways** (conduction neglected):

$$
Q_{out} = C + R + E + C_{res} + E_{res}
$$

**Step 3:** At thermal comfort, net heat stored $S = 0$:

$$
M - W = C + R + E + C_{res} + E_{res}
$$

**Step 4:** Rearranging:

$$
M - W - C - R - E - C_{res} - E_{res} = 0
$$

**Explanation of Each Term:**

| Symbol | Term | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| $M$ | Metabolic heat | Internal heat generation by biochemical reactions in the body |
| $W$ | External work | Mechanical work output by the body (e.g., walking) |
| $C$ | Convective loss (skin) | Sensible heat loss by air motion across skin |
| $R$ | Radiative loss (skin) | Heat loss by electromagnetic radiation from skin to cooler surfaces |
| $E$ | Evaporative loss (skin) | Latent heat loss via sweat evaporation |
| $C_{res}$ | Respiratory convective | Heat used to warm inhaled air |
| $E_{res}$ | Respiratory evaporative | Heat used to humidify inhaled air |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often **omit the respiratory losses** ($C_{res}$ and $E_{res}$) or forget to subtract $W$ from $M$. Both errors cost 1–2 marks each. Always write **all six loss terms explicitly**.

---

### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Model Question]

> **(a)** Discuss **Fanger's PMV-PPD model**. State its significance in building design. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the **Adaptive Thermal Comfort Model**. How does it differ from Fanger's steady-state model? **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution for (a)

**Fanger's PMV-PPD Model (1970):**
Developed by **Prof. P. O. Fanger** at Kansas State University, this is the most widely used thermal comfort model in HVAC and building standards.

**Step 1:** Define **thermal load $L$** as the imbalance between heat generation and heat loss:

$$
L = (M - W) - (C + R + E + C_{res} + E_{res})
$$

**Step 2:** The **Predicted Mean Vote** is given by:

$$
PMV = [0.303 \cdot e^{-0.036\,M} + 0.028] \cdot L
$$

**Step 3:** The **Predicted Percentage Dissatisfied** is:

$$
PPD = 100 - 95 \cdot e^{-0.03353\,PMV^{4} - 0.2179\,PMV^{2}}
$$

**Significance in Building Design:**

1. **HVAC Sizing:** Engineers use PMV to size AC plants that maintain comfort within ±0.5 PMV.
2. **Code Compliance:** ASHRAE 55, ISHRAE, and EN 16798 use PMV-PPD as the **benchmark** for indoor thermal environments.
3. **Smart Building Controls:** Real-time PMV calculation enables adaptive set-points.
4. **Energy Efficiency:** Designing to PMV = 0 (instead of overcooling) saves **20–30% AC energy**.

#### ✅ Model Solution for (b)

**Adaptive Thermal Comfort Model:**

Unlike Fanger's steady-state heat balance model, the adaptive model recognizes that **occupants are not passive recipients** of their environment — they actively adapt through:

- **Behavioural adaptation** (opening windows, changing clothing, using fans).
- **Physiological adaptation** (acclimatization to local climate).
- **Psychological adaptation** (expectations based on outdoor climate).

The **de Dear & Brager Adaptive Model (1998)** is expressed as:

$$
T_{comf} = 0.31 \cdot T_{out,m} + 17.8 \quad \text{(°C)}
$$

where $T_{out,m}$ is the **monthly mean outdoor temperature**.

**Key Differences from Fanger's Model:**

| Aspect | Fanger (Steady-State) | Adaptive Model |
|---|---|---|
| **Assumption** | Occupant is passive | Occupant adapts actively |
| **Best For** | AC / mechanical ventilation | Naturally ventilated buildings |
| **Climate** | Universal | Climate-specific (expectations matter) |
| **Clothing** | Fixed assumption | Occupants adjust |
| **Ventilation** | Mechanical, controlled | Natural, occupant-controlled |
| **Region** | Global | Strong in tropical climates (Kerala) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-Kerala Context:** Kerala's hot-humid climate makes the **adaptive model particularly relevant** because most low-rise residential and educational buildings rely on natural ventilation. Fanger's model overpredicts cooling loads in such buildings.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones (Cumulative up to 4–6 marks lost per question):**
>
> 1. **Unit confusion:** Writing $I_{cl}$ in `m²·K/W` when the question expects `clo`, or vice-versa. Always state the unit alongside the numerical value.
> 2. **Sign error in L:** Writing $L = \text{heat loss} - \text{heat generation}$ instead of the correct form. This flips the entire PMV answer.
> 3. **Forgetting $W$:** External work is zero for most sedentary tasks, but you MUST state it explicitly.
> 4. **PPD = 5% misconception:** Saying "PPD is zero at PMV = 0" — wrong. PPD has a **minimum of 5%** even at PMV = 0.
> 5. **Skipping respiratory losses:** Always include both $C_{res}$ and $E_{res}$.
> 6. **No diagram in long answers:** A well-labeled heat-balance block diagram (see Section 4) typically earns **1–2 extra marks**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **30-Second Recall Box — Memorize Before Exam:**

- ☐ **Thermal Comfort** = "Condition of mind expressing satisfaction with thermal environment" (ASHRAE 55).
- ☐ **Six Factors:** Air Temperature, Mean Radiant Temperature, Air Velocity, Humidity, Metabolic Rate, Clothing Insulation.
- ☐ **Two Categories:** Environmental (first 4) + Personal (last 2).
- ☐ **1 met** = 58.2 W/m² ; **1 clo** = 0.155 m²·K/W.
- ☐ **PMV Range:** −3 (Cold) → 0 (Neutral) → +3 (Hot).
- ☐ **PPD Formula:** $PPD = 100 - 95 \cdot e^{-0.03353\,PMV^{4} - 0.2179\,PMV^{2}}$.
- ☐ **PPD Minimum** = 5% (never zero, even at PMV = 0).
- ☐ **Fanger's Heat Balance:** $M - W - C - R - E - C_{res} - E_{res} = 0$ at comfort.
- ☐ **Stefan-Boltzmann constant in R equation:** $3.96 \times 10^{-8}$ W/(m²·K⁴).
- ☐ **Adaptive Model** is preferred for naturally ventilated buildings (e.g., Kerala climate).
- ☐ **Kerala-Specific Comfort Range:** $t_a$ = 23–30 °C, RH = 40–70%, $v_a$ = 0.05–0.5 m/s (NBC 2016).
- ☐ **Fanger's Model** is best for AC buildings; **Adaptive Model** is best for naturally ventilated buildings.
- ☐ **PMV = 0** means average thermal sensation is "neutral" — but **5% PPD remains** due to individual variability.
- ☐ **Clothing area factor** $f_{cl}$ is dimensionless (typically 1.0–1.5); increases with thicker clothing.
- ☐ **Mean Radiant Temperature** can be measured using a **globe thermometer** (blackened copper sphere).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effective Temperature: Thermal Comfort Indices – ET
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET834 - FUNDAMENTALS OF BUILDING PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Effective Temperature: Thermal Comfort Indices – ET

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Effective Temperature (ET)** is an empirical, single-number thermal comfort index that combines three environmental parameters — **dry-bulb temperature (DBT)**, **wet-bulb temperature (WBT)**, and **air velocity (V)** — into a unified metric representing human perception of warmth. It was first developed by **C.P. Yaglou and W.C. Vernon in 1923** at the ASHVE (American Society of Heating and Ventilating Engineers) Pittsburgh Laboratory.

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> *"Effective Temperature is that temperature of still, saturated air (i.e., 100 % relative humidity, zero air movement) which produces the same sensation of warmth or comfort on the human body as the actual ambient environment characterized by a given dry-bulb temperature, wet-bulb temperature, humidity, and air velocity."*

Mathematically, this is represented as:

$$
ET = f(T_{db},\, T_{wb},\, V)
$$

where the function $f$ is determined empirically from the **Basic Effective Temperature Chart** rather than from a closed-form algebraic equation.

> [!NOTE] **Reference Frame of ET:**
> The index is always *anchored* to **100 % RH, still air, 0.5 clo clothing**, sedentary activity (1.0 met). All real environments are mapped onto this reference frame.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Twin Room" Intuition

Imagine two identical rooms used for the same office work:

- **Room A:** $30\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ DBT, $20\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ WBT (very dry), with a strong ceiling fan blowing $2\,\mathrm{m/s}$ air.
- **Room B:** $26\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ DBT, $26\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ WBT (100 % saturated, like a steam room), with still air.

Even though the *thermometer* in Room A reads $30\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and Room B reads $26\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$, an occupant stepping from one to the other might report that **Room A feels just as warm as Room B**, or even *cooler*, because of the breeze and dryness.

> **The "twin room"** that produces *the very same sensation* as the real room — but filled with **stagnant, saturated air** — defines the **Effective Temperature**. It is a *perceptual* temperature, not a *physical* one.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Indoor comfort band (ET):** $18\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ to $26\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for sedentary office work.
- **Indoor winter ET:** $18\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ to $21\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$.
- **Indoor summer ET:** $22\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ to $26\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$.
- **Reference state:** $T_{db} = T_{wb} = ET$ (i.e., 100 % RH + still air).
- **Activity baseline:** Sedentary (1.0 met), 0.5 clo indoor winter clothing.
- **Global comfort boundaries:** ET below $10\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ or above $30\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ is uninhabitable for prolonged sedentary activity.

> [!TIP] **Why this matters for Building Planning:** The KTU syllabus places this topic in Module 3 because the **orientation, fenestration, ventilation, and HVAC sizing** of a building are *designed* to keep the indoor ET inside the comfort band throughout the year, accounting for the local climate.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The Three Governing Variables of ET

ET is a function of **three primary environmental variables**. The student must understand how each shifts the ET reading.

| Variable | Symbol | Effect on ET | Practical Cause in Buildings |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Dry-Bulb Temperature** | $T_{db}$ | Direct positive effect | Solar gain through glass, conduction through walls, internal heat gains |
| **Wet-Bulb Temperature / Humidity** | $T_{wb}$ | Direct positive effect (elevates ET above DBT in still air; *depresses* ET at low humidity) | Latent heat, monsoon infiltration, evaporative cooling performance |
| **Air Velocity** | $V$ (m/s) | Negative effect (faster air → lower ET at high temps; *raises* ET at very low temps) | Ceiling fans, cross-ventilation, wind-driven natural ventilation |

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Reasoning Behind the ET Index

1. **Step 1 — Human body as a heat engine:** The body continuously generates metabolic heat (~75–100 W sedentary) and must reject it to the environment via **conduction, convection, radiation, and evaporation**.
2. **Step 2 — Three cooling pathways depend on three variables:**
   - Convective cooling $\propto (T_{skin} - T_{db})$ and $V$.
   - Evaporative cooling $\propto (P_{skin} - P_{a})$ where $P_{a}$ depends on humidity ($T_{wb}$ proxy).
   - Radiative cooling $\propto (T_{skin} - T_{mr})$ — *not directly included in basic ET*.
3. **Step 3 — Same sensation ⇒ same ET:** Subjects in a controlled chamber compare the sensation under varying $(T_{db}, T_{wb}, V)$ combinations against a **reference condition of 100 % RH, still air**. The reference DBT that produces identical warmth is *defined* as the ET.
4. **Step 4 — Lines of constant warmth are plotted on the $T_{db}$–$T_{wb}$ plane** for several discrete air velocities (0, 0.25, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 4.0 m/s). The result is the **Basic Effective Temperature Chart**.
5. **Step 5 — Read-off method:** In practice, an engineer measures $T_{db}$, $T_{wb}$, and $V$ at the occupied zone, locates the point on the chart, and reads off the ET.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE] **Important:** Effective Temperature itself is **NOT** computed from a closed-form algebraic formula in the classical 1923 sense; it is **read from a psychrometric chart of lines of equal warmth**. However, the following relationships are essential for KTU derivations and numerical problems.

| # | Equation | Description | Units | Notes / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $ET = f(T_{db}, T_{wb}, V)$ | Master functional definition | °C | Read from ET chart |
| 2 | $ET = T_{db} \big\vert_{T_{wb}=T_{db},\,V=0}$ | At 100 % RH + still air, $ET = DBT$ | °C | Reference state identity |
| 3 | $CET = f(T_{g}, T_{wb}, V)$ | **Corrected ET** (globe temperature replaces DBT) | °C | Includes radiation |
| 4 | $T_{g} = T_{db} + 1.8 \cdot \frac{(T_{g} - T_{db})}{...}$ | Globe thermometer relation (full form below) | °C | Standard 150 mm black globe |
| 5 | $SET = T_{sk,\,ref} + \Gamma_{cl}$ | **Standard Effective Temperature** (Gagge 1971) | °C | Two-node model |
| 6 | $T_{mrt} \approx T_{g} - 1.8 \cdot V^{0.5} \cdot (T_{g} - T_{db})$ | Mean radiant temp from globe (approx.) | °C | $V$ in m/s |
| 7 | $PMV \approx 0.303 \cdot e^{-0.036M} + 0.028$ | Fanger PMV coefficient (for reference) | — | $M$ in met |
| 8 | $\Delta T_{wb} = T_{wb,\,air} - T_{wb,\,ref}$ | Deviation of wet-bulb from reference | °C | Used in chart cross-checks |

> [!WARNING] **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **ET (Effective Temperature)** with **WBT (Wet-Bulb Temperature)**. WBT is a *physical measurement*; ET is a *sensation* index. They are numerically equal *only* at the reference state (100 % RH, still air).

### 2.4 Why Basic ET Is Insufficient — Motivation for Modern Indices

The 1923 ET chart was built for **still-air chamber experiments** with limited radiant control. In real buildings, the following factors break the ET assumption:

- **Radiant asymmetry** (hot windows, cold walls): not in basic ET.
- **Clothing variation** (0–1.5 clo range in practice): not in basic ET.
- **Activity level** (office 1.0 met to industrial 4 met): not in basic ET.
- **High air speeds:** ET *over-estimates* the cooling benefit of moving air.

These limitations led to the **Corrected Effective Temperature (CET, 1932)** and later the **Standard Effective Temperature (SET, 1971)** and **PMV/PPD (Fanger, 1970)**, which the KTU syllabus covers in subsequent modules.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Role of ET |
|---|---|
| **HVAC sizing** | ET used as a quick design target for indoor conditions |
| **Thermal zoning** | Buildings designed so that year-round ET stays in $18$–$26\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ band |
| **Orientation & sun-shading design** | Windows sized/oriented to prevent summer ET from exceeding $26\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ |
| **Cross-ventilation design** | Air velocity selection to depress ET in hot-dry climates |
| **Green building rating (GRIHA/IGBC)** | ET referenced in indoor thermal comfort credits |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation of Mean Radiant Temperature from Globe Thermometer Reading

The **globe thermometer** reading $T_g$ is influenced by the surrounding air temperature $T_{db}$ and the radiant mean temperature $T_{mrt}$. The energy balance on the 150 mm black-bulb copper sphere yields the standard ASHRAE relation.

**Energy in** (radiation absorbed) = **Energy out** (convective loss):

$$
h_c \cdot A \cdot (T_g - T_{db}) = h_r \cdot A \cdot (T_g - T_{mrt})
$$

where $h_c$ is the convective heat-transfer coefficient and $h_r$ is the linearized radiative heat-transfer coefficient.

Solving for $T_{mrt}$:

$$
T_{mrt} = T_g + \dfrac{h_c}{h_r}\,(T_{db} - T_g)
$$

With the standard ASHRAE approximation for $h_c/h_r$ in indoor conditions:

$$
\dfrac{h_c}{h_r} \approx 1.8 \cdot \sqrt{V} \quad \text{where } V \text{ is in m/s}
$$

Substituting:

$$
T_{mrt} \approx T_g + 1.8 \cdot \sqrt{V}\,(T_{db} - T_g)
$$

Rearranging into the standard textbook form:

$$
T_{mrt} \approx T_g - 1.8 \cdot \sqrt{V}\,(T_g - T_{db})
$$

**This is the operative formula used in CET calculations.**

### 3.2 Analytical Worked Example — Determining ET from Conditions

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):** A classroom has the following measured conditions: dry-bulb temperature $T_{db} = 30\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$, wet-bulb temperature $T_{wb} = 22\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$, and air velocity from a ceiling fan $V = 1.0\,\mathrm{m/s}$. Determine the **Effective Temperature** using the standard chart-reading method, and comment on whether the classroom meets comfort conditions.

**Step 1 — Convert to chart coordinates.**
The Basic ET Chart plots $T_{db}$ on the **horizontal axis** and $T_{wb}$ on the **vertical axis**, with curved lines of constant ET for various air velocities.

- $T_{db} = 30\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ (horizontal)
- $T_{wb} = 22\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ (vertical)
- $V = 1.0\,\mathrm{m/s}$ → use the $V = 1.0\,\mathrm{m/s}$ family of curves.

**Step 2 — Locate the point on the $V = 1.0\,\mathrm{m/s}$ chart.**
Following standard ASHVE chart interpolation at $T_{db} = 30,\, T_{wb} = 22$:

$$
ET \approx 25.5\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with comfort band.**
Summer comfort upper limit: $ET_{max} = 26\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$.

$$
ET = 25.5\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C} < 26\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Acceptable.}
$$

> [!NOTE] **Comment:** The classroom is *just inside* the comfort band. The fan contributes a $4.5\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ depression (from $30\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ DBT to $25.5\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ET), illustrating the *cooling efficiency* of moving air in moderately hot-dry conditions.

### 3.3 Analytical Worked Example — CET Including Radiation

**Problem Statement:** A glass-walled sunlit reading room shows $T_{db} = 28\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$, $T_{wb} = 20\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$, $V = 0.1\,\mathrm{m/s}$. A globe thermometer reads $T_g = 34\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$. Compute the **Corrected Effective Temperature (CET)**.

**Step 1 — Compute $T_{mrt}$.**
Using $T_{mrt} = T_g - 1.8\sqrt{V}\,(T_g - T_{db})$:

$$
\sqrt{V} = \sqrt{0.1} \approx 0.316
$$

$$
T_g - T_{db} = 34 - 28 = 6\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}
$$

$$
1.8 \cdot 0.316 \cdot 6 \approx 3.41\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}
$$

$$
T_{mrt} = 34 - 3.41 = 30.59\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply CET chart at $T_{db}$ replaced by $T_{mrt}$.**

- $T_{mrt} = 30.6\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ (use as horizontal)
- $T_{wb} = 20\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ (vertical)
- $V = 0.1\,\mathrm{m/s}$ (still-air chart used; very low velocity)

Reading the corrected chart:

$$
CET \approx 25.8\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with the basic ET (without radiation correction).**
If we naively used the basic chart with $T_{db} = 28\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$:

$$
ET_{basic} \approx 24.0\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}
$$

The difference:

$$
CET - ET_{basic} = 25.8 - 24.0 = 1.8\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Engineering Insight:** The radiation from the glass walls *adds* nearly $2\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ to the perceived warmth. This is why buildings in hot climates require **external shading, low-emissivity glazing, or insulated cavity walls** — they reduce $T_{mrt}$ and therefore CET.

### 3.4 Summary Matrix of Comfort Boundaries (Board-Ready)

| Climate / Season | Activity (met) | Clothing (clo) | Lower ET Limit | Upper ET Limit | Preferred ET |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Winter indoor | 1.0 | 1.0 | 17 °C | 22 °C | 19–21 °C |
| Summer indoor | 1.0 | 0.5 | 19 °C | 26 °C | 22–24 °C |
| Hot-dry desert | 1.0 | 0.3 | — | 30 °C (fan-assisted) | 24–27 °C |
| Hot-humid coastal | 1.0 | 0.5 | 21 °C | 26 °C | 23–25 °C |
| Light industrial | 2.0 | 0.6 | 16 °C | 22 °C | 18–20 °C |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Functional Flow — How ET Is Determined from Field Measurements

The diagram below maps the logical flow from raw environmental measurements to the final ET reading, including how ET relates to the other thermal comfort indices in the KTU syllabus.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[MEASURED INPUTS] --> B[DRY BULB T TDB]
    A --> C[WET BULB T TWB]
    A --> D[AIR VELOCITY V MS]
    A --> E[GLOBE T TG]
    B --> F{WHICH INDEX}
    C --> F
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F -->|ET 1923| G[USE BASIC ET CHART WITH TDB TWB V]
    F -->|CET 1932| H[COMPUTE TMRT FROM TG AND V]
    H --> I[READ CET CHART WITH TMRT TWB V]
    F -->|SET 1971| J[TWO NODE THERMOREGULATION MODEL]
    J --> K[OUTPUT SET]
    G --> L[FINAL COMFORT DECISION]
    I --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M{IS ET IN COMFORT BAND 18 TO 26 C}
    M -->|YES| N[OCCUPANT COMFORT ACHIEVED]
    M -->|NO| O[MODIFY DESIGN HVAC VENTILATION SHADING]
    O --> P[RECHECK ET]
    P --> L
```

### 4.2 Comparative Block Architecture — ET vs CET vs SET

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BASIC[INDEX EVOLUTION]
        ET[ET 1923 TDB TWB V]
        CET[CET 1932 TMRT TWB V]
        SET[SET 1971 TWO NODE MODEL]
        PMV[PMV PPD 1970 FANGER]
    end
    ET -->|ADDED RADIATION| CET
    CET -->|ADDED CLOTHING AND ACTIVITY| SET
    SET -->|ADDED FULL HEAT BALANCE| PMV
    ET2[NO RADIATION]:::lim --> ET
    CET2[NO CLOTHING]:::lim --> CET
    SET2[COMPLEX TO COMPUTE]:::lim --> SET
    classDef lim fill:#FCE4D6,stroke:#C00000,color:#000000
```

### 4.3 Comfort Zone Layout on the ET Chart (Schematic Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[ET CHART PLANE] --> B[ZONE 1 COLD TWB 10 TO 15]
    A --> C[ZONE 2 COMFORT TWB 17 TO 22]
    A --> D[ZONE 3 WARM TWB 23 TO 26]
    A --> E[ZONE 4 HOT TWB 27 PLUS]
    C --> F[ACCEPTABLE FOR SEDENTARY 1.0 MET]
    B --> G[NEED HEATING AND HUMIDITY CONTROL]
    D --> H[NEED VENTILATION AND DEHUMIDIFICATION]
    E --> I[UNINHABITABLE UNLESS MECHANICALLY COOLED]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — ET-Based Building Design Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[SITE CLIMATE DATA TDB TWB V SOLAR] --> P2[CALCULATE OUTDOOR ET]
    P2 --> P3[DEFINE INDOOR ET TARGET 22 TO 24 C]
    P3 --> P4[DESIGN ENVELOPE ORIENTATION WWR SHADING]
    P4 --> P5[DESIGN VENTILATION WINDOW FANS]
    P5 --> P6[DESIGN HVAC BACKUP]
    P6 --> P7[SIMULATE INDOOR ET USING SOFTWARE]
    P7 --> P8{IN COMFORT BAND}
    P8 -->|YES| P9[DESIGN APPROVED]
    P8 -->|NO| P4
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**Q1.** Define the term **Effective Temperature**. State the reference conditions at which Effective Temperature equals the dry-bulb temperature.

**Model Answer:**
Effective Temperature is an empirical index that combines dry-bulb temperature, wet-bulb temperature, and air velocity into a single value representing the human sensation of warmth. It is defined as the temperature of still, saturated air (100 % RH, zero air movement) that produces the same sensation of warmth as the actual environment. **At 100 % relative humidity and zero air velocity, the effective temperature numerically equals the dry-bulb temperature.**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2.** What are the **three primary environmental variables** that determine the Effective Temperature? Briefly explain the role of air velocity.

**Model Answer:**
The three primary variables are:

1. **Dry-bulb temperature** ($T_{db}$)
2. **Wet-bulb temperature / humidity** ($T_{wb}$)
3. **Air velocity** ($V$)

Air velocity enhances convective and evaporative heat loss from the skin. At high temperatures, increased $V$ *lowers* the effective temperature (cooler sensation). At very low temperatures, increased $V$ can *raise* ET (wind-chill reversal — but in the comfort range, it generally depresses ET).

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Full Module Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Pattern Note:** Per KTU 2024 ESE regulation, each module question carries 14 marks and offers an **internal choice** between two alternatives. Both alternatives are presented below.

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Effective Temperature: Definition, Variables, Chart Use**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**Q.A (a)** *Explain in detail the concept of Effective Temperature. State the limitations of the basic ET index. (7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Effective Temperature (ET) is an empirical thermal comfort index developed by Yaglou and Vernon in 1923. It integrates dry-bulb temperature, wet-bulb temperature, and air velocity into a single number representing human perception of warmth. It is referenced to **100 % RH, still air**.

**Parameters:**
- Dry-bulb temperature $T_{db}$
- Wet-bulb temperature $T_{wb}$ (proxy for humidity)
- Air velocity $V$ (m/s)

**Procedure:** The Basic Effective Temperature Chart (sometimes called the Comfort Chart) provides families of curves for $V = 0, 0.25, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 4.0$ m/s, plotted on a $T_{db}$ vs $T_{wb}$ plane. The engineer locates the measured point and reads off the ET.

**Comfort Band:** $18\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ to $26\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for sedentary indoor activity.

**Limitations of Basic ET:**

1. **Radiation not included** — ignores mean radiant temperature; this is critical near hot glass or cold walls.
2. **Clothing not included** — assumes a fixed 0.5 clo; does not adapt to seasonal variation.
3. **Activity not included** — assumes sedentary 1.0 met; cannot be applied to industrial work.
4. **Over-emphasis on humidity at high air speeds** — the chart assigns disproportionate weight to humidity under moving air.
5. **No physiological model** — purely empirical, no heat-balance foundation.

**Q.A (b)** *A conference room has $T_{db} = 26\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$, $T_{wb} = 19\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$, and air velocity $V = 0.5\,\mathrm{m/s}$. Determine the Effective Temperature from the chart and state whether the room is in the comfort zone. (7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Identify chart coordinates.**
- $T_{db} = 26\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ (horizontal axis)
- $T_{wb} = 19\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ (vertical axis)
- $V = 0.5\,\mathrm{m/s}$ → use the 0.5 m/s family of curves.

**Step 2 — Chart reading.**
On the standard ASHVE Basic ET Chart at $T_{db} = 26\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and $T_{wb} = 19\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ on the $V = 0.5\,\mathrm{m/s}$ curves:

$$
\boxed{ET \approx 22.5\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with comfort band.**
Comfort band: $18\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ to $26\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ (summer indoor, sedentary).

$$
22.5\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C} \in [18,\, 26]\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \textbf{Comfortable.}
$$

**Step 4 — Design remark.**
The room is in the comfort zone, so no HVAC intervention is required. The moderate air velocity of 0.5 m/s is appropriate.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Corrected ET, Radiation, and SET**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**Q.B (a)** *What is Corrected Effective Temperature? How is the mean radiant temperature determined from a globe thermometer? Derive the relevant expression. (7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Corrected Effective Temperature (CET)** — also called *Modified ET* — was developed in 1932 to overcome the *radiation blindness* of the basic ET. In CET, the **dry-bulb temperature is replaced by the mean radiant temperature (T_mrt)**, so the chart input becomes $(T_{mrt}, T_{wb}, V)$.

**Derivation of $T_{mrt}$ from globe thermometer reading $T_g$:**

Energy balance on the black-bulb sphere (steady state):

$$
h_c\,(T_g - T_{db}) = h_r\,(T_g - T_{mrt})
$$

Solving for $T_{mrt}$:

$$
T_{mrt} = T_g + \dfrac{h_c}{h_r}(T_{db} - T_g)
$$

With ASHRAE approximation $h_c/h_r \approx 1.8 \sqrt{V}$ (V in m/s):

$$
\boxed{T_{mrt} \approx T_g - 1.8\,\sqrt{V}\,(T_g - T_{db})}
$$

This expression allows computation of $T_{mrt}$ from a single instrument (the globe thermometer) plus dry-bulb reading and air velocity.

**Q.B (b)** *A factory floor has $T_{db} = 32\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$, $T_{wb} = 24\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$, $V = 0.2\,\mathrm{m/s}$, and globe temperature $T_g = 38\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$. Compute the Corrected Effective Temperature and comment on the design implication. (7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Compute $T_{mrt}$.**

$$
\sqrt{V} = \sqrt{0.2} \approx 0.447
$$

$$
T_g - T_{db} = 38 - 32 = 6\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}
$$

$$
1.8 \cdot 0.447 \cdot 6 \approx 4.83\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}
$$

$$
T_{mrt} = 38 - 4.83 = 33.17\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}
$$

**Step 2 — Read CET chart at $(T_{mrt} = 33.2\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}, T_{wb} = 24\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}, V = 0.2\,\mathrm{m/s})$.**

$$
\boxed{CET \approx 28.0\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with summer comfort upper limit of $26\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$.**

$$
CET = 28\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C} > 26\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \textbf{Hot, outside comfort band.}
$$

**Step 4 — Design implications.**

1. **Roof insulation** must be upgraded to reduce $T_{mrt}$.
2. **Exhaust fans or high-volume low-speed (HVLS) fans** to raise $V$ to 1–2 m/s.
3. **Localised radiant shielding** over workstations.
4. **Evaporative coolers** (suitable in dry factory environment).
5. If interventions cannot lower CET below $26\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$, **mechanical refrigeration** must be provided.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks (Kerala Board Examiners' Pattern):**
> 1. **Conflating ET and WBT** — they are equal *only* at 100 % RH, still air. Examiner's *deduct 1 mark* if you write "ET = WBT" without qualifying the reference state.
> 2. **Forgetting to state the reference state** in the definition — 50 % of the marks for the definition question depend on this.
> 3. **In CET problems, using $T_{db}$ instead of $T_{mrt}$** on the chart — this defeats the whole purpose of "Corrected" ET. Common 2-mark deduction.
> 4. **In $T_{mrt}$ derivation, dropping the $\sqrt{V}$ term or using 1.8 V instead of $1.8\sqrt{V}$** — a 1-mark error that propagates throughout the calculation.
> 5. **Forgetting the comfort-band upper/lower limits** when commenting on "is it comfortable" — examiner expects explicit numerical comparison, not a vague "yes" or "no."
> 6. **Mixing up CET (Corrected ET) with SET (Standard ET)** — they are *not* the same. CET uses a chart; SET uses a two-node physiological model.
> 7. **Skipping units in the final answer** — write °C, not just the number.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** ET = temperature of still, 100 % RH air giving the same sensation as the real environment.
- **Reference state:** $T_{db} = T_{wb} = ET$ at $V = 0$.
- **Three inputs to chart:** $T_{db}, T_{wb}, V$.
- **Comfort band:** $18\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ to $26\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ (sedentary, 0.5–1.0 clo).
- **Winter ET:** $18$–$21\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$; **Summer ET:** $22$–$26\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$.
- **History:** Yaglou & Vernon, 1923, ASHVE Pittsburgh Lab.
- **Limitations:** no radiation, no clothing, no activity, no physiological basis.
- **CET (1932):** replaces $T_{db}$ with $T_{mrt}$; reads off a similar chart.
- **Globe thermometer relation:** $T_{mrt} = T_g - 1.8\sqrt{V}\,(T_g - T_{db})$.
- **SET (1971, Gagge):** two-node thermoregulation model, includes clothing & activity.
- **Fanger PMV (1970):** full predictive mean vote model — the modern gold standard.
- **Air velocity effect:** high $V$ *depresses* ET in hot conditions; over-estimates cooling at very high $V$ in basic ET chart.
- **Humidity effect:** raises ET in still air more than in moving air.
- **Radiation effect:** not in basic ET; very important near hot glass or cold walls.
- **Engineering use:** HVAC sizing, ventilation design, orientation, sun-shading, IGBC/GRIHA credits.
- **Numerical tip:** In every $T_{mrt}$ problem, show $\sqrt{V}$ calculation explicitly.
- **Examiner watch:** Always state the reference state, comfort band limits, and the units °C.

---

**END OF NOTES — MODULE 3: EFFECTIVE TEMPERATURE (THERMAL COMFORT INDICES – ET)**
**OECET834 — FUNDAMENTALS OF BUILDING PLANNING | KTU 2024 SCHEME**

topics.title: CET Charts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET834 - FUNDAMENTALS OF BUILDING PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** CET Charts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of CET Charts

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**CET Charts** in the context of KTU Building Planning refer to a standardized set of pre-tabulated **Cost Estimation Tables (CET)** and reference charts that provide rapid, approximate quantities of materials, labour, costs, and dimensional data for various building components. These charts function as visual engineering tools that compress empirical data, thumb rules, and statistical relationships into readily usable graphical or tabular formats for preliminary estimation and planning purposes.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, CET Charts encompass:
- **Material Consumption Charts** (per unit volume/area of construction)
- **Standard Dimension Charts** for doors, windows, stairs, and rooms
- **Cost Index Charts** tracking inflation-adjusted construction costs
- **Thumb-Rule Charts** for preliminary quantity estimation
- **Component-wise Cost Distribution Charts** (pie/bar format)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** CET Charts are NOT detailed Bills of Quantities (BOQ). They are *preliminary estimation tools* used during the **planning and concept stage** before detailed drawings are finalized. They are derived from CPWD schedules, PWD SoR (Schedule of Rates), and IS Code recommendations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are cooking a large meal for 50 guests. You don't calculate each grain of rice — you use a **standard recipe chart** that says "1 kg rice serves 15 people." CET Charts are the **construction industry's "recipe cards"**.

> **Analogy:** Just as a tailor uses a **standard size chart** (S, M, L, XL measurements) to quickly estimate fabric needed before taking exact measurements, a planner uses CET Charts to estimate materials, costs, and dimensions **before** detailed design drawings are prepared.

### 1.3 Classification of CET Charts

| **Chart Type** | **Primary Use** | **Stage of Project** |
|---|---|---|
| Material Quantity Charts | Quick material take-off | Conceptual Stage |
| Cost Index Charts | Inflation adjustment | Throughout |
| Thumb-Rule Charts | Sanity check on estimates | Pre-tender Stage |
| Dimension Standard Charts | Component sizing | Design Stage |
| Cost Distribution Charts | Budget allocation | Planning Stage |

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition Box:**
> - **Thumb Rule** = An approximate rule-of-thumb used in civil engineering estimation.
> - **Plinth Area Rate (PAR)** = Cost per square foot of plinth area used in CET Charts.
> - **Cost Index** = Multiplier applied to base-year costs to reflect current market rates.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Standard Plinth Area Coverage** for residential buildings: **40% to 75%** of plot area
- **Standard Floor Area Ratio (FAR/FSI)**: varies from **1.0 to 4.0** depending on city zoning
- **Standard Carpet Area to Built-up Area ratio**: **0.70 to 0.85**
- **Concrete density (nominal)**: **25 kN/m³** (or **2400 kg/m³**)
- **Standard brick wall thickness**: **½ brick (115 mm)**, **1 brick (230 mm)**, **1½ brick (345 mm)**
- **Standard door dimensions**: $0.9 \text{ m} \times 2.1 \text{ m}$ (residential)
- **Standard room height**: $3.0 \text{ m}$ to $3.6 \text{ m}$ (residential)
- **Standard stair rise**: $150 \text{ mm}$ to $190 \text{ mm}$
- **Standard stair tread**: $250 \text{ mm}$ to $300 \text{ mm}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation of CET Charts

CET Charts operate on three foundational principles rooted in **empirical engineering data** and **statistical averaging** of completed projects. Understanding these principles is critical for KTU examinations.

**Principle 1: Statistical Average Assumption**
CET Charts assume that similar building components consume approximately equal quantities of materials across geographic regions. The chart value represents the **arithmetic mean** of historical project data, often with a **±10% tolerance band**.

**Principle 2: Linear Scaling with Area/Volume**
Most CET Charts follow a **linear proportional relationship** between building area (or volume) and material consumption. This is mathematically expressed as:

$$Q = k \cdot A^n$$

where $Q$ = quantity of material, $A$ = area (or volume), $k$ = chart coefficient, and $n \approx 1$ for most materials (linear scaling).

**Principle 3: Cost Index Adjustment**
Since raw material costs fluctuate, CET Charts incorporate a **Cost Index multiplier** to update historical data to current market conditions.

### 2.2 Categories of CET Charts (Detailed)

#### A. Material Consumption Charts (Per m² of Plinth Area)

These charts indicate the approximate quantity of major materials required per square meter of plinth area for a standard residential building.

| **Material** | **Quantity per m² of Plinth Area** | **Unit** |
|---|---|---|
| Cement | 0.4 to 0.6 | Bags (50 kg) |
| Steel | 4.0 to 5.0 | kg |
| Sand | 0.4 to 0.5 | m³ |
| Aggregate (Coarse) | 0.7 to 0.9 | m³ |
| Bricks | 500 to 550 | Nos. |
| Water | 1800 to 2000 | Litres |
| Concrete (M20) | 0.35 to 0.45 | m³ |

#### B. Cost Distribution Chart (Typical Residential Building)

| **Component** | **% of Total Cost** |
|---|---|
| Structural (Beam, Column, Slab) | 25% to 30% |
| Brickwork / Masonry | 15% to 20% |
| Flooring | 10% to 12% |
| Finishing (Plaster, Paint) | 10% to 15% |
| Doors & Windows | 8% to 10% |
| Plumbing & Sanitary | 8% to 12% |
| Electrical | 6% to 8% |
| Miscellaneous | 5% to 8% |

#### C. Standard Dimension Chart for Building Components

| **Component** | **Standard Size** | **IS Code Reference** |
|---|---|---|
| Door (External) | $1.0 \text{ m} \times 2.1 \text{ m}$ to $1.2 \text{ m} \times 2.1 \text{ m}$ | IS 1003 |
| Door (Internal) | $0.9 \text{ m} \times 2.1 \text{ m}$ | IS 1003 |
| Window | $1.0 \text{ m} \times 1.2 \text{ m}$ to $1.5 \text{ m} \times 1.5 \text{ m}$ | IS 1003 |
| Ventilator | $0.6 \text{ m} \times 0.6 \text{ m}$ | IS 1003 |
| Stair Rise | $150 \text{ mm}$ to $190 \text{ mm}$ | IS 6942 |
| Stair Tread | $250 \text{ mm}$ to $300 \text{ mm}$ | IS 6942 |
| Kitchen Counter Height | $800 \text{ mm}$ to $900 \text{ mm}$ | NBC 2016 |
| Stair Headroom | $\geq 2.2 \text{ m}$ | NBC 2016 |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Formulas for CET Chart Calculations**

| **#** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{\text{brick}} = L \times B \times H$ | Volume of brickwork | m³ |
| 2 | $N_{\text{bricks}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{brick}}}{V_{\text{1 brick with mortar}}}$ | Number of bricks | Nos. |
| 3 | $V_{\text{1 brick+mortar}} = 0.20 \times 0.10 \times 0.10$ | Standard brick + mortar vol | m³ |
| 4 | $W_{\text{steel}} = R_{\text{steel}} \times V_{\text{RCC}}$ | Steel weight from chart | kg |
| 5 | $Q_{\text{cement}} = R_{\text{cement}} \times A_{\text{plinth}}$ | Cement from chart | bags |
| 6 | $C_{\text{current}} = C_{\text{base}} \times \dfrac{I_{\text{current}}}{I_{\text{base}}}$ | Cost index adjustment | ₹ |
| 7 | $\text{Carpet Area} = \text{Built-up Area} - \text{Wall Area}$ | Carpet area relation | m² |
| 8 | $2R + T = 580 \text{ mm}$ to $600 \text{ mm}$ | Stair formula (Rise $R$, Tread $T$) | mm |
| 9 | $\text{Plinth Area Rate (PAR)} = \dfrac{\text{Total Cost}}{\text{Plinth Area}}$ | Cost per unit area | ₹/m² |
| 10 | $\text{FAR (FSI)} = \dfrac{\text{Total Floor Area}}{\text{Plot Area}}$ | Floor area ratio | Dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** CET Charts are widely used by **quantity surveyors, planning engineers, and government PWD officials** to perform rapid feasibility studies, budget approvals, and tender evaluations before detailed estimation begins. The CPWD (Central Public Works Department) publishes annual **Schedule of Rates (SoR)** that form the backbone of these charts in India.

### 2.4 Practical Application in Engineering

1. **Pre-tender Estimation:** Contractors use CET Charts to quickly verify whether a quoted tender price is reasonable.
2. **Loan Sanction:** Banks like SBI, HDFC use cost-per-square-foot charts for home loan approval.
3. **Government Budgeting:** PWD/CPWD uses these charts for initial budget allocation in annual plans.
4. **Feasibility Studies:** Architects use them to test multiple design options before finalizing.
5. **Valuation of Buildings:** Property valuers use depreciation-adjusted CET charts for asset valuation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Number of Bricks from Plinth Area Using CET Chart
