**Problem Statement:** Estimate the number of bricks required for a residential building with a plinth area of $120 \text{ m}^2$, assuming brickwork in superstructure for ½ brick thick walls.

**Step 1:** Identify the wall area from plinth area using thumb rule.
- **Logic:** Typically, wall area $\approx 1.5 \times$ plinth area for a standard single-storey building.

$$A_{\text{wall}} = 1.5 \times A_{\text{plinth}}$$

$$A_{\text{wall}} = 1.5 \times 120 = 180 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2:** Identify the wall thickness.
- **Logic:** Given as ½ brick thick.

$$T_{\text{wall}} = 115 \text{ mm} = 0.115 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate the volume of brickwork.

$$V_{\text{brickwork}} = A_{\text{wall}} \times T_{\text{wall}}$$

$$V_{\text{brickwork}} = 180 \times 0.115$$

$$V_{\text{brickwork}} = 20.7 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 4:** Calculate the volume of one standard brick with mortar.

$$V_{\text{1 brick+mortar}} = 0.20 \times 0.10 \times 0.10$$

$$V_{\text{1 brick+mortar}} = 0.002 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 5:** Calculate the total number of bricks.

$$N_{\text{bricks}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{brickwork}}}{V_{\text{1 brick+mortar}}}$$

$$N_{\text{bricks}} = \dfrac{20.7}{0.002}$$

$$N_{\text{bricks}} = 10{,}350 \text{ bricks}$$

**Step 6:** Verification using CET Chart (alternate method).
- **Logic:** CET chart says ~500 bricks per m² of plinth area for ½ brick walls.

$$N_{\text{bricks (chart)}} = 500 \times 120 = 60{,}000 \text{ bricks}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Discrepancy Note:** The two methods differ because the thumb-rule "1.5×" assumes only external wall area, while the CET chart includes all internal partitions. For detailed estimation, detailed drawings are mandatory. CET charts are *preliminary* tools only.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Cost Index Adjustment

**Problem Statement:** A building cost ₹25,00,000 in 2018 when the cost index was 120. Estimate the current cost if the current cost index is 165.

**Step 1:** Identify the formula.

$$C_{\text{current}} = C_{\text{base}} \times \dfrac{I_{\text{current}}}{I_{\text{base}}}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the values.

$$C_{\text{current}} = 25{,}00{,}000 \times \dfrac{165}{120}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the ratio.

$$\dfrac{165}{120} = 1.375$$

**Step 4:** Calculate the current cost.

$$C_{\text{current}} = 25{,}00{,}000 \times 1.375$$

$$C_{\text{current}} = 34{,}37{,}500$$

> **Result:** The current estimated cost is **₹34,37,500** (approximately ₹34.38 Lakhs).

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Stair Dimension Calculation (Building Planning Chart)

**Problem Statement:** Design a staircase for floor-to-floor height of $3.0 \text{ m}$ with tread of $270 \text{ mm}$.

**Step 1:** Apply the standard stair formula.

$$2R + T = 580 \text{ mm (residential)}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $T = 270$ mm.

$$2R + 270 = 580$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $R$.

$$2R = 580 - 270 = 310$$

$$R = 155 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4:** Calculate the total number of risers.
- **Logic:** Total height ÷ rise per step.

$$N_{\text{risers}} = \dfrac{3000}{155} = 19.35$$

Round to nearest integer: $N_{\text{risers}} = 20$

**Step 5:** Recalculate actual rise.

$$R_{\text{actual}} = \dfrac{3000}{20} = 150 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6:** Calculate number of treads.
- **Logic:** Treads = Risers − 1 (per flight).

$$N_{\text{treads}} = 20 - 1 = 19$$

**Step 7:** Calculate the going (horizontal length) of stair.

$$\text{Going} = 19 \times 270 = 5130 \text{ mm} = 5.13 \text{ m}$$

> **Result:** Stair requires **20 risers, 19 treads**, with rise of **150 mm** and tread of **270 mm**. Total horizontal length of flight = **5.13 m**.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Estimation Methods

| **Parameter** | **CET Chart Method** | **Detailed BOQ Method** | **Thumb-Rule Method** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Accuracy | $\pm 10\%$ | $\pm 2\%$ | $\pm 20\%$ |
| Time Required | 30 minutes | 8 to 24 hours | 5 minutes |
| Data Required | Chart values | Detailed drawings | Approximate area |
| Use Stage | Conceptual | Tender/Final | Sanity check |
| KTU Exam Relevance | **High** | Medium | High |
| Cost Involved | Free | Engineering fee required | Free |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow: CET Chart Application Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Project Initiation] --> B[Collect Building Parameters]
    B --> B1[Plinth Area]
    B --> B2[Number of Floors]
    B --> B3[Wall Thickness]
    B --> B4[Location / Cost Index]
    C[Select Appropriate CET Chart] --> C1[Material Chart]
    C --> C2[Cost Index Chart]
    C --> C3[Dimension Chart]
    C --> C4[Cost Distribution Chart]
    D[Apply Chart Coefficients] --> E[Compute Quantities]
    E --> E1[Cement Bags]
    E --> E2[Steel kg]
    E --> E3[Brick Count]
    E --> E4[Concrete m3]
    F[Apply Cost Index Multiplier] --> G[Compute Total Cost]
    G --> H[Compare with Market Rate]
    H --> H1{Within +/- 10 percent?}
    H1 -- Yes --> I[Accept Estimate]
    H1 -- No --> J[Recalibrate with Detailed BOQ]
    I --> K[Finalize Preliminary Budget]
    J --> K
```

### 4.2 Diagram: Cost Distribution Pie Representation (Mermaid Bar Chart)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CD[Cost Distribution of Residential Building]
        direction TB
        S1[Structural: 28 percent]
        S2[Masonry: 18 percent]
        S3[Flooring: 11 percent]
        S4[Finishing: 12 percent]
        S5[Doors and Windows: 9 percent]
        S6[Plumbing: 10 percent]
        S7[Electrical: 7 percent]
        S8[Misc: 5 percent]
    end
```

### 4.3 Diagram: CET Chart Decision Tree for Material Estimation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Given Plinth Area] --> Q1{Building Type}
    Q1 -- Residential --> R1[Use 500 bricks per m2]
    Q1 -- Commercial --> R2[Use 450 bricks per m2]
    Q1 -- Industrial --> R3[Use 350 bricks per m2]
    R1 --> Apply[Apply Cost Index]
    R2 --> Apply
    R3 --> Apply
    Apply --> End[Final Material Cost]
```

### 4.4 Diagram: Cost Index Adjustment Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Base Year Cost CB] --> B[Base Year Index IB]
    C[Current Year Index IC] --> D[Compute Ratio IC over IB]
    B --> D
    D --> E[Multiply CB by Ratio]
    E --> F[Current Cost CC]
    F --> G{Verify with PWD SoR}
    G -- Match --> H[Approved]
    G -- Mismatch --> I[Investigate Discrepancy]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: Define CET Charts and list any four types used in building planning.**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]**

**Model Answer:**

CET Charts are standardized pre-tabulated reference charts used for rapid, approximate estimation of materials, costs, and dimensions during the **conceptual planning stage** of a building project, before detailed drawings are available.

**Four types of CET Charts are:**

1. **Material Consumption Charts** – Indicate quantity of cement, steel, bricks per unit plinth area.
2. **Cost Index Charts** – Used to adjust historical costs to current market rates.
3. **Standard Dimension Charts** – Provide standard sizes for doors, windows, stairs, etc.
4. **Thumb-Rule Charts** – Provide quick approximation rules (e.g., 500 bricks per m² plinth area).

---

#### **Question 2: What is the Plinth Area Rate (PAR) method of estimation? Write its formula.**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Repeated Pattern]**

**Model Answer:**

The **Plinth Area Rate (PAR) method** is a preliminary estimation technique in which the total cost of a building is estimated by multiplying the **plinth area** of the building by a **standard rate per unit plinth area** derived from previously executed similar projects.

**Formula:**

$$\text{Total Cost} = \text{Plinth Area} \times \text{Rate per m}^2$$

$$\text{Plinth Area Rate (PAR)} = \dfrac{\text{Total Cost of Building}}{\text{Total Plinth Area in m}^2}$$

The PAR method is highly useful in CET Charts for quick budget estimation during the initial planning stage.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks):**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Likely Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the various types of CET Charts used in building planning. Discuss their advantages and limitations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A residential building has a plinth area of $150 \text{ m}^2$. Using standard CET Chart values, estimate the quantities of (i) cement (ii) steel (iii) bricks (iv) coarse aggregate. Assume the building is a standard single-storey structure. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A(a):**

**Types of CET Charts:**

1. **Material Consumption Charts:** Provide per-unit-area consumption of cement, steel, aggregate, sand, bricks, water. Used to derive quantities without detailed drawings.

2. **Cost Index Charts:** Show the variation of construction cost over years using a base year. Allows updating old estimates to current prices.

3. **Standard Dimension Charts:** Tabulate standard sizes for building components such as doors ($0.9 \text{ m} \times 2.1 \text{ m}$), windows ($1.2 \text{ m} \times 1.2 \text{ m}$), stair rise ($150 \text{ mm}$), etc.

4. **Cost Distribution Charts:** Pie or bar charts showing percentage cost distribution across components (e.g., structural 28%, masonry 18%, finishing 12%).

5. **Thumb-Rule Charts:** Empirical rules like "500 bricks per m² plinth area" or "4.5 kg steel per m² plinth area" for quick sanity checks.

**Advantages:**
- Speed: Estimate prepared in minutes.
- Low cost: No detailed drawings required.
- Useful at planning/feasibility stage.
- Useful for cross-verification of detailed estimates.

**Limitations:**
- Low accuracy (±10% to ±20% error).
- Not suitable for tender preparation.
- Assumes standard conditions; fails for unusual designs.
- Does not account for site-specific factors.
- Cannot replace detailed BOQ for billing purposes.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A(b):**

**Given:** Plinth Area $= 150 \text{ m}^2$

**Step 1:** Cement quantity (using CET Chart: 0.5 bags per m²)

$$Q_{\text{cement}} = 0.5 \times 150 = 75 \text{ bags}$$

**Step 2:** Steel quantity (using CET Chart: 4.5 kg per m²)

$$W_{\text{steel}} = 4.5 \times 150 = 675 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3:** Bricks (using CET Chart: 500 bricks per m²)

$$N_{\text{bricks}} = 500 \times 150 = 75{,}000 \text{ bricks}$$

**Step 4:** Coarse aggregate (using CET Chart: 0.8 m³ per m²)

$$V_{\text{agg}} = 0.8 \times 150 = 120 \text{ m}^3$$

> **Final Results:**
> - Cement: **75 bags**
> - Steel: **675 kg**
> - Bricks: **75,000 Nos.**
> - Coarse aggregate: **120 m³**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Alternate Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the Cost Index method of updating construction estimates with a suitable example. The cost of a building in 2020 was ₹30 Lakhs when the cost index was 110. Find the current cost if the present cost index is 170. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Design a staircase for a residential building with floor-to-floor height of $3.0 \text{ m}$ using the standard formula $2R + T = 580 \text{ mm}$. Calculate the number of risers, treads, and the horizontal going of the flight. Assume tread $= 280 \text{ mm}$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B(a):**

**Cost Index Method Explanation:**

The Cost Index method updates the cost of an old estimate to current market rates using a published cost index that tracks material and labour price changes over time.

**Formula:**

$$C_{\text{current}} = C_{\text{base}} \times \dfrac{I_{\text{current}}}{I_{\text{base}}}$$

**Given:**
- $C_{\text{base}} = ₹30{,}00{,}000$ (in 2020)
- $I_{\text{base}} = 110$
- $I_{\text{current}} = 170$

**Calculation:**

$$C_{\text{current}} = 30{,}00{,}000 \times \dfrac{170}{110}$$

$$\dfrac{170}{110} = 1.5454...$$

$$C_{\text{current}} = 30{,}00{,}000 \times 1.5454 = 46{,}36{,}363.64$$

> **Result:** The current estimated cost is **₹46,36,364 (approximately ₹46.36 Lakhs)**.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B(b):**

**Step 1:** Apply the standard formula.

$$2R + T = 580 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $T = 280$ mm.

$$2R + 280 = 580$$

$$2R = 300$$

$$R = 150 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate number of risers.

$$N_{\text{risers}} = \dfrac{\text{Floor Height}}{R} = \dfrac{3000}{150} = 20 \text{ risers}$$

**Step 4:** Calculate number of treads.
- Treads = Risers − 1

$$N_{\text{treads}} = 20 - 1 = 19 \text{ treads}$$

**Step 5:** Calculate horizontal going.

$$\text{Going} = 19 \times 280 = 5320 \text{ mm} = 5.32 \text{ m}$$

> **Final Result:**
> - Rise $R = 150$ mm
> - Tread $T = 280$ mm
> - Number of Risers = 20
> - Number of Treads = 19
> - Total Going = 5.32 m

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Unit Confusion:** Students often write "m²" instead of "m³" for volume of brickwork, or forget to convert mm to m in thickness calculation. **Always show unit conversion explicitly.**
>
> 2. **Missing Cost Index Year:** When solving cost index problems, students forget to state "Base Year" and "Current Year" clearly. Examiners allocate 1 mark for proper notation.
>
> 3. **Stair Formula Error:** Using $R + T = 580$ mm (wrong) instead of $2R + T = 580$ mm (correct). This single error loses 2–3 marks.
>
> 4. **Tread Count Error:** Writing $N_{\text{treads}} = N_{\text{risers}}$ instead of $N_{\text{treads}} = N_{\text{risers}} - 1$.
>
> 5. **Forgetting Mortar Volume:** When calculating number of bricks, students use brick volume $0.0019$ m³ instead of brick+ mortar volume $0.002$ m³.
>
> 6. **Not Mentioning Tolerances:** CET Charts are approximate; examiners expect students to mention **"±10% accuracy"** somewhere in the answer.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist for CET Charts:**

- **CET** stands for **Cost Estimation Tables/Charts** — preliminary tools, NOT final bills.
- **Five Types of CET Charts:** Material, Cost Index, Dimension, Cost Distribution, Thumb Rule.
- **Thumb Rule for Bricks:** 500 bricks per m² of plinth area (½ brick walls).
- **Thumb Rule for Steel:** 4.0 to 5.0 kg per m² of plinth area.
- **Thumb Rule for Cement:** 0.4 to 0.6 bags per m² of plinth area.
- **Cost Index Formula:** $C_{\text{current}} = C_{\text{base}} \times \dfrac{I_{\text{current}}}{I_{\text{base}}}$
- **PAR Formula:** $\text{PAR} = \dfrac{\text{Total Cost}}{\text{Plinth Area}}$
- **Stair Formula (Residential):** $2R + T = 580 \text{ mm}$ to $600 \text{ mm}$.
- **Stair Formula (Public):** $2R + T = 600 \text{ mm}$ to $640 \text{ mm}$.
- **Maximum Tread:** 300 mm | **Minimum Rise:** 100 mm | **Maximum Rise:** 190 mm.
- **Carpet Area Ratio:** Built-up Area × 0.70 to 0.85.
- **Standard Door:** $0.9 \text{ m} \times 2.1 \text{ m}$ (internal), $1.0 \text{ m} \times 2.1 \text{ m}$ (external).
- **Standard Window:** $1.0 \text{ m} \times 1.2 \text{ m}$ to $1.5 \text{ m} \times 1.5 \text{ m}$.
- **Plinth Height:** $0.45 \text{ m}$ to $0.6 \text{ m}$ above ground level.
- **Headroom for Stair:** Minimum 2.2 m.
- **Cost Distribution:** Structural (28%) + Masonry (18%) + Finishing (12%) + Others (42%).
- **Accuracy Range:** CET Charts = ±10% to ±20%; Detailed BOQ = ±2%.
- **CPWD SoR** is the standard reference for cost index in India.
- **FAR/FSI** = Total Floor Area / Plot Area.
- **Always state Base Year and Current Year** in cost index problems.
- **Always show unit conversions** (mm → m, m² → m³) explicitly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bioclimatic Chart
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET834 - FUNDAMENTALS OF BUILDING PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Bioclimatic Chart

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **BIOCLIMATIC CHART** — A graphical analytical tool (originally developed by **Victor Olgyay, 1963**) that correlates the **outdoor climatic parameters** (dry-bulb temperature, relative humidity, wind, and solar radiation) with the **human thermal comfort zone**, in order to derive appropriate **passive architectural design strategies** for a given geographic location.

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme, the bioclimatic chart is positioned as a **pre-design analytical instrument** that helps an architect select building form, orientation, wall-roof construction, openings, shading devices, and ventilation patterns based on the local macro/micro-climate.

### 1.1.1 Olgyay's Original Chart vs Givoni's Modified Chart

| Feature | Olgyay's Bioclimatic Chart (1963) | Givoni's Bioclimatic Chart (1969) |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Identify passive design strategy | Same, but quantitatively extended |
| X-axis | Dry-bulb temperature (°C) | Dry-bulb temperature (°C) |
| Y-axis | Humidity ratio (kg water/kg dry air) | Absolute humidity / moisture content |
| Comfort zone | Single polygon (approx. 40 % of chart) | Refined polygon with sub-zones |
| Strategies shown | 4 basic strategies | 8–10 detailed strategies |
| Reference | *Design with Climate* | *Man, Climate and Architecture* |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Climate Prescription Pad"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy:** Imagine a doctor examining a patient. The patient (a *site/location*) comes with "symptoms" — a hot, humid, cold, or dry climate. The doctor (the *architect*) uses a diagnostic chart (the *Bioclimatic Chart*) which, like a prescription pad, tells him exactly what "medicine" the building needs. The "medicines" are passive strategies like *shading, insulation, cross-ventilation, courtyards, or evaporative cooling*.
>
> So, if the site reports **temperature = 35 °C** and **relative humidity = 80 %**, the chart immediately "prescribes" **high thermal mass + cross-ventilation + shading** as the correct passive design mix.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metrics (Universal Comfort Assumptions):**
> - **Internal Heat Generation by Human Body:** ≈ **100 W** (sedentary adult)
> - **Clothing Insulation (clo):** **0.6 – 0.8 clo** (light tropical wear)
> - **Air Velocity (no wind reference):** **< 0.1 m/s** (still air baseline)
> - **Mean Radiant Temperature:** Assumed equal to air temperature
> - **Reference Relative Humidity for Comfort:** **30 % – 70 %**
> - **Comfort Dry-Bulb Temperature Band:** **20 °C – 27 °C** (for tropical Indian context, ASHRAE 55-2020 extended range)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bioclimatic Chart is NOT a Climate Classification tool** — it is a *Design Strategy Selection* tool. While a climatic classification (like Köppen's) describes the climate, the bioclimatic chart tells the architect *what to do about it*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Conceptual Framework of the Chart

The Bioclimatic Chart is a **two-dimensional psychrometric-style plot** where:

- **X-axis (horizontal):** **Dry-Bulb Temperature (°C)** — measures the actual air temperature using a standard thermometer exposed to air but shielded from radiation.
- **Y-axis (vertical):** **Absolute Humidity / Moisture Content** — expressed either as *humidity ratio (w)*, *wet-bulb temperature*, or *relative humidity (%)*.

A point on the chart represents a single climatic condition. The **comfort zone** is drawn as a polygon (typically a hexagonal/octagonal shape) representing all combinations of temperature and humidity within which **80 % of sedentary humans feel thermally neutral**.

## 2.2 Logical Breakdown — How the Chart Works

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 1 — Plot the Local Climate Data**
> Hourly/monthly outdoor temperature and humidity values of the site are plotted as a cluster of points (or an envelope) on the chart. The spread of these points reveals the climatic character of the site.
>
> **Step 2 — Overlay the Comfort Zone**
> The standardized comfort polygon is superimposed. Any climate point falling **inside** the polygon requires **no special architectural intervention** — only basic design.
>
> **Step 3 — Identify the Off-Zone Quadrants**
> Climate points falling in specific off-comfort regions trigger specific design strategies:

| Climatic Quadrant on Chart | Condition | Required Passive Strategy |
|---|---|---|
| **Lower-left** (Cool + Dry) | Cold winters, low humidity | **Solar Gain + Insulation** |
| **Lower-right** (Hot + Dry) | Hot desert, arid | **Evaporative Cooling + Thermal Mass** |
| **Upper-left** (Cool + Humid) | Cold damp climates | **Wind Protection + Heating** |
| **Upper-right** (Hot + Humid) | Hot tropical | **Shading + Cross-Ventilation + Dehumidification** |
| **Far below comfort** | Extreme cold | **High Insulation + Solar Gain + Internal Heat** |
| **Far above comfort** | Extreme heat | **Evaporation + High Mass + Ventilation** |

> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** Used by **LEED, GRIHA, ECBC (India)** certified projects to justify passive design decisions and earn **Energy & Atmosphere credits**.

## 2.3 The Eight Fundamental Passive Strategies (Olgyay's Typology)

1. **Radiation (R)** — Solar gain via south-facing glazing in cold climate.
2. **Wind Protection (W)** — Buffer spaces, recessed entrances, courtyard typology.
3. **Low Humidity (Evaporative Cooling) (E)** — Fountains, water bodies, courtyards.
4. **High Humidity Control** — Dehumidification via desiccants or mass walls.
5. **Convective Cooling (Cv)** — Cross-ventilation, induced stack effect.
6. **Convective Heating** — Heated air circulation in cold climates.
7. **Induced Evaporation (I)** — Wind catchers, qanats, evaporative roofs.
8. **No Special Measure (N)** — When climate falls within comfort zone.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CORE FORMULAS / METRICS FOR BIOCLIMATIC CHART INTERPRETATION**

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Humidity Ratio | $w = 0.622 \cdot \dfrac{P_v}{P_a - P_v}$ | kg water / kg dry air | Y-axis plotting |
| 2 | Saturation Vapour Pressure (Magnus) | $P_{vs} = 0.6108 \cdot e^{\left(\dfrac{17.27 \cdot T}{T + 237.3}\right)}$ | kPa | Find RH from T & Td |
| 3 | Relative Humidity | $RH = \dfrac{P_v}{P_{vs}(T)} \times 100$ | % | Locate point on Y-axis |
| 4 | Wet-Bulb Temperature | $T_w = T - \dfrac{(P_{vs}(T) - P_v)}{1.8 \cdot p}$ | °C | Alternate Y-axis |
| 5 | Mean Radiant Temperature | $T_{mrt} \approx T_{air}$ (still air) | °C | Reference for comfort |
| 6 | Operative Temperature | $T_{op} = \dfrac{T_{air} + T_{mrt}}{2}$ (still air) | °C | Used inside comfort zone |
| 7 | PMV Index Range (Fanger) | $-0.5 \leq PMV \leq +0.5$ | dimensionless | Defines comfort zone |
| 8 | Clo Value (Tropical) | $I_{cl} = 0.6$ clo | clo | Indian clothing baseline |
| 9 | Metabolic Rate (Sedentary) | $M = 1.0$ met = **58.2 W/m²** | W/m² | Heat gain assumed |
| 10 | Effective Temperature (ET) | Read directly from chart lines | °C | Combined T + RH effect |

> [!NOTE]
> **Clo Definition:** **1 clo = 0.155 m²·K/W** — the thermal resistance of a typical business suit. Tropical Indian attire (light cotton) ≈ **0.4–0.6 clo**.

### 2.4.1 The Comfort Zone Polygon (Standard Form)

For the **standard adult (70 kg, 1.7 m² BSA, 0.6 clo, 1.0 met, still air)** the comfort polygon vertices are approximately:

$$\text{Lower Bound: } T_{db} \approx 20\,°C, \quad RH \approx 30\,\%$$

$$\text{Upper Bound: } T_{db} \approx 27\,°C, \quad RH \approx 70\,\%$$

> Any plotted point **inside** this polygon ⇒ *No special architectural intervention required* (Strategy = **N**).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Converting Dry-Bulb Temperature & Relative Humidity to a Plottable Point

**Problem:** A site in **Kerala (Thiruvananthapuram)** has outdoor dry-bulb temperature **T = 33 °C** and relative humidity **RH = 78 %**. Convert this to coordinates suitable for plotting on Olgyay's Bioclimatic Chart.

**Step 1 — Compute Saturation Vapour Pressure at T = 33 °C**

$$P_{vs}(T) = 0.6108 \cdot e^{\left(\dfrac{17.27 \cdot T}{T + 237.3}\right)} \;\; \text{kPa}$$

$$\text{Exponent} = \dfrac{17.27 \times 33}{33 + 237.3} = \dfrac{569.91}{270.3} = 2.1083$$

$$P_{vs}(33\,°C) = 0.6108 \times e^{2.1083} = 0.6108 \times 8.236$$

$$P_{vs} = 5.031 \;\; \text{kPa}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Actual Vapour Pressure (P_v)**

$$P_v = \dfrac{RH}{100} \times P_{vs} = 0.78 \times 5.031 = 3.924 \;\; \text{kPa}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Humidity Ratio (w)**

$$w = 0.622 \times \dfrac{P_v}{P_{atm} - P_v}$$

$$w = 0.622 \times \dfrac{3.924}{101.325 - 3.924} = 0.622 \times \dfrac{3.924}{97.401}$$

$$w = 0.622 \times 0.0403 = 0.02506 \;\; \text{kg/kg dry air}$$

**Step 4 — Plottable Coordinates**

$$X\text{-axis} = T_{db} = 33\,°C$$
$$Y\text{-axis} = w = 0.02506 \;\; \text{kg/kg}$$

> **Conclusion:** The point (33, 0.0251) lies in the **upper-right quadrant** of the chart (Hot + Humid), triggering the passive strategy prescription: **Shading + Cross-Ventilation + Thermal Mass with Dehumidification**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Computing Operative Temperature for Indoor Comfort Validation

**Problem:** A naturally ventilated classroom in Kerala has indoor air temperature **T_air = 30 °C** and surface (wall) temperature **T_surface = 29 °C** with air velocity **v = 0.3 m/s**. Compute the **Operative Temperature (T_op)** and determine if it falls within the comfort zone.

**Step 1 — Compute the Convective Heat Transfer Coefficient (h_c)**

For low air velocity (forced convection regime):

$$h_c = 10.4 \cdot \sqrt{v} \;\; \text{W/m²·K (at surface temp ≈ 30 °C)}$$

$$h_c = 10.4 \times \sqrt{0.3} = 10.4 \times 0.5477 = 5.70 \;\; \text{W/m²·K}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Mean Radiant Temperature (T_mrt)**

Assume equal surface emissivity (ε = 0.9) for simplicity:

$$T_{mrt} \approx T_{surface} = 29\,°C$$

**Step 3 — Compute T_op using weighted formula**

$$T_{op} = A \cdot T_{air} + (1 - A) \cdot T_{mrt}$$

where

$$A = \dfrac{h_c}{h_c + h_r}, \quad h_r \approx 4 \cdot \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot T_{mrt}^3$$

For ε = 0.9, T_mrt = 302.15 K:

$$h_r = 4 \times 0.9 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (302.15)^3$$

$$(302.15)^3 = 2.758 \times 10^7$$

$$h_r = 4 \times 0.9 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 2.758 \times 10^7 = 5.63 \;\; \text{W/m²·K}$$

$$A = \dfrac{5.70}{5.70 + 5.63} = 0.503$$

$$T_{op} = 0.503 \times 30 + 0.497 \times 29 = 15.09 + 14.41 = 29.5\,°C$$

**Step 4 — Comparison with Comfort Range**

$$T_{op} = 29.5\,°C \;\; \text{vs} \;\; \text{Comfort Range} = 20\text{–}27\,°C$$

> **Result:** 29.5 °C is **2.5 °C above** the upper comfort limit ⇒ **Ventilation enhancement required** (increase v, add ceiling fans, induce stack ventilation).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Reading a Bioclimatic Chart

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this matrix to instantly read any plotted climatic point:**

| Chart Region | Climate Character | Building Form | Wall | Roof | Openings | Key Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Inside Polygon** | Mild/Temperate | Compact | Standard | Standard | Standard | None special (N) |
| **Below Polygon (Cool-Dry)** | Cold Continental | Compact, low surface area | High insulation, double wall | Insulated roof | Small, south-facing | Solar Gain (R) |
| **Right of Polygon (Hot-Dry)** | Desert / Arid | Compact, courtyards | High thermal mass (stone/adobe) | Insulated, high mass | Small, deep-set | Evaporative Cooling (E) |
| **Upper-Right (Hot-Humid)** | Tropical (Kerala-type) | Open, verandah, courtyards | Light, ventilated | Insulated + ventilated cavity | Large, cross-ventilated | Convective Cooling (Cv) + Shading |
| **Upper-Left (Cool-Humid)** | Marine / Cold-humid | Compact, sheltered | Moisture-resistant | Sloped, ventilated | Small, protected | Wind Protection (W) + Heating |
| **Far-Right (Extreme Heat)** | Hot arid (Rajasthan) | Recessed, earth-sheltered | High mass | High mass, double roof | Very small | All passive + Evaporation |
| **Far-Left (Extreme Cold)** | Alpine (Ladakh) | Compact, low surface | Super-insulated | Insulated + snow load | Triple-glazed, south | Solar + Internal Heat |

### 3.3.1 Worked Numerical Example — Applying the Matrix to Kerala

**Given:** T_db = 33 °C, RH = 78 % (Thiruvananthapuram typical day)

**Step 1:** From earlier derivation, w = 0.0251 kg/kg.

**Step 2:** Plot point (33, 0.0251) → falls in **Upper-Right Region (Hot-Humid)**.

**Step 3:** Reading the matrix → Apply:
- **Building Form:** Open plan, courtyard, verandah, double-skin facade
- **Wall:** Lightweight hollow concrete, cavity wall, weatherboard
- **Roof:** Insulated, ventilated cavity, sloped, high SRI paint
- **Openings:** Large (30–40 % of floor area), cross-ventilated, external chhajja
- **Strategy:** **Cv (Convective Cooling) + Solar Shading**

**Step 4 — Cooling Load Reduction Potential:**

For a standard 100 m² house in Kerala, adopting these strategies yields:

$$\text{Annual Cooling Load Reduction} \approx 30\text{–}45\,\%$$

$$\text{Carbon Emission Reduction} \approx 1.2 \text{–} 1.8 \;\; \text{tCO}_2 \text{ / year}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bioclimatic Chart — Block Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Site Climate Data T_db, RH, Wind, Solar] --> B[Compute Humidity Ratio w]
    B --> C[Plot Point on Bioclimatic Chart]
    C --> D{Point Location?}
    D -->|Inside Comfort Zone| E[Strategy N - No Special Measure]
    D -->|Hot-Humid Upper Right| F[Cv - Convective Cooling + Shading]
    D -->|Hot-Dry Right| G[E - Evaporative Cooling + Mass]
    D -->|Cool-Dry Lower Left| H[R - Solar Gain + Insulation]
    D -->|Cool-Humid Upper Left| I[W - Wind Protection + Heating]
    F --> J[Building Design Prescription]
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    E --> J
    J --> K[Output: Form, Wall, Roof, Openings, HVAC Backup]
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Layout of the Olgyay Chart Zones

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph AXIS["Axes of Bioclimatic Chart"]
        X["X-axis: Dry-Bulb Temperature in degrees C"]
        Y["Y-axis: Humidity Ratio / Moisture"]
    end

    subgraph HOTZONE["Hot Zones Right Side"]
        H1["Hot-Humid Upper Right - Kerala, Mumbai"]
        H2["Hot-Dry Lower Right - Rajasthan, Dubai"]
    end

    subgraph COLDZONE["Cold Zones Left Side"]
        C1["Cool-Dry Lower Left - Ladakh, Leh"]
        C2["Cool-Humid Upper Left - Ooty, Shillong"]
    end

    subgraph COMFORT["Central Comfort Zone"]
        CZ["Comfort Polygon - 20 to 27 C, 30 to 70 percent RH"]
    end

    H1 --> CZ
    H2 --> CZ
    C1 --> CZ
    C2 --> CZ
    X --> H1
    X --> H2
    X --> C1
    X --> C2
    Y --> H1
    Y --> C1
```

## 4.3 Application Pipeline — From Climate Data to Building Form

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Collect 12 month hourly T and RH data] --> S2[Step 2: Compute hourly w and ET]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Plot envelope on Bioclimatic Chart]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Identify dominant off-comfort quadrant]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Look up passive strategy table]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Select building form and orientation]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Specify wall, roof, window details]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Validate with Energy Simulation eQUEST or DesignBuilder]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9: Final Building Plan]
```

## 4.4 Strategy-to-Building-Element Mapping Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    STRAT[Passive Strategy] -->|Solar Gain| E1[South Glazing, Trombe Wall]
    STRAT -->|Evaporative Cooling| E2[Fountain, Courtyard, Wet Roof]
    STRAT -->|Convective Cooling| E3[Cross Vents, Stack Vent, Wind Tower]
    STRAT -->|Wind Protection| E4[Recessed Entry, Lobby, Buffer]
    STRAT -->|Thermal Mass| E5[Stone Wall, Adobe, Concrete Roof]
    STRAT -->|Insulation| E6[Cavity Wall, Double Roof, Double Glazing]
    STRAT -->|Shading| E7[Chhajja, Fins, Pergola, Vegetal Cover]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define Bioclimatic Chart. Name its originator.**
> [!NOTE]
> **[KTU University Exam – May 2023 | Module 3 | 3 Marks]**
>
> **Model Answer:**
> A bioclimatic chart is a graphical tool that relates outdoor climatic conditions (dry-bulb temperature, humidity, solar radiation, wind) to the human thermal comfort zone, in order to prescribe appropriate passive architectural design strategies for a given location. It was developed by **architect Victor Olgyay in 1963** and published in his book *"Design with Climate: Bioclimatic Approach to Architectural Regionalism"*. **(3 marks)**

### **Q2. List any four passive design strategies identified by Olgyay's Bioclimatic Chart.**
> [!NOTE]
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 | Module 3 | 3 Marks]**
>
> **Model Answer:**
> The four key passive strategies (out of Olgyay's eight) are:
> 1. **Solar Radiation Gain (R)** – for cool-dry climates
> 2. **Evaporative Cooling (E)** – for hot-dry climates
> 3. **Convective Cooling (Cv)** – for hot-humid climates
> 4. **Wind Protection (W)** – for cool-humid climates **(3 marks — 0.75 each)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks — Full Module Internal Choice)

### **Q. (a)** Explain in detail the concept of the Bioclimatic Chart. Describe its components, axes, comfort zone, and how the eight passive strategies are derived from it. **(7 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 3 | Part B Sub-Q (a) | 7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(1) Concept (1.5 marks):**
A Bioclimatic Chart is a psychrometric-style two-dimensional graph developed by **Victor Olgyay (1963)**. It maps outdoor climatic parameters against the **human thermal comfort zone** to determine appropriate **passive design responses** for a building.

**(2) Axes and Components (2 marks):**
- **X-axis:** Dry-bulb temperature (°C), typically ranging 0–45 °C.
- **Y-axis:** Humidity (absolute moisture content or wet-bulb temperature or RH %).
- **Comfort Zone:** A polygon representing combinations of T and humidity within which 80 % sedentary humans feel thermally neutral — typically **20–27 °C, 30–70 % RH** for tropical reference.
- **Reference Subject:** Standard adult (70 kg, 1.0 met, 0.6 clo, still air, no radiation gain).

**(3) Eight Passive Strategies (2.5 marks):**

| Symbol | Strategy | Climate Zone |
|---|---|---|
| N | No special measure | Inside comfort zone |
| R | Solar radiation gain | Cool-dry |
| E | Evaporative cooling | Hot-dry |
| Cv | Convective cooling | Hot-humid |
| W | Wind protection | Cool-humid |
| H | High mass | Diurnal swing regions |
| I | Induced ventilation | High humidity, low wind |
| DC | Dehumidification | Very humid |

**(4) Application Logic (1 mark):**
Plot site's hourly/monthly climate data as an envelope on the chart. Compare with comfort zone. The off-zone region where most points fall indicates the **dominant climate**, and the corresponding strategy is selected as the **primary design response**.

### **Q. (b)** Using Olgyay's Bioclimatic Chart, identify the climate of **Kerala (Thiruvananthapuram)** and recommend suitable passive design strategies for a residential building. **(7 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 3 | Part B Sub-Q (b) | 7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(1) Climatic Data of Thiruvananthapuram (2 marks):**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Mean Daily Max T_db | 31–33 °C |
| Mean Daily Min T_db | 23–25 °C |
| Annual RH Range | 70–95 % |
| Annual Rainfall | **1700 mm** (high) |
| Diurnal Swing | 6–8 °C (moderate) |
| Prevailing Wind | SW Monsoon (4–6 m/s in June–Aug) |

**(2) Plotting on Bioclimatic Chart (1.5 marks):**
The plotted cluster of monthly average points falls predominantly in the **upper-right quadrant** of the chart (T = 28–33 °C, RH = 70–90 %), i.e., the **Hot-Humid Zone**, well outside and above the comfort polygon.

**(3) Recommended Passive Strategies (2.5 marks):**
- **Convective Cooling (Cv):** Large cross-ventilated openings (30–40 % of floor area), induced stack ventilation through clerestories, courtyard plan.
- **Solar Shading:** External chhajjas, vertical fins, west-wall vegetal screens, SRI > 0.7 roof finish.
- **Lightweight Construction:** Hollow concrete block walls, ventilated double-skin facade, double-roof with cavity.
- **Wind Capture:** Monsoon wind orientation (plan oriented to receive SW winds), recessed balconies, jalis (perforated screens).

**(4) Expected Outcome (1 mark):**
Annual cooling energy reduction of **30–45 %**, thermal comfort improved by **2–3 °C** operative temperature reduction, achieving **GRIHA 3-Star** equivalent rating.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

### **Q. (a)** Compare Olgyay's Bioclimatic Chart and Givoni's Bioclimatic Chart. Discuss their advantages and limitations in the Indian context. **(7 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Module 3 | Part B Sub-Q (a) | 7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(1) Olgyay's Chart (1.5 marks):**
- **Originator:** Victor Olgyay, 1963, *Design with Climate*.
- **Axes:** T_db (X) vs Humidity (Y).
- **Strategies:** 4 main (R, E, Cv, W) + 4 supplementary.
- **Comfort Zone:** Single polygon based on effective temperature (ET).
- **Application:** Macro-level climate-to-strategy mapping.

**(2) Givoni's Chart (1.5 marks):**
- **Originator:** Baruch Givoni, 1969, *Man, Climate and Architecture*.
- **Axes:** Same as Olgyay.
- **Strategies:** 8–10 detailed strategies with sub-zones (H = High Mass, AC = Air-Conditioned, Dehumidification, etc.).
- **Comfort Zone:** Refined using **Fanger's PMV model**, with separate summer/winter zones.
- **Application:** Building-component-level design decisions.

**(3) Comparative Table (2.5 marks):**

| Feature | Olgyay's Chart | Givoni's Chart |
|---|---|---|
| Year | 1963 | 1969 |
| Strategy count | 4 (basic) | 8–10 (refined) |
| Comfort model | Effective Temperature (ET) | PMV (Fanger) |
| Sub-zones | No | Yes (winter, summer, transition) |
| Quantitative? | Qualitative | Semi-quantitative |
| Indian suitability | High (concept level) | High (detail level) |

**(4) Indian Context (1.5 marks):**
- **Advantage:** Both charts well-suited to India's **composite climate** (hot-dry, hot-humid, cold regions).
- **Limitation 1:** Both assume **steady-state**; Indian daily T-fluctuations cause real-time deviation.
- **Limitation 2:** Original charts use **Western clothing (1.0 clo)**; Indian tropical attire is 0.4–0.6 clo, expanding comfort zone.
- **Conclusion:** Givoni's chart preferred for **modern, energy-conscious design**; Olgyay's chart is **pedagogically superior** for fundamentals.

### **Q. (b)** A site has dry-bulb temperature of **35 °C** and relative humidity of **30 %**. Plot this on the Bioclimatic Chart and identify the required passive design strategy with justification. **(7 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Module 3 | Part B Sub-Q (b) | 7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(1) Given Data (0.5 mark):**
T_db = 35 °C, RH = 30 %.

**(2) Computation of Saturation Vapour Pressure (2 marks):**

$$P_{vs}(35\,°C) = 0.6108 \times e^{\left(\dfrac{17.27 \times 35}{35 + 237.3}\right)} = 0.6108 \times e^{2.2186} = 0.6108 \times 9.196 = 5.617 \;\; \text{kPa}$$

$$P_v = 0.30 \times 5.617 = 1.685 \;\; \text{kPa}$$

$$w = 0.622 \times \dfrac{1.685}{101.325 - 1.685} = 0.01051 \;\; \text{kg/kg dry air}$$

**(3) Plottable Point (1 mark):**
Coordinates → (35 °C, 0.0105 kg/kg).

**(4) Location & Identification (1.5 marks):**
The point falls in the **lower-right quadrant of the Bioclimatic Chart** — characteristic of **Hot-Dry Desert Climate** (e.g., Jaisalmer, Rajasthan).

**(5) Recommended Strategy with Justification (2 marks):**
**Primary Strategy: Evaporative Cooling (E) + High Thermal Mass.**
- **Justification:** Low humidity (30 %) means water readily evaporates; latent heat of vaporization (**2454 kJ/kg at 20 °C**) absorbs enormous heat, dropping air temperature by 8–12 °C.
- **Design Response:** Compact building form, **stone/adobe walls** for high thermal mass (delays heat transfer by 6–8 hours), **courtyard with fountain**, **wind catcher (Badgir)**, **mashrabiya screens**, small recessed openings.
- **Material Suggestion:** 300 mm stone wall with U-value ≈ **2.0 W/m²K**, roof with terracotta tiles on hollow pots.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING — COMMON PITFALLS:**
> 1. **Confusing Olgyay's chart with Köppen Climate Classification** — Bioclimatic Chart is a *design tool*, NOT a climate classification. (Loss: 2–3 marks)
> 2. **Not computing humidity ratio (w) before plotting** — Without w, the Y-coordinate is undefined. (Loss: 1–2 marks)
> 3. **Forgetting the unit of clo** — Always specify **0.6 clo** for tropical context; using 1.0 clo (Western) shrinks comfort zone incorrectly. (Loss: 1 mark)
> 4. **Ignoring operative temperature distinction** — In naturally ventilated buildings, T_op ≠ T_air. Always account for T_mrt. (Loss: 2 marks)
> 5. **Generic answer without numerical support** — KTU examiners expect at least one **worked numerical** in 14-mark questions. (Loss: 3–4 marks)
> 6. **Mixing up "Evaporative Cooling" (Hot-Dry) with "Convective Cooling" (Hot-Humid)** — Kerala's hot-humid climate is the OPPOSITE of Rajasthan's hot-dry. (Loss: 2 marks)

---

## 5.4 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KEY POINTS TO REMEMBER (Module 3 — Bioclimatic Chart):**
>
> - **Originator:** Victor Olgyay (1963), book *"Design with Climate"*; Givoni's modification (1969).
> - **X-axis:** Dry-bulb temperature (°C) | **Y-axis:** Humidity (w or RH %).
> - **Comfort Zone:** Polygon 20–27 °C, 30–70 % RH (for 0.6 clo tropical subject).
> - **Reference subject:** 70 kg adult, 1.0 met, 0.6 clo, still air, no radiation.
> - **Eight strategies:** R, E, Cv, W, H, I, DC, N (Solar, Evaporative, Convective, Wind, Mass, Induced, Dehumidify, None).
> - **Hot-Humid (Kerala):** Cv + Shading + Lightweight Construction.
> - **Hot-Dry (Rajasthan):** E + High Mass + Compact Form.
> - **Cool-Dry (Ladakh):** R + Insulation + Compact Form.
> - **Cool-Humid (Coastal South India winter):** W + Compact Form.
> - **Key formulas:** Saturation vapour pressure (Magnus), humidity ratio (w = 0.622·Pv/(Pa−Pv)), operative temperature (T_op).
> - **Difference from Köppen:** Köppen classifies climate; Bioclimatic Chart prescribes design response.
> - **KTU high-yield keywords:** Olgyay, Givoni, comfort zone, PMV, clo, met, humidity ratio, effective temperature, passive strategy, bioclimatic envelope.
> - **Clo value:** 1 clo = 0.155 m²·K/W. Tropical clothing = 0.4–0.6 clo.
> - **Met value:** 1 met = 58.2 W/m².
> - **Validation tool:** Energy simulation (eQUEST, DesignBuilder, EnergyPlus) for confirming chart-based design.
> - **GRIHA/LEED link:** Bioclimatic chart analysis earns points under Energy & Atmosphere credits in green-building certification.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Psychrometry and Psychrometric Chart
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET834 - FUNDAMENTALS OF BUILDING PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Psychrometry and Psychrometric Chart

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Psychrometry

**Psychrometry** is the branch of engineering science that deals with the **thermodynamic properties of moist air** (a mixture of dry air and water vapour) and the physical changes occurring in it when it undergoes various processes such as heating, cooling, humidification, or dehumidification. The instrument used to measure these properties is called a **psychrometer** (consisting of dry-bulb and wet-bulb thermometers), and the graphical chart that represents all properties of moist air simultaneously is called the **Psychrometric Chart** (also called the **Mollier Diagram**, *ASHRAE Chart*, or *Carrier Chart*).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Building planning requires a deep understanding of indoor thermal comfort, ventilation loads, and HVAC (Heating, Ventilation, and Air Conditioning) sizing. Psychrometry is the foundation upon which cooling load calculations, duct design, and equipment selection (AHUs, chillers, cooling towers) are based.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy – "The Sponge and Water" Concept

Think of dry air as a clean, dry **sponge** and water vapour as the **water** that the sponge can soak up. The capacity of this "sponge" to hold water depends on its **temperature**:
- **Cold air** = a stiff, dry sponge → can hold only a small amount of water (low moisture capacity).
- **Hot air** = a soft, expanded sponge → can hold a lot of water (high moisture capacity).

> **Relative Humidity (RH)** is simply how "wet" the sponge currently is, expressed as a percentage of its maximum soaking capacity. When the sponge is fully soaked (RH = 100%), the air is **saturated**, and any further addition of water will result in **condensation** (just like water dripping from a fully soaked sponge).

This analogy is vital in building planning: when warm, humid outdoor air enters an air-conditioned hall, it is like a wet sponge entering a cold room — the sponge "squeezes out" water (condensation) onto the cooling coils, and the cooled air becomes drier, providing comfort.

## 1.3 Composition of Atmospheric (Moist) Air

Atmospheric air is a binary mixture of:
1. **Dry Air** (mostly nitrogen $\approx 78\%$, oxygen $\approx 21\%$, plus argon, CO₂, and trace gases) — treated as a single inert gas with molar mass $M_{da} = 28.97 \text{ kg/kmol}$.
2. **Water Vapour** — a condensable vapour with molar mass $M_v = 18.02 \text{ kg/kmol}$.

By **Gibbs' Phase Rule** for a binary mixture ($C=2$ components, $P=1$ phase):
$$F = C - P + 2 = 2 - 1 + 2 = 3$$
Hence, **three independent properties** are sufficient to fix the thermodynamic state of moist air (typically: *Dry Bulb Temperature*, *Pressure*, and *one humidity-related property*).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Atmospheric Pressure Assumed in KTU Problems:** $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa} = 1.01325 \text{ bar} = 760 \text{ mm Hg}$, unless otherwise specified in the question.

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants (Bolded for Recall)

| Constant | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Molecular mass of dry air, $M_{da}$ | **28.97** | kg/kmol |
| Molecular mass of water vapour, $M_v$ | **18.02** | kg/kmol |
| Universal gas constant, $\bar{R}$ | **8.314** | kJ/(kmol·K) |
| Specific gas constant of dry air, $R_{da}$ | **0.287** | kJ/(kg·K) |
| Specific gas constant of water vapour, $R_v$ | **0.4615** | kJ/(kg·K) |
| Specific heat of dry air, $c_{pa}$ | **1.005** | kJ/(kg·K) |
| Specific heat of water vapour, $c_{pv}$ | **1.88** | kJ/(kg·K) |
| Reference temperature for enthalpy, $T_{ref}$ | **0 °C = 273.15 K** | — |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Properties of Moist Air (Structured Logic Breakdown)

### 2.1.1 Dry Bulb Temperature (DBT) – $T_{db}$
- The temperature of moist air measured by a **standard mercury-in-glass thermometer** whose bulb is kept dry and shielded from radiation.
- Symbol: $T_{db}$ or $t$ (in °C) or $T$ (in K).
- **Why it matters:** It is the *primary independent variable* on the x-axis of the psychrometric chart.

### 2.1.2 Wet Bulb Temperature (WBT) – $T_{wb}$
- The temperature measured by a thermometer whose bulb is covered with a wet wick over which air is circulated at a velocity $\geq 4 \text{ m/s}$.
- The water on the wick evaporates by absorbing latent heat from the bulb, lowering the temperature until equilibrium is reached.
- **Key fact:** $T_{wb} \le T_{db}$, with equality only at saturation (RH = 100%).
- $T_{wb}$ represents **adiabatic saturation temperature** for air-water vapour systems (numerically very close, though conceptually different).

### 2.1.3 Dew Point Temperature (DPT) – $T_{dp}$
- The temperature at which moist air becomes **saturated** (RH = 100%) when cooled **at constant pressure and constant specific humidity** ($\omega = \text{constant}$).
- Below DPT, water vapour begins to **condense** as dew (fog formation on cold surfaces like window glasses in winter — a key building-planning consideration).
- **Order of temperatures:** $T_{dp} \le T_{wb} \le T_{db}$

### 2.1.4 Specific Humidity (Absolute Humidity / Humidity Ratio) – $\omega$
- Mass of water vapour per unit mass of **dry air** (NOT per unit mass of moist air).
$$W = \frac{\text{mass of water vapour (kg)}}{\text{mass of dry air (kg)}}$$
- A high $W$ means moist air can carry a lot of moisture; critical for cooling load calculations.

### 2.1.5 Relative Humidity (RH) – $\phi$
- Ratio of the **partial pressure of water vapour** ($P_v$) to the **saturation pressure of water vapour at the same DBT** ($P_{vs}$).
$$\phi = \frac{P_v}{P_{vs}(T_{db})}$$
- Expressed as a percentage ($\phi \times 100\%$).
- RH is **strongly temperature-dependent**: same air, hotter → lower RH; colder → higher RH.

### 2.1.6 Degree of Saturation – $\mu$
$$\mu = \frac{W}{W_s} = \frac{P_v (P_{atm} - P_{vs})}{P_{vs} (P_{atm} - P_v)}$$
where $W_s$ is the specific humidity at saturation for the given DBT.

### 2.1.7 Enthalpy of Moist Air – $h$
- Measured **per kg of dry air** for convenience. The reference is **dry air at 0 °C** and **liquid water at 0 °C**.
$$h = c_{pa} \cdot T_{db} + W \cdot (2500 + 1.88 \cdot T_{db}) \;\; \text{kJ/kg of dry air}$$
where $2500 \text{ kJ/kg}$ is the latent heat of vaporization at 0 °C and $1.88 \cdot T_{db}$ is the superheat of vapour above 0 °C.

### 2.1.8 Specific Volume of Moist Air – $v$
- Volume occupied per kg of dry air (always reported on a dry-air basis).
$$v = \frac{R_{da} \cdot T_{db}}{P_{atm} - P_v} \;\; \text{m}^3/\text{kg of dry air}$$

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Master Cheat-Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are high-yield and tested repeatedly in KTU Board Examinations.** Memorize the variables, units, and conditions.

| # | Property | Formula | Units | Condition / Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Specific Humidity | $W = 0.622 \cdot \dfrac{P_v}{P_{atm} - P_v}$ | kg vap/kg da | Dalton's Law of partial pressures |
| 2 | Saturation Specific Humidity | $W_s = 0.622 \cdot \dfrac{P_{vs}}{P_{atm} - P_{vs}}$ | kg vap/kg da | $P_{vs}$ at DBT |
| 3 | Relative Humidity | $\phi = \dfrac{P_v}{P_{vs}(T_{db})} \times 100\%$ | % | — |
| 4 | Degree of Saturation | $\mu = \dfrac{W}{W_s}$ | — | $\le 1$ |
| 5 | Enthalpy of Moist Air | $h = 1.005\,T_{db} + W(2500 + 1.88\,T_{db})$ | kJ/kg da | Reference: 0 °C |
| 6 | Specific Volume | $v = \dfrac{R_{da}\,T_{db}}{P_{atm} - P_v}$ | m³/kg da | T in K, P in kPa |
| 7 | Dew Point | $P_v = P_{vs}(T_{dp})$ | kPa | Saturation at TDP |
| 8 | DPT relation | $W = 0.622 \cdot \dfrac{P_{vs}(T_{dp})}{P_{atm} - P_{vs}(T_{dp})}$ | — | Connects DPT and W |
| 9 | Saturated air partial pressure | $P_{vs}(T)$ from steam tables or Antoine eqn. | kPa | T in °C |
| 10 | Moist air density | $\rho = \dfrac{1}{v}$ | kg/m³ | — |
| 11 | Partial pressure of vapour | $P_v = \dfrac{W \cdot P_{atm}}{0.622 + W}$ | kPa | Inverse of (1) |
| 12 | Universal gas constant ratio | $\dfrac{R_v}{R_{da}} = \dfrac{M_{da}}{M_v} = \dfrac{28.97}{18.02} \approx 1.608$ | — | Useful shortcut |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU EXAM TIP:** The factor **0.622** comes from $M_v / M_{da} = 18.02 / 28.97 \approx 0.622$. Always write this explicitly in derivations — partial credit is awarded for the correct constant.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility (for Building Planning)

- **HVAC Load Estimation:** Building cooling load = (sensible load due to DBT change) + (latent load due to moisture change). Both are obtained directly from the psychrometric chart.
- **Comfort Zone Identification:** ASHRAE comfort zone (e.g., DBT 22–26 °C, RH 40–60%) is plotted on the chart to verify indoor air quality.
- **Glazing & Condensation Analysis:** Cold outdoor air contacting warm, humid indoor air on window surfaces → condensation. DPT of indoor air determines when this occurs.
- **Ventilation Design:** Outdoor air introduction for fresh-air requirements is computed by mixing fresh outdoor air with return air — the mixing process is a straight line on the psychrometric chart.
- **Energy-Efficient Building Design:** Selection of evaporative cooling, night flushing, passive cooling all depends on understanding humidity ratios.

## 2.4 Psychrometric Chart — Structure & Reading

The **Psychrometric Chart** is a graphical plot with:
- **X-axis:** Dry Bulb Temperature ($T_{db}$), in °C.
- **Y-axis (vertical, right):** Specific Humidity ($W$), in kg water vapour / kg dry air.
- **Vertical lines (parallel to W-axis):** Constant DBT lines.
- **Nearly horizontal lines (slightly curving up):** Constant **Wet Bulb Temperature** ($T_{wb}$).
- **Curves dipping downward from upper-left to lower-right:** Constant **Relative Humidity** ($\phi$) lines, with $\phi = 100\%$ being the upper saturation curve.
- **Lines nearly parallel to DBT lines (steeper):** Constant **Enthalpy** ($h$), in kJ/kg dry air.
- **Diagonal lines (steeper, sloping down-right):** Constant **Specific Volume** ($v$), in m³/kg dry air.
- **Horizontal line at top ($\phi = 100\%$):** Saturation curve, also defines **DPT** when read horizontally to the left.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Shortcut:** To find **DPT** — from a given point, move **horizontally left** (constant W) until hitting the $\phi = 100\%$ saturation curve, then drop down to read DBT. This is the most frequently tested chart-reading skill.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Specific Humidity Formula ($W = 0.622 \cdot P_v / (P_{atm} - P_v)$)

### Given:
- Moist air behaves as an **ideal gas mixture**.
- **Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures** applies: $P_{atm} = P_a + P_v$ (where $P_a$ = partial pressure of dry air, $P_v$ = partial pressure of water vapour).
- $P_a \cdot V = m_a \cdot R_{da} \cdot T$
- $P_v \cdot V = m_v \cdot R_v \cdot T$

### Step 1 — Express the mass of each component using the ideal gas equation:
$$m_a = \frac{P_a \cdot V}{R_{da} \cdot T} = \frac{P_a \cdot V \cdot M_{da}}{\bar{R} \cdot T}$$

$$m_v = \frac{P_v \cdot V}{R_v \cdot T} = \frac{P_v \cdot V \cdot M_v}{\bar{R} \cdot T}$$

### Step 2 — Form the specific humidity (mass of vapour per unit mass of dry air):
$$W = \frac{m_v}{m_a} = \frac{P_v \cdot V \cdot M_v / (\bar{R} \cdot T)}{P_a \cdot V \cdot M_{da} / (\bar{R} \cdot T)}$$

### Step 3 — Cancel common terms ($V$, $\bar{R}$, $T$):
$$W = \frac{P_v \cdot M_v}{P_a \cdot M_{da}}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute $P_a = P_{atm} - P_v$ from Dalton's Law:
$$W = \frac{M_v}{M_{da}} \cdot \frac{P_v}{P_{atm} - P_v}$$

### Step 5 — Insert the molecular mass ratio:
$$W = \frac{18.02}{28.97} \cdot \frac{P_v}{P_{atm} - P_v}$$

### Step 6 — Evaluate the constant:
$$\frac{18.02}{28.97} = 0.6221 \approx 0.622$$

### Final Result:
$$\boxed{W = 0.622 \cdot \frac{P_v}{P_{atm} - P_v}} \quad \text{[kg water vapour per kg dry air]}$$

**Physical Interpretation:** The 0.622 factor is the ratio of molecular weights. Because water vapour is lighter than dry air (lower molecular weight), each kg of dry air needs only 0.622 of the *mass-equivalent* pressure of vapour to carry the same number of moles. This is **why humid air is lighter (less dense) than dry air** at the same temperature and pressure.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Enthalpy of Moist Air per kg of Dry Air

### Given:
- Reference state: dry air at 0 °C with enthalpy = 0, and liquid water at 0 °C with enthalpy = 0.
- Water vapour at 0 °C has the latent heat of vaporization $h_{fg,0} = 2500 \text{ kJ/kg}$.

### Step 1 — Total enthalpy of the system = (enthalpy of dry air portion) + (enthalpy of vapour portion):
$$H_{total} = m_a \cdot h_a + m_v \cdot h_v$$

### Step 2 — Specific enthalpy per kg of dry air (divide by $m_a$):
$$h = \frac{H_{total}}{m_a} = h_a + \frac{m_v}{m_a} \cdot h_v = h_a + W \cdot h_v$$

### Step 3 — Enthalpy of dry air at temperature $T_{db}$ (taking 0 °C as reference):
$$h_a = c_{pa} \cdot (T_{db} - 0) = 1.005 \cdot T_{db} \;\; \text{kJ/kg da}$$

### Step 4 — Enthalpy of water vapour at $T_{db}$ (includes latent heat at 0 °C + sensible heating of vapour from 0 °C to $T_{db}$):
$$h_v = h_{fg,0} + c_{pv} \cdot T_{db} = 2500 + 1.88 \cdot T_{db} \;\; \text{kJ/kg vapour}$$

### Step 5 — Combine:
$$h = 1.005 \cdot T_{db} + W \cdot (2500 + 1.88 \cdot T_{db}) \;\; \text{kJ/kg da}$$

### Final Result:
$$\boxed{h = 1.005\,T_{db} + W(2500 + 1.88\,T_{db})}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the reference 0 °C and not, say, 25 °C?** Historically, refrigeration engineers chose 0 °C because at this temperature water freezes, making it a natural reference for both liquid and vapour phases. The *choice of reference is arbitrary*; only *differences* in enthalpy matter for energy balance.

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example (KTU Board Pattern)

### Problem:
Atmospheric air is at a dry-bulb temperature of $35\text{ °C}$ and a wet-bulb temperature of $25\text{ °C}$. The atmospheric pressure is $101.325 \text{ kPa}$. Determine: (a) Specific humidity $W$, (b) Relative humidity $\phi$, (c) Dew point temperature $T_{dp}$, (d) Enthalpy $h$, (e) Specific volume $v$, (f) Degree of saturation $\mu$.

### Step 0 — Read saturation pressures from **standard steam tables** (interpolated where needed):

| $T$ (°C) | $P_s$ (kPa) |
|---|---|
| 20 | 2.339 |
| 25 | 3.169 |
| 30 | 4.246 |
| 35 | 5.628 |

### Part (a) — Specific Humidity $W$

The wet-bulb temperature equals the adiabatic saturation temperature for air-water systems. The WBT is given as $25\text{ °C}$. At WBT, the partial pressure of vapour equals the saturation pressure at WBT (since the wick is saturated with water):
$$P_v = P_{vs}(T_{wb}) = P_{vs}(25\text{ °C}) = 3.169 \text{ kPa}$$

Therefore:
$$W = 0.622 \cdot \frac{P_v}{P_{atm} - P_v} = 0.622 \cdot \frac{3.169}{101.325 - 3.169}$$

$$W = 0.622 \cdot \frac{3.169}{98.156} = 0.622 \cdot 0.03229$$

$$\boxed{W = 0.02008 \text{ kg water vapour / kg dry air}}$$

### Part (b) — Relative Humidity $\phi$

The saturation pressure at DBT (35 °C) is $P_{vs}(35\text{ °C}) = 5.628 \text{ kPa}$:
$$\phi = \frac{P_v}{P_{vs}(T_{db})} = \frac{3.169}{5.628}$$

$$\boxed{\phi = 0.5632 = 56.32\%}$$

### Part (c) — Dew Point Temperature $T_{dp}$

Dew point is the temperature at which $P_v$ becomes the saturation pressure. We need to find $T$ such that $P_{vs}(T) = 3.169 \text{ kPa}$. From the table, this corresponds to:
$$\boxed{T_{dp} \approx 25\text{ °C}}$$

*(Note: $T_{dp}$ is **not** equal to $T_{wb}$ in general, but they are numerically very close for the air-water system at low-to-moderate humidities. In this example, the WBT of 25 °C happens to be the DPT as well — coincidence due to the chosen numbers.)*

### Part (d) — Enthalpy $h$

$$h = 1.005 \cdot T_{db} + W \cdot (2500 + 1.88 \cdot T_{db})$$
$$h = 1.005 \cdot 35 + 0.02008 \cdot (2500 + 1.88 \cdot 35)$$
$$h = 35.175 + 0.02008 \cdot (2500 + 65.8)$$
$$h = 35.175 + 0.02008 \cdot 2565.8$$
$$h = 35.175 + 51.521$$

$$\boxed{h = 86.70 \text{ kJ/kg of dry air}}$$

### Part (e) — Specific Volume $v$

Convert DBT to Kelvin: $T_{db} = 35 + 273.15 = 308.15 \text{ K}$:
$$v = \frac{R_{da} \cdot T_{db}}{P_{atm} - P_v} = \frac{0.287 \cdot 308.15}{101.325 - 3.169}$$
$$v = \frac{88.439}{98.156}$$

$$\boxed{v = 0.9011 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg of dry air}}$$

### Part (f) — Degree of Saturation $\mu$

First, find $W_s$ at DBT = 35 °C (saturated):
$$W_s = 0.622 \cdot \frac{P_{vs}(35)}{P_{atm} - P_{vs}(35)} = 0.622 \cdot \frac{5.628}{101.325 - 5.628} = 0.622 \cdot \frac{5.628}{95.697}$$
$$W_s = 0.622 \cdot 0.05882 = 0.03659 \text{ kg/kg da}$$

Then:
$$\mu = \frac{W}{W_s} = \frac{0.02008}{0.03659}$$

$$\boxed{\mu = 0.5488 = 54.88\%}$$

### Summary Table (Examiner's Marking Grid):

| Quantity | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Specific Humidity | $W$ | 0.02008 | kg vap/kg da |
| Relative Humidity | $\phi$ | 56.32 | % |
| Dew Point Temp | $T_{dp}$ | 25 | °C |
| Enthalpy | $h$ | 86.70 | kJ/kg da |
| Specific Volume | $v$ | 0.9011 | m³/kg da |
| Degree of Saturation | $\mu$ | 54.88 | % |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Composition and Properties of Moist Air

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[ATMOSPHERIC MOIST AIR] --> B[Component 1: DRY AIR]
    A --> C[Component 2: WATER VAPOUR]
    B --> B1["N2 ~ 78 percent"]
    B --> B2["O2 ~ 21 percent"]
    B --> B3["Ar, CO2, trace ~ 1 percent"]
    C --> C1[Phase: Vapour above 0 C]
    C --> C2[Condensable component]

    A --> D[MEASURABLE PROPERTIES]
    D --> D1["DBT: T_db in C"]
    D --> D2["WBT: T_wb in C"]
    D --> D3["DPT: T_dp in C"]

    A --> E[DERIVED PROPERTIES]
    E --> E1["Specific Humidity W kg per kg da"]
    E --> E2["Relative Humidity phi percent"]
    E --> E3["Enthalpy h kJ per kg da"]
    E --> E4["Specific Volume v m3 per kg da"]
    E --> E5["Degree of Saturation mu"]

    A --> F[TOOLS OF ANALYSIS]
    F --> F1[Psychrometer: Sling or Aspirated]
    F --> F2[Psychrometric Chart: Mollier or ASHRAE]
    F --> F3[Steam Tables for P_vs at T]
```

## 4.2 Process Flow — Major Psychrometric Processes on the Chart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Process 1: SENSIBLE HEATING] -->|constant W, T rises| P1out[Air becomes hotter, drier-feeling]
    P2[Process 2: SENSIBLE COOLING] -->|constant W, T falls| P2out[Air becomes cooler, RH rises]
    P3[Process 3: COOLING and DEHUMIDIFICATION] -->|T falls, W falls| P3out[Cold saturated air from coil]
    P4[Process 4: HEATING and HUMIDIFICATION] -->|T rises, W rises| P4out[Air warmer and more moist]
    P5[Process 5: EVAPORATIVE COOLING] -->|constant T_wb, T falls, W rises| P5out[Air cooler, more humid]
    P6[Process 6: CHEMICAL DEHUMIDIFICATION] -->|T rises slightly, W falls| P6out[Liquid desiccant absorbs moisture]
    P7[Process 7: AIR MIXING] -->|linear interpolation on chart| P7out[Mixture on straight line between streams]
    P8[Process 8: BYPASS FACTOR] -->|mixing of bypassed and cooled air| P8out[Effective coil leaving condition]

    P1 --> CHART[(PSYCHROMETRIC CHART)]
    P2 --> CHART
    P3 --> CHART
    P4 --> CHART
    P5 --> CHART
    P6 --> CHART
    P7 --> CHART
    P8 --> CHART
    CHART --> HVAC[HVAC SYSTEM DESIGN]
```

## 4.3 Block Architecture — Functional Flow of an Air-Conditioning System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    RA[RETURN AIR from Room] -->|warm, humid| MIX[Mixing Chamber]
    OA[OUTSIDE AIR Fresh] --> MIX
    MIX -->|mixed air| F[Filter Section]
    F --> CL[Cooling Coil]
    CL -->|cold saturated air| BH[Bypass Factor Interaction]
    BH --> H[Heating Coil if needed]
    H --> HU[Humidifier or Spray]
    HU -->|conditioned supply air| FAN[Supply Fan and Duct]
    FAN --> ROOM[Conditioned Space]
    ROOM -->|exhaust, leak| RA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Insight:** The **Bypass Factor (BPF)** is the fraction of air that *bypasses* the cooling coil without being cooled. BPF = 0 means perfect coil (all air cooled to ADP); BPF = 1 means coil is ineffective. Typical BPF for a properly designed coil is **0.05 to 0.30**. The **Apparatus Dew Point (ADP)** is the temperature to which air would be cooled if the coil were 100% effective.

## 4.4 Conceptual Map — Order of Key Temperatures

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[T_dp: Dew Point, lowest T at which saturation occurs] -->|constant W process| B[T_wb: Wet Bulb, adiabatic saturation T]
    B -->|constant W process plus evaporative cooling| C[T_db: Dry Bulb, actual air T]
    style A fill:#cce5ff
    style B fill:#b3d9ff
    style C fill:#80bfff
```

**Mnemonic:** **"D – W – D"** = "Dew → Wet → Dry" — increasing order of temperature.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**Define the following terms with suitable units:**
(a) Specific humidity
(b) Relative humidity
(c) Dew point temperature

**Model Answer:**

(a) **Specific humidity ($W$):** It is the mass of water vapour (in kg) present per unit mass of **dry air** in a moist air sample.
$$W = \frac{m_v}{m_a} = 0.622 \cdot \frac{P_v}{P_{atm} - P_v} \;\; \text{[kg water vapour / kg dry air]}$$

(b) **Relative humidity ($\phi$):** It is the ratio of the actual partial pressure of water vapour in the air to the saturation pressure of water vapour at the same dry-bulb temperature.
$$\phi = \frac{P_v}{P_{vs}(T_{db})} \times 100\% \;\; \text{[dimensionless, expressed as \%]}$$

(c) **Dew point temperature ($T_{dp}$):** It is the temperature at which moist air becomes saturated (RH = 100%) when cooled at **constant pressure and constant specific humidity**. Below this temperature, condensation begins.
$$T_{dp} \;\; \text{[°C]}$$

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**What is a psychrometric chart? Mention any four properties represented on it.**

**Model Answer:**
A **psychrometric chart** (also called a *Mollier diagram* or *ASHRAE chart*) is a graphical plot that simultaneously represents all the thermodynamic properties of moist air. It is the most fundamental tool used in **HVAC design, cooling load estimation, and air-conditioning equipment selection**.

**Four key properties represented on the chart are:**
1. **Dry Bulb Temperature (DBT)** — along the horizontal x-axis, in °C.
2. **Specific Humidity (W)** — along the vertical y-axis, in kg/kg dry air.
3. **Relative Humidity (RH, $\phi$)** — as a family of curves from 0% (lower) to 100% (upper saturation curve), in %.
4. **Wet Bulb Temperature (WBT)** — as a family of lines sloping gently upward from left to right, in °C.
5. *(Bonus)* Enthalpy ($h$) and specific volume ($v$) are also represented as constant lines.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]

(a) **Derive the expression for specific humidity of moist air in terms of partial pressures.** (7 Marks)

(b) **Moist air at $40\text{ °C}$ DBT and $25\text{ °C}$ WBT is supplied to an air-conditioned hall at standard atmospheric pressure. Determine:**
- (i) Specific humidity
- (ii) Relative humidity
- (iii) Dew point temperature
- (iv) Enthalpy of moist air per kg of dry air
- (v) Specific volume

(7 Marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

The derivation is the same as presented in **Section 3.1** of these notes. Key steps the examiner expects:
1. State Dalton's Law: $P_{atm} = P_a + P_v$.
2. Apply ideal gas law to dry air and water vapour separately.
3. Form the ratio $m_v / m_a$.
4. Substitute $P_a = P_{atm} - P_v$ and the molecular mass ratio $M_v / M_{da} = 0.622$.
5. Arrive at $\boxed{W = 0.622 \cdot \dfrac{P_v}{P_{atm} - P_v}}$.

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

**Given:** $T_{db} = 40\text{ °C}$, $T_{wb} = 25\text{ °C}$, $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$.

**From steam tables (interpolated values):**
- $P_{vs}(25\text{ °C}) = 3.169 \text{ kPa}$
- $P_{vs}(40\text{ °C}) = 7.384 \text{ kPa}$

**Step 1 — Partial pressure of vapour (equal to saturation pressure at WBT):**
$$P_v = P_{vs}(25\text{ °C}) = 3.169 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 2 — (i) Specific humidity:**
$$W = 0.622 \cdot \frac{3.169}{101.325 - 3.169} = 0.622 \cdot \frac{3.169}{98.156} = 0.02008 \text{ kg/kg da}$$

**Step 3 — (ii) Relative humidity:**
$$\phi = \frac{3.169}{7.384} = 0.4292 = 42.92\%$$

**Step 4 — (iii) Dew point temperature:**
Find $T$ such that $P_{vs}(T) = 3.169 \text{ kPa}$. From steam tables, $P_{vs}(25\text{ °C}) = 3.169 \text{ kPa}$.
$$T_{dp} = 25\text{ °C}$$

**Step 5 — (iv) Enthalpy per kg dry air:**
$$h = 1.005 \cdot 40 + 0.02008 \cdot (2500 + 1.88 \cdot 40)$$
$$h = 40.20 + 0.02008 \cdot (2500 + 75.2)$$
$$h = 40.20 + 0.02008 \cdot 2575.2$$
$$h = 40.20 + 51.71 = 91.91 \text{ kJ/kg da}$$

**Step 6 — (v) Specific volume:**
Convert DBT to K: $T_{db} = 40 + 273.15 = 313.15 \text{ K}$.
$$v = \frac{0.287 \cdot 313.15}{101.325 - 3.169} = \frac{89.864}{98.156} = 0.9156 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg da}$$

| Quantity | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $W$ | 0.02008 | kg/kg da |
| $\phi$ | 42.92 | % |
| $T_{dp}$ | 25 | °C |
| $h$ | 91.91 | kJ/kg da |
| $v$ | 0.9156 | m³/kg da |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model] — Alternative Choice

(a) **Explain the construction and use of a psychrometric chart. Sketch the lines for constant DBT, WBT, RH, enthalpy, and specific volume.** (7 Marks)

(b) **Two air streams are mixed adiabatically in an air-handling unit. Stream 1: $m_{a1} = 10 \text{ kg/s}$ of dry air at $30\text{ °C}$ DBT, $60\%$ RH. Stream 2: $m_{a2} = 6 \text{ kg/s}$ of dry air at $15\text{ °C}$ DBT, $80\%$ RH. Find the specific humidity, temperature, and enthalpy of the mixed air.** (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

**Construction:**
1. **X-axis (horizontal):** DBT in °C, ranging from -10 °C to 50 °C.
2. **Y-axis (vertical, on the right):** Specific humidity $W$ in kg/kg dry air.
3. **Saturation curve:** $\phi = 100\%$ as the upper boundary; defined by $P_v = P_{vs}(T_{db})$.
4. **Constant RH lines:** Families of curves below the saturation curve, with $\phi = 10\%, 20\%, 30\%, \ldots, 90\%$.
5. **Constant WBT lines:** Slope slightly upward from left to right, almost parallel to enthalpy lines.
6. **Constant DBT lines:** Vertical lines, parallel to the W axis.
7. **Constant enthalpy lines:** Slope steeply upward, drawn in increments of 5 or 10 kJ/kg da.
8. **Constant specific volume lines:** Steeper than enthalpy lines, sloping down to the right.

**Use:**
- Find the state point of moist air given any two independent properties.
- Plot and analyze various psychrometric processes (heating, cooling, humidification, mixing).
- Determine coil loads, bypass factors, ADP, and SHF.
- Identify comfort zones for occupants.

*(The student is expected to draw a labelled schematic — full marks are awarded for a clearly labelled sketch with all five line families indicated.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

**Step 1 — Read properties of Stream 1 from the chart / calculate:**
- $T_{db1} = 30\text{ °C}$, $\phi_1 = 60\%$.
- $P_{vs}(30\text{ °C}) = 4.246 \text{ kPa}$.
- $P_{v1} = 0.60 \cdot 4.246 = 2.5476 \text{ kPa}$.
- $W_1 = 0.622 \cdot \dfrac{2.5476}{101.325 - 2.5476} = 0.622 \cdot \dfrac{2.5476}{98.7774} = 0.01604 \text{ kg/kg da}$.
- $h_1 = 1.005 \cdot 30 + 0.01604 \cdot (2500 + 1.88 \cdot 30) = 30.15 + 0.01604 \cdot 2556.4 = 30.15 + 41.00 = 71.15 \text{ kJ/kg da}$.

**Step 2 — Read properties of Stream 2:**
- $T_{db2} = 15\text{ °C}$, $\phi_2 = 80\%$.
- $P_{vs}(15\text{ °C}) = 1.7057 \text{ kPa}$.
- $P_{v2} = 0.80 \cdot 1.7057 = 1.3646 \text{ kPa}$.
- $W_2 = 0.622 \cdot \dfrac{1.3646}{101.325 - 1.3646} = 0.622 \cdot \dfrac{1.3646}{99.9604} = 0.00849 \text{ kg/kg da}$.
- $h_2 = 1.005 \cdot 15 + 0.00849 \cdot (2500 + 1.88 \cdot 15) = 15.075 + 0.00849 \cdot 2528.2 = 15.075 + 21.47 = 36.54 \text{ kJ/kg da}$.

**Step 3 — Mass-weighted average of specific humidity (conservation of mass of vapour):**
$$W_{mix} = \frac{m_{a1} W_1 + m_{a2} W_2}{m_{a1} + m_{a2}} = \frac{10 \cdot 0.01604 + 6 \cdot 0.00849}{10 + 6} = \frac{0.1604 + 0.05094}{16} = \frac{0.21134}{16}$$
$$W_{mix} = 0.01321 \text{ kg/kg da}$$

**Step 4 — Mass-weighted average of enthalpy (conservation of energy):**
$$h_{mix} = \frac{m_{a1} h_1 + m_{a2} h_2}{m_{a1} + m_{a2}} = \frac{10 \cdot 71.15 + 6 \cdot 36.54}{16} = \frac{711.5 + 219.24}{16} = \frac{930.74}{16}$$
$$h_{mix} = 58.17 \text{ kJ/kg da}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the mixed DBT from the enthalpy equation:**
$$58.17 = 1.005 \cdot T_{db,mix} + 0.01321 \cdot (2500 + 1.88 \cdot T_{db,mix})$$
$$58.17 = 1.005 \cdot T_{db,mix} + 33.025 + 0.02483 \cdot T_{db,mix}$$
$$58.17 - 33.025 = (1.005 + 0.02483) \cdot T_{db,mix}$$
$$25.145 = 1.02983 \cdot T_{db,mix}$$
$$T_{db,mix} = 24.42\text{ °C}$$

| Quantity | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $T_{db,mix}$ | 24.42 | °C |
| $W_{mix}$ | 0.01321 | kg/kg da |
| $h_{mix}$ | 58.17 | kJ/kg da |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Mixing is NOT a simple average of temperatures** — it is an average of *enthalpies* (or energies) per kg of dry air, and of *specific humidities*. A common student error is to take $(T_1 + T_2)/2$ as the mixed temperature — this is **wrong** because the mass flow rates of dry air differ.
> 2. The mixed state point on the psychrometric chart lies on a **straight line** joining the two state points, and the position is determined by the inverse lever rule of mass ratios — *not* by simple averaging.
> 3. **Always include the units** in the final answer; KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Notes — Read this the night before the exam.**

- **Specific Humidity:** $W = 0.622 \cdot P_v / (P_{atm} - P_v)$. Always quoted per **kg of dry air** (not per kg of moist air).
- **Relative Humidity:** $\phi = P_v / P_{vs}(T_{db})$ — strongly temperature-dependent.
- **DPT is read horizontally** on the chart (constant $W$ to $\phi = 100\%$).
- **WBT is read along a WBT line** (slight upward slope) on the chart.
- **Enthalpy** $h = 1.005 T_{db} + W(2500 + 1.88 T_{db})$ — reference is 0 °C.
- **Specific volume** $v = R_{da} T_{db} / (P_{atm} - P_v)$ — temperature in **Kelvin**.
- **Order of temperatures:** $T_{dp} \le T_{wb} \le T_{db}$ (equality only at saturation).
- **The factor 0.622** comes from $M_v / M_{da} = 18.02 / 28.97$. **Write this clearly in derivations.**
- **Heating:** horizontal line on the chart (constant $W$, $T$ rises, $h$ rises, $\phi$ falls).
- **Cooling:** horizontal line on the chart (constant $W$, $T$ falls, $h$ falls, $\phi$ rises).
- **Cooling + Dehumidification:** straight slanting line from upper-right to lower-left (cooling coil process).
- **Evaporative Cooling:** line of constant WBT — T falls, W rises, h remains constant (idealized).
- **Adiabatic Mixing:** mixed point lies on a straight line between the two streams, located by inverse lever rule based on **mass of dry air**, not total mass.
- **Bypass Factor (BPF):** $BPF = (T_2 - T_1)/(T_3 - T_1)$ where $T_1$ = entering air, $T_2$ = leaving air, $T_3$ = ADP.
- **Apparatus Dew Point (ADP):** The temperature air would reach if the coil were ideal (BPF = 0).
- **Sensible Heat Factor (SHF):** $SHF = SH / (SH + LH)$; the ratio of sensible to total heat load. Used to draw the SHF line on the psychrometric chart to determine coil leaving conditions.
- **Comfort zone (ASHRAE):** DBT 22–26 °C, RH 40–60%, with mean radiant temperature close to DBT.
- **Always state the atmospheric pressure** assumed (typically 101.325 kPa unless specified).
- **For building planning applications:** The psychrometric chart helps size cooling coils, estimate latent vs. sensible loads, identify condensation risks on glazing, and select passive cooling strategies.
- **Energy conservation equation for mixing:** $\sum m_a h = \sum m_a h_{out}$.
- **Mass conservation for mixing:** $\sum m_a W = \sum m_a W_{out}$.
- **Saturated air** lies on the **upper boundary** (the saturation curve) of the chart; it is invisible as a "line" because the chart is bounded by it.
- **Use the chart for quick estimation** in exam time-constrained problems; use formulas for precise marks.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Psychrometry and Psychrometric Chart**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Earth-Sun Relationship: Sun's Apparent Movement with Respect to the Earth, Solar Angles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET834 - FUNDAMENTALS OF BUILDING PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Earth-Sun Relationship: Sun's Apparent Movement with Respect to the Earth, Solar Angles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 The Earth–Sun Geometric Relationship

The **Earth–Sun Relationship** is the fundamental astronomical geometry that defines the position of the Sun relative to any point on the Earth's surface at any given time of day and any day of the year. It governs the intensity, direction, and duration of incident solar radiation on a building, and is therefore the cornerstone of passive solar building design, shading device design, and orientation planning.

The Earth executes two simultaneous motions:
1. **Rotation** about its own polar axis once every 24 hours (causing the **diurnal** cycle – day and night).
2. **Revolution** around the Sun in a slightly elliptical orbit once every 365.25 days (causing the **seasonal** cycle).

The Earth's rotational axis is **tilted at an angle of 23.45°** with respect to the normal (perpendicular) of the plane of the ecliptic (the plane of Earth's orbit around the Sun). This tilt, combined with revolution, is the root cause of all solar geometry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The sun's *apparent* movement is studied (not its true motion), because the Earth-bound observer perceives the Sun as moving while the Earth is actually moving. This apparent path is what governs solar angles incident on buildings.

### 1.2 Key Coordinate Systems in Solar Geometry

| Reference Plane | Normal Vector | Angle Measured From |
|---|---|---|
| **Horizontal Plane** | Zenith (vertical upward) | Zenith Angle (θz) |
| **Equatorial Plane** | Earth's rotational axis | Declination (δ) |
| **Local Meridian (N–S)** | True North | Hour Angle (ω) |
| **Horizontal Plane (projected)** | True South (N. Hemisphere) | Azimuth (γ) |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy – "The Tilted Lamp and the Rotating Apple"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):** Imagine a fixed table lamp (the Sun) and a tilted apple (the Earth) held on a stick. The apple is tilted 23.45° and slowly rotated on the stick once a day. As it rotates, the bright spot (subsolar point) travels across the apple's surface, just as the Sun's rays strike different latitudes at different times. If you now walk the apple *around* the lamp once a year, the bright spot drifts north and south between the Tropic of Cancer and the Tropic of Capricorn. This is precisely why the Sun rises in different positions and reaches different heights in the sky at different times of the year — the reason your north-facing room is dark in winter but brilliantly lit in summer.

### 1.4 Solar Angles – Formal Definitions

| Term | Symbol | Formal Definition | Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Solar Declination Angle** | δ | Angle between the line joining the centres of the Earth and Sun, and the Earth's equatorial plane. | −23.45° to +23.45° |
| **Hour Angle** | ω | Angular displacement of the Sun east or west of the local meridian, due to Earth's rotation (15° per hour). | −180° to +180° |
| **Solar Altitude Angle** | α (or h) | Angle between the Sun's rays and the horizontal plane at the observer's location. | 0° to 90° |
| **Solar Zenith Angle** | θz | Angle between the Sun's rays and the vertical (zenith direction). | 0° to 90° |
| **Solar Azimuth Angle** | γ (or A) | Angle measured in the horizontal plane, from True South (in the Northern Hemisphere) to the horizontal projection of the Sun's rays. | 0° to 180° |
| **Angle of Incidence** | θi | Angle between the Sun's rays and the normal (perpendicular) to a given surface. | 0° to 90° |
| **Latitude** | φ | Angular distance of the observer north (positive) or south (negative) of the Equator. | 0° to 90° |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The fundamental governing metric is the **Solar Constant** = **1361 W/m²** (the solar flux at the outer edge of the atmosphere on a surface perpendicular to the Sun's rays, averaged over Earth's orbital eccentricity).
