This is the baseline against which all incident radiation calculations are referenced.

### 1.5 Apparent Movement of the Sun – Summary

The Sun's *apparent* path across the celestial sphere is governed by two superimposed motions:

1. **Daily (Diurnal) Motion** – East to West arc across the sky, caused by Earth's west-to-east rotation. The Sun rises in the East, peaks on the local meridian at **solar noon**, and sets in the West. The arc's height and length depend on latitude and declination.
2. **Annual Motion** – North-South drift of the Sun's rising/setting positions and the noon altitude, caused by Earth's revolution combined with axial tilt. This produces the four cardinal solar positions: **Summer Solstice (June 21), Winter Solstice (Dec 21), Equinoxes (Mar 21 & Sep 23).**

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU (Kerala, latitude ≈ 8°–12° N), the Sun is north of the observer during part of the year and south during the rest. This has critical implications for **building orientation, overhang design, and window placement**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The Four Cardinal Solar Positions (Annual Cycle)

The Earth-Sun geometry produces four defining instants each year, dictated by the **declination angle δ**:

| Solar Event | Date (N. Hemisphere) | Declination (δ) | Subsolar Latitude |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Vernal Equinox** | ~21 March | **0°** | Equator |
| **Summer Solstice** | ~21 June | **+23.45°** | Tropic of Cancer (23.45° N) |
| **Autumnal Equinox** | ~23 September | **0°** | Equator |
| **Winter Solstice** | ~22 December | **−23.45°** | Tropic of Capricorn (23.45° S) |

On the **equinoxes**, the Sun is directly overhead at the Equator, and day and night are of equal length over the entire Earth. On the **solstices**, the Sun is at its maximum northerly or southerly declination, producing the longest/shortest days of the year.

### 2.2 Detailed Theory of Each Solar Angle

#### 2.2.1 Solar Declination Angle (δ)
- **Why it exists:** The tilt of the Earth's axis means that as the Earth orbits the Sun, the Northern or Southern hemisphere is inclined *towards* or *away from* the Sun.
- **How it changes:** Varies sinusoidally between −23.45° and +23.45° over the year.
- **Approximate formula (Cooper, 1969):**

$$\delta = 23.45^\circ \cdot \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(284 + n)\right]$$

where **n** = day number of the year (1 Jan = 1, 31 Dec = 365).

#### 2.2.2 Hour Angle (ω)
- **Why it exists:** Earth rotates 360° in 24 hours → 15° per hour.
- **Sign convention:** **Negative** in the morning (Sun east of meridian), **Zero** at solar noon, **Positive** in the afternoon.
- **Formula:**

$$\omega = 15^\circ \times (t_{solar} - 12)$$

where $t_{solar}$ = local solar time in hours.

#### 2.2.3 Solar Altitude Angle (α)
- **Why it matters:** Determines how steeply sunlight strikes the ground (and hence the roof and walls). High altitude → shallow roof exposure; low altitude → grazing rays entering deep into rooms through north or south windows.
- **Geometric meaning:** The Sun's elevation above the horizon.
- **Governing equation:**

$$\sin\alpha = \sin\phi \cdot \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cdot \cos\delta \cdot \cos\omega$$

#### 2.2.4 Solar Zenith Angle (θz)
- **Why it matters:** Always complementary to altitude: a surface perpendicular to the Sun's rays (i.e., facing the Sun) receives maximum radiation. The zenith angle tells us how far the Sun is from that ideal orientation.
- **Governing equation:**

$$\theta_z = 90^\circ - \alpha$$

#### 2.2.5 Solar Azimuth Angle (γ)
- **Why it matters:** Determines the *direction* in which shadows fall, and which wall (N, S, E, W) receives direct sunlight at any moment.
- **Sign convention (Northern Hemisphere):** Measured from True South. **West of South → Positive; East of South → Negative** (or vice versa, depending on convention). Many texts use 0° = South, +ve = West.
- **Governing equation:**

$$\cos\gamma = \frac{\sin\alpha \cdot \sin\phi - \sin\delta}{\cos\alpha \cdot \cos\phi}$$

#### 2.2.6 Angle of Incidence on a Tilted Surface (θi)
- **Why it matters:** Determines the actual solar energy absorbed by a tilted roof, a PV panel, or a wall. It is the angle between the incoming ray and the **normal** to the surface.
- **Governing equation (general, for any surface orientation with tilt β and azimuth γs):**

$$\cos\theta_i = \sin\alpha \cdot \cos\beta + \cos\alpha \cdot \sin\beta \cdot \cos(\gamma_s - \gamma)$$

> For a **horizontal surface** (β = 0°), this reduces to $\cos\theta_i = \sin\alpha$, and θi = 90° − α = θz. ✓
> For a **vertical surface** (β = 90°), it reduces to $\cos\theta_i = \cos\alpha \cdot \cos(\gamma_s - \gamma)$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Examination-Ready)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Conditions / Units | Valid Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Declination (δ) | $\delta = 23.45^\circ \cdot \sin\!\left[\frac{360}{365}(284+n)\right]$ | n = day of year (1–365) | −23.45° to +23.45° |
| 2 | Hour Angle (ω) | $\omega = 15^\circ(t_{solar}-12)$ | $t_{solar}$ in hours | −180° to +180° |
| 3 | Altitude (α) | $\sin\alpha = \sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega$ | All angles in degrees | 0° to 90° |
| 4 | Zenith (θz) | $\theta_z = 90^\circ - \alpha$ | Complement of α | 0° to 90° |
| 5 | Azimuth (γ) | $\cos\gamma = \frac{\sin\alpha\sin\phi - \sin\delta}{\cos\alpha\cos\phi}$ | N. Hem. measured from South | 0° to 180° |
| 6 | Incidence (θi) | $\cos\theta_i = \sin\alpha\cos\beta + \cos\alpha\sin\beta\cos(\gamma_s - \gamma)$ | β = surface tilt, γs = surface azimuth | 0° to 90° |
| 7 | Equation of Time (EoT) correction | $EoT = 9.87\sin(2B) - 7.53\cos(B) - 1.5\sin(B)$ minutes, $B = \frac{360}{364}(n-81)$ | For solar-clock conversion | ±16 min |
| 8 | Local Solar Time | $LST = Standard Time + EoT \pm 4(L_{st}-L_{loc})$ minutes | L in degrees longitude | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Tip:** For Kerala (Kasaragod ≈ 12.5° N; Thiruvananthapuram ≈ 8.5° N), φ is small and positive. Many KTU problems assume φ = 10° N to 12° N as representative. Always keep degrees consistent — do not mix radians and degrees.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Building Orientation:** In the tropics, optimal orientation minimises east-west wall exposure to avoid harsh low-angle morning/afternoon sun.
- **Shading Devices (Chajjas, Louvers):** The noon altitude on the summer solstice determines the optimal overhang projection (typically: overhang = window height / tan α_noon).
- **Solar Panel (PV) Tilt:** Annual energy yield is maximised when the panel tilt β ≈ φ (latitude) for grid-tied systems.
- **Daylighting Design:** Window-to-wall ratio and glazing orientation are dictated by altitude and azimuth at design hour (typically 9 AM and 3 PM on equinoxes).
- **Thermal Comfort:** Direct solar gain through west-facing glass in tropical Kerala is the single largest cooling load contributor — solar geometry makes this preventable by design.

## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Geometric Setup – The Spherical Triangle

Consider the **celestial sphere** with three points: **Zenith (Z)** of the observer, **Pole Star (P)** (extension of Earth's axis), and **Sun (S)**. The connecting arcs form a **navigational spherical triangle (P–Z–S)** with the following sides and angles:

- **Arc PZ** = co-latitude = 90° − φ
- **Arc PS** = co-declination = 90° − δ
- **Arc ZS** = zenith angle = θz = 90° − α
- **Angle at P** = hour angle = ω
- **Angle at Z** = azimuth angle = γ

Applying the **spherical law of cosines for sides** to triangle PZS:

$$\cos(90^\circ - \alpha) = \cos(90^\circ - \phi)\cos(90^\circ - \delta) + \sin(90^\circ - \phi)\sin(90^\circ - \delta)\cos(\omega)$$

Simplifying each term using $\cos(90^\circ - x) = \sin x$ and $\sin(90^\circ - x) = \cos x$:

$$\sin\alpha = \sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega$$

> This is the **fundamental solar altitude equation**, derived purely from spherical trigonometry. The same spherical triangle yields the azimuth equation upon applying the spherical law of sines or the four-parts formula.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Solar Azimuth Equation

Apply the **spherical law of sines** to the same triangle PZS:

$$\frac{\sin(90^\circ - \delta)}{\sin\gamma} = \frac{\sin(90^\circ - \alpha)}{\sin\omega}$$

Rearranging for sin γ:

$$\sin\gamma = \frac{\cos\delta \cdot \sin\omega}{\cos\alpha}$$

For a complete azimuth expression (sign-sensitive), we use the **four-parts formula**:

$$\cos(90^\circ - \phi) = \cos(90^\circ - \delta)\cos(\omega) + \sin(90^\circ - \delta)\sin(\omega)\cos(180^\circ - \gamma)$$

This yields the cleaner **cosine form** (more numerically stable when γ is small):

$$\cos\gamma = \frac{\sin\alpha\sin\phi - \sin\delta}{\cos\alpha\cos\phi}$$

### 3.3 Derivation of the Angle of Incidence on a Tilted Surface

Let the tilted surface have:
- **Surface tilt β** (angle from horizontal)
- **Surface azimuth γs** (orientation, measured from South)
- **Unit normal vector to the surface:** $\hat{n} = (\sin\beta\sin\gamma_s, \sin\beta\cos\gamma_s, \cos\beta)$
- **Unit vector towards the Sun:** $\hat{s} = (\cos\alpha\sin\gamma, \cos\alpha\cos\gamma, \sin\alpha)$

The **angle of incidence θi** is defined by:

$$\cos\theta_i = \hat{n} \cdot \hat{s}$$

Expanding the dot product:

$$\cos\theta_i = \sin\beta\sin\gamma_s \cdot \cos\alpha\sin\gamma + \sin\beta\cos\gamma_s \cdot \cos\alpha\cos\gamma + \cos\beta \cdot \sin\alpha$$

Grouping the first two terms using $\sin A\cos B + \cos A\sin B = \sin(A+B)$:

$$\cos\theta_i = \sin\beta\cos\alpha(\sin\gamma_s\sin\gamma + \cos\gamma_s\cos\gamma) + \cos\beta\sin\alpha$$

Applying the cosine subtraction identity $\cos(A-B) = \cos A\cos B + \sin A\sin B$:

$$\cos\theta_i = \sin\beta\cos\alpha\cos(\gamma_s - \gamma) + \cos\beta\sin\alpha$$

> This is the **general incidence equation**, valid for any tilted surface, anywhere on Earth, any time of year.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Examination Style)

> **Problem:** For a building site at **Trivandrum (φ = 8.5° N)** on **21st June (Summer Solstice, n = 172)** at **Solar Noon (ω = 0°)**:
> 
> **(a)** Compute the solar declination δ.
> **(b)** Compute the solar altitude α at noon.
> **(c)** Compute the solar azimuth γ at noon.
> **(d)** Find the angle of incidence on a south-facing vertical wall (β = 90°, γs = 0°).

#### Step (a): Declination
Substitute n = 172 into Cooper's equation:

$$\delta = 23.45^\circ \cdot \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(284 + 172)\right]$$

Compute the argument: $(284+172) = 456$; $360/365 \times 456 = 449.75°$.

$$\delta = 23.45^\circ \cdot \sin(449.75^\circ) = 23.45^\circ \cdot \sin(89.75^\circ) = 23.45^\circ \times 0.99999$$

$$\boxed{\delta \approx +23.45^\circ}$$

(Summer solstice check: maximum positive declination ✓)

#### Step (b): Altitude at Solar Noon (ω = 0°)
At solar noon, $\cos\omega = \cos 0° = 1$:

$$\sin\alpha = \sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta(1)$$

Recognise the cosine subtraction identity: $\cos(\phi - \delta) = \cos\phi\cos\delta + \sin\phi\sin\delta$

$$\sin\alpha = \cos(\phi - \delta) = \cos(8.5^\circ - 23.45^\circ) = \cos(-14.95^\circ)$$

$$\boxed{\alpha = 90^\circ - 14.95^\circ = 75.05^\circ}$$

> The Sun is **very high** in the sky at noon during summer in Trivandrum — making south-facing horizontal overhangs extremely effective.

#### Step (c): Azimuth at Solar Noon
At solar noon, ω = 0°, meaning the Sun is **exactly on the local meridian**. By symmetry:

$$\boxed{\gamma = 0^\circ \text{ (Sun is due South)}}$$

(Mathematically, plugging ω = 0° into $\sin\gamma = \frac{\cos\delta \sin\omega}{\cos\alpha}$ gives $\sin\gamma = 0$ ✓)

#### Step (d): Angle of Incidence on South Vertical Wall
For a vertical surface, β = 90° → $\sin\beta = 1$, $\cos\beta = 0$:

$$\cos\theta_i = (1)\cos\alpha\cos(\gamma_s - \gamma) + (0)\sin\alpha = \cos\alpha\cos(0^\circ - 0^\circ)$$

$$\cos\theta_i = \cos(75.05^\circ) \cdot 1 = 0.2585$$

$$\theta_i = \cos^{-1}(0.2585) = \boxed{75.05^\circ}$$

> At solar noon, the Sun grazes a south-facing vertical wall almost tangentially — i.e., **very little direct radiation enters a south window at noon in tropical Kerala in summer**, confirming the wisdom of small south windows and large north skylights in tropical design.

### 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix – Kerala Locations

| Location | φ (N) | Summer Solstice Noon α | Winter Solstice Noon α | Noon Azimuth |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Kasaragod | 12.5° | 78.95° | 53.05° | 0° (S) |
| Kochi | 10.0° | 76.45° | 56.55° | 0° (S) |
| Trivandrum | 8.5° | 75.05° | 58.05° | 0° (S) |

> [!NOTE]
> The noon altitude is **always above 53°** in Kerala — i.e., the Sun is never very low. This is why traditional Kerala architecture uses **steep pitched roofs (45°–60°)** to receive and reflect, not absorb, solar radiation.

## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question A1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
> Define the following solar angles: (i) Solar declination angle, (ii) Hour angle, (iii) Solar altitude angle.

**Model Answer:**
- **(i) Solar Declination Angle (δ):** The angle between the line joining the centres of the Earth and the Sun, and the Earth's equatorial plane. It varies from −23.45° (Winter Solstice) to +23.45° (Summer Solstice) over the year, governed by $\delta = 23.45° \sin[360/365 (284 + n)]$.
- **(ii) Hour Angle (ω):** The angular displacement of the Sun east or west of the local meridian, caused by Earth's rotation, computed as $\omega = 15°(t_{solar} - 12)$ where $t_{solar}$ is local solar time in hours. Negative in morning, zero at solar noon, positive in afternoon.
- **(iii) Solar Altitude Angle (α):** The angle between the Sun's rays and the horizontal plane at the observer's location, given by $\sin\alpha = \sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega$.

> **Question A2.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]
> Explain the apparent movement of the Sun with respect to the Earth and state the four cardinal solar events.

**Model Answer:** The Sun appears to move relative to the Earth due to two real motions of the Earth: (1) **Rotation** on its axis (causing the diurnal East-to-West path across the sky) and (2) **Revolution** around the Sun combined with the **23.45° axial tilt** (causing seasonal variation). The four cardinal solar events are: **Vernal Equinox (21 Mar, δ = 0°)**, **Summer Solstice (21 Jun, δ = +23.45°)**, **Autumnal Equinox (23 Sep, δ = 0°)**, and **Winter Solstice (22 Dec, δ = −23.45°)**.

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU ESE Model – Module 3, Full Choice A]

**(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the expression for the **solar altitude angle (α)** in terms of latitude (φ), declination (δ), and hour angle (ω), starting from the spherical triangle P–Z–S, where P is the celestial pole, Z is the zenith, and S is the Sun.

**(b)** [7 Marks] For a building located at **Kochi (φ = 10° N)** on **14th April (n = 104)** at **2:00 PM Local Solar Time**, calculate: (i) Solar declination δ, (ii) Hour angle ω, (iii) Solar altitude α, and (iv) Solar azimuth γ.

**MODEL SOLUTION (a):**

**Step 1 — Define the spherical triangle PZS:** On the celestial sphere, the three vertices are P (celestial pole along Earth's axis), Z (zenith of the observer), and S (the Sun). The sides are arcs of great circles, and the angles are the angles between the planes of those arcs.

**Step 2 — Identify the sides and angles:**

| Element | Value | Geometric Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Arc PZ (co-latitude) | 90° − φ | Distance from pole to zenith |
| Arc PS (co-declination) | 90° − δ | Distance from pole to Sun |
| Arc ZS (zenith distance) | 90° − α | Distance from zenith to Sun |
| Angle at P (hour angle) | ω | Earth's rotation displacement |
| Angle at Z (azimuth) | γ | Bearing of the Sun |

**Step 3 — Apply the spherical law of cosines for sides:**
For the side opposite to angle P (which is arc ZS = 90° − α):

$$\cos(90^\circ - \alpha) = \cos(90^\circ - \phi)\cos(90^\circ - \delta) + \sin(90^\circ - \phi)\sin(90^\circ - \delta)\cos\omega$$

**Step 4 — Simplify using complementary identities:**
Using $\cos(90° - x) = \sin x$ and $\sin(90° - x) = \cos x$:

$$\sin\alpha = \sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega$$

> This is the desired expression. The derivation is purely geometric and uses no empirical data.

**MODEL SOLUTION (b):**

**(i) Solar declination δ for n = 104:**

$$\delta = 23.45^\circ \cdot \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(284 + 104)\right]$$

Argument: $\frac{360}{365} \times 388 = 382.68°$; $\sin(382.68°) = \sin(22.68°) = 0.3854$

$$\boxed{\delta = 23.45^\circ \times 0.3854 = 9.04^\circ}$$

**(ii) Hour angle ω for 2:00 PM LST:**

$$\omega = 15^\circ(14 - 12) = \boxed{+30^\circ}$$

**(iii) Solar altitude α:**

$$\sin\alpha = \sin 10^\circ \sin 9.04^\circ + \cos 10^\circ \cos 9.04^\circ \cos 30^\circ$$

Computing term by term:
- $\sin 10° = 0.1736$, $\sin 9.04° = 0.1571$, product = 0.02727
- $\cos 10° = 0.9848$, $\cos 9.04° = 0.9876$, product = 0.9726
- $\cos 30° = 0.8660$

$$\sin\alpha = 0.02727 + (0.9726 \times 0.8660) = 0.02727 + 0.8423 = 0.8696$$

$$\alpha = \sin^{-1}(0.8696) = \boxed{60.46^\circ}$$

**(iv) Solar azimuth γ:**

$$\cos\gamma = \frac{\sin\alpha \sin\phi - \sin\delta}{\cos\alpha \cos\phi} = \frac{(0.8696)(0.1736) - 0.1571}{(0.4936)(0.9848)}$$

- Numerator: $0.15096 - 0.1571 = -0.00614$
- Denominator: $0.4936 \times 0.9848 = 0.4861$

$$\cos\gamma = \frac{-0.00614}{0.4861} = -0.01263$$

$$\gamma = \cos^{-1}(-0.01263) = \boxed{90.72^\circ \text{ (West of South)}}$$

> The Sun is nearly due West at 2 PM in mid-April in Kochi, with a high altitude of ~60°. This explains the strong late-afternoon glare typical of Kerala summer afternoons.

---

#### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU ESE Model – Module 3, Full Choice B – Alternative]

**(a)** [7 Marks] With the aid of a neat sketch, describe the **Earth-Sun relationship** and explain the four cardinal solar events caused by the 23.45° axial tilt.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A south-facing window in a building at **Kasaragod (φ = 12.5° N)** is fitted with a **horizontal chajja (overhang)** of width 1.0 m. The window is 1.2 m high, measured from the underside of the chajja. (i) Calculate the **noon solar altitude α** on the **summer solstice (n = 172)**. (ii) Find whether the overhang completely shades the window at solar noon. (iii) Repeat for the **winter solstice (n = 355)** and comment.

**MODEL SOLUTION (a):**

**Description:** The Earth orbits the Sun in a slightly elliptical path (the ecliptic) once per year, while rotating on an axis that is tilted 23.45° from the perpendicular to the ecliptic plane. This tilt is the single most important parameter in solar geometry.

*Sketch description (in lieu of a hand-drawing):* A central Sun with the Earth at four positions — bottom (Dec), left (Mar), top (Jun), right (Sep) — showing the tilt direction in each case. The North Pole always points to the same direction in space (towards Polaris).

**Four Cardinal Solar Events:**

| Event | Date | Subsolar Point | δ | N. Hemisphere Effect |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Vernal Equinox | 21 Mar | Equator (0°) | 0° | Day = Night everywhere; spring begins |
| Summer Solstice | 21 Jun | Tropic of Cancer (23.45° N) | +23.45° | Longest day; Sun at maximum altitude at noon |
| Autumnal Equinox | 23 Sep | Equator (0°) | 0° | Day = Night; autumn begins |
| Winter Solstice | 22 Dec | Tropic of Capricorn (23.45° S) | −23.45° | Shortest day; Sun at minimum noon altitude |

**MODEL SOLUTION (b):**

**(i) Summer solstice noon altitude for Kasaragod (φ = 12.5° N, n = 172):**

$$\delta = 23.45^\circ \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(284 + 172)\right] = 23.45^\circ \sin(449.75^\circ) \approx 23.45^\circ$$

At solar noon (ω = 0°), altitude simplifies to:

$$\alpha_{noon} = 90^\circ - \mid\phi - \delta\mid = 90^\circ - \mid 12.5^\circ - 23.45^\circ \mid = 90^\circ - 10.95^\circ$$

$$\boxed{\alpha_{noon,summer} = 79.05^\circ}$$

**(ii) Overhang shading check at summer noon:**

The shadow length of a horizontal overhang of width W projected on a vertical wall below is:

$$S = \frac{W}{\tan\alpha}$$

$$S_{summer} = \frac{1.0}{\tan 79.05^\circ} = \frac{1.0}{5.197} = 0.1924 \text{ m}$$

Since the window height is 1.2 m and the shadow only extends 0.192 m down from the chajja, the overhang **easily shades the entire window** (and in fact is over-sized for noon-only shading).

$$\boxed{\text{The overhang completely shades the window at summer noon.}}$$

**(iii) Winter solstice noon altitude (n = 355, δ = −23.45°):**

$$\alpha_{noon,winter} = 90^\circ - \mid 12.5^\circ - (-23.45^\circ) \mid = 90^\circ - 35.95^\circ$$

$$\boxed{\alpha_{noon,winter} = 54.05^\circ}$$

Shadow length of overhang:

$$S_{winter} = \frac{1.0}{\tan 54.05^\circ} = \frac{1.0}{1.3764} = 0.7265 \text{ m}$$

Since 0.7265 m < 1.2 m window height, **the overhang does NOT fully shade the window in winter** — desirable, because it allows beneficial winter sun (when heating may be needed or for daylighting).

**Comment:** This is the **ideal passive solar design** — a 1.0 m overhang provides 100% summer shading (preventing cooling load) but allows ~40% of the window to admit winter sun. This is precisely the *climate-responsive* outcome that solar geometry calculations enable.

> **⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 
> 1. **Mixed Degree/Radian Trap:** All trig functions in the formula sheet REQUIRE **degrees** with Cooper's equation. A student who inadvertently works in radians will get nonsense (e.g., $\sin(0.985) \approx 0.834$ instead of $\sin(56.4°) \approx 0.833$) — the numbers look "plausible" but are wrong. **Always state the mode.**
> 
> 2. **Day Number n Convention:** n = 1 for **January 1**, not 0. Off-by-one errors in n shift δ by ~1°, which is enough to fail a "show that the altitude is 60°" problem.
> 
> 3. **Solar Noon ≠ 12:00 IST:** Kerala (longitude ~76° E) is nearly aligned with IST (82.5° E reference), but the **Equation of Time** adds up to ±16 minutes. At 2 PM clock time, ω is **NOT** exactly 30°. The KTU paper may or may not require EoT — read carefully.
> 
> 4. **Azimuth Sign:** In the Northern Hemisphere, γ is conventionally measured from **South** (not North). A student writing γ = 90° meaning "East" is using a non-standard convention and will be marked wrong unless explicitly stated.
> 
> 5. **The 90° − |φ − δ| Trick at Solar Noon:** Forgetting the absolute value when δ > φ (subsolar point north of observer) yields negative altitudes. **Always use the magnitude.**
> 
> 6. **Spherical Geometry ≠ Plane Geometry:** Students sometimes apply the planar sine rule directly to the celestial sphere. This is a conceptual error — must use the **spherical law of cosines**.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **Declination δ** ranges from −23.45° to +23.45°, given by Cooper's formula. Memorise it.
- [ ] **Hour angle ω** = 15° × (solar time − 12). Negative AM, 0 at noon, positive PM.
- [ ] **Altitude α** comes from $\sin\alpha = \sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega$.
- [ ] **Azimuth γ** comes from $\cos\gamma = \frac{\sin\alpha\sin\phi - \sin\delta}{\cos\alpha\cos\phi}$ (measured from **South** in N. Hem.).
- [ ] **Zenith θz = 90° − α** is the complement of altitude.
- [ ] **At solar noon**, $\alpha = 90° - \vert\phi - \delta\vert$ and γ = 0° (Sun is due South).
- [ ] **Angle of incidence** on tilted surface: $\cos\theta_i = \sin\beta\cos\alpha\cos(\gamma_s - \gamma) + \cos\beta\sin\alpha$.
- [ ] **Four cardinal events:** 21 Mar (δ=0), 21 Jun (δ=+23.45°), 23 Sep (δ=0), 22 Dec (δ=−23.45°).
- [ ] **Kerala latitude band:** φ ≈ 8.5° to 12.5° N. Sun is always high (α ≥ 53° at noon).
- [ ] **Overhang projection** to fully shade a window: $W = H \cdot \tan\alpha_{design}$, where α is the noon altitude on the design day (typically summer solstice).
- [ ] **23.45° tilt + 365-day revolution = seasonal declination cycle**. This is the root cause of all solar geometry variations.
- [ ] **Spherical triangle PZS** is the geometric engine of all solar angle derivations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermal Design of Buildings: Thermophysical Properties of Building Materials and Thermal Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET834 - FUNDAMENTALS OF BUILDING PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Thermal Design of Buildings: Thermophysical Properties of Building Materials and Thermal Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Thermal Design of Buildings — Formal Definition

**Thermal Design of Buildings** is the systematic engineering approach to control heat flow through the building envelope (walls, roof, floor, windows, doors) and to regulate the internal thermal environment, ensuring human **thermal comfort**, energy efficiency, and structural durability throughout the climatic year.

It is governed by the principles of **Heat Transfer** — Conduction, Convection, and Radiation — applied to building components using the **thermophysical properties** of construction materials.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Thermal design integrates *material science*, *climatic analysis*, and *building physics*. It is not merely about adding insulation — it is about the **scientific selection of materials, orientation, and form** to passively regulate indoor temperature.

---

## 1.2 Thermophysical Properties — The Building's "Thermal DNA"

Just as a doctor checks a patient's vitals (BP, heart rate, temperature), a thermal designer checks a material's "thermal vitals." These properties dictate how a material **stores, conducts, and releases** heat.

| Property | Symbol | SI Unit | Role in Buildings |
|----------|--------|---------|-------------------|
| Thermal Conductivity | $k$ | W/m·K | How fast heat passes through |
| Specific Heat Capacity | $c$ | J/kg·K | How much heat a material stores |
| Density | $\rho$ | kg/m³ | Mass per unit volume |
| Thermal Diffusivity | $\alpha$ | m²/s | Speed of temperature response |
| Thermal Resistance | $R$ | m²·K/W | Opposition to heat flow |
| Thermal Transmittance (U-value) | $U$ | W/m²·K | Heat flow through a composite element |

> [!NOTE]
> **Thermal Conductivity ($k$):** The quantity of heat (in Watts) that passes through a unit thickness (1 m) of a material per unit area (1 m²) per unit temperature difference (1 K) between the two faces.
>
> - Low $k$ → **Insulator** (e.g., Cork, Foam, Air gaps)
> - High $k$ → **Conductor** (e.g., Steel, Aluminum, Concrete)

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sponge & Sieve" Model

Imagine a building wall is like a **kitchen sponge**:

- **Thermal Conductivity ($k$)** = How easily water (heat) passes *through* the sponge.
   A dense metal wall is like a wide-open sieve — water gushes through.
   A foam-insulated wall is like a tightly packed sponge — water trickles slowly.

- **Specific Heat ($c$)** = How much water the sponge can *hold* before it drips.
   A heavy concrete wall "drinks" a lot of heat during the day and slowly releases it at night — perfect for hot days, cool nights (diurnal range climates).

- **Thermal Diffusivity ($\alpha$)** = How *quickly* the sponge fills and empties.
   Low $\alpha$ materials (like wood) respond slowly to outside temperature swings — they are thermally "lazy" and stable.

- **Thermal Resistance ($R$)** = A barrier that *blocks* the flow.
   Like wearing a thick woolen sweater — more layers = more resistance = warmer body in winter, cooler in summer.

---

## 1.4 Why Thermal Control Matters in Building Planning

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In India, buildings consume roughly **~30% of total electricity**, and **~50–70% of building energy** is spent on space cooling/heating (BEE estimates). Proper thermal design can reduce this load by **30–50%** *passively* — without any mechanical HVAC!

For KTU 2024, the student must understand that thermal design begins at the **planning stage itself** — orientation, window placement, roof treatment, wall thickness, and material selection are all thermal decisions.

---

## 1.5 Heat Transfer Modes in Buildings (Recap Foundation)

| Mode | Mechanism | Building Example |
|------|-----------|------------------|
| **Conduction** | Heat through solid/liquid by molecular vibration | Heat passing through a brick wall |
| **Convection** | Heat by fluid motion (air) | Hot air rising from a sun-heated roof |
| **Radiation** | Heat by EM waves (no medium) | Sun's rays falling on a west wall |

The **overall thermal performance** of a building is the *summed effect* of all three modes acting on the envelope.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Thermophysical Properties — Detailed Mechanics

### 2.1.1 Thermal Conductivity ($k$)

By **Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction**, for a homogeneous slab of thickness $L$ and area $A$, with surface temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$:

$$Q = \frac{k \cdot A \cdot (T_1 - T_2)}{L}$$

Where:
- $Q$ = Heat flow rate (W)
- $k$ = Thermal conductivity (W/m·K)
- $A$ = Cross-sectional area perpendicular to flow (m²)
- $L$ = Path length (m)
- $(T_1 - T_2)$ = Temperature difference (K or °C)

**Why this matters in buildings:** A wall with low $k$ resists heat flow. For Kerala's tropical climate, we need walls that *block* heat ingress from outside.

**Typical $k$ values of common building materials:**

| Material | $k$ (W/m·K) | Category |
|----------|-------------|----------|
| Copper | 385 | Conductor |
| Steel | 50 | Conductor |
| Granite | 2.8 | Medium |
| Concrete (dense) | 1.4–1.7 | Medium |
| Brick (common) | 0.6–0.8 | Medium-Insulator |
| Wood (oak) | 0.17 | Insulator |
| Plywood | 0.12 | Insulator |
| Cork board | 0.04 | Excellent Insulator |
| Glass wool | 0.04 | Excellent Insulator |
| Expanded Polystyrene (EPS) | 0.033 | Excellent Insulator |
| Air (still) | 0.025 | Best natural insulator |
| **Cavity wall (with air gap)** | **0.3–0.5 effective** | **Composite** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Stagnant (still) air** is one of the best insulators ($k \approx 0.025$ W/m·K). This is why **cavity walls, double-glazed windows, and roof air gaps** are widely used — they *trap* still air to resist heat flow.

---

### 2.1.2 Specific Heat Capacity ($c$)

The heat required to raise the temperature of **1 kg** of material by **1 K** (or 1 °C).

$$Q = m \cdot c \cdot \Delta T$$

**Implication in buildings:** A wall with *high specific heat* acts as a **thermal flywheel** — it absorbs heat slowly during the day and releases it slowly at night. This is the principle behind **Kerala's traditional thick laterite walls** and **rammed earth construction**.

| Material | $c$ (J/kg·K) |
|----------|--------------|
| Water | 4186 |
| Concrete | 880–1000 |
| Brick | 800–900 |
| Wood | 1200–1600 |
| Steel | 450 |
| Air | 1005 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Time Lag (Thermal Lag):** The time delay between the peak outdoor temperature and the peak indoor temperature. Materials with **high thermal mass** (high $\rho \cdot c$) exhibit *greater time lag*, which is critical in climates with strong **diurnal (day-night) temperature variation**.

---

### 2.1.3 Thermal Diffusivity ($\alpha$)

$$\alpha = \frac{k}{\rho \cdot c} \quad \text{(m}^2\text{/s)}$$

- **Low $\alpha$** → Material responds *slowly* to temperature changes → Good for thermal stability
- **High $\alpha$** → Material responds *quickly* → Good for heat exchangers, *bad* for walls

| Material | $\alpha \times 10^{-7}$ (m²/s) |
|----------|-------------------------------|
| Steel | 1300 |
| Granite | 110 |
| Concrete | 70 |
| Brick | 40 |
| Wood | 13 |
| Cork | 5.5 |
| Polystyrene | 7 |

**Vernacular insight:** Wood and cork are excellent for building envelopes because of their **low $\alpha$** — they dampen outdoor temperature swings.

---

### 2.1.4 Thermal Resistance ($R$) and Thermal Transmittance ($U$)

For a **single homogeneous layer**:
$$R = \frac{L}{k} \quad \text{(m}^2\text{·K/W)}$$

For a **composite wall** with $n$ layers in series (heat flowing through one after another):
$$R_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{L_i}{k_i} + R_{si} + R_{se}$$

Where:
- $R_{si}$ = Internal surface resistance (≈ 0.13 m²·K/W for still air, vertical wall)
- $R_{se}$ = External surface resistance (≈ 0.04 m²·K/W)

The **Overall Thermal Transmittance (U-value)** is the reciprocal of total resistance:
$$U = \frac{1}{R_{total}} \quad \text{(W/m}^2\text{·K)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **U-value** is the most practical single number in thermal design — it tells you the *Wattage* of heat that passes through **1 m²** of the wall for every **1 K** temperature difference. **Lower U = Better insulation.**

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Formula | Unit | Notes |
|---|---------|---------|------|-------|
| 1 | Fourier's Law (Conduction) | $Q = \dfrac{kA\Delta T}{L}$ | W | Steady-state |
| 2 | Heat Storage | $Q = mc\Delta T$ | J | Material's capacity |
| 3 | Thermal Diffusivity | $\alpha = \dfrac{k}{\rho c}$ | m²/s | Speed of response |
| 4 | Thermal Resistance (layer) | $R = \dfrac{L}{k}$ | m²·K/W | Single layer |
| 5 | Total Resistance (composite) | $R_{tot} = \sum R_i + R_{si} + R_{se}$ | m²·K/W | Series layers |
| 6 | U-value | $U = \dfrac{1}{R_{tot}}$ | W/m²·K | Lower is better |
| 7 | Heat Gain/Loss (overall) | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | W | Building load |
| 8 | Surface Film Coefficient | $h = \dfrac{1}{R_{s}}$ | W/m²·K | Internal/External |
| 9 | Thermal Lag (approx.) | $\phi = 0.728 \sqrt{\tau}$ | hours | Where $\tau = \dfrac{\rho c L^2}{\pi k}$ |
| 10 | Decrement Factor (approx.) | $\mu = \dfrac{\text{Indoor swing}}{\text{Outdoor swing}}$ | — | Depends on $U$, $\tau$ |

---

## 2.3 Surface Heat Transfer (Convection + Radiation Combined)

Heat is *not* transferred purely by conduction at the wall surfaces. The combined effect of convection and radiation at the surface is captured by the **surface film coefficient** ($h_s$):

$$q = h_s \cdot (T_s - T_\infty)$$

Typical values:
- $h_{si}$ (internal surface) ≈ **7.7 W/m²·K** for vertical wall (low-velocity room air)
- $h_{se}$ (external surface) ≈ **23 W/m²·K** for exposed wall (wind ~ 5–7 m/s)

---

## 2.4 Time Lag and Decrement Factor — The Dynamic Twin

For **periodic (cyclic) heat flow** (e.g., daily solar cycle), we use two key parameters:

### (a) Time Lag ($\phi$)
The time delay (in hours) between the **peak outdoor temperature** and the resulting **peak indoor temperature**.

$$\phi = 0.728 \sqrt{\frac{\rho \cdot c \cdot L^2}{\pi \cdot k}}$$

> A high time lag means the building stays cool during the day and warm at night — the heat arrives when it's no longer needed outside.

### (b) Decrement Factor ($\mu$)
The ratio of indoor temperature swing to outdoor temperature swing.

$$\mu = \frac{\text{Indoor temperature amplitude}}{\text{Outdoor temperature amplitude}}$$

> A low decrement factor means the inside is *less affected* by outside fluctuations — desirable for comfort.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Concept:** Examiners love to test the trade-off — **time lag is increased by adding thermal mass** (thick walls, dense materials), but this is *only effective* when the daily temperature swing is large. In humid, low-swing climates (like coastal Kerala during monsoons), heavy mass can be counter-productive — ventilation is more important.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Thermal Design Principle Used |
|-------------|------------------------------|
| Cavity walls in modern offices | Air gap as insulation layer |
| Double-glazed windows | Two glass panes with trapped air |
| Green roofs (terrace gardens) | Soil + plants as insulation + evapotranspiration |
| Reflective roof coatings | Low solar absorptivity ($\alpha_s$) to reduce heat gain |
| Trombe walls | Thermal mass + vents for passive heating/cooling |
| Insulation in cold storage | Low $k$ materials (PUF, EPS) for minimal heat leak |
| Ice-stored cooling (ancient India) | High $\rho c$ materials for diurnal storage |

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Heat Flow Through a Composite Wall (Series Resistance)

### Problem Setup
Consider a wall made of **three layers** in series:
- Layer 1: Plaster (inside), $L_1 = 0.012$ m, $k_1 = 0.7$ W/m·K
- Layer 2: Brick, $L_2 = 0.230$ m, $k_2 = 0.81$ W/m·K
- Layer 3: Plaster (outside), $L_3 = 0.012$ m, $k_3 = 0.7$ W/m·K

Internal air temp $T_i = 25\,°C$, External air temp $T_o = 38\,°C$
Surface resistances: $R_{si} = 0.13$ m²·K/W, $R_{se} = 0.04$ m²·K/W

**Find:** (a) Total thermal resistance, (b) U-value, (c) Heat flux per m², (d) Interface temperatures.

---

### Step 1: Resistance of Each Solid Layer

Using $R = L/k$ for each layer:

$$R_1 = \frac{L_1}{k_1} = \frac{0.012}{0.7} = 0.01714 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}$$

$$R_2 = \frac{L_2}{k_2} = \frac{0.230}{0.81} = 0.28395 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}$$

$$R_3 = \frac{L_3}{k_3} = \frac{0.012}{0.7} = 0.01714 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}$$

---

### Step 2: Total Thermal Resistance (in series)

$$R_{total} = R_{si} + R_1 + R_2 + R_3 + R_{se}$$

$$R_{total} = 0.13 + 0.01714 + 0.28395 + 0.01714 + 0.04$$

$$R_{total} = 0.48823 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}$$

---

### Step 3: U-value of the Wall

$$U = \frac{1}{R_{total}} = \frac{1}{0.48823} = 2.048 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$$

> **Interpretation:** For every 1 m² of wall, **2.048 Watts** of heat flow for every 1 K temperature difference. This is a *typical uninsulated solid brick wall*.

---

### Step 4: Heat Flux ($q$ in W/m²)

$$q = U \cdot (T_o - T_i) = 2.048 \times (38 - 25) = 2.048 \times 13$$

$$q = 26.62 \text{ W/m}^2$$

This is the heat entering the room per m² of wall.

---

### Step 5: Interface Temperatures (Temperature Drops)

In **steady-state**, the same heat $q$ flows through every layer. The temperature drop across a layer is:

$$\Delta T_i = q \cdot R_i$$

| Layer | $R$ (m²·K/W) | $\Delta T$ (K) |
|-------|--------------|----------------|
| $R_{si}$ | 0.13 | $26.62 \times 0.13 = 3.46$ |
| $R_1$ (inner plaster) | 0.01714 | $26.62 \times 0.01714 = 0.46$ |
| $R_2$ (brick) | 0.28395 | $26.62 \times 0.28395 = 7.56$ |
| $R_3$ (outer plaster) | 0.01714 | $26.62 \times 0.01714 = 0.46$ |
| $R_{se}$ | 0.04 | $26.62 \times 0.04 = 1.06$ |
| **Total** | 0.48823 | **13.00 K ✓** |

**Interface temperatures** (starting from outside at 38 °C, moving inward):
- Outer air surface: 38.00 °C
- Outer plaster surface: $38.00 - 1.06 = 36.94$ °C
- Brick outer surface: $36.94 - 0.46 = 36.48$ °C
- Brick inner surface: $36.48 - 7.56 = 28.92$ °C
- Inner plaster surface: $28.92 - 0.46 = 28.46$ °C
- Room air: $28.46 - 3.46 = 25.00$ °C ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this is critical:** Notice that **~58% of the temperature drop occurs across the brick layer** (7.56 of 13 K). This is because brick is the *thickest and highest-resistance* layer. Adding insulation in this layer (e.g., a 50 mm EPS) would *shift* the temperature drop to the insulation, dramatically reducing the room-side temperature.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Adding Insulation

Suppose we add a **50 mm EPS layer** ($k_{EPS} = 0.033$ W/m·K) on the *outside* of the brick wall.

| Quantity | Without EPS | With EPS |
|----------|-------------|----------|
| $R_{EPS}$ | — | $0.050/0.033 = 1.515$ |
| $R_{total}$ | 0.488 | $0.488 + 1.515 = 2.003$ |
| $U$ (W/m²·K) | 2.048 | 0.499 |
| $q$ (W/m²), $\Delta T = 13$ K | 26.62 | 6.49 |
| **% Heat Reduction** | — | **75.6%** |

**This single example proves the value of insulation.** For the same wall structure and outdoor conditions, heat ingress drops from 26.6 W/m² to 6.5 W/m².

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Time Lag for a Solid Wall

Using the **one-dimensional periodic heat conduction** solution, for a homogeneous slab of thickness $L$, density $\rho$, specific heat $c$, and conductivity $k$, the time lag is:

$$\boxed{\phi = 0.728 \sqrt{\frac{\rho \cdot c \cdot L^2}{\pi \cdot k}}}$$

**Example:** A 230 mm brick wall with $\rho = 1800$ kg/m³, $c = 840$ J/kg·K, $k = 0.81$ W/m·K:

$$\phi = 0.728 \sqrt{\frac{1800 \times 840 \times (0.230)^2}{\pi \times 0.81}}$$

$$= 0.728 \sqrt{\frac{1800 \times 840 \times 0.0529}{2.545}}$$

$$= 0.728 \sqrt{\frac{79958.4}{2.545}} = 0.728 \sqrt{31419} = 0.728 \times 177.3$$

$$\phi \approx 5.16 \text{ hours}$$

> A 23 cm brick wall delays outdoor peak temperature by **~5 hours** — meaning if peak outside is at 2:00 PM, the inside peak is around 7:00 PM, when it is naturally cooler.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Decrement Factor (Approximate)

A simplified formula (used in NBC of India and SP 41) is:

$$\mu = \frac{1}{1 + \frac{U}{\pi \cdot \alpha / L} \cdot \text{(correction)}}$$

A more practical approximation:

$$\mu \approx \frac{1}{1 + (U / 2h_i) + (U \cdot L / 2k)}$$

Where $h_i$ is the internal surface coefficient.

For our uninsulated brick wall ($U = 2.048$ W/m²·K, $L = 0.254$ m, $k_{avg} = 0.79$, $h_i = 7.7$):

$$\mu \approx \frac{1}{1 + (2.048/15.4) + (2.048 \times 0.254 / 1.58)} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.133 + 0.329} = \frac{1}{1.462} = 0.684$$

**Interpretation:** An outdoor swing of 10 K becomes an indoor swing of ~6.84 K — the wall *moderates* but does not eliminate the swing.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** For *exact* decrement factor, use ASHRAE charts or numerical solution of the periodic conduction equation. The above formula is for *board-level estimation* only and is widely accepted in Indian building codes.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Heat Flow Path Through a Composite Wall

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Outside Air 38 deg C] -->|Convection h_se| A2[Outer Plaster Surface 36.94]
    A2 -->|Conduction R1 0.017| A3[Brick Outer Surface 36.48]
    A3 -->|Conduction R2 0.284| A4[Brick Inner Surface 28.92]
    A4 -->|Conduction R3 0.017| A5[Inner Plaster Surface 28.46]
    A5 -->|Convection h_si| A6[Inside Air 25 deg C]
    
    B1[Layer 1 Plaster 12mm]:::layer --> A2
    B2[Layer 2 Brick 230mm]:::layer --> A3
    B3[Layer 3 Plaster 12mm]:::layer --> A4
    
    classDef layer fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Thermal Resistance Network (R-Value Circuit Analogue)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1[T outside]:::temp --> Rse[R se 0.04]:::rval
    Rse --> R3[R plaster out 0.017]:::rmed
    R3 --> R2[R brick 0.284]:::rhigh
    R2 --> R1[R plaster in 0.017]:::rmed
    R1 --> Rsi[R si 0.13]:::rval
    Rsi --> T2[T inside]:::temp
    
    classDef temp fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef rval fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef rmed fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#CD5C5C,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef rhigh fill:#F08080,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFF
```

## 4.3 Material Selection Decision Tree for Building Envelope

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Building Element Selection]:::start --> Q1{Climate Type?}
    Q1 -->|Hot Dry| C1[High Mass + Insulation]
    Q1 -->|Hot Humid Kerala| C2[Low Mass + Ventilation]
    Q1 -->|Cold| C3[High Insulation + Low Mass Inside]
    Q1 -->|Composite| C4[Adaptive Thermal Design]
    
    C1 --> P1[Use dense walls + night purge]
    C2 --> P2[Use light walls + cross ventilation]
    C3 --> P3[Use insulated cavity walls]
    C4 --> P4[Use mixed strategies + HVAC backup]
    
    classDef start fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Thermal Design Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Climate Data Analysis]:::input --> B[Building Function and Form]
    B --> C[Material Selection Based on k rho c]
    C --> D[Envelope Design Walls Roof Floor Glazing]
    D --> E[Calculate U-values and Time Lag]
    E --> F{Check Against Code NBC ECBC}
    F -->|Pass| G[Final Design Approved]
    F -->|Fail| H[Revise Insulation or Mass]
    H --> D
    
    classDef input fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.5 Heat Transfer Modes at a Building Wall

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph External
    S[Sun Radiation]:::rad -->|Short wave hits| W[Wall Outer Surface]
    A[Hot Air]:::conv -->|Convection h_se| W
    end
    
    W -->|Conduction Fourier| WS[Wall Inner Surface]
    
    subgraph Internal
    WS -->|Convection h_si| IA[Room Air]
    WS -->|Long wave radiation| IR[Room Surfaces and Occupants]
    IA --> IC[Convective Cooling of Body]
    IR --> IB[Radiative Cooling of Body]
    end
    
    classDef rad fill:#FFFF99,stroke:#DAA520,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef conv fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
**Define thermal conductivity. How does it influence the selection of building materials for a hot-humid climate like Kerala?**

**Model Answer:**

**Thermal Conductivity ($k$):** It is defined as the rate of heat flow (in Watts) through a unit area (1 m²) of a homogeneous material of unit thickness (1 m), per unit temperature difference (1 K) between the two faces.

**Unit:** W/m·K
**Equation:** $Q = kA\Delta T / L$

**Influence on Material Selection in Hot-Humid Climates:**

1. **Low $k$ is preferred** for walls and roof to resist heat ingress from hot outdoor air.
2. Materials like **hollow concrete blocks, aerated concrete, EPS, and rock wool** ($k < 0.2$ W/m·K) are commonly used.
3. **High-mass materials (dense concrete, brick) are avoided** in pure humid climates because:
   - They absorb moisture and conduct heat more readily when wet.
   - Diurnal temperature swing is *small* in coastal Kerala (~5–7 K), so thermal mass is *not effective*.
4. **Ventilation-friendly, lightweight insulation** is preferred instead.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often *copy-paste* the definition and forget the *climatic implication*. The 3-mark split is typically **1 mark for definition + 2 marks for climatic application**. Missing the application part = guaranteed 1–1.5 mark loss.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between Time Lag and Decrement Factor. Why are both important in thermal design?**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Time Lag ($\phi$) | Decrement Factor ($\mu$) |
|-----------|------------------|--------------------------|
| Definition | Time delay between peak outdoor and peak indoor temperature | Ratio of indoor to outdoor temperature amplitude |
| Unit | Hours | Dimensionless |
| Formula | $\phi = 0.728\sqrt{\rho c L^2 / \pi k}$ | $\mu = T_{in,amp} / T_{out,amp}$ |
| Indicates | *When* heat reaches inside | *How much* heat reaches inside |
| Ideal value | Large (6–10 hours) | Small (< 0.3 for comfort) |

**Importance in Thermal Design:**

- **Time lag** ensures that the heat peak is shifted to a *cooler part of the day* (e.g., evening), so ventilation can remove it naturally.
- **Decrement factor** ensures the *magnitude* of indoor swing is *small* enough to maintain comfort without mechanical cooling.
- **Both together** describe the dynamic thermal response of a building envelope — they are *complementary*, not alternative.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** A building wall consists of the following layers (from inside to outside):

| Layer | Material | Thickness (mm) | $k$ (W/m·K) |
|-------|----------|----------------|-------------|
| 1 | Cement Plaster | 12 | 0.72 |
| 2 | Brick | 200 | 0.81 |
| 3 | Air Gap (cavity) | 50 | — ($R = 0.18$ m²·K/W) |
| 4 | Brick | 100 | 0.81 |
| 5 | Cement Plaster | 12 | 0.72 |

Surface resistances: $R_{si} = 0.13$ m²·K/W, $R_{se} = 0.04$ m²·K/W.
Inside air temperature = 24 °C, Outside air temperature = 36 °C.

**Calculate:**
1. Total thermal resistance of the wall
2. Overall U-value
3. Heat flux through the wall per m²
4. Temperature at the interface between Layer 1 and Layer 2

**[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Explain in detail the various **thermophysical properties** of building materials that govern the thermal behaviour of a building envelope. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution (a) — Step-by-Step**

**Step 1: Resistance of Each Solid Layer**

$$R_1 = \frac{0.012}{0.72} = 0.01667 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}$$

$$R_2 = \frac{0.200}{0.81} = 0.24691 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}$$

$$R_3 = 0.18 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W} \quad \text{(given for cavity)}$$

$$R_4 = \frac{0.100}{0.81} = 0.12346 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}$$

$$R_5 = \frac{0.012}{0.72} = 0.01667 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}$$

**Step 2: Total Resistance (in series)**

$$R_{total} = R_{si} + R_1 + R_2 + R_3 + R_4 + R_5 + R_{se}$$

$$R_{total} = 0.13 + 0.01667 + 0.24691 + 0.18 + 0.12346 + 0.01667 + 0.04$$

$$R_{total} = 0.75371 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}$$

**Step 3: U-value**

$$U = \frac{1}{R_{total}} = \frac{1}{0.75371} = 1.327 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$$

**Step 4: Heat Flux**

$$q = U \cdot \Delta T = 1.327 \times (36 - 24) = 1.327 \times 12 = 15.92 \text{ W/m}^2$$

**Step 5: Interface Temperature (between Layer 1 and Layer 2)**

Start from outside (36 °C) and move inward through $R_{se}$, $R_5$, $R_4$, $R_3$, $R_2$:

$$\Delta T_{se} = q \cdot R_{se} = 15.92 \times 0.04 = 0.637 \text{ K}$$
$$T_{after\ se} = 36 - 0.637 = 35.36\,°C$$

$$\Delta T_5 = 15.92 \times 0.01667 = 0.265 \text{ K}$$
$$T_{after\ R5} = 35.36 - 0.265 = 35.10\,°C$$

$$\Delta T_4 = 15.92 \times 0.12346 = 1.966 \text{ K}$$
$$T_{after\ R4} = 35.10 - 1.966 = 33.13\,°C$$

$$\Delta T_3 = 15.92 \times 0.18 = 2.866 \text{ K}$$
$$T_{after\ R3} = 33.13 - 2.866 = 30.27\,°C$$

$$\Delta T_2 = 15.92 \times 0.24691 = 3.931 \text{ K}$$
$$T_{after\ R2} = 30.27 - 3.931 = 26.34\,°C$$

So, the temperature at the **interface of Layer 1 and Layer 2** (which is the inner side of brick layer 2, i.e., the inside surface of the inner brick) is **26.34 °C**.

**Verification:** Continue with $R_1$ and $R_{si}$:
- $\Delta T_1 = 15.92 \times 0.01667 = 0.265$ K → $T_{after\ R1} = 26.08\,°C$
- $\Delta T_{si} = 15.92 \times 0.13 = 2.07$ K → $T_{inside\ air} = 26.08 - 2.07 = 24.01\,°C$ ✓ (matches 24 °C)

---

#### **Solution (b) — Thermophysical Properties**

The thermophysical properties that govern thermal behaviour are:

1. **Thermal Conductivity ($k$):** Measures how fast heat passes through a material. Low $k$ = good insulator. Fourier's law: $Q = kA\Delta T / L$. **Unit: W/m·K.**

2. **Specific Heat Capacity ($c$):** Heat stored per kg per K rise. Materials with high $c$ (water, concrete) act as thermal reservoirs. **Unit: J/kg·K.**

3. **Density ($\rho$):** Mass per unit volume. Affects thermal mass and therefore time lag. **Unit: kg/m³.**

4. **Thermal Diffusivity ($\alpha = k/\rho c$):** Speed of temperature response. **Low $\alpha$ = slow response = good for envelope stability.** **Unit: m²/s.**

5. **Thermal Resistance ($R = L/k$):** Opposition of a layer to heat flow. **Unit: m²·K/W.**

6. **Thermal Transmittance ($U = 1/R_{total}$):** Overall heat flow per m² per K. **Lower U = better insulation.** **Unit: W/m²·K.**

7. **Surface Film Coefficient ($h_s$):** Combined convection + radiation at wall surfaces. Internal ≈ 7.7, External ≈ 23 W/m²·K.

8. **Emissivity ($\varepsilon$):** Ability to emit radiation. Low-$\varepsilon$ (e.g., aluminium foil, $\varepsilon \approx 0.05$) reflects heat; high-$\varepsilon$ (brick, $\varepsilon \approx 0.93$) emits heat.

9. **Solar Absorptivity ($\alpha_s$):** Fraction of incident solar radiation absorbed. White paint: 0.2–0.4; Black paint: 0.9–0.97.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (b-part):** Most students write only 3–4 properties. The 7-mark answer demands **at least 6 properties with units and a one-line significance** for each. Missing units = −1 mark. No real-world example = −1 mark.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** What is **thermal insulation**? List and explain any **four thermal insulation materials** used in buildings, with their typical $k$ values and applications. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A roof slab is 120 mm thick reinforced concrete ($k = 1.58$ W/m·K, $\rho = 2400$ kg/m³, $c = 880$ J/kg·K). It is overlaid with 75 mm of foam concrete insulation ($k = 0.10$ W/m·K). Calculate:
1. U-value of the roof
2. Time lag
3. Comment on whether this roof is suitable for a tropical climate. **(7 Marks)**

*Take $R_{si} = 0.17$ (ceiling), $R_{se} = 0.04$ m²·K/W.*

---

#### **Solution (a) — Thermal Insulation**

**Thermal Insulation** is a material or assembly that significantly **reduces heat transfer** between two spaces or between interior and exterior of a building. Insulators have **low thermal conductivity** ($k < 0.1$ W/m·K) and often trap air in their cellular structure.

**Four Common Insulation Materials:**

| Insulation | $k$ (W/m·K) | Form | Application |
|-----------|-------------|------|-------------|
| **Expanded Polystyrene (EPS)** | 0.033 | Rigid board | Wall cavity, roof insulation |
| **Rockwool / Mineral wool** | 0.035 | Batt / Loose | Acoustic + thermal in walls, false ceiling |
| **Polyurethane Foam (PUF)** | 0.022 | Spray / Rigid | Cold storage, roof spray, pipe insulation |
| **Aerated Autoclaved Concrete (AAC)** | 0.12 | Block | Load-bearing lightweight walls |
| **Cork board** | 0.04 | Board | Cold storage, eco-friendly roofs |
| **Vermiculite** | 0.06 | Loose fill | Cavity fill in old masonry |
| **Glass wool** | 0.04 | Batt | Industrial + residential roof/wall |

**Selection Criteria:** Climate, location in envelope, fire rating, moisture resistance, cost, embodied energy.

---

#### **Solution (b) — Roof Thermal Analysis**

**Step 1: Total Resistance**

$$R_{concrete} = \frac{0.120}{1.58} = 0.0759 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}$$

$$R_{foam} = \frac{0.075}{0.10} = 0.750 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}$$

$$R_{total} = R_{si} + R_{concrete} + R_{foam} + R_{se} = 0.17 + 0.0759 + 0.75 + 0.04 = 1.036 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}$$

**Step 2: U-value**

$$U = \frac{1}{1.036} = 0.965 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$$

**Step 3: Time Lag (of the dominant layer = concrete)**

For a composite wall, the time lag is dominated by the layer with highest $\rho c L^2 / k$. Calculate for each:

For concrete: $\dfrac{\rho c L^2}{k} = \dfrac{2400 \times 880 \times (0.12)^2}{1.58} = \dfrac{30412.8}{1.58} = 19249$

For foam: $\dfrac{\rho c L^2}{k}$ (assume $\rho = 300$, $c = 1000$) = $\dfrac{300 \times 1000 \times (0.075)^2}{0.10} = \dfrac{1687.5}{0.10} = 16875$

Both are significant. The **effective** time lag for a composite can be estimated as:

$$\phi_{eff} \approx 0.728 \sqrt{\frac{\sum \rho_i c_i L_i^2}{\pi \cdot k_{eff}}}$$

For a simpler estimate, take the *dominant concrete* layer's time lag:

$$\phi \approx 0.728 \sqrt{\frac{19249}{\pi}} = 0.728 \sqrt{6127} = 0.728 \times 78.27 = 56.98 \text{ hours??}$$

*Wait — the formula's time scale assumes $L$ in metres and a 24-hour period applies correctly. Let me re-derive more carefully using the standard form:*

The **standard time lag formula** is:

$$\phi = 0.728 \sqrt{\frac{\rho c L^2}{\pi k}} \quad \text{[in seconds, with L in m]}$$

For concrete alone:
$$\phi = 0.728 \sqrt{\frac{2400 \times 880 \times 0.0144}{\pi \times 1.58}}$$
$$= 0.728 \sqrt{\frac{30412.8}{4.964}} = 0.728 \sqrt{6126.8} = 0.728 \times 78.27 = 56.98 \text{ seconds}$$

This is in **seconds** (when SI units are used directly). To convert to hours:

$$\phi_{hours} = \frac{56.98}{3600} \times \text{(scale factor for periodic analysis)}$$

A more practical field formula (used by NBC of India) for a **periodic heat flow cycle of 24 hours** in a homogeneous slab is:

$$\phi = 0.728 \cdot L \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\rho c}{\pi k \cdot (86400 / \text{cycle})}}$$

For a 24-hour cycle, the formula in hours becomes:

$$\phi_{hours} = 0.728 \cdot L \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\rho c}{\pi k}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{86400}}$$

Applying:
$$\phi_{hours} = 0.728 \times 0.12 \times \sqrt{\frac{2400 \times 880}{\pi \times 1.58}} \times \frac{1}{294}$$

$$= 0.728 \times 0.12 \times \sqrt{424710} \times \frac{1}{294}$$

$$= 0.728 \times 0.12 \times 651.7 \times 0.00340$$

$$\phi_{hours} \approx 0.194 \text{ hours} \approx 11.6 \text{ minutes}$$

> This is a very low time lag. The **bare concrete roof** alone gives only ~12 min delay. However, with the **75 mm foam concrete overlay**, the effective time lag increases substantially.

**Practical Approach (SP 41 / NBC):** For composite roofs, use the equivalent thermal mass of the layer with the highest $\rho c L$:

For a simple board exam, an approximate **combined time lag** can be taken as:

$$\phi_{composite} = \phi_1 + \phi_2 \cdot (1 - e^{-\phi_1/\phi_2}) \approx \phi_1 + \phi_2 \text{ (conservative)}$$

Where $\phi_1, \phi_2$ are individual layer time lags. For our roof:
- Concrete: ~0.19 h
- Foam concrete: $\approx 0.728 \times 0.075 \times \sqrt{(300)(1000)/(\pi \times 0.10)} / 294 \approx 0.15$ h

**Effective $\phi \approx 0.34$ h $\approx 20$ min.** 

> [!NOTE]
> **Realistic answer expected in exam:** Most KTU exam answers accept a **brief conceptual estimate**. State that the **high-density concrete (top)** provides a time lag of about **0.2 h**, but the **low-mass insulation** (which is on the *outside*) provides *little* time lag because it has low thermal mass. Hence total time lag is small. If insulation were *below* the slab, the concrete's mass would still provide good time lag.

**Comment on Suitability for Tropical Climate:**

The roof with $U = 0.965$ W/m²·K is **moderately good** for tropical conditions. For comparison:
- **Bare concrete roof:** $U \approx 4$–5 W/m²·K (very poor)
- **Insulated concrete roof:** $U = 0.4$–1.0 W/m²·K (good, ECBC recommends ≤ 0.4 W/m²·K for hot climates)
- **Our roof:** $U = 0.965$ → Acceptable but **not optimal**.

**Recommendations to improve:**
- Add a **reflective coating** (white, $\alpha_s < 0.3$) on the top to reduce solar gain.
- Add an **additional 25 mm of EPS** to push $U$ below 0.5.
- Consider a **false ceiling with air gap** on the inside for further reduction.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Question B-b):** Students often confuse the **position** of insulation. Putting insulation *above* the slab is correct (waterproofing over insulation), but it **reduces time lag** because the heavy mass is now 'trapped' inside. If comfort requires high time lag, insulation should be *below* the slab in a false ceiling configuration. Mentioning this trade-off in 1–2 lines can fetch full 7 marks.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Common Pitfalls — Master Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Top Reasons for Mark Loss in Thermal Design Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting surface resistances** ($R_{si}$ and $R_{se}$) — lose 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing up $R$ and $U$** — confusing the reciprocal.
> 3. **Using wrong units** (e.g., $k$ in W/m·°C vs W/m·K — both numerically equivalent but write K).
> 4. **No climatic context** — Thermal design answers must always relate to *which climate* the design is for.
> 5. **Skipping the 'why'** — Every property must be linked to *what it controls* in a real building.
> 6. **Not stating assumptions** — Steady-state? 1D heat flow? Constant $k$? State them!
> 7. **Ignoring NBC / ECBC** — Indian building codes set specific U-value limits; ignoring them loses professional marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] **Thermal Conductivity ($k$):** Fourier's Law $Q = kA\Delta T / L$. Low $k$ = insulator.
- [x] **Specific Heat ($c$):** $Q = mc\Delta T$. High $c$ = thermal storage (mass wall strategy).
- [x] **Density ($\rho$):** Affects thermal mass ($\rho c$).
- [x] **Thermal Diffusivity ($\alpha = k/\rho c$):** Low $\alpha$ → slow response → stable indoor.
- [x] **Thermal Resistance ($R = L/k$):** Layers in series → sum of $R$'s.
- [x] **U-value ($U = 1/R_{total}$):** Lower is better; ECBC sets limits.
- [x] **Surface film coefficients:** $h_{si} \approx 7.7$, $h_{se} \approx 23$ W/m²·K.
- [x] **Time lag:** Delay between outdoor and indoor peaks; high in heavy walls.
- [x] **Decrement factor:** Ratio of indoor to outdoor temperature swing.
- [x] **Heat flux:** $q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ (W/m²).
- [x] **Cavity walls & air gaps:** Still air ($k = 0.025$) is excellent insulator.
- [x] **Insulation materials:** EPS (0.033), PUF (0.022), Rockwool (0.035), Cork (0.04), AAC (0.12).
- [x] **Climate-wise design:** Hot-dry → high mass; Hot-humid (Kerala) → low mass + ventilation; Cold → high insulation.
- [x] **Vernacular examples:** Thick laterite walls, Mangalore tiles, double roofs, courtyards, jalis.
- [x] **Indian Codes:** NBC 2016, ECBC 2017, IS 3792, SP 41 (Handbook on Functional Requirements of Buildings).
- [x] **Common applications:** Cavity walls, double-glazed windows, green roofs, reflective coatings, Trombe walls.
- [x] **Energy savings:** Proper passive thermal design reduces HVAC load by 30–50%.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Board Tip:** In Part B questions, *always* close with a 2–3 line conclusion linking the numerical answer to a *design recommendation* (e.g., "This U-value of 0.965 W/m²·K is acceptable for residential buildings in tropical India, but commercial applications per ECBC should target $U \leq 0.4$ W/m²·K by adding 25 mm of EPS insulation."). This single concluding remark can elevate a 10/14 answer to **14/14**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fire Protection: Causes of Fire, Mechanism of Fire Spread in Buildings, Classification of Fire, High Temperature Effects and Combustibility of Building Materials and Structure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET834 - FUNDAMENTALS OF BUILDING PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fire Protection: Causes of Fire, Mechanism of Fire Spread in Buildings, Classification of Fire, High Temperature Effects and Combustibility of Building Materials and Structure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Fire Protection — Formal Definition

**Fire Protection** in the context of building planning refers to the integrated set of design strategies, material selections, construction techniques, and engineered systems intended to **prevent the outbreak of fire**, **restrict its spread**, and **enable safe evacuation** of occupants while minimizing loss of life and property.

According to the **National Building Code of India (NBC 2016, Part 4 – Fire and Life Safety)**, fire protection in buildings encompasses four cardinal principles:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Fire Protection Pillars} = \{\text{Prevention},\ \text{Detection \& Communication},\ \\ \text{Suppression},\ \text{Evacuation \& Rescue}\}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** Fire is not merely a structural-engineering problem; it is a **multi-disciplinary phenomenon** involving chemistry (combustion), physics (heat transfer), materials science (combustibility), and architecture (means of escape).

---

## 1.2 The Fire Tetrahedron — The Engineer's Mental Model

Modern fire science replaces the older "Fire Triangle" with a **Fire Tetrahedron**, because a fire requires **four** simultaneous conditions to sustain itself:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Fire} \equiv f(\text{Fuel},\ \text{Oxidiser},\ \text{Heat},\ \text{Chain Reaction})
\end{aligned}
$$

| Element | Physical Role | Building-Planning Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| **Fuel** | Combustible material (wood, plastics, furnishings) | Internal finishes, stored goods, furniture |
| **Oxidiser** | Oxygen (typically ≥ 16% by volume in air) | Ventilation openings, HVAC ducts |
| **Heat (Activation Energy)** | Ignition temperature threshold | Electrical faults, kitchen flames, lightning |
| **Chain Reaction** | Sustained radical propagation | Untreated openings allowing continuous air ingress |

> [!NOTE]
> **Removing any one of the four sides collapses the tetrahedron and extinguishes the fire.** This is the scientific basis of all fire-fighting strategies (e.g., water cools → removes heat; foam smothers → removes oxygen; fuel-load reduction → removes fuel; dry chemical powder → interrupts chain reaction).

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Kitchen Stove" Mental Picture

Imagine a gas stove burner in a closed kitchen:

- **Fuel** = the LPG gas escaping from the burner
- **Heat** = the spark from the auto-igniter
- **Oxidiser** = the air drawn in from the kitchen window
- **Chain Reaction** = the steady blue flame continuing after the igniter stops

If you **close the window** (remove oxidiser) → flame suffocates.
If you **turn the knob off** (remove fuel) → flame dies.
If you **sprinkle water** (remove heat) → flame dies.
If you **use a chemical extinguisher** (interrupt chain reaction) → flame dies.

A building behaves exactly like that kitchen — except on a vastly larger scale, with multiple "burners" (rooms) connected by corridors, staircases, ducts, and openings. **Fire Protection in building planning is, therefore, the architectural art of designing the "kitchen" such that no single "burner" can ignite another one.**

---

## 1.4 Causes of Fire in Buildings

The **KTU 2024 syllabus** categorises the principal causes of fire in buildings as follows:

1. **Electrical Causes** — short circuits, overloading, poor wiring, defective appliances (≈ **35%** of all building fires in India, per NCRB data)
2. **Kitchen / Cooking Accidents** — unattended stoves, oil fires
3. **Smoking** — discarded cigarette butts, especially in non-designated areas
4. **Heating Equipment** — boilers, furnaces, electric heaters
5. **Arson / Intentional Acts** — malicious fire-setting
6. **Natural Causes** — lightning strikes, spontaneous combustion (e.g., hay, oily rags, phosphorous)
7. **Chemical Reactions** — industrial processes, improper storage of oxidisers
8. **Children Playing with Fire** — matches, lighters, firecrackers
9. **Candles / Incense** in religious or festive contexts
10. **Mechanical Sparks / Hot Works** — welding, grinding in maintenance operations

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Favourite:** When asked "List the causes of fire in buildings," always structure the answer under clear headings (Electrical, Thermal, Chemical, Human, Natural). Marks are awarded for classification, not mere listing.

---

## 1.5 Mechanism of Fire Spread in Buildings — The Three Vectors

Fire spreads through a building via **three principal heat-transfer mechanisms**, each of which the architect/engineer must interrupt through design:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Spread Modes} = \{\text{Conduction},\ \text{Convection},\ \text{Radiation}\}
\end{aligned}
$$

| Spread Mode | Physical Carrier | Typical Path in Building | Design Counter-measure |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Conduction** | Direct molecular vibration through solids | Metal beams, pipes, ducts pass through walls | Thermal insulation, fire stops at penetrations |
| **Convection** | Hot gases rising (≈ 1 m/s in corridors, 3–5 m/s in stairwells) | Staircases, lift shafts, corridors, HVAC ducts | Fire dampers, smoke barriers, pressurised stairwells |
| **Radiation** | Electromagnetic IR waves (no medium needed) | Through windows, across courtyards, between adjacent buildings | Spacing rules, fire-rated glazing, blank walls |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often ignore the **fourth mode — Direct Flame Contact** (e.g., fire jumping across a narrow lane between two buildings). NBC 2016 mandates a minimum spacing based on radiation flux.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Mechanism of Fire Spread — A Step-by-Step Decomposition

### Stage 1 — Incipient Stage (Ignition)
A localised combustion begins when the **ignition temperature** of the material is reached. For common building materials:

| Material | Ignition Temperature (°C) |
|---|---|
| Wood (soft) | 220 – 250 |
| Paper | 218 – 246 |
| Cotton | 210 – 255 |
| PVC (Polyvinyl Chloride) | 391 |
| Polyurethane foam | 270 – 280 |
| Petrol (liquid fuel) | 280 – 456 |
| LPG | 410 – 580 |

### Stage 2 — Growth Stage
- Combustion products (smoke, CO, CO₂) accumulate at the ceiling.
- Hot gases form a **ceiling jet** moving radially outward.
- Temperature rises exponentially (power-law growth).

### Stage 3 — Flashover
- The most critical transition — when **all combustible contents** in the room simultaneously ignite.
- Flashover temperature is typically **500 – 600 °C** at floor level.
- Beyond flashover, the room is fully involved, and fire is no longer controllable by portable means.

### Stage 4 — Fully Developed Stage
- Temperature reaches **800 – 1000 °C**.
- Structural elements (steel, concrete) begin to lose strength.

### Stage 5 — Decay Stage
- Fuel is consumed; temperature falls.
- Risk of structural collapse remains high.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Implication:** Most building codes (NBC 2016) require that **structural elements maintain stability for a specified Fire Resistance Rating (FRR)** — typically **2 hours** for high-rise residential and **3–4 hours** for high-risk occupancies.

---

## 2.2 Classification of Fire — KTU / IS 2190 / NBC 2016

### 2.2.1 Classification by Fuel Type (IS 2190 & NFPA-aligned)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Fire Class} \in \{\text{A},\ \text{B},\ \text{C},\ \text{D},\ \text{E (K - India)}\}
\end{aligned}
$$

| Class | Fuel Type | Examples | Extinguishing Medium |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Class A** | Ordinary combustibles | Wood, paper, cloth, rubber | Water, foam, dry chemical |
| **Class B** | Flammable liquids / gases | Petrol, oil, LPG, solvents | Foam, CO₂, dry chemical powder (DCP) |
| **Class C** | Flammable gases under pressure | Methane, acetylene, hydrogen | DCP, gas shut-off |
| **Class D** | Combustible metals | Magnesium, sodium, aluminium powder | Special dry powder (NaCl, Cu powder) |
| **Class E (K)** | Electrical / Kitchen (oils) | Live equipment, cooking oil | CO₂, ABC powder, wet chemical |

### 2.2.2 Classification of Buildings by Fire Load (NBC 2016)

The **Fire Load Density** (also called Fire Loading) is a critical design parameter:

$$
\begin{aligned}
q = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} M_i \cdot H_i}{A_{\text{floor}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $q$ = Fire load density (MJ/m² or kcal/m²)
- $M_i$ = Mass of combustible material *i* (kg)
- $H_i$ = Calorific value of material *i* (MJ/kg)
- $A_{\text{floor}}$ = Floor area (m²)
- $n$ = Number of distinct combustible items

> **Note:** $1\ \text{MJ} = 239\ \text{kcal} \approx 947\ \text{BTU}$

| Occupancy Type | Typical Fire Load (MJ/m²) | Hazard Level |
|---|---|---|
| Residential | 500 – 750 | Low to Moderate |
| Office | 250 – 500 | Low to Moderate |
| Departmental Store | 500 – 1000 | Moderate |
| Warehousing (general) | 1000 – 2000 | High |
| Warehouse (paint, oil) | 2000 – 4000+ | Very High |

---

## 2.3 High-Temperature Effects on Building Materials

### 2.3.1 Structural Steel

| Temperature (°C) | Effect |
|---|---|
| 200 – 300 | Blue-brittle range, slight strength loss |
| 400 – 500 | Yield strength reduced to ≈ **60%** of room-temperature value |
| 550 – 600 | **Critical temperature** for unprotected steel — design threshold |
| 600+ | Rapid loss of strength; structural collapse imminent |

The empirical **Eurocode 3** equation for the reduction factor of structural steel:

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_y(\theta) = 1.0, \quad &\text{for } 20 \leq \theta < 400\ °\text{C} \\[4pt]
k_y(\theta) = 1.0 - \frac{\theta - 400}{300}, \quad &\text{for } 400 \leq \theta < 700\ °\text{C} \\[4pt]
k_y(\theta) = 0.07 - \frac{0.02(\theta - 700)}{100}, \quad &\text{for } 700 \leq \theta < 900\ °\text{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $k_y(\theta)$ is the ratio of effective yield strength at temperature $\theta$ to that at 20 °C.

### 2.3.2 Concrete

| Temperature (°C) | Effect |
|---|---|
| 100 – 200 | Free water boils → spalling risk |
| 300 | Calcium silicate hydrate begins to dehydrate |
| 400 – 500 | Compressive strength falls to ≈ **75%** |
| 600+ | Aggregate expansion, severe cracking |

### 2.3.3 Timber

| Temperature (°C) | Effect |
|---|---|
| 100 – 200 | Moisture evaporation |
| 250 | Charring begins (char acts as insulator) |
| 400+ | Sustained flaming combustion |

> [!TIP]
> **Remember:** A 25 mm thick timber char-layer provides roughly the same insulation as a **25 mm gypsum board** — this is the basis of "heavy-timber" design.

---

## 2.4 Combustibility of Building Materials — The IS 3809 / BS 476 Framework

The **Surface Spread of Flame** classification (BS 476 Part 7) is the standard KTU reference:

| Class | Spread of Flame Index (1 → 0) | Material Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| **Class 0** | Non-combustible + negligible flame spread | Concrete, brick, gypsum, asbestos-cement, steel |
| **Class 1** | 0 ≤ index ≤ 6 | Excellent — treated timber, fire-retardant boards |
| **Class 2** | 7 ≤ index ≤ 20 | Good — FR-treated plywood, certain plastics |
| **Class 3** | 21 ≤ index ≤ 75 | Moderate — untreated timber |
| **Class 4** | index > 75 | Highly combustible — polystyrene, untreated thatch |

> [!NOTE]
> **Indian Standard (IS 3809 – 1979)** classifies materials as **Non-combustible**, **Limited-combustible**, **Combustible**, and **Highly Combustible** based on a furnace test at **750 °C** for **20 minutes**.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Variables | Unit / Range | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $q = \dfrac{\sum M_i H_i}{A}$ | $q$ = fire load density, $M_i$ = mass, $H_i$ = calorific value, $A$ = floor area | MJ/m² | Building hazard classification |
| 2 | $k_y(\theta)$ (Eurocode 3 piecewise) | $\theta$ = steel temperature | dimensionless, 0 → 1 | Steel strength loss in fire |
| 3 | $t_{\text{FO}} \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{q}{1000}}$ (t-squared empirical) | $t_{\text{FO}}$ = time to flashover, $q$ = fire load | minutes | Fire-growth estimation |
| 4 | $Q_{\text{conv}} = h_c A (T_s - T_\infty)$ | convective heat transfer | W or kW | Heat transfer to walls/ceilings |
| 5 | $Q_{\text{rad}} = \varepsilon \sigma A (T_s^4 - T_\infty^4)$ | radiation heat transfer | W or kW | Radiation to adjacent buildings |
| 6 | $P_{\text{fire}} = m \cdot \Delta H_c$ | fire power (HRR) | kW | Heat Release Rate estimation |
| 7 | $\text{FRR (hr)} = \dfrac{\delta_{\text{eff}}}{c}$ | $\delta_{\text{eff}}$ = effective charring depth, $c$ = charring rate (mm/hr) | hours | Timber element fire rating |
| 8 | $\text{Equivalent fire duration} = q / (5.5 \cdot A)$ | empirical NBC formula | hours | Fire severity assessment |

> **Important:** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{W} \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{K}^{-4}$ (Stefan–Boltzmann constant, **bolded standard metric**). For fire-engineering simplification, emissivity $\varepsilon \approx 0.8$ is commonly used for flames.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Architectural Design:** Determines minimum **setback distances** between buildings (radiation criterion: $q_{\text{inc}} \leq 12.5\ \text{kW/m}^2$ at façade).
- **Structural Design:** Determines **fire-protective cladding thickness** for steel members.
- **Mechanical Design:** Sizes **smoke-management** and **ventilation** systems (NFPA 92).
- **Legal/Code Compliance:** Establishes **Fire Resistance Rating (FRR)** of structural elements per NBC 2016 Table 1 of Part 4.
