# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Equivalent Fire Duration from Fire Load

The **Equivalent Fire Duration** (also called the *Standard Fire Duration*) translates an actual fire load into the equivalent duration of the standard ISO 834 / ASTM E119 furnace test that would cause the same structural damage.

### Step 1 — Fire Load (q)

The total fire load is the heat energy released by complete combustion of all combustible contents:

$$
Q_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} M_i \cdot H_i \quad [\text{MJ}]
$$

### Step 2 — Fire Load Density (q')

$$
q' = \frac{Q_{\text{total}}}{A_{\text{floor}}} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} M_i H_i}{A_{\text{floor}}} \quad [\text{MJ/m}^2]
$$

### Step 3 — Empirical Conversion to Time

Per the **National Building Code of India (Part 4, Section 4.4.1)** and **Eurocode 1 Part 1-2** (Annex A), the equivalent fire duration is empirically:

$$
t_{\text{eq}} = \frac{q'}{k \cdot \rho \cdot c_p \cdot \theta_{\text{cr}}} \cdot \text{adjustment factors}
$$

For a quick estimate (used in the **Japanese / Pettersson-Odeen** method), the equivalent time is approximated as:

$$
t_{\text{eq}} \approx \frac{q'}{3300} \quad [\text{hours}]
$$

where the constant **3300** is approximately the heat-energy release rate per m² of floor area in a fully developed compartment fire (in kW·h/m² per hour).

> **Assumption:** Ventilation-controlled fire, compartment factor 1.0, opening factor 0.04 m½.

### Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A residential office room of floor area $A = 50\ \text{m}^2$ contains the following combustible contents:

| Item | Mass $M_i$ (kg) | Calorific Value $H_i$ (MJ/kg) | Energy $M_i H_i$ (MJ) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Wooden furniture | 200 | 17.0 | 3400 |
| Paper / books | 80 | 16.0 | 1280 |
| Cotton curtains | 25 | 18.0 | 450 |
| PVC cable insulation | 15 | 25.0 | 375 |
| **Total** | | | **5505** |

**Step (a):** Compute total fire energy.

$$
Q_{\text{total}} = \sum M_i H_i = 5505\ \text{MJ}
$$

**Step (b):** Compute fire load density.

$$
q' = \frac{5505}{50} = 110.1\ \text{MJ/m}^2
$$

**Step (c):** Convert to equivalent fire duration.

$$
t_{\text{eq}} = \frac{110.1}{3300} = 0.0334\ \text{hours} \approx 2.0\ \text{minutes}
$$

> **Interpretation:** A fire load of 110 MJ/m² represents a **Low-Hazard** occupancy (residential, typically 500–750 MJ/m²). Such an office would need at least **2-hour FRR** for structural elements per NBC 2016.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Heat Release Rate (HRR) and Fire Growth

The **$t$-squared fire growth model** (NFPA / Eurocode) is the standard KTU reference:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q(t) = \alpha \cdot t^2 \quad [\text{kW}]
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\alpha$ is the fire-growth coefficient, classified as:

| Fire Growth Category | $\alpha$ (kW/s²) | Time to reach 1000 kW | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Slow** | 0.00293 | ≈ 600 s | Densely packed wood pallets |
| **Medium** | 0.01172 | ≈ 300 s | Office furniture, retail |
| **Fast** | 0.0469 | ≈ 150 s | Stacked cardboard, PU foam |
| **Ultra-fast** | 0.1876 | ≈ 75 s | Flammable liquid pool fire |

### Worked Example — Time to Reach 5000 kW for a "Fast" Fire

Given $\alpha = 0.0469\ \text{kW/s}^2$ and target $Q = 5000\ \text{kW}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
5000 &= 0.0469 \cdot t^2 \\[4pt]
t^2 &= \frac{5000}{0.0469} = 106,609.8 \\[4pt]
t &= \sqrt{106,609.8} \approx 326.5\ \text{seconds} \approx 5.4\ \text{minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Implication for design:** A "fast" fire reaches dangerous HRR in under 6 minutes — leaving very little time for evacuation in a high-rise unless means of escape are pre-engineered (pressurised staircases, smoke-proof lobbies, etc.).

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Radiation Heat Flux to Adjacent Building

Consider two parallel buildings separated by a clear distance $D$. The incident radiation at the façade of the second building is given by the **view-factor model**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_{\text{inc}}'' = \frac{\varepsilon \cdot Q_{\text{rad}}}{4 \pi D^2} \cdot F_{1-2} \quad [\text{kW/m}^2]
\end{aligned}
$$

For a simple case (point-source approximation, two parallel façades of equal height $H$ and width $W$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{1-2} = \frac{1}{2} \left[ \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (D/H)^2}} + \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (D/W)^2}} \right] - \frac{1}{\pi} \arctan\!\left(\frac{H \cdot W}{D \cdot \sqrt{H^2 + W^2 + D^2}}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Numerical Simplification — Worked Example

For two buildings, each façade $H = 20\ \text{m}$, $W = 30\ \text{m}$, separated by $D = 10\ \text{m}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{1-2} &\approx 0.18 \\[4pt]
Q_{\text{rad}} &= \varepsilon \cdot Q_{\text{total fire}} = 0.8 \cdot 5000 = 4000\ \text{kW} \\[4pt]
q_{\text{inc}}'' &= \frac{4000}{4 \pi (10)^2} \cdot 0.18 = \frac{4000}{1256.6} \cdot 0.18 \approx 0.57\ \text{kW/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Threshold Check:** NBC 2016 mandates $q_{\text{inc}}'' \leq 12.5\ \text{kW/m}^2$ at the property line and $\leq 2.5\ \text{kW/m}^2$ at openings. The 0.57 kW/m² value is well within the safe limit — these two buildings are *too close for a major fire*, but *safe for typical residential fires*.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Combustibility vs. Fire Resistance

| Material | Combustibility (IS 3809) | Surface Spread (BS 476 Pt 7) | Fire Resistance (hr) | KTU Use-Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Reinforced Concrete | Non-combustible | Class 0 | 2 – 4 | Columns, beams, slabs |
| Structural Steel (unprotected) | Non-combustible | Class 0 | 0.25 – 0.5 (collapses at 550 °C) | Frames; needs fireproofing |
| Structural Steel (with 25 mm gypsum) | Non-combustible | Class 0 | 1.5 – 2.0 | High-rise columns |
| Clay Brick (230 mm) | Non-combustible | Class 0 | 4 – 6 | Load-bearing walls |
| Timber (untreated) | Combustible | Class 3 – 4 | 0.5 – 1.0 (chars) | Doors, formwork |
| Timber (FR-treated) | Limited-combustible | Class 1 | 1.0 – 1.5 | Public buildings |
| Gypsum Board (12 mm) | Non-combustible | Class 0 | 0.25 (single layer) | Partition walls, cladding |
| PVC (rigid) | Combustible | Class 4 | < 0.1 | Electrical conduits — to be avoided |
| Polystyrene (EPS) | Highly Combustible | Class 4 | < 0.05 | Insulation — *banned in many occupancies* |
| Glass (ordinary) | Non-combustible | Class 0 | < 0.1 (shatters) | Windows — needs fire-rated version |
| Glass (wired/fire-rated) | Non-combustible | Class 0 | 0.5 – 2.0 | Stairwell enclosures |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** Explain the **Fire Tetrahedron**. Why is it preferred over the older Fire Triangle in modern fire-protection design?

**Model Answer (10 lines):**

The Fire Tetrahedron represents the four elements essential for a fire to initiate and sustain:
(1) **Fuel** — combustible material such as wood, paper, plastic, or furnishings.
(2) **Heat** — energy at or above the ignition temperature of the fuel.
(3) **Oxidiser** — typically atmospheric oxygen at ≥ 16% concentration.
(4) **Uninhibited Chain Reaction** — free-radical propagation that sustains combustion.

It is preferred over the older Fire Triangle because the Triangle considered only fuel, heat, and oxygen — it failed to explain why certain fires (e.g., burning metals, gas fires) could be extinguished only by breaking the chemical chain reaction. The Tetrahedron adds the **chain-reaction** side, explaining why dry-chemical powder extinguishers (which interrupt the chain reaction) are needed for Class B and C fires. **Removing any one of the four sides collapses the tetrahedron and extinguishes the fire.**

> Marks split: 2 marks for listing the four elements, 1 mark for explaining why it is preferred.

---

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`** Classify fires as per **IS 2190**. For each class, give **one example** and the **correct extinguishing medium**.

**Model Answer (5–8 lines):**

| Class | Fuel | Example | Extinguishing Medium |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | Ordinary combustibles | Wood, paper, cloth | Water / foam |
| B | Flammable liquids | Petrol, oil | Foam / DCP / CO₂ |
| C | Flammable gases | LPG, methane | DCP + gas shut-off |
| D | Combustible metals | Magnesium powder | Special dry powder |
| E (K) | Electrical / cooking oil | Live panels, kitchen oil | CO₂ / wet chemical |

> Marks split: 1 mark for each correctly classified row.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — Choice 1

**`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`** *(a)* Explain in detail the **various causes of fire in buildings** with suitable examples. *(7 marks)*
*(b)* Discuss the **mechanism of fire spread** in multi-storey buildings, covering **conduction, convection, radiation, and direct flame contact**. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Part (a) Model Solution

Causes of fire in buildings can be classified into **six broad categories**:

1. **Electrical Causes (≈ 35% of fires in India):**
   - Short circuits due to aged/damaged wiring
   - Overloading of circuits beyond rated capacity
   - Defective appliances and poor-quality switches
   - Example: A 1980s residential building with 1.5 mm² wiring on a 16 A air-conditioner circuit.

2. **Kitchen / Cooking Accidents:**
   - Unattended cooking oil reaching auto-ignition temperature
   - LPG leaks from faulty regulators
   - Example: A restaurant deep-fryer fire spreading to the exhaust duct.

3. **Smoking and Careless Disposal:**
   - Cigarette butts in non-designated areas
   - Discarded matchsticks near upholstery
   - Example: Office fire caused by a smouldering cigarette in a waste-paper bin.

4. **Heating Equipment and Mechanical Sparks:**
   - Furnaces, boilers, electric space heaters
   - Welding and grinding sparks
   - Example: Fire ignited by a spark during façade welding of an under-construction high-rise.

5. **Chemical and Industrial Causes:**
   - Improper storage of oxidisers (e.g., sodium hypochlorite with acids)
   - Spontaneous combustion of oily rags, hay, phosphorous
   - Example: Paint-warehouse fire due to a chlorine-based oxidiser.

6. **Natural and Human Causes:**
   - Lightning strikes, arson, children playing with fire
   - Festive / religious candles, incense, fire-crackers
   - Example: A religious building fire caused by an unattended incense stick igniting drapery.

> **Conclusion:** A well-designed building must address all six categories through **prevention-by-design** (e.g., FR-treated interiors, smoke detection, automatic sprinkler systems).

---

#### Part (b) Model Solution

The **mechanism of fire spread** in multi-storey buildings is governed by **four modes** of heat transfer:

**1. Conduction (Solid-to-Solid Transfer):**
Heat travels through solid elements — metal beams, steel pipes, electrical conduits, and reinforced concrete. In multi-storey buildings, **unprotected steel members** can conduct heat to combustible materials on the opposite side of a wall within minutes. *Counter-measure:* fire-stop seals at every wall/beam penetration, **fire-resistant insulation** (mineral wool, gypsum).

**2. Convection (Fluid/Gas Transfer):**
Hot combustion gases rise buoyantly (≈ 1 m/s in corridors, 3–5 m/s in vertical shafts). In a multi-storey building, this creates the **"chimney effect"** — fresh air enters at lower floors, gets heated, and rises through staircases, lift shafts, and service ducts. *Counter-measure:* **pressurised stairwells** (positive pressure 12–25 Pa), **fire dampers** in ducts, **smoke curtains** at floor lobbies.

**3. Radiation (Electromagnetic Transfer):**
Radiant heat travels in straight lines at the speed of light. Through windows, a fully developed fire can radiate across to the adjacent building façade, igniting its materials. NBC 2016 mandates that the incident heat flux at the property line shall not exceed **12.5 kW/m²** and at unprotected openings **2.5 kW/m²**.

**4. Direct Flame Contact (Physical Flame Spread):**
Flames can physically lick across small separations (e.g., a narrow lane between buildings) or jump across intervening floors through **external cladding materials** (e.g., the 2017 Grenfell Tower fire in London, where aluminium-composite-panel cladding accelerated vertical flame spread). *Counter-measure:* **fire-rated external walls**, **spandrel barriers** between floors, **FR Class 0 cladding**.

**Conclusion:** A multi-storey building must be designed as a **vertical compartmentalised system** — each floor acting as an independent fire-cell, with **fire-rated doors, fire dampers, and pressurised staircases** interrupting all four heat-transfer modes.

---

### Question B — Choice 2 (Alternative)

**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** *(a)* Describe the **classification of fires** and the **classification of buildings by fire load** as per the National Building Code. *(7 marks)*
*(b)* Explain the **high-temperature effects on steel, concrete, and timber**. What is the concept of **Fire Resistance Rating (FRR)**? *(7 marks)*

---

#### Part (a) Model Solution

**Classification of Fires (IS 2190):** Fires are classified into **five classes** — A, B, C, D, and E (or K) — based on the type of fuel involved, as detailed in **Section 2.2.1** of these notes. The correct extinguishing medium for each class is given in the table there.

**Classification of Buildings by Fire Load (NBC 2016, Part 4):**

The **fire load density** $q$ is defined as:

$$
q = \frac{\sum M_i H_i}{A_{\text{floor}}} \quad [\text{MJ/m}^2]
$$

Based on the fire load, NBC 2016 classifies buildings into **four hazard categories**:

| Hazard Category | Fire Load Density (MJ/m²) | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Low Hazard (LH)** | < 500 | Residential flats, schools |
| **Moderate Hazard (MH)** | 500 – 1500 | Offices, hospitals, hotels |
| **High Hazard (HH)** | 1500 – 3000 | Departmental stores, libraries |
| **Very High Hazard (VHH)** | > 3000 | Paint warehouses, oil godowns, chemical stores |

This classification determines:
- The required **Fire Resistance Rating (FRR)** of structural members
- The **maximum compartment size** permissible
- The **density of fire-extinguishing systems**
- The **occupancy load** allowed per floor

---

#### Part (b) Model Solution

**High-Temperature Effects:**

**(i) Structural Steel:**
- Below 200 °C: minor effects, slight discoloration (blue-brittle range at 250–300 °C).
- 400 °C: yield strength falls to ≈ 60% of room-temperature value.
- **550 °C – 600 °C: critical temperature** — unprotected steel loses structural stability.
- 600 °C+: rapid collapse. *Counter-measure:* fire-rated cladding (gypsum, vermiculite, intumescent paint).

**(ii) Concrete:**
- Below 200 °C: water-vapour pressure can cause **spalling** (surface flaking).
- 300 °C: calcium-silicate-hydrate gel begins to dehydrate.
- 400–500 °C: compressive strength falls to ≈ 75% of design value.
- 600 °C+: aggregate expansion, severe cracking, loss of bond with reinforcement.

**(iii) Timber:**
- 100–200 °C: free moisture evaporates.
- **250 °C: charring begins** — the surface layer converts to charcoal.
- 400 °C+: sustained flaming combustion. The **char layer** is, however, a good insulator — providing ≈ 25 mm of effective protection per hour of exposure, which is the principle of *heavy-timber* design.

**Fire Resistance Rating (FRR):**

The **Fire Resistance Rating** of a structural element is the duration (in hours) for which the element continues to perform its load-bearing and/or separating function when exposed to a standard furnace test (ISO 834 / ASTM E119). The standard test follows the time-temperature curve:

$$
T(t) = 20 + 345 \cdot \log_{10}(8t + 1) \quad [\text{°C}]
$$

where $t$ is the time in minutes. For example, $T(30) = 842\ °\text{C}$, $T(60) = 945\ °\text{C}$, $T(120) = 1049\ °\text{C}$.

**Typical FRR requirements (NBC 2016):**
- Residential buildings: **2 hours**
- High-rise commercial: **3 hours**
- Hospitals, schools (assembly): **2 hours**
- Warehouses (high-hazard): **4 hours**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on this topic — and how to avoid it:**
>
> 1. **Conflating Fire Triangle and Fire Tetrahedron:** The Triangle is *three elements* (fuel, heat, oxygen). The Tetrahedron adds the **chain reaction**. Mixing up the two costs at least **1–2 marks** in Part A.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the unit on fire load:** Always write $q$ in **MJ/m²** (or kcal/m²). The numerical answer without a unit gets **zero credit** in numerical-type questions.
>
> 3. **Ignoring the "critical temperature" for steel:** The magic number is **550–600 °C** for unprotected steel. Writing "400 °C" or "700 °C" loses a mark.
>
> 4. **Writing only the names of fire classes without examples and extinguishing media:** Each Part A "Classify fires" answer needs **at least 3–5 lines of supporting text** beyond the table.
>
> 5. **Not specifying "Indian Standard" codes:** Always cite **IS 2190** for fire classification and **IS 3809** for combustibility. Generic answers without code reference are penalised in 14-mark questions.
>
> 6. **Skipping the "design counter-measure":** In spread-mechanism questions, students describe the mode but forget the architectural counter-measure. The examiner awards marks for **both**.
>
> 7. **Failing to draw a sketch in 14-mark answers:** Even a rough **hand-drawn sketch** of the fire tetrahedron or spread mechanism earns **1–2 extra marks** as per KTU valuation norms.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU University Exam:**

- ✅ **Fire Tetrahedron** = Fuel + Heat + Oxygen + Chain Reaction (NOT just 3 elements).
- ✅ **Fire Triangle** = the *obsolete* model — know it for context, but always answer with the Tetrahedron.
- ✅ **Fire spread modes** = Conduction, Convection, Radiation, Direct Flame Contact (four, not three).
- ✅ **Chimney effect** in multi-storey buildings — hot gases rise through staircases, lift shafts, ducts.
- ✅ **Fire classes (IS 2190):** A = solids, B = liquids, C = gases, D = metals, E/K = electrical/oil.
- ✅ **Extinguishing media:** Water (A), Foam (B), DCP (B/C), CO₂ (B/E), Special Powder (D), Wet Chemical (K).
- ✅ **Fire load formula:** $q = \frac{\sum M_i H_i}{A_{\text{floor}}}$ — always in **MJ/m²**.
- ✅ **Building hazard categories (NBC 2016):** LH < 500, MH 500–1500, HH 1500–3000, VHH > 3000 MJ/m².
- ✅ **Critical temperature for steel:** **550–600 °C** — design threshold for unprotected steel members.
- ✅ **Concrete spalling** begins at **200 °C**; **strength falls to 75%** at **400–500 °C**.
- ✅ **Timber charring** begins at **250 °C**; the **char layer insulates** the inner wood.
- ✅ **Combustibility classes (BS 476 Pt 7):** Class 0 (non-combustible) → Class 4 (highly combustible).
- ✅ **Fire Resistance Rating (FRR)** = time in **hours** for which a structural element maintains stability under standard furnace test.
- ✅ **ISO 834 standard fire curve:** $T(t) = 20 + 345 \cdot \log_{10}(8t + 1)$ °C.
- ✅ **NBC 2016 radiation limits:** $q_{\text{inc}}'' \leq 12.5\ \text{kW/m}^2$ at property line; $\leq 2.5\ \text{kW/m}^2$ at openings.
- ✅ **$t$-squared fire growth model:** $Q(t) = \alpha t^2$; fire-growth categories: slow, medium, fast, ultra-fast.
- ✅ **Stefan–Boltzmann constant:** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{W} \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{K}^{-4}$.
- ✅ **Emissivity** of flames ≈ **0.8** (for design simplification).
- ✅ **Four pillars of fire protection:** Prevention, Detection, Suppression, Evacuation.
- ✅ **Compartmentation** + **Pressurised staircases** + **Sprinklers** = the modern KTU/NBC answer for high-rise safety.

> **Golden Rule for KTU 14-Mark Answers:** Always structure your answer as **Definition → Classification → Mechanism → Effects → Design Counter-measures → Conclusion.** Examiners reward the structure as much as the content.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Architectural Design Aspects: Basic Anthropometrics, Human Functions and Their Implications for Space Requirements, Movement and Circulation Diagrams, Special Interpretations, Various Activities and Their Relationship with Spaces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET834 - FUNDAMENTALS OF BUILDING PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Architectural Design Aspects: Basic Anthropometrics, Human Functions and Their Implications for Space Requirements, Movement and Circulation Diagrams, Special Interpretations, Various Activities and Their Relationship with Spaces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Basic Anthropometrics — Formal Definition

**Anthropometrics** is the scientific study of the measurements and proportions of the human body — bone density, muscle mass, and other physical attributes. In architectural design, anthropometrics is the quantitative foundation for deriving spatial dimensions: clearances, reach limits, body envelopes, and circulation widths. The KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly treats anthropometrics as the **primary design input** for any habitable space.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anthropometrics (KTU Terminology):** *The systematic measurement of human body dimensions — static (body at rest) and dynamic (body in motion) — used to determine ergonomic dimensions, functional clearances, and minimum spatial requirements in architectural planning.*

The two principal branches of anthropometrics used in building planning are:
- **Static Anthropometrics** — Body dimensions in fixed, standing or seated postures (e.g., eye height, shoulder breadth, sitting height).
- **Dynamic Anthropometrics** — Body measurements during movement (e.g., walking stride length, arm reach while bending, climbing stair riser height).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "T-Shirt Sized" Approach

Imagine designing a **classroom desk** for a 5-year-old child. If you make it the same size as a desk for an engineering student, the child cannot reach the surface. If you make it the size for a basketball player, the student feels cramped. **Anthropometrics solves this by providing a range of measurements** (typically 5th percentile female to 95th percentile male) so that a design accommodates 90% of the population comfortably — the same way a T-shirt sold in "size L" fits most adults but not everyone.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Term — Percentile in Design:** Most architectural dimensions are designed for the **5th percentile (shortest) to 95th percentile (tallest)** range. Reach heights are designed to the 5th percentile (so shorter persons can reach), while overhead clearances use the 95th percentile (so taller persons don't bump their heads).

## 1.3 Standard Human Dimensions Used in KTU Curriculum

The table below lists the most frequently referenced human body dimensions in building planning. All values represent the **50th percentile (median) Indian adult anthropometric data**, which is the basis for NBC (National Building Code of India) 2016 and IS 13868.

| Body Dimension | Symbol | Value (mm) | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Standing Eye Height | E | **1650** | Window sill height, sign placement |
| Shoulder Height | S | **1400** | Shelf height, switchboards |
| Standing Height | H | **1700** | Door height, ceiling clearance |
| Sitting Height | — | **900** | Desk surface height, chair back |
| Knee Height (seated) | K | **500** | Desk clearance under-surface |
| Elbow Height (seated) | — | **650** | Work surface height |
| Reach Forward (upright) | R | **700** | Cabinet depth, control reach |
| Reach Upward (vertical) | Rv | **2100** | Upper shelf limit |
| Shoulder Breadth | B | **450** | Minimum passage width |
| Body Depth (front-back) | D | **230** | Corridor single-person width |
| Body Width (seated) | Ws | **500** | Seating width, desk width |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Clearance Rule:** Functional space = **Body dimension + Activity envelope + Safety buffer (typically 50–150 mm)**. Never design a space at the *exact* body dimension — humans are not static rectangles, they need wiggle room.

## 1.4 Human Functions & Their Implications for Space Requirements

**Human functions** are the activities that occupants perform within a space. Each function requires a specific spatial envelope, defined by:
1. The body posture (standing, seated, reclined, crawling).
2. The equipment/furniture involved.
3. The social interaction pattern (solitary, group, public).

The KTU module identifies **five core human functions**:

### 1.4.1 Resting (Sleeping)
- Function: Reclined horizontal posture for 6–8 hours.
- Anthropometric requirement: Body length + pillow allowance + turning space.
- **Minimum bed dimension**: 900 mm × 1900 mm (single); 1500 mm × 2000 mm (double).
- **Implication for room size**: Bedroom minimum **9.5 m²** (NBC) to allow bed, wardrobe, and circulation.

### 1.4.2 Working (Office/Study)
- Function: Seated task with desk interaction.
- Anthropometric requirement: Seat + desk clearance + chair pull-out + legroom.
- **Standard desk height**: **750 mm**; chair seat: **450 mm**.
- **Implication**: Workstation footprint ≈ 1500 mm × 1800 mm minimum.

### 1.4.3 Movement (Circulation)
- Function: Walking, turning, climbing.
- Anthropometric requirement: Stride length (≈ 700 mm) + shoulder width + turning radius.
- **Minimum corridor width**: **1100 mm** (single file); **1800 mm** (two persons side by side).

### 1.4.4 Social Interaction (Gathering/Dining)
- Function: Group conversation, dining, lounging.
- Anthropometric requirement: Conversation distance (1200–3600 mm) + seating arrangement.
- **Implication**: Living room typically 12–20 m² for a 4–6 person gathering.

### 1.4.5 Hygiene (Toilet/Bath)
- Function: Standing + seated + bending postures in confined space.
- Anthropometric requirement: Crouched body envelope + fixture dimensions.
- **Minimum WC cubicle**: 900 mm × 1200 mm (NBC).

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversation Distance Hall (Edward T. Hall — Proxemics):** This is a key KTU high-yield concept.
> - **Intimate distance:** 0–450 mm
> - **Personal distance:** 450 mm – 1.2 m
> - **Social distance:** 1.2 m – 3.6 m
> - **Public distance:** > 3.6 m
> These distances **directly determine room sizes** for different social functions.

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Static vs Dynamic Anthropometrics — Conceptual Decomposition

### 2.1.1 Static Anthropometrics
- Body is in **fixed, measurable posture** (standing, sitting, kneeling).
- Measurements taken in **standardized reference planes** (Frankfurt plane for head, vertical for standing).
- Used for: Furniture dimensions, fixture heights, doorway widths, ceiling heights.
- **Limitation:** Cannot account for the variation introduced by clothing, tools, and movement.

### 2.1.2 Dynamic Anthropometrics
- Body is **in motion** (walking, reaching, climbing, lifting).
- Captures the **range of motion (ROM)** of joints and limbs.
- Used for: Stair riser-tread design, corridor turning radius, ergonomic workstation reach zones.
- **Example:** Stair riser height is derived from the dynamic measurement of leg-lift capability (max comfortable riser = **150–190 mm** for adults).

## 2.2 The Concept of "Functional Space" vs "Built Space"

A critical theoretical distinction in KTU Module 4:

$$\text{Built Space} = \text{Functional Space} + \text{Circulation Space} + \text{Buffer Space}$$

Where:
- **Functional Space** = area required for the primary activity (e.g., bed footprint, desk footprint).
- **Circulation Space** = area required to access and use the functional space (paths to enter, open doors, sit down).
- **Buffer Space** = clearance for safety, comfort, and future flexibility.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The table below summarizes all critical equations and rules used in Module 4 problems. These appear repeatedly in KTU university exams.

| # | Parameter | Formula / Rule | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Minimum Ceiling Height (residential) | H_min | **2.75 – 3.0** | m |
| 2 | Door Height (residential) | H_door | **2.1** | m |
| 3 | Door Width (residential) | W_door | **0.9 – 1.0** | m |
| 4 | Corridor Width (single file) | W_corridor = B + 2·δ | **1.1** | m |
| 5 | Corridor Width (two abreast) | W_corridor = 2B + δ | **1.8** | m |
| 6 | Stair Riser (R) | 150 ≤ R ≤ 190 | **150** | mm |
| 7 | Stair Tread (T) | T = 600 - 2R | derived | mm |
| 8 | Stair Width (public) | W_stair | **1.5** | m |
| 9 | Headroom on Stairs | H_head = H + 50 | **2.0** | m |
| 10 | Turning Circle Radius (wheelchair) | r = 750 | **0.75** | m |
| 11 | Conversation Distance (intimate) | d_i | **0 – 0.45** | m |
| 12 | Conversation Distance (personal) | d_p | **0.45 – 1.2** | m |
| 13 | Conversation Distance (social) | d_s | **1.2 – 3.6** | m |
| 14 | Conversation Distance (public) | d_pub | **> 3.6** | m |
| 15 | Desk Height (seated work) | H_desk | **0.75** | m |
| 16 | Chair Seat Height | H_seat | **0.45** | m |
| 17 | Kitchen Counter Height | H_counter | **0.85 – 0.90** | m |
| 18 | Switch Board Height | H_switch | **1.2** | m |
| 19 | Window Sill Height | H_sill | **0.9 – 1.0** | m |
| 20 | Bathroom Door Width | W_bath | **0.75** | m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Symbols used in table (avoiding pipes for markdown safety):**
> - $\delta$ = safety buffer (typically 0.1 m)
> - $B$ = shoulder breadth (≈ 0.45 m for adult)
> - $H$ = human standing height (≈ 1.7 m)
> - $R$ = riser, $T$ = tread
> - $d$ = distance

## 2.4 The 5th–95th Percentile Design Rule (Deep Dive)

Anthropometric data is never used as a single number. Instead, the design range is bracketed by **percentiles** of the population:

- **5th Percentile (P5):** Smallest 5% of the population is excluded. Used for **reach-related dimensions** (e.g., switch height — must be reachable by the shortest person).
- **95th Percentile (P95):** Largest 5% excluded. Used for **clearance dimensions** (e.g., door height — must clear the tallest person).
- **50th Percentile (P50, Median):** Used for **average-fit dimensions** (e.g., chair seat height for general seating).

$$\text{Design Range} = [P_5, P_{95}]$$

For an Indian adult male population (per IS standards):
- P5 standing height ≈ **1600 mm**
- P50 standing height ≈ **1700 mm**
- P95 standing height ≈ **1810 mm**

Therefore, the **door height** must be designed for P95 = 1810 mm + shoe heel (50 mm) + clearance (240 mm) = **2100 mm**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the asymmetry in use:** Reach is asymmetric in human body — short people have proportionally less vertical reach than tall people have "extra" overhead clearance needs. So we use **P5 for reach, P95 for clearance**.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The anthropometric approach is foundational to:
- **Universal Design (barrier-free architecture):** Wheelchair turning radius, ramp gradients.
- **Ergonomics in workplace design:** VDU workstation layout.
- **Hospital planning:** Patient bed dimensions, OT clearances, corridor widths for gurneys.
- **Educational spaces:** Desk-chair height matching for child anthropometry.
- **Fire safety design:** Egress width calculation based on crowd flow rate and body envelope.

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Stair Riser-Tread Relationship

The most mathematically derivable concept in Module 4 is the **stair riser-tread formula** based on the average human stride. This is a **frequently asked 14-mark problem** in KTU ESE.

### Problem Setup
A person ascending stairs uses a stride that is shorter than walking on flat ground. Through experimental anthropometric studies, the **Blondel Formula** (for stairs) was derived:

$$2R + T = 600 \text{ to } 640 \text{ mm}$$

Where:
- $R$ = riser height (mm)
- $T$ = tread width (mm)
- The constant 600–640 mm = average human stride length on stairs (one full step cycle).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Start with the empirical relationship. Average human walking stride on flat ground = **700 mm**. On stairs, the stride is reduced because of vertical lift, so:

$$
\text{Str}_{\text{stair}} = 2R + T
$$

**Step 2:** Equate to experimental observation:

$$
2R + T = 600 \text{ mm (minimum acceptable)}
$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange to express T in terms of R:

$$
T = 600 - 2R
$$

**Step 4:** Apply the ergonomic constraint: Riser must be between 150 mm and 190 mm (P5 to P95 of leg-lift capability).

$$
150 \leq R \leq 190
$$

**Step 5:** Compute T for the boundary values:

- If $R = 150$ mm: $T = 600 - 2(150) = 300$ mm
- If $R = 190$ mm: $T = 600 - 2(190) = 220$ mm

**Step 6:** Conclusion — for a standard residential stair:

$$
\boxed{R = 150 \text{ mm}, \quad T = 300 \text{ mm}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For public buildings**, NBC 2016 mandates slightly relaxed ratios:
> $$2R + T = 630 \text{ mm (preferred)}$$
> with R = 150 mm and T = 330 mm. **This is the standard 14-mark derivation in KTU exams.**

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Q:** A public building stair has 12 risers. The riser height is fixed at 150 mm. Calculate the total vertical rise and the optimum tread width. Verify the Blondel formula.

**Solution:**

$$
\text{Vertical Rise} = n \times R = 12 \times 150 = 1800 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
T = 600 - 2R = 600 - 2(150) = 300 \text{ mm}
$$

Verification:

$$
2R + T = 2(150) + 300 = 600 \text{ mm} \checkmark
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Vertical Rise} = 1.8 \text{ m}, \quad \text{Tread} = 300 \text{ mm}}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Minimum Corridor Width

### Setup
Corridor width must allow two persons to pass each other OR one person to walk with a clearance buffer.

### Step-by-Step

**Step 1:** Identify body dimensions.
- Shoulder breadth (P95 male): $B = 510$ mm
- Body sway allowance: $\delta = 50$ mm per side

**Step 2:** For single-file walking:

$$
W_{\text{single}} = B + 2\delta = 510 + 2(50) = 610 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3:** Add maneuvering buffer for foot placement:

$$
W_{\text{single, final}} = 1100 \text{ mm (standard)}
$$

**Step 4:** For two persons side-by-side (with one carrying a bag — adds 100 mm):

$$
W_{\text{double}} = 2B + 2\delta + \text{bag} = 2(510) + 2(50) + 100 = 1220 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5:** Add comfort buffer:

$$
W_{\text{double, final}} = 1800 \text{ mm (standard)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always quote both the *theoretical minimum* (calculated) and the *code-prescribed standard* (NBC) in your answer. Examiners reward this duality.

## 3.3 Derivation: Conversation Circle in Living Room

For a **living room designed for 6 people to sit in a circle** and have a "social" conversation:

### Step 1:** Identify proxemic distance required
- For social conversation, distance between any two participants: $d = 2.0$ m (within 1.2–3.6 m range).

**Step 2:** Geometric setup. Six chairs on a circle:

$$
\text{Radius of seating circle: } r = \frac{d}{2} = 1.0 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3:** Calculate the seating circle area:

$$
A_{\text{seating}} = \pi r^2 = \pi (1.0)^2 = 3.14 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 4:** Add circulation buffer (1.0 m walking aisle around seating):

$$
A_{\text{total}} = \pi (r + 1.0)^2 = \pi (2.0)^2 = 12.57 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 5:** Round up to nearest practical room size:

$$
\boxed{A_{\text{living room}} \approx 14 \text{ – } 16 \text{ m}^2}
$$

## 3.4 Movement and Circulation Diagrams — Theoretical Framework

A **circulation diagram** (also called a **bubble diagram** or **activity relationship diagram**) is a schematic representation of how occupants move between spaces. The KTU syllabus requires students to:

1. Identify all activities in a building.
2. Determine the **adjacency relationships** (which activities should be next to each other).
3. Determine the **sequence relationships** (which activities must precede others).
4. Convert the diagram to a **space layout** (plan).

### Three Types of Circulation Patterns (Analytical Matrix)

| Pattern Type | Description | Example Use | Spatial Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Linear** | Spaces arranged in a single line | Museum galleries, dormitory corridors | Long narrow building, easy wayfinding |
| **Radial** | Central hub with spokes | Atrium buildings, hospitals with central nurse station | Compact footprint, central node dominance |
| **Loop / Circuit** | Closed path returns to start | Exhibition halls, race tracks | Continuous flow, no dead ends |
| **Grid / Network** | Multiple intersecting paths | Office floors, urban plazas | Flexibility, redundancy |
| **Cluster** | Organic grouping of spaces | Villages, organic campuses | Informal, contextual |

## 3.5 "Special Interpretations" in Architectural Design

The KTU module lists **special interpretations** that translate anthropometric data into architectural decisions:

1. **Modular Coordination:** Designing in multiples of a basic module (e.g., 100 mm or 300 mm grid) so all components (doors, windows, tiles, bricks) align with anthropometric dimensions.
2. **Reach Zones:** Dividing a wall into four vertical zones based on reach capability:
   - Floor to **600 mm**: Storage (out of easy reach)
   - **600–1200 mm**: Primary work zone (most accessible)
   - **1200–1800 mm**: Secondary display zone
   - Above **1800 mm**: Overhead storage (requires step stool)
3. **Turning Radius:** A wheelchair requires a 1500 mm diameter turning circle; design all accessible rooms around this.
4. **Edge Effect:** People naturally avoid the absolute edge of a room — leave a 300–450 mm "edge zone" for circulation along walls.

## 3.6 Activities and Their Relationship with Spaces — Master Matrix

The following matrix is a **high-yield KTU exam table** that maps every common building activity to its required space and key dimensions.

| Activity | Required Posture | Key Anthropometric Input | Minimum Space (m²) | Typical Room |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Sleeping | Reclined | Body length (1900 mm) + turning | 9.5 | Bedroom |
| Studying (seated) | Seated + writing | Elbow height (650 mm), reach (700 mm) | 4.5 | Study room |
| Cooking | Standing + reaching | Standing eye height (1650 mm), counter (850 mm) | 6.0 | Kitchen |
| Dining (4 persons) | Seated around table | Chair (450 mm) + table (750 mm) | 8.0 | Dining room |
| Bathing | Standing | Body envelope (700×700 mm) | 2.5 | Bathroom |
| Toileting | Seated | Body + fixture (400×500 mm) | 1.5 | WC |
| Washing (basin) | Standing bent | Reach forward (700 mm) | 1.0 | Wash basin area |
| Watching TV | Seated relaxed | Viewing distance = 3× screen diagonal | 12.0 | Living room |
| Conversation (group) | Seated facing | Proxemic distance (2.0 m) | 14.0 | Living room |
| Walking (corridor) | Walking | Stride (700 mm) + shoulder (450 mm) | 1.1 wide | Corridor |
| Climbing stairs | Walking + lifting | Riser (150 mm), tread (300 mm) | 1.2 wide | Staircase |
| Parking (car) | Seated in vehicle | Car envelope (5×2.5 m) | 12.5 | Garage |

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Anthropometric Body Dimensions Reference

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0[Human Body Standing] --> A1[Head Zone 0 to 300 mm]
    A0 --> A2[Eye Level 1650 mm]
    A0 --> A3[Shoulder 1400 mm]
    A0 --> A4[Elbow 1050 mm]
    A0 --> A5[Hip 900 mm]
    A0 --> A6[Knee 450 mm]
    A0 --> A7[Ankle 50 mm]
    A0 --> A8[Total Height 1700 mm]
    A1 --> A11[Overhead Clearance Zone]
    A2 --> A22[Window Sill and Sign Zone]
    A3 --> A33[Shelf and Switchboard Zone]
    A4 --> A44[Counter and Desk Top Zone]
    A5 --> A55[Workbench and Storage Zone]
    A6 --> A66[Stool and Lower Storage Zone]
    A7 --> A77[Floor and Foot Zone]
    A8 --> A88[Door Height and Ceiling Zone]
```

## 4.2 Activity-to-Space Mapping Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    B1[Human Function Input] --> B2[Posture Analysis]
    B2 --> B3[Body Dimension Lookup]
    B3 --> B4[Add Activity Envelope]
    B4 --> B5[Add Safety Buffer 50 to 150 mm]
    B5 --> B6[Functional Space Derived]
    B6 --> B7[Add Circulation Space]
    B7 --> B8[Add Buffer Space]
    B8 --> B9[Final Built Space]
    B1 --> BA[Resting]
    B1 --> BB[Working]
    B1 --> BC[Moving]
    B1 --> BD[Socializing]
    B1 --> BE[Hygiene]
    BA --> B2
    BB --> B2
    BC --> B2
    BD --> B2
    BE --> B2
```

## 4.3 Circulation Pattern Taxonomy

```mermaid
graph TD
    C0[Movement and Circulation Patterns] --> C1[Linear Pattern]
    C0 --> C2[Radial Pattern]
    C0 --> C3[Loop Circuit Pattern]
    C0 --> C4[Grid Network Pattern]
    C0 --> C5[Cluster Organic Pattern]
    C1 --> C11[Example Museum Gallery]
    C1 --> C12[Long Narrow Footprint]
    C2 --> C21[Example Atrium Hospital]
    C2 --> C22[Compact Central Node]
    C3 --> C31[Example Exhibition Hall]
    C3 --> C32[Continuous No Dead End]
    C4 --> C41[Example Office Floor]
    C4 --> C42[Flexible Redundant]
    C5 --> C51[Example Village Campus]
    C5 --> C52[Informal Contextual]
```

## 4.4 Reach Zone Analysis (Wall Section)

```mermaid
graph TD
    D0[Vertical Wall Zones] --> D1[Floor to 600 mm Low Storage]
    D0 --> D2[600 to 1200 mm Primary Work Zone]
    D0 --> D3[1200 to 1800 mm Display Zone]
    D0 --> D4[Above 1800 mm Overhead Storage]
    D1 --> D11[Requires Bending or Stooping]
    D2 --> D21[Most Comfortable Reach]
    D3 --> D31[Eye Level Easy View]
    D4 --> D41[Requires Step Ladder]
```

## 4.5 Bubble Diagram Concept (Adjacency Mapping)

```mermaid
graph LR
    E1[Entrance] --- E2[Foyer]
    E2 --- E3[Living Room]
    E2 --- E4[Dining Room]
    E2 --- E5[Kitchen]
    E3 --- E4
    E3 --- E6[Master Bedroom]
    E3 --- E7[Bathroom]
    E4 --- E5
    E6 --- E7
    E5 --- E8[Store]
    E6 --- E9[Children Bedroom]
    E1 --> EA[Arrows Show Movement]
    E2 --> EA
    E3 --> EA
    E4 --> EA
    E5 --> EA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Bubble Diagram:** The circles represent **spaces (rooms)**, and the **lines represent circulation paths**. **Thick/double lines** indicate primary movement; **single lines** indicate secondary access. KTU exams often ask students to draw or interpret such diagrams for a given program (e.g., 2BHK house).

## 4.6 Proxemics (Conversation Distance) Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    F0[Hall Proxemic Distances] --> F1[Intimate 0 to 450 mm]
    F0 --> F2[Personal 450 mm to 1.2 m]
    F0 --> F3[Social 1.2 m to 3.6 m]
    F0 --> F4[Public Above 3.6 m]
    F1 --> F11[Whisper Embrace Sleep]
    F2 --> F21[Friends Family]
    F3 --> F31[Acquaintances Colleagues]
    F4 --> F41[Public Speaker Audience]
```

## 4.7 Stair Riser-Tread Geometric Construction

```mermaid
graph TD
    G0[Stair Design Inputs] --> G1[Riser R 150 to 190 mm]
    G0 --> G2[Tread T 220 to 300 mm]
    G1 --> G3[Apply Blondel Formula 2R plus T equals 600 mm]
    G2 --> G3
    G3 --> G4[Solve for T given R]
    G4 --> G5[Compute Vertical Rise n times R]
    G4 --> G6[Compute Going Length n minus 1 times T]
    G5 --> G7[Final Stair Geometry Verified]
    G6 --> G7
```

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define **anthropometrics**. List any four human body dimensions used in architectural space design.

**Model Answer:**
Anthropometrics is the scientific study of human body measurements and proportions, used to determine ergonomic dimensions and spatial requirements in building design. It includes both static (body at rest) and dynamic (body in motion) measurements.

Four body dimensions used in architectural space design:
1. **Standing height (H = 1700 mm)** — used for door height, ceiling clearance.
2. **Eye level (1650 mm)** — used for window sill height, sign placement.
3. **Shoulder breadth (B = 450 mm)** — used for corridor width, doorway width.
4. **Reach upward (2100 mm)** — used for upper shelf height limit.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** What is a **circulation diagram**? List its three main types.

**Model Answer:**
A circulation diagram (also called a bubble diagram or activity relationship diagram) is a schematic representation showing the movement and flow of occupants between different spaces in a building. It represents rooms as circles/bubbles and movement paths as connecting lines, helping designers plan efficient layouts.

Three main types of circulation patterns:
1. **Linear Pattern** — spaces arranged in a straight sequence (e.g., museum galleries).
2. **Radial Pattern** — central hub with spaces radiating outward (e.g., atrium buildings).
3. **Loop/Circuit Pattern** — closed path returning to start (e.g., exhibition halls).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** **(a)** Explain the concept of **static and dynamic anthropometrics** with suitable examples. Discuss their importance in architectural design. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A public building stair has a floor-to-floor height of **3.6 m**. Design the stair by calculating the **number of risers, riser height, tread width, and total going length**. Verify the Blondel formula. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Static Anthropometrics:**
Static anthropometrics refers to body measurements taken when the body is in **fixed, stationary postures** — standing, sitting, kneeling, or lying. These are the "snapshot" dimensions of the human form.

**Examples:**
- **Standing height (H)** = 1700 mm → determines door height.
- **Eye level** = 1650 mm → determines window sill height for unobstructed view.
- **Sitting height** = 900 mm → determines back-rest height of chair.
- **Shoulder breadth** = 450 mm → determines minimum corridor width.

**Dynamic Anthropometrics:**
Dynamic anthropometrics refers to body measurements taken when the body is **in motion** — walking, climbing, reaching, bending, lifting. These capture the **range of motion (ROM)** of joints and limbs.

**Examples:**
- **Stride length** = 700 mm → determines stair tread width and corridor width.
- **Vertical reach** = 2100 mm → determines upper shelf limit.
- **Leg-lift height** = 450 mm → determines maximum stair riser height.
- **Arm reach while bending** = 600 mm → determines lower shelf depth.

**Importance in Architectural Design:**

1. **Ergonomic fit:** Ensures spaces accommodate the user's body comfortably, reducing fatigue and injury.
2. **Inclusive design:** Using 5th–95th percentile data ensures the design serves 90% of the population.
3. **Code compliance:** Anthropometric data is the basis for NBC 2016 and IS 13868 dimensions.
4. **Functional efficiency:** Furniture and fixtures fit properly; circulation paths are correctly sized.
5. **Universal design:** Enables barrier-free access for differently-abled users (wheelchair turning, ramp slope).
6. **Safety:** Proper headroom, stair geometry, and door widths prevent accidents.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Hint:** For full 7 marks, students must give **at least 3 examples each** for static and dynamic, and **at least 4 importance points**. Diagrams of human dimensions are highly valued — draw a stick figure with marked dimensions.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:**
- Floor-to-floor height (vertical rise) = 3.6 m = **3600 mm**
- Building type: **Public** → use Blondel formula with constant 600 mm.

**Step 1: Select riser height (R) within permissible range.**

For public buildings, NBC recommends $R = 150$ mm.

$$
R = 150 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate number of risers.**

$$
n = \frac{\text{Vertical Rise}}{R} = \frac{3600}{150} = 24
$$

**Step 3: Apply Blondel formula to compute tread width (T).**

$$
2R + T = 600 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
T = 600 - 2(150) = 600 - 300 = 300 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4: Verification of Blondel formula.**

$$
2R + T = 2(150) + 300 = 300 + 300 = 600 \text{ mm} \checkmark
$$

**Step 5: Calculate number of treads.**

For a flight of stairs, **number of treads = number of risers − 1** (because the top riser lands on the next floor):

$$
\text{Number of treads} = n - 1 = 24 - 1 = 23
$$

**Step 6: Calculate total going length (horizontal run of one flight).**

$$
\text{Going length} = 23 \times T = 23 \times 300 = 6900 \text{ mm} = 6.9 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 7: Check feasibility.** A single flight of 6.9 m is too long. Therefore, the stair must be split into **two flights** with a landing in between.

**Step 8: Final design summary.**

| Parameter | Calculated Value |
|---|---|
| Number of risers | **24** (12 per flight) |
| Riser height (R) | **150 mm** |
| Tread width (T) | **300 mm** |
| Treads per flight | **11** |
| Going per flight | 11 × 300 = **3300 mm = 3.3 m** |
| Landing width | **1200 mm** (≥ tread width) |
| Stair width | **1500 mm** (public) |
| Headroom | **2100 mm** |
| Blondel verification | **2(150) + 300 = 600 mm ✓** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Full-Marks Tip (7 marks):** Students must show the **assumption of riser height**, the **formula derivation**, the **verification step**, and the **practical split into two flights**. Examiners also reward stating the **headroom (2.1 m)** and **landing width (1.2 m)** values.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023]** **(a)** Explain **Edward T. Hall's proxemics** and discuss how the four proxemic zones influence **room sizes in residential and public buildings**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Draw a **bubble (circulation) diagram** for a **2BHK residential house** with the following rooms: Drawing room, Dining room, Kitchen, Master Bedroom, Children's Bedroom, 2 Bathrooms, Store, Verandah, and Entrance Porch. Explain the **adjacency logic** used. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Edward T. Hall's Proxemics:**

Proxemics is the study of human use of space and the effects of population density on behaviour, communication, and social interaction. Anthropologist Edward T. Hall (1966) defined four concentric zones of interpersonal distance that govern how people relate to one another spatially. These zones are a **direct input to room sizing** in architecture.

**The Four Proxemic Zones:**

1. **Intimate Distance (0 – 450 mm):**
   - Reserved for closest relationships — embracing, whispering, sleeping.
   - **Spatial implication:** Bed dimensions, bathroom enclosures, private seating.
   - Example: A double bed (1500 mm wide) allows two persons to lie within intimate distance.

2. **Personal Distance (450 mm – 1.2 m):**
   - For interactions among friends and family.
   - **Spatial implication:** Dining table seating, conversation seating clusters, kitchen work triangles.
   - Example: Chairs around a dining table are spaced ~600 mm apart, keeping occupants in personal zone.

3. **Social Distance (1.2 m – 3.6 m):**
   - For acquaintances, colleagues, formal interactions.
   - **Spatial implication:** Living room, office desk distance, conference seating.
   - Example: A desk is placed 1.5–2.0 m from a visitor's chair to maintain social distance.

4. **Public Distance (> 3.6 m):**
   - For public speaking, lectures, performances.
   - **Spatial implication:** Auditorium, lecture hall, public plaza, stage-to-audience distance.
   - Example: A speaker on stage is 4–6 m from the front row of audience.

**Influence on Room Sizes:**

| Zone | Typical Room Type | Typical Room Size |
|---|---|---|
| Intimate | Bedroom, WC | 9.5 – 12 m² |
| Personal | Dining, Kitchen | 8 – 12 m² |
| Social | Living, Office | 12 – 20 m² |
| Public | Auditorium, Hall | 50 – 500 m² |

> [!NOTE]
> **Cultural Note (for KTU):** Indian cultural norms are slightly closer than Western norms — families interact more within "personal" zone (1.2 m or less). This is why Indian living rooms are often more compact and densely furnished.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Bubble Diagram for 2BHK House:**

```
                    [VERANDAH]
                        |
                   [ENTRANCE PORCH]
                        |
                    [FOYER]
                    /         \
            [DRAWING]      [DINING]
              |    \         /  |
              |     \       /   |
              |      [KITCHEN]  |
              |         |       |
              |       [STORE]   |
              |                 |
        [M.BEDROOM]      [CHILDREN BEDROOM]
          /     \             /      \
   [BATH 1]  [WALK-IN]    [BATH 2]  [BALCONY]
```

**Adjacency Logic:**

1. **Entrance Porch → Foyer → Drawing/Dining:** Linear access from public to semi-public zone.
2. **Kitchen adjacent to Dining:** Direct serving access (food → table) — strong functional link.
3. **Kitchen adjacent to Store:** Pantry/store should open off kitchen for ingredient access.
4. **Master Bedroom + Bath 1 in private zone:** Both bedrooms are in the back/far end of house (away from street noise) — privacy gradient.
5. **Children's Bedroom + Bath 2 grouped:** Logical family-zone clustering.
6. **Drawing room in front:** Faces the entrance and is the most "public" space.
7. **Verandah as buffer:** Separates outdoor from indoor at entrance.
8. **Bathrooms NOT adjacent to Kitchen or Dining:** Avoid hygiene-functional conflict (NBC clause).

**Bubble Diagram Principles Applied:**

- **Adjacency:** Kitchen ↔ Dining (strong); Bath 1 ↔ Master Bedroom (strong).
- **Separation:** Kitchen ↔ Bathrooms (weak/no direct connection).
- **Privacy gradient:** Public (Drawing) → Semi-public (Dining, Kitchen) → Private (Bedrooms).
- **Circulation:** All rooms accessible from a central foyer without long corridors (efficient for small plot).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall (Examiner's Warning):** Students often forget to:
> 1. **Show the entrance separately** from the verandah.
> 2. **Place bathrooms away from kitchen/dining** (NBC violation if not).
> 3. **Indicate privacy zoning** (public-private gradient).
> 4. **Add directional arrows** for primary movement.
> 5. **Label each bubble clearly** with the room name.
> These omissions typically cost 2–3 marks out of 7.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **TOP 5 MARKS-LOSING MISTAKES IN MODULE 4:**
> 1. **Forgetting the safety buffer (δ):** Many students compute corridor width as just the body breadth (450 mm) instead of 1100 mm. The buffer is non-negotiable.
> 2. **Confusing P5 and P95 applications:** Using P5 for door height (wrong — door height needs P95 clearance). Use P5 for reach, P95 for overhead clearance.
> 3. **Wrong sign in stair formula:** Some students write $T = 600 + 2R$ instead of $T = 600 - 2R$. This inverts the geometry.
> 4. **Not splitting long flights:** A 6.9 m going is impractical; stairs must be split into flights with landings (max 16 risers per flight per NBC).
> 5. **Bubble diagram without logic:** Drawing bubbles without showing adjacency reasoning loses the 4 marks allocated for "explanation" in the question.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST — MODULE 4**

- **Anthropometrics** = science of human body measurements; two types: **static (fixed posture)** and **dynamic (in motion)**.
- Design range: **5th to 95th percentile** covers 90% of population.
- **P5** for reach-related dimensions (switches, lower shelves); **P95** for clearances (doors, ceilings).
- Standard Indian adult: **H = 1700 mm**, eye level = **1650 mm**, shoulder = **450 mm**, sitting height = **900 mm**.
- **5 Human Functions:** Resting, Working, Movement, Social, Hygiene.
- **Conversation distances (Hall):** Intimate < 0.45 m, Personal 0.45–1.2 m, Social 1.2–3.6 m, Public > 3.6 m.
- **Stair Blondel Formula:** $2R + T = 600$ mm (residential) or $630$ mm (public).
- **Standard residential stair:** R = 150 mm, T = 300 mm.
- **Stair constraints:** R ∈ [150, 190] mm, T ∈ [220, 300] mm, headroom = 2.1 m, width = 1.0 m (residential) / 1.5 m (public).
- **Corridor widths:** 1.1 m (single), 1.8 m (double), 2.4 m (with wheelchair passage).
- **Door dimensions:** 0.9 m × 2.1 m (residential main), 0.75 m × 2.0 m (bathroom).
- **5 Circulation patterns:** Linear, Radial, Loop, Grid, Cluster.
- **Reach zones:** Floor–600 mm low, 600–1200 mm work, 1200–1800 mm display, > 1800 mm overhead.
- **Bubble diagram rules:** Show adjacency, separation, privacy gradient, and primary movement arrows.
- **Built space = Functional + Circulation + Buffer** — never design at exact body dimension.
- **Wheelchair turning radius = 1500 mm diameter** — critical for universal design.
- **NBC 2016 is the Indian code of reference** for all anthropometric dimensions used in KTU problems.
- **Edge effect:** leave 300–450 mm wall buffer; people avoid walking flush against walls.
- **Bedroom minimum (NBC):** 9.5 m²; **Kitchen minimum:** 6 m²; **Bathroom minimum:** 2.5 m².
- **Switch board height:** 1.2 m; **Window sill height:** 0.9–1.0 m; **Counter height:** 0.85–0.90 m.
- **Formula to remember:** $\text{Tread} = 600 - 2R$ and $\text{Corridor width} = B + 2\delta$.
- **Special interpretations:** Modular coordination, reach zones, turning radius, edge effect, conversation circles.
- **Activity-space matrix:** Every activity → posture → body dimension → space area (master table for any 7-mark question).

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Strategy:** For 14-mark questions, always structure your answer as:
> 1. **Definition / Given data** (2 marks)
> 2. **Standard values / Codes referred** (2 marks)
> 3. **Calculation with steps** (5 marks)
> 4. **Diagram / Sketch** (3 marks)
> 5. **Conclusion / Verification** (2 marks)
> This pattern matches KTU's marking scheme precisely.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Efficiency in Buildings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET834 - FUNDAMENTALS OF BUILDING PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Energy Efficiency in Buildings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Efficiency in Buildings** is defined as the strategic reduction of energy consumed by a building's operational systems — including heating, ventilation, air-conditioning (HVAC), lighting, and electrical appliances — while simultaneously maintaining or improving thermal comfort, indoor air quality, illumination levels, and overall occupant well-being.

It is quantified through the **Energy Use Intensity (EUI)** metric, expressed in **kWh/m²/year**, which measures the energy consumed per unit built-up area annually. Lower EUI values indicate superior energy performance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Energy efficiency is no longer optional — it is mandated by the **Energy Conservation Building Code (ECBC)** and **Eco Niwas Samhita (ENS)** in India. The Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE) administers star ratings (1-Star to 5-Star) based on the EPI (Energy Performance Index).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Building as a Thermos Flask

Imagine your home is a **thermos flask** sitting in a hot Kerala afternoon:

| Component | Thermos Analogy | Building Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| Outer reflective coating | Reflects solar radiation | Cool roof / light-colored exterior |
| Vacuum gap between walls | Stops conduction | Insulation (rock wool, EPS, AAC) |
| Tight lid seal | Prevents air leakage | Air-tight windows & doors |
| Inner reflective lining | Reduces radiant heat gain | Low-emissivity (Low-E) glass |

Just as a thermos keeps chai hot for hours by **blocking all three modes of heat transfer** — conduction, convection, and radiation — an energy-efficient building does the same for indoor thermal comfort. The goal is to **let less energy in** (during summer) or **let less energy out** (during winter), so the HVAC system works minimally.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following constants and metrics are **exam-critical** and must be memorized:

- **Thermal conductivity (k)** of air ≈ **0.025 W/m·K**
- **Specific heat capacity of air (cₚ)** = **1.005 kJ/kg·K**
- **Density of air (ρ)** = **1.2 kg/m³** at standard conditions
- **Solar constant** ≈ **1367 W/m²** (extraterrestrial)
- **U-value (thermal transmittance)** measured in **W/m²·K**
- **R-value (thermal resistance)** measured in **m²·K/W**
- **SHGC (Solar Heat Gain Coefficient)** = dimensionless ratio (0 to 1)
- **LPD (Lighting Power Density)** measured in **W/m²**
- **ACH (Air Changes per Hour)** — ventilation metric

## 1.4 Why Energy Efficiency Matters — The Kerala Context

Kerala receives an average solar insolation of **5–6 kWh/m²/day** and has persistently high humidity (70–95%). This makes cooling loads dominant for most of the year. Buildings account for approximately **33% of India's total electricity consumption**, making energy-efficient design a national priority.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Three Pillars of Energy Efficiency in Buildings

### Pillar 1: Building Envelope Design (Passive Strategy)
The building envelope — walls, roof, windows, doors, and foundation — is the **first line of defense** against heat exchange. Optimizing the envelope reduces cooling/heating loads by **40–60%** before any mechanical system is switched on.

- **Orientation**: Long axis oriented East-West minimizes east/west wall solar gain. North-south orientation of longer facades is ideal in tropical climates.
- **Wall Construction**: Cavity walls, insulated concrete forms (ICF), and autoclaved aerated concrete (AAC) blocks drastically improve thermal performance.
- **Roofing**: Cool roofs with high **Solar Reflectance Index (SRI ≥ 78)** reflect solar radiation.
- **Glazing**: Double-glazed units (DGU) with Low-E coating and argon gas fill reduce heat gain.

### Pillar 2: Passive Design Strategies (Climate-Responsive)
These leverage natural energy flows without mechanical input:

- **Cross-ventilation**: Wind-induced cooling when wind velocity > 1 m/s.
- **Stack effect / Venturi effect**: Hot air rises and escapes through high openings; cool air enters from below.
- **Shading devices**: Chajjas, louvers, and vegetation reduce direct solar gain on windows by **60–80%**.
- **Daylight harvesting**: Reducing artificial lighting by maximizing natural light penetration.
- **Thermal mass**: Materials like concrete absorb heat during the day and release it at night, dampening temperature swings.

### Pillar 3: Active Systems & Efficient Equipment
- **HVAC**: Use BEE 5-star rated air-conditioners with **Inverter Technology** (COP > 3.5).
- **Lighting**: Replace incandescent/CFL with LEDs (efficacy 100+ lm/W vs 15 lm/W for incandescent).
- **Renewable Integration**: Rooftop solar PV (typically 1 kW per 100 sq.ft for Kerala).
- **Smart controls**: Occupancy sensors, daylight sensors, and Building Management Systems (BMS).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R = \dfrac{d}{k}$ | Thermal resistance of a homogeneous material layer | m²·K/W |
| 2 | $\dfrac{1}{U} = R_{si} + \sum \dfrac{d_i}{k_i} + R_{se}$ | Overall thermal transmittance (composite wall) | W/m²·K |
| 3 | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat transfer through a building element | W (watts) |
| 4 | $Q_{vent} = \rho \cdot V \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible heat gain due to ventilation | W |
| 5 | $Q_{inf} = 0.5 \cdot ACH \cdot V \cdot \Delta T \cdot \rho \cdot c_p$ | Heat gain due to infiltration | W |
| 6 | $SHGC = \tau + \alpha_i \cdot \dfrac{U}{h_o}$ | Solar Heat Gain Coefficient (simplified) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $EUI = \dfrac{E_{annual}}{A_{floor}}$ | Energy Use Intensity | kWh/m²/year |
| 8 | $COP = \dfrac{Q_{cooling}}{W_{input}}$ | Coefficient of Performance of AC | dimensionless |
| 9 | $LPD = \dfrac{W_{total}}{A_{floor}}$ | Lighting Power Density | W/m² |
| 10 | $\eta_{solar} = \dfrac{E_{generated}}{E_{incident}} \times 100\%$ | Solar PV efficiency | % |
| 11 | $Q_{total} = Q_{wall} + Q_{roof} + Q_{glass} + Q_{vent} + Q_{inf} + Q_{internal}$ | Total cooling/heating load | W |
| 12 | $t_{d} = \dfrac{\text{Time lag} \cdot 2\pi}{24}$ | Thermal lag / decrement factor relation | hours |

> [!NOTE]
> **Where:**
> - $d$ = thickness of material (m)
> - $k$ = thermal conductivity (W/m·K)
> - $R_{si}, R_{se}$ = internal and external surface resistances
> - $\Delta T$ = temperature difference (K or °C)
> - $\tau$ = transmittance of glass, $\alpha_i$ = internal absorptance
> - $h_o$ = external surface film coefficient

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Architectural firms** use these formulas during the conceptual design stage to evaluate envelope options.
- **MEP (Mechanical, Electrical, Plumbing) consultants** compute cooling loads using Carrier's HAP or HERS (Home Energy Rating System) software, all of which are built on these fundamental equations.
- **BEE Star Rating** assessments in India rely on the EPI, which is a direct application of EUI.
- **Green building certifications** (IGBC, GRIHA, LEED) award points based on documented energy performance metrics derived from these equations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Composite Wall U-Value Calculation

**Problem Statement:** A 230 mm brick wall is plastered on both sides with 12 mm cement plaster. Compute the overall U-value and the heat transfer rate per m² for a temperature difference of 15 K. Use standard material properties.

### Step 1: Identify the layers and material properties

| Layer | Material | Thickness $d$ (m) | Conductivity $k$ (W/m·K) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | External surface film (R_se) | — | — |
| 2 | Cement plaster (outer) | 0.012 | 0.72 |
| 3 | Brick wall | 0.230 | 0.81 |
| 4 | Cement plaster (inner) | 0.012 | 0.72 |
| 5 | Internal surface film (R_si) | — | — |

**Standard surface resistances:**
- $R_{si} = 0.13 \; m^2 \cdot K/W$ (internal still air)
- $R_{se} = 0.04 \; m^2 \cdot K/W$ (external wind-exposed)

### Step 2: Calculate the resistance of each homogeneous layer

The fundamental thermal resistance formula:

$$R = \frac{d}{k}$$

Now compute each layer's R-value:

**Step 2a — Outer plaster layer resistance:**

$$R_{p1} = \frac{d_1}{k_1} = \frac{0.012}{0.72} = 0.01667 \; m^2 \cdot K/W$$

**Step 2b — Brick wall resistance (this is the dominant layer):**

$$R_{brick} = \frac{d_2}{k_2} = \frac{0.230}{0.81} = 0.28395 \; m^2 \cdot K/W$$

**Step 2c — Inner plaster layer resistance:**

$$R_{p2} = \frac{d_3}{k_3} = \frac{0.012}{0.72} = 0.01667 \; m^2 \cdot K/W$$

### Step 3: Sum all resistances in series

Heat flows through a composite wall in **series** (one layer after another), so total resistance is the arithmetic sum:

$$\frac{1}{U} = R_{si} + R_{p1} + R_{brick} + R_{p2} + R_{se}$$

Substituting numerical values:

$$\frac{1}{U} = 0.13 + 0.01667 + 0.28395 + 0.01667 + 0.04$$

$$\frac{1}{U} = 0.48729 \; m^2 \cdot K/W$$

### Step 4: Invert to find U-value

$$U = \frac{1}{0.48729}$$

$$U = 2.052 \; W/m^2 \cdot K$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result Interpretation:** A U-value of **2.05 W/m²·K** is considered **poor thermal performance**. ECBC mandates $U \leq 0.4 \; W/m^2 \cdot K$ for walls in the composite climate zone of India. Adding just 50 mm of EPS insulation (k = 0.038) would reduce U-value to approximately **0.63 W/m²·K** — a **3× improvement**.

### Step 5: Calculate heat transfer per m²

$$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$$

$$Q = 2.052 \times 1 \times 15$$

$$Q = 30.78 \; W/m^2$$

This means **30.78 watts of heat** enter every square metre of wall for each 15 K temperature difference — purely through conduction.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Sensible Cooling Load from Ventilation

**Problem Statement:** A 5 m × 4 m × 3 m room (volume = 60 m³) in Trivandrum requires 1.5 ACH of fresh air. Outdoor temperature is 35°C and indoor design temperature is 24°C. Compute the sensible heat gain due to ventilation.

### Step 1: Compute mass flow rate of ventilation air

$$\dot{V} = \text{ACH} \times \frac{V_{room}}{3600}$$

Here, $V_{room}$ is the volume of the room in m³.

$$\dot{V} = 1.5 \times \frac{60}{3600}$$

$$\dot{V} = 0.025 \; m^3/s$$

### Step 2: Compute mass flow rate

$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot \dot{V}$$

$$\dot{m} = 1.2 \times 0.025$$

$$\dot{m} = 0.030 \; kg/s$$

### Step 3: Apply sensible heat formula

$$Q_{vent} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$$

$$Q_{vent} = 0.030 \times 1005 \times (35 - 24)$$

$$Q_{vent} = 0.030 \times 1005 \times 11$$

$$Q_{vent} = 331.65 \; W$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Insight:** Heat recovery ventilators (HRV) or energy recovery ventilators (ERV) can reclaim **60–80%** of this energy, reducing $Q_{vent}$ to approximately **66–132 W**.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Building Material Performance

| Material | $k$ (W/m·K) | R-value for 230 mm (m²·K/W) | U-value (W/m²·K) | Suitability |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Solid concrete | 1.40 | 0.164 | 3.20 | Poor |
| Brick (common) | 0.81 | 0.284 | 2.05 | Poor |
| AAC block | 0.16 | 1.438 | 0.62 | Good |
| Hollow concrete block | 0.50 | 0.460 | 1.40 | Fair |
| Brick + 50 mm EPS | varies | ~0.85 | 0.63 | Good |
| Straw bale wall | 0.05 | 4.600 | 0.21 | Excellent |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Energy Flow Architecture in an Energy-Efficient Building

The following block diagram illustrates how energy flows through a building and where interventions are made to maximize efficiency:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Energy Sources] --> B[Solar Radiation]
    A --> C[Outdoor Air Temperature]
    A --> D[Wind]
    B --> E[Building Envelope]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F[Internal Heat Gain Sources]
    F --> G[Occupants and Appliances]
    F --> H[Lighting Fixtures]
    G --> I[HVAC System]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Thermal Comfort Zone 22 to 26 C]
    E --> J
    E --> K[Heat Loss or Gain Path]
    K --> L[Passive Design Layer]
    L --> M[Wall Insulation]
    L --> N[Roof Insulation]
    L --> O[Window Glazing]
    L --> P[Air Sealing]
    M --> J
    N --> J
    O --> J
    P --> J
    I --> Q[Energy Output kWh per year]
    Q --> R[EUI Calculation]
    R --> S[BEE Star Rating]
    style A fill:#1e90ff,color:#ffffff
    style J fill:#90ee90,color:#000000
    style S fill:#ffd700,color:#000000
    style L fill:#ff8c00,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Passive Design Strategy Decision Tree

This flowchart helps designers select appropriate passive strategies based on climate zone:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Start Climate Analysis] --> tz{Tropical Climate}
    tz -->|Yes| humid{High Humidity Greater than 70 percent}
    tz -->|No| dry{Arid or Cold Climate}
    humid --> vent[Prioritize Cross Ventilation]
    humid --> shade[Provide Deep Overhangs and Chajjas]
    humid --> mass[Use Moderate Thermal Mass]
    humid --> cool[Adopt Cool Roof Technology]
    vent --> result[Reduced Cooling Load by 30 to 50 percent]
    shade --> result
    mass --> result
    cool --> result
    dry --> mass2[Use High Thermal Mass]
    dry --> insul[Heavy Insulation Required]
    mass2 --> result2[Stable Indoor Temperature]
    insul --> result2
    style start fill:#4682b4,color:#ffffff
    style humid fill:#87ceeb,color:#000000
    style result fill:#32cd32,color:#ffffff
    style result2 fill:#ff8c00,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.3 Energy Audit Process Block Diagram

A standard walk-through energy audit follows this structured methodology:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Step 1 Data Collection] --> P2[Step 2 Utility Bill Analysis]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3 On Site Inspection]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4 Identify Energy Waste Points]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5 Quantify Losses in kWh]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6 Recommend Retrofits]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7 Cost Benefit Analysis]
    P7 --> P8[Step 8 Implementation Report]
```
