<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define the term "Energy Use Intensity" (EUI) and state its significance in evaluating energy efficiency of buildings.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Energy Use Intensity (EUI)** is defined as the total annual energy consumed by a building divided by its total built-up floor area.

$$EUI = \frac{E_{annual}}{A_{floor}} \quad \text{[units: kWh/m}^2/\text{year]}$$

**Significance:**
- It normalizes energy consumption across buildings of different sizes, enabling fair comparison.
- Lower EUI indicates better energy performance.
- It is the **primary metric** used by BEE for star rating and by ECBC for compliance.
- Typical benchmarks: **Residential ≤ 50 kWh/m²/yr (5-Star)**, **Commercial ≤ 90 kWh/m²/yr (ECBC compliant)**.

*(Word count: ~75; 3-mark answer)*

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Differentiate between U-value and R-value of a building material.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | U-value (Thermal Transmittance) | R-value (Thermal Resistance) |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Rate of heat flow through unit area per unit temperature difference | Resistance offered by a material to heat flow |
| **Units** | W/m²·K | m²·K/W |
| **Formula** | $U = \dfrac{Q}{A \cdot \Delta T}$ | $R = \dfrac{d}{k}$ |
| **Interpretation** | **Lower is better** (less heat passes) | **Higher is better** (more resistance) |
| **Relationship** | $U = \dfrac{1}{R}$ | $R = \dfrac{1}{U}$ |

*(3-mark table-based answer)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the various **passive design strategies** for energy efficiency in tropical buildings like those in Kerala. Discuss orientation, ventilation, shading, and thermal mass.

**Model Answer:**

**1. Building Orientation (2 marks):**
- Long axis oriented **East-West** minimizes east and west wall exposure to harsh low-angle morning and afternoon sun.
- North-south orientation of longer facades is preferred.
- This single design decision can reduce cooling load by **10–15%**.

**2. Natural Ventilation (2 marks):**
- **Cross-ventilation** achieved by placing openings on opposite walls.
- **Stack effect** uses vertical openings (low intake, high exhaust) for buoyancy-driven cooling.
- **Venturi effect** narrows passages to accelerate wind speed.
- Effective when outdoor wind speed > 1 m/s and indoor-outdoor temperature difference exists.

**3. Shading Devices (2 marks):**
- Horizontal chajjas (overhangs) on south-facing windows block high summer sun.
- Vertical fins on east/west windows block low-angle morning/evening sun.
- **Vegetation shading** by trees reduces surface temperature by **5–10°C**.
- Combined shading can reduce solar heat gain through windows by **60–80%**.

**4. Thermal Mass (1 mark):**
- Materials like concrete, brick, and AAC absorb heat during day and release at night.
- Stabilizes indoor temperature fluctuations.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A composite wall consists of the following layers (outside to inside):
- 12 mm cement plaster ($k = 0.72$ W/m·K)
- 230 mm brick wall ($k = 0.81$ W/m·K)
- 50 mm expanded polystyrene (EPS) insulation ($k = 0.038$ W/m·K)
- 12 mm gypsum plaster ($k = 0.35$ W/m·K)

Take $R_{si} = 0.13$ and $R_{se} = 0.04$ m²·K/W. Calculate:
(i) The overall U-value.
(ii) Heat transfer per m² for $\Delta T = 12$ K.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Calculate resistance of each layer**

$$R_{cement} = \frac{0.012}{0.72} = 0.0167 \; m^2 \cdot K/W$$

$$R_{brick} = \frac{0.230}{0.81} = 0.2839 \; m^2 \cdot K/W$$

$$R_{EPS} = \frac{0.050}{0.038} = 1.3158 \; m^2 \cdot K/W$$

$$R_{gypsum} = \frac{0.012}{0.35} = 0.0343 \; m^2 \cdot K/W$$

**Step 2: Sum total resistance**

$$\frac{1}{U} = R_{si} + R_{cement} + R_{brick} + R_{EPS} + R_{gypsum} + R_{se}$$

$$\frac{1}{U} = 0.13 + 0.0167 + 0.2839 + 1.3158 + 0.0343 + 0.04$$

$$\frac{1}{U} = 1.8207 \; m^2 \cdot K/W$$

**Step 3: Invert for U-value**

$$U = \frac{1}{1.8207} = 0.549 \; W/m^2 \cdot K$$

**(i) U = 0.549 W/m²·K** — This meets ECBC requirements for tropical climate zones.

**Step 4: Calculate heat transfer per m²**

$$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$$

$$Q = 0.549 \times 1 \times 12 = 6.59 \; W/m^2$$

**(ii) Q = 6.59 W/m²** — A reduction of nearly **79%** compared to the uninsulated wall (30.78 W/m² from earlier derivation).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is a **cool roof**? Explain its construction, working principle, and benefits with reference to the Indian climatic context.

**Model Answer:**

> A **cool roof** is a roofing system designed to reflect more sunlight and absorb less heat than a standard roof, achieving lower roof surface temperature.

**Construction (3 marks):**
- **Reflective coating**: White elastomeric or acrylic paint with high solar reflectance.
- **Reflective tiles/shingles**: Ceramic, concrete, or metal tiles with light colors.
- **Green roof**: Vegetation layer over waterproofing membrane.
- **Built-up cool roof**: Multiple layers including insulation board, waterproofing, and reflective top layer.

**Working Principle (2 marks):**
- High **Solar Reflectance (SR ≥ 0.6)** — reflects 60%+ of incident solar radiation.
- High **Thermal Emittance (ε ≥ 0.85)** — re-emits absorbed heat as infrared radiation.
- Combined metric: **Solar Reflectance Index (SRI)** — higher SRI = cooler roof.

**Benefits (2 marks):**
- Reduces roof surface temperature by **20–30°C**.
- Reduces indoor temperature by **2–5°C**, cutting AC energy by **15–25%**.
- Extends roof life by reducing thermal cycling stress.
- Mitigates urban heat island effect.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A residential building has the following annual energy consumption data:
- Lighting: 1,800 kWh
- Fans: 1,200 kWh
- Air-conditioning: 4,500 kWh
- Other appliances: 1,500 kWh

Total built-up area = 120 m². Calculate the **EUI** and determine the **BEE star rating bracket**.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Total annual energy consumption**

$$E_{total} = 1800 + 1200 + 4500 + 1500 = 9000 \; kWh$$

**Step 2: Calculate EUI**

$$EUI = \frac{E_{total}}{A_{floor}}$$

$$EUI = \frac{9000}{120}$$

$$EUI = 75 \; kWh/m^2/\text{year}$$

**Step 3: BEE Star Rating Reference (Residential)**

| EUI Range (kWh/m²/yr) | BEE Star Rating |
|---|---|
| ≤ 50 | 5-Star |
| 51–65 | 4-Star |
| 66–80 | 3-Star |
| 81–95 | 2-Star |
| 96–110 | 1-Star |
| > 110 | Not Rated |

**Conclusion:** EUI = 75 kWh/m²/year falls in the **66–80 bracket**, so the building qualifies for **BEE 3-Star Rating**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Most Common Mistake):**
> - **Unit confusion**: Forgetting to convert area from ft² to m². Always verify units — 1 m² = 10.76 ft².
> - **Wrong sign in ΔT**: Temperature difference must always be **positive**; use $|T_{out} - T_{in}|$ or write explicitly as $\Delta T = T_{hot} - T_{cold}$.
> - **Omitting surface resistances**: A wall's U-value **must include** $R_{si}$ and $R_{se}$ for accuracy.
> - **Treating U and R as the same**: Many students confuse them; remember $U = 1/R_{total}$.
> - **BEE table values**: Always quote the table values from current BEE guidelines; outdated values lose marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Energy Efficiency in Buildings**

- **Definition**: Reducing energy use while maintaining comfort; quantified by EUI.
- **EUI** = Annual Energy / Floor Area (kWh/m²/year).
- **U-value**: Lower is better; units W/m²·K.
- **R-value**: Higher is better; units m²·K/W; $R = d/k$.
- **Composite wall**: $1/U = R_{si} + \sum(d_i/k_i) + R_{se}$.
- **Heat transfer**: $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$.
- **Ventilation load**: $Q_{vent} = \rho \cdot V \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$.
- **Cool roof**: SR ≥ 0.6, ε ≥ 0.85, SRI high.
- **Passive strategies**: Orientation (E-W axis), cross-ventilation, shading, thermal mass.
- **Active systems**: BEE 5-star AC, LED lighting, solar PV, BMS.
- **Standards**: ECBC (commercial), ENS (residential), BEE star ratings.
- **Insulation materials**: EPS ($k = 0.038$), rock wool ($k = 0.04$), AAC ($k = 0.16$).
- **Surface resistances**: $R_{si} = 0.13$, $R_{se} = 0.04$ m²·K/W (standard still air).
- **Target U-value** (wall, ECBC tropical): $U \leq 0.4$ W/m²·K.
- **Golden formula trio**: $R = d/k$, $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$, $EUI = E/A$.
- **Kerala-specific note**: High humidity → prioritize ventilation + dehumidification; avoid heavy thermal mass without night flushing.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Assessment in Buildings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET834 - FUNDAMENTALS OF BUILDING PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Energy Assessment in Buildings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Energy Assessment in Buildings** is the systematic, scientific, and engineering process of evaluating, quantifying, and analyzing the energy consumption, energy performance, and thermal behavior of a building envelope, systems, and services. It establishes a baseline of energy use, identifies avenues for conservation, and recommends design/retrofit interventions to optimize energy efficiency while maintaining occupant comfort, indoor environmental quality, and functional performance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition Highlight:**
> Energy assessment in buildings refers to the measurement, verification, and analysis of energy flows in a building (heating, cooling, lighting, ventilation, and electrical loads) to evaluate conformance against benchmarks such as the **Energy Conservation Building Code (ECBC)** and rating systems like **GRIHA, LEED-India, and BEE Star Rating**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Body Metaphor

Imagine a building as a **living human body**:
- The **building envelope** (walls, roof, windows, floor) behaves like **skin and clothing** — it regulates heat exchange with the environment.
- The **HVAC system** acts like the **lungs and circulatory system** — it breathes in fresh air and pumps conditioned air.
- The **electrical lighting and appliances** are the **digestive system** — they consume "food" (electricity) to perform work.
- An **energy audit** is essentially a **medical health check-up** — doctors (energy auditors) measure vital signs (energy use, U-values, OTTV, lighting power density) to diagnose the "health" of the building and prescribe treatments (retrofit measures).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:** Just as a doctor uses stethoscopes and blood tests, an energy auditor uses instruments like **Blower Door Tests, Infrared Thermography, Lux Meters, and Data Loggers** to quantify hidden energy losses in buildings.

## 1.3 Purpose and Objectives of Energy Assessment

The core objectives can be summarized under the **5-E Framework**:

| Letter | Objective | Engineering Significance |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| **E1** | **Evaluate** Baseline Energy Use | Establishes the existing EPI (Energy Performance Index) in **kWh/m²/year** |
| **E2** | **Examine** Building Envelope | Quantifies OTTV, U-values, SHGC, and air leakage rates |
| **E3** | **Enforce** Code Compliance | Verifies adherence to **ECBC 2017 / ECBC+ 2024** prescriptive requirements |
| **E4** | **Engineer** Retrofit Solutions | Recommends cost-effective ECMs (Energy Conservation Measures) |
| **E5** | **Enhance** Occupant Comfort | Ensures thermal, visual, and indoor air quality benchmarks are met |

## 1.4 Scope of Energy Assessment

The assessment covers four primary energy domains in buildings:

1. **Building Envelope Energy** — heat conduction, radiation, and infiltration through walls, roof, glazing, and floor.
2. **HVAC Energy** — energy consumed by heating, ventilation, and air-conditioning systems.
3. **Lighting Energy** — energy consumed by artificial illumination systems.
4. **Plug and Process Loads** — energy consumed by equipment, appliances, and machinery.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants Used in Energy Assessment:**
> - **Solar Constant (I₀)** = **1367 W/m²** (extra-terrestrial radiation outside the atmosphere)
> - **Stefan-Boltzmann Constant (σ)** = **5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²·K⁴**
> - **Thermal Conductivity of Air (k_air)** ≈ **0.026 W/m·K** at 300 K
> - **Standard Reference Temperature Difference (ΔT)** = **15 K** (as per ECBC for tropical Indian climate)

## 1.5 Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) of Building Energy

The following KPIs are universally adopted in KTU curriculum and ECBC:

- **EPI** — Energy Performance Index (**kWh/m²/year**)
- **OTTV** — Overall Thermal Transfer Value (**W/m²**)
- **RTTV** — Roof Thermal Transfer Value (**W/m²**)
- **LPD** — Lighting Power Density (**W/m²**)
- **U-value** — Thermal Transmittance (**W/m²·K**)
- **SHGC** — Solar Heat Gain Coefficient (dimensionless, 0 to 1)
- **ACH** — Air Changes per Hour (dimensionless)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework of Building Energy Assessment

The energy assessment methodology is grounded in the **First Law of Thermodynamics** (Conservation of Energy), applied to a building as a control volume. For any building, the energy balance can be expressed as:

**Energy In = Energy Stored + Energy Out + Energy Consumed**

In practical building energy terms:

$$Q_{solar} + Q_{internal} + Q_{HVAC} = Q_{conduction} + Q_{infiltration} + Q_{ventilation} + Q_{stored}$$

Where:
- $Q_{solar}$ = Heat gained from solar radiation through envelope
- $Q_{internal}$ = Internal heat gains from occupants, lights, and equipment
- $Q_{HVAC}$ = Energy supplied by HVAC system
- $Q_{conduction}$ = Heat transfer through building envelope
- $Q_{infiltration}$ = Heat loss/gain due to air leakage
- $Q_{ventilation}$ = Heat loss/gain due to mechanical ventilation
- $Q_{stored}$ = Heat stored in building thermal mass

## 2.2 Phases of Energy Assessment (Step-by-Step Logic)

The assessment follows a structured **6-phase protocol**:

### Phase 1: Walk-Through Audit (Preliminary)
- **Why?** Quick identification of glaring energy wastage.
- **How?** Visual inspection, occupant interviews, utility bill analysis.

### Phase 2: Detailed Energy Audit
- **Why?** Quantification of energy flows at component level.
- **How?** Instrumented measurements, on-site data logging for **7 to 30 days**.

### Phase 3: Energy Modeling & Simulation
- **Why?** Predictive analysis of design alternatives.
- **How?** Software tools like **EnergyPlus, eQuest, DesignBuilder, TRACE 700**.

### Phase 4: Benchmarking
- **Why?** Compare with similar buildings or standards.
- **How?** Use **CBECS, ECBC, BEE Star ratings** as benchmarks.

### Phase 5: Identification of ECMs (Energy Conservation Measures)
- **Why?** Recommend actionable retrofit options.
- **How?** Cost-benefit analysis using **Simple Payback Period** and **NPV**.

### Phase 6: Implementation & M&V (Measurement & Verification)
- **Why?** Validate actual savings against predictions.
- **How?** Follow **IPMVP (International Performance Measurement & Verification Protocol)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** This systematic methodology is used worldwide by energy auditors certified under **BEE (Bureau of Energy Efficiency)** in India, **LEED AP**, and **ASHRAE Building Energy Assessment Professional (BEAP)** programs.

## 2.3 The Concept of OTTV (Overall Thermal Transfer Value)

The **OTTV** is the single most critical metric in tropical building energy assessment. It quantifies the total heat gain through the building envelope per unit area of the external wall, accounting for conduction, radiation, and air infiltration.

**For Walls:**

$$OTTV = U_w \times (1 - WFR) \times \Delta T + U_w \times WFR \times \Delta T + SHGC \times I_{max}$$

Where:
- $U_w$ = Thermal transmittance of wall (**W/m²·K**)
- $WFR$ = Window-to-Wall Ratio (dimensionless, 0 to 1)
- $\Delta T$ = Temperature difference between outdoor and indoor (**K**)
- $SHGC$ = Solar Heat Gain Coefficient of glazing
- $I_{max}$ = Maximum solar radiation incident on wall (**W/m²**)

**For Roofs (RTTV):**

$$RTTV = U_r \times \Delta T + SHGC_{roof} \times I_{roof,max}$$

**ECBC Prescriptive Limit:** For compliance, **OTTV ≤ 50 W/m²** and **RTTV ≤ 30 W/m²** for the composite climate of India.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Unit | Application |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---|
| 1 | $Q = U \times A \times \Delta T$ | Conduction heat transfer | W | Wall/roof heat loss |
| 2 | $Q = m \times c_p \times \Delta T$ | Sensible heat from ventilation | W | Fresh air load |
| 3 | $Q = \dot{m} \times h_{fg}$ | Latent heat from ventilation | W | Humidity load |
| 4 | $Q = SHGC \times A \times I$ | Solar heat gain through glazing | W | Window heat gain |
| 5 | $R = \frac{L}{k \times A}$ | Thermal resistance | m²·K/W | Insulation design |
| 6 | $U = \frac{1}{R_{total}}$ | Overall heat transfer coefficient | W/m²·K | Composite wall |
| 7 | $OTTV \le 50$ W/m² | ECBC wall compliance | W/m² | Envelope check |
| 8 | $RTTV \le 30$ W/m² | ECBC roof compliance | W/m² | Roof check |
| 9 | $EPI = \frac{E_{annual}}{A_{floor}}$ | Energy Performance Index | kWh/m²/yr | Building rating |
| 10 | $LPD = \frac{P_{lights}}{A_{floor}}$ | Lighting Power Density | W/m² | Lighting design |
| 11 | $ACH = \frac{Q_{air}}{V_{room}}$ | Air Changes per Hour | 1/hr | Ventilation check |
| 12 | $S = \frac{Annual\ Savings}{Cost\ of\ ECM}$ | Simple Payback Period | Years | Investment decision |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Table Notation Rule:** In the formulas above, all vertical bars (for grouping) have been replaced with the LaTeX command `\vert` or removed entirely to preserve markdown table integrity. Always use parentheses or backslashes for absolute value notation in your answer sheets.

## 2.5 Energy Conservation Building Code (ECBC) Overview

The ECBC, launched by **BEE (Bureau of Energy Efficiency)** in **2007** and updated in **2017**, sets minimum energy performance standards for commercial buildings with a connected load of **≥ 100 kW** or **contract demand ≥ 600 kVA**.

> [!NOTE]
> **ECBC 2017 Compliance Pathways (Must Remember for KTU):**
> 1. **Prescriptive Path** — Meet fixed U-value, SHGC, OTTV, and LPD limits.
> 2. **Whole Building Performance Path** — Demonstrate that the proposed building consumes at least **25% less energy** than the ECBC-compliant baseline building.
> 3. **Trade-off Path** — Compensate one non-compliant element by over-performing another (e.g., higher wall insulation for poor glazing).

## 2.6 Star Rating Program for Buildings (BEE)

The BEE Star Rating Program rates buildings (operational, not design) on a **1 to 5 star** scale based on **EPI (Energy Performance Index)** in **kWh/m²/year**.

| Star Rating | EPI Range (Office Buildings) | Performance Category |
|:---:|:---:|:---|
| ⭐ 1 Star | 180 – 200 | Conventional Building |
| ⭐⭐ 2 Star | 150 – 180 | Below Average |
| ⭐⭐⭐ 3 Star | 120 – 150 | Average Compliant |
| ⭐⭐⭐⭐ 4 Star | 90 – 120 | Energy Efficient |
| ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ 5 Star | ≤ 90 | Highly Energy Efficient |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** In India, **GRIHA (Green Rating for Integrated Habitat Assessment)** is the national rating system with levels 1, 3, 5 (certified), 7 (silver), 9 (gold), and 10 (platinum). Internationally, **LEED (Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design)** by USGBC uses Certified, Silver, Gold, and Platinum tiers.

## 2.7 Passive Design Strategies (No-Energy-Cost Cooling/Heating)

These strategies are evaluated during energy assessment to maximize the use of natural forces:

1. **Building Orientation** — Long axis East-West to minimize wall solar exposure.
2. **Window Shading** — Overhangs, fins, and chajjas reduce direct solar gain.
3. **Building Form** — Compact shape reduces surface-to-volume ratio (heat loss).
4. **Thermal Mass** — High mass walls (brick, stone) dampen indoor temperature swings.
5. **Cross Ventilation** — Wind-driven natural ventilation reduces cooling load.
6. **Daylighting** — Skylights, light shelves reduce artificial lighting demand.
7. **Roof Insulation** — High-albedo paints, false ceilings, insulation boards reduce roof heat gain.
8. **Landscaping** — Trees, green roofs reduce ambient temperature by **2 – 4°C** via evapotranspiration.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of U-Value for a Composite Wall

### Problem Context
A composite wall consists of three layers: an outer **20 mm cement plaster** (k = 0.72 W/m·K), a middle **230 mm brick masonry** (k = 0.81 W/m·K), and an inner **15 mm gypsum plaster** (k = 0.17 W/m·K). Air films on both sides have surface resistances of **R_si = 0.13 m²·K/W** and **R_se = 0.04 m²·K/W**. Determine the **U-value** of the wall.

### Step-by-Step Analytical Solution

**Step 1: Identify the layers and their properties.**

The composite wall has the following structure (from outside to inside):

1. Outside air film with thermal resistance $R_{se}$ = **0.04 m²·K/W**
2. Cement plaster of thickness $L_1$ = **0.020 m** and conductivity $k_1$ = **0.72 W/m·K**
3. Brick masonry of thickness $L_2$ = **0.230 m** and conductivity $k_2$ = **0.81 W/m·K**
4. Gypsum plaster of thickness $L_3$ = **0.015 m** and conductivity $k_3$ = **0.17 W/m·K**
5. Inside air film with thermal resistance $R_{si}$ = **0.13 m²·K/W**

**Step 2: Apply the thermal resistance formula for each layer.**

For each solid layer, the thermal resistance is given by:

$$R_{layer} = \frac{L}{k}$$

**Step 3: Calculate the resistance of each solid layer individually.**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_1 &= \frac{L_1}{k_1} = \frac{0.020}{0.72} = 0.0278 \ \text{m}^2\cdot\text{K/W} \\
R_2 &= \frac{L_2}{k_2} = \frac{0.230}{0.81} = 0.2840 \ \text{m}^2\cdot\text{K/W} \\
R_3 &= \frac{L_3}{k_3} = \frac{0.015}{0.17} = 0.0882 \ \text{m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Sum the total thermal resistance.**

For series-arranged layers, the total resistance is the algebraic sum:

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{total} &= R_{si} + R_1 + R_2 + R_3 + R_{se} \\
R_{total} &= 0.13 + 0.0278 + 0.2840 + 0.0882 + 0.04 \\
R_{total} &= 0.5700 \ \text{m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Compute the U-value as the reciprocal of total resistance.**

$$U = \frac{1}{R_{total}} = \frac{1}{0.5700} = 1.754 \ \text{W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$$

**Step 6: Verify against ECBC prescriptive limits.**

The ECBC 2017 specifies a maximum U-value of **0.4 W/m²·K** for opaque walls in the composite climate zone. Since the calculated U-value (1.754 W/m²·K) far exceeds this limit, the wall is **NON-COMPLIANT** and requires an additional insulation layer.

**Step 7: Determine the required insulation thickness.**

To achieve $U_{target}$ = 0.4 W/m²·K:

$$R_{target} = \frac{1}{0.4} = 2.5 \ \text{m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{insulation} &= R_{target} - R_{current} = 2.5 - 0.5700 = 1.93 \ \text{m}^2\cdot\text{K/W} \\
L_{insulation} &= R_{insulation} \times k_{insulation} = 1.93 \times 0.04 = 0.0772 \ \text{m} \\
L_{insulation} &\approx 78 \ \text{mm of insulation (e.g., expanded polystyrene)}
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of OTTV for a Building Wall

### Problem Context
A west-facing wall of a commercial building in Chennai has the following properties:
- Gross wall area = 100 m²
- Glazing area (windows) = 30 m²
- WFR (Window-to-Wall Ratio) = 0.30
- U-value of opaque wall $U_w$ = 1.754 W/m²·K
- SHGC of single-glazed window = 0.65
- Maximum solar radiation on west wall $I_{max}$ = 600 W/m²
- $\Delta T$ (outdoor-indoor design) = 15 K

Determine the **OTTV** and check ECBC compliance.

### Step-by-Step Analytical Solution

**Step 1: Apply the OTTV formula for walls.**

$$OTTV = U_w \times (1 - WFR) \times \Delta T + U_w \times WFR \times \Delta T + SHGC \times I_{max}$$

**Step 2: Substitute the values into the formula.**

$$\begin{aligned}
OTTV &= 1.754 \times (1 - 0.30) \times 15 + 1.754 \times 0.30 \times 15 + 0.65 \times 600 \\
OTTV &= 1.754 \times 0.70 \times 15 + 1.754 \times 0.30 \times 15 + 390 \\
OTTV &= 18.42 + 7.89 + 390 \\
OTTV &= 416.31 \ \text{W/m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Interpret the result.**

The computed OTTV of **416.31 W/m²** is drastically higher than the ECBC 2017 limit of **50 W/m²**. This indicates the building envelope will impose an enormous cooling load and is not energy-efficient.

**Step 4: Recommend corrective measures.**

To reduce OTTV below 50 W/m², the following interventions are suggested:
- Replace single-glazed window with **double-glazed low-E glass** (SHGC < 0.25)
- Add **thermal insulation** in the wall cavity to reduce $U_w$ to **0.4 W/m²·K**
- Install **external shading devices** (chajjas/overhangs) to reduce incident solar radiation
- Reduce WFR to 0.20 by resizing windows

## 3.3 Derivation of Lighting Power Density (LPD)

### Problem Context
An office floor has an area of **500 m²** and uses **120 fluorescent luminaires**, each rated at **40 W**. Calculate the LPD and verify against ECBC limits.

### Step-by-Step Analytical Solution

**Step 1: Calculate the total connected lighting load.**

$$P_{total} = n \times P_{lamp} = 120 \times 40 = 4800 \ \text{W}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the Lighting Power Density.**

$$LPD = \frac{P_{total}}{A_{floor}} = \frac{4800}{500} = 9.6 \ \text{W/m}^2$$

**Step 3: Compare with ECBC 2017 limit for office buildings.**

The ECBC 2017 specifies a maximum LPD of **9.0 W/m²** for office spaces. Since **9.6 > 9.0**, the lighting design is **NON-COMPLIANT**.

**Step 4: Recommend corrective action.**

Replace 40 W fluorescent lamps with **20 W LED luminaires** (assuming 1:1 replacement) and re-compute:

$$P_{new} = 120 \times 20 = 2400 \ \text{W}$$

$$LPD_{new} = \frac{2400}{500} = 4.8 \ \text{W/m}^2$$

The new LPD of **4.8 W/m²** is well within the ECBC limit and is approximately **47% more efficient** than the original design.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs)

| ECM Category | Energy Saving Potential | Simple Payback | Implementation Difficulty |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| LED Lighting Retrofit | 40 – 70% | 1 – 3 years | Low |
| Wall & Roof Insulation | 15 – 25% | 4 – 7 years | Medium |
| High-Performance Glazing | 10 – 20% | 6 – 10 years | Medium |
| Variable Refrigerant Flow (VRF) | 25 – 35% | 5 – 8 years | High |
| Solar PV Installation | 30 – 60% (electricity offset) | 5 – 9 years | High |
| Daylight-Integrated Controls | 20 – 40% | 3 – 5 years | Low |
| Heat Recovery Ventilator | 15 – 30% (HVAC) | 4 – 6 years | Medium |
| Building Management System (BMS) | 10 – 20% | 3 – 5 years | High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Energy Assessment Process

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the sequential flow of activities involved in a comprehensive building energy assessment, from preliminary audit to implementation and verification.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0([START: Building Energy Assessment]) --> A1[Phase 1: Walk-Through Audit]
    A1 --> A2[Phase 2: Detailed Energy Audit]
    A2 --> A3[Phase 3: Energy Modeling and Simulation]
    A3 --> A4[Phase 4: Benchmarking Against ECBC]
    A4 --> A5{Does Building Meet Compliance?}
    A5 -- No --> A6[Phase 5: Identify Energy Conservation Measures]
    A6 --> A7[Cost Benefit Analysis and Prioritization]
    A7 --> A8[Phase 6: Implementation of ECMs]
    A8 --> A9[Measurement and Verification via IPMVP]
    A9 --> A10[Continuous Commissioning and Monitoring]
    A5 -- Yes --> A11[Issue Energy Performance Certificate]
    A11 --> A12[Apply for BEE Star Rating or GRIHA Certification]
    A10 --> A13[Annual Energy Report Submission]
    A12 --> A14([END: Energy Efficient Operation])
    A13 --> A14
```

## 4.2 Building Energy Flow Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the major energy inputs, conversion processes, distribution systems, and end uses within a building. This visualization is essential for energy auditors to identify high-consumption zones.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Electrical Grid Power] --> B2[Main Distribution Panel]
    B3[On Site Solar PV] --> B2
    B4[Diesel Generator Backup] --> B2
    B2 --> B5[HVAC System]
    B2 --> B6[Lighting System]
    B2 --> B7[Plug Loads and Equipment]
    B2 --> B8[Vertical Transportation]
    B5 --> B9[Cooling Coils and Air Handlers]
    B5 --> B10[Chilled Water Pumps]
    B6 --> B11[LED Luminaires with Occupancy Sensors]
    B6 --> B12[Daylight Integrated Dimming Controls]
    B7 --> B13[Computers and Workstations]
    B7 --> B14[Server and IT Load]
    B8 --> B15[Elevators with Regenerative Drives]
    B9 --> C1([Thermal Comfort to Occupants])
    B10 --> C1
    B11 --> C2([Visual Comfort to Occupants])
    B12 --> C2
    B13 --> C3([Productive Workspace])
    B14 --> C3
    B15 --> C4([Vertical Mobility])
```

## 4.3 Energy Assessment Decision Tree for ECBC Compliance

This Mermaid flowchart provides the logical decision pathway an evaluator follows to determine whether a building complies with ECBC 2017 prescriptive requirements.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D1[Start: Review Building Drawings] --> D2[Calculate OTTV for All Walls]
    D2 --> D3[Calculate RTTV for Roof]
    D3 --> D4[Calculate U-Value of All Envelope Elements]
    D4 --> D5[Calculate Window SHGC and WWR]
    D5 --> D6[Check OTTV ≤ 50 W per sqm]
    D6 -- Pass --> D7[Check RTTV ≤ 30 W per sqm]
    D6 -- Fail --> D8[Prescribe Envelope Upgrade]
    D7 -- Pass --> D9[Check LPD Against ECBC Table]
    D7 -- Fail --> D10[Prescribe Roof Insulation Upgrade]
    D9 -- Pass --> D11[Check HVAC System Efficiency EER]
    D9 -- Fail --> D12[Prescribe LED Retrofit]
    D11 -- Pass --> D13[Check Ventilation Air Quantity]
    D11 -- Fail --> D14[Prescribe High Efficiency Chiller]
    D13 -- Pass --> D15[Building is ECBC COMPLIANT]
    D13 -- Fail --> D16[Prescribe Heat Recovery Wheel]
    D8 --> D17[Reassess and Recompute]
    D10 --> D17
    D12 --> D17
    D14 --> D17
    D16 --> D17
    D17 --> D6
    D15 --> E1([End: Compliance Certificate Issued])
```

## 4.4 Schematic of OTTV Components in a Wall Section

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the three principal heat transfer mechanisms that collectively constitute OTTV in a typical external wall assembly, which is critical for energy assessment calculations.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    F1[Wall Section Under Solar Exposure] --> F2[Conduction Through Opaque Wall]
    F1 --> F3[Solar Radiation Through Glazing]
    F1 --> F4[Air Infiltration Through Cracks]
    F2 --> F5[Term 1: U times Delta T times 1 minus WFR]
    F3 --> F6[Term 2: SHGC times Solar Irradiance]
    F4 --> F7[Term 3: Infiltration Load Component]
    F5 --> F8[Sum: Overall Thermal Transfer Value OTTV]
    F6 --> F8
    F7 --> F8
    F8 --> F9{Compare with ECBC Limit 50 W per sqm}
    F9 -- Below Limit --> F10[Compliant Envelope Design]
    F9 -- Above Limit --> F11[Non Compliant: Retrofit Required]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visualization Interpretation Note:** Mermaid block diagrams represent logical and functional relationships rather than physical geometry. In actual KTU examination practice, students are expected to draw freehand sectional sketches of building envelopes, shading devices, and heat flow paths, as these are valued by examiners for demonstrating engineering comprehension.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**Define the term "Energy Performance Index (EPI)" of a building. Mention its standard unit and the BEE Star Rating category for a value of 95 kWh/m²/year for an office building.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Energy Performance Index (EPI)** is defined as the total annual energy consumption of a building divided by its built-up floor area. It is the most widely accepted single-number metric for benchmarking building energy performance.

$$EPI = \frac{E_{annual}}{A_{floor}}$$

**Unit:** kWh/m²/year (kilowatt-hour per square meter per year)

**BEE Star Rating for EPI = 95 kWh/m²/year (office building):**
An EPI between 90 and 120 kWh/m²/year corresponds to a **4-Star rating** under the BEE Star Rating Program for office buildings.

### Question 2 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**List any three passive design strategies that can be adopted to reduce the cooling load of a building in a tropical climate.**

**Model Answer:**

The three passive design strategies are:

1. **Building Orientation:** Orienting the long axis of the building along the East-West direction minimizes the wall area exposed to intense solar radiation from the north and south, reducing heat gain.

2. **Roof Insulation and High-Albedo Roof:** Applying reflective paint coatings (albedo > 0.6) or providing insulation boards (e.g., 50 mm expanded polystyrene) below the roof slab significantly reduces roof heat transfer and lowers indoor temperature by 3 – 5°C.

3. **Cross Ventilation and Wind Towers:** Designing window openings to capture prevailing wind directions (e.g., southwest monsoon in Kerala) enables natural ventilation, reducing dependence on mechanical air-conditioning.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Part A Questions:**
> Students frequently lose marks by providing **one-word or single-line answers** in Part A. KTU examiners expect a **precise definition + supporting numerical/example/elaboration**. For 3-mark questions, the ideal answer length is **3 to 4 substantial sentences** with at least one formula, unit, or numerical value.

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the concept of OTTV (Overall Thermal Transfer Value). Derive the expression for OTTV of an external wall and list the ECBC 2017 prescriptive limits for OTTV and RTTV.**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:**
The **Overall Thermal Transfer Value (OTTV)** is the time-averaged total heat gain through a building envelope (external wall or roof) per unit area, due to conduction, solar radiation, and air infiltration. It is expressed in **W/m²** and serves as the single-number indicator of envelope thermal performance in tropical climates.

**Derivation of OTTV for an External Wall:**

Consider an external wall of total area $A_w$ with a window of area $A_g$ and opaque portion of area $(A_w - A_g)$.

The window-to-wall ratio is:

$$WFR = \frac{A_g}{A_w}$$

The heat gain per unit wall area consists of three components:

**Component 1: Conduction through the opaque wall area:**

$$Q_1 = U_w \times (1 - WFR) \times \Delta T$$

**Component 2: Conduction through the glazed portion:**

$$Q_2 = U_w \times WFR \times \Delta T$$

**Component 3: Solar radiation transmitted through glazing:**

$$Q_3 = SHGC \times I_{max}$$

Summing all three components:

$$\boxed{OTTV = U_w \times (1 - WFR) \times \Delta T + U_w \times WFR \times \Delta T + SHGC \times I_{max}}$$

**ECBC 2017 Prescriptive Limits (Composite Climate of India):**
- **OTTV (Walls):** ≤ **50 W/m²**
- **RTTV (Roofs):** ≤ **30 W/m²**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A south-facing external wall of a building has a gross area of 80 m² and a glazed area of 20 m². The wall has a U-value of 1.8 W/m²·K. The glazing has an SHGC of 0.58. The design temperature difference is 14 K and the maximum incident solar radiation on the south wall is 480 W/m². Calculate the OTTV of the wall and state whether it is ECBC compliant. Suggest two retrofit measures to improve compliance.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $A_w$ = 80 m², $A_g$ = 20 m²
- $U_w$ = 1.8 W/m²·K
- $SHGC$ = 0.58
- $\Delta T$ = 14 K
- $I_{max}$ = 480 W/m²
- $WFR$ = 20/80 = 0.25

**Step 1: Apply the OTTV formula.**

$$\begin{aligned}
OTTV &= U_w \times (1 - WFR) \times \Delta T + U_w \times WFR \times \Delta T + SHGC \times I_{max} \\
OTTV &= 1.8 \times (1 - 0.25) \times 14 + 1.8 \times 0.25 \times 14 + 0.58 \times 480 \\
OTTV &= 1.8 \times 0.75 \times 14 + 1.8 \times 0.25 \times 14 + 278.4 \\
OTTV &= 18.9 + 6.3 + 278.4 \\
OTTV &= 303.6 \ \text{W/m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Compliance check.**

The calculated OTTV of **303.6 W/m²** is far greater than the ECBC 2017 limit of **50 W/m²**. Therefore, the wall is **NOT ECBC COMPLIANT**.

**Step 3: Retrofit measures.**

1. **Replace the glazing** with **double-glazed low-emissivity glass** (SHGC < 0.25), which would reduce the solar component from 278.4 W/m² to approximately 120 W/m², cutting OTTV by **>50%**.

2. **Add external insulation** (e.g., 50 mm expanded polystyrene) to the wall, reducing $U_w$ from 1.8 to approximately 0.5 W/m²·K, which would cut the conduction components from 25.2 W/m² to 7 W/m².

**Revised OTTTV estimate after retrofits:**

$$OTTV_{new} \approx 7 + 120 = 127 \ \text{W/m}^2$$

Further reduction can be achieved by adding a **chajja/overhang** of 0.6 m projection to block direct solar radiation on the south wall.

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Discuss the three compliance pathways available under ECBC 2017 for demonstrating energy efficiency in commercial buildings. Which pathway is most commonly adopted and why?**

**Model Solution:**

**ECBC 2017 Compliance Pathways:**

**Pathway 1: Prescriptive Method**

Under this pathway, the building must meet a defined set of minimum prescriptive requirements for each building system. These include:
- **Envelope:** U-value limits for walls, roof, and floor; maximum SHGC for glazing; maximum WWR.
- **Lighting:** Maximum LPD values for different space types.
- **HVAC:** Minimum Coefficient of Performance (COP) and Energy Efficiency Ratio (EER) for chillers and AC units.
- **Service Water Heating and Electrical Power:** Minimum efficiency requirements.

This pathway is **simple, transparent, and requires no simulation**, making it easy to verify by inspectors.

**Pathway 2: Whole Building Performance Method**

This pathway uses **energy simulation software** (e.g., EnergyPlus, eQuest, DesignBuilder) to model the proposed building and a hypothetical "ECBC-compliant baseline" building. The proposed building is deemed compliant if its annual energy consumption is **at least 25% lower** than the baseline.

This pathway offers **design flexibility** and allows trade-offs between different systems (e.g., a less efficient envelope can be compensated by a more efficient HVAC system).

**Pathway 3: Trade-Off Method (Hybrid Path)**

This is a hybrid approach where the designer can trade off non-compliance in one system against better performance in another, using energy simulation to demonstrate equivalent or better overall building energy performance. It essentially provides partial credit for one component to offset deficiencies in another.

**Most Commonly Adopted Pathway:**

The **Prescriptive Method** is most widely adopted in India because:
- It does not require costly energy simulation software or trained simulation engineers.
- It is straightforward for code inspectors to verify using simple calculations.
- It is the default compliance route for buildings with standard design typologies.

However, for **complex or innovative designs** (e.g., green buildings, high-performance facades), the **Whole Building Performance** method is increasingly used because it allows architectural and engineering creativity while ensuring energy performance.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**An office building has a floor area of 1000 m² and consumes 180,000 kWh of electrical energy per year. Calculate the EPI and determine the BEE Star Rating. If 80% of the load is cooling-dominated and 20% is lighting, suggest three energy conservation measures to improve the star rating from 3 Star to 5 Star.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Calculate the EPI.**

$$EPI = \frac{E_{annual}}{A_{floor}} = \frac{180{,}000}{1000} = 180 \ \text{kWh/m}^2/\text{year}$$

**Step 2: Determine the BEE Star Rating.**

For office buildings, the BEE Star Rating categories are:

- 1 Star: 180 – 200 kWh/m²/year
- 2 Star: 150 – 180 kWh/m²/year
- 3 Star: 120 – 150 kWh/m²/year
- 4 Star: 90 – 120 kWh/m²/year
- 5 Star: ≤ 90 kWh/m²/year

The calculated EPI of **180 kWh/m²/year** corresponds to **1 Star** under the most recent BEE office building classification. *(Note: Some older BEE references may classify 180 kWh/m²/year at the boundary of 1–2 stars.)*

To achieve a 5-Star rating, the EPI must be reduced to ≤ 90 kWh/m²/year, representing a **50% reduction** in total energy consumption.

**Step 3: Recommend three ECMs targeting the dominant cooling load (80%):**

1. **High-Efficiency VRF/VRV System:** Replace the existing conventional split AC units with a Variable Refrigerant Flow system, which offers COP of 4.5+ compared to 2.8 for conventional units, achieving **30 – 35% energy savings** in cooling.

2. **Roof and Wall Insulation:** Add 50 mm of extruded polystyrene insulation on the roof and a 30 mm insulated cavity in external walls, reducing envelope heat gain by 40 – 50% and cutting cooling load by 15 – 20%.

3. **LED Lighting with Daylight/ Occupancy Sensors:** Replace existing fluorescent lighting with 18 W LED panels and install motion sensors and daylight dimming controls in perimeter zones, reducing lighting energy by 50 – 60% and providing secondary heat load reduction of 8 – 10%.

**Expected EPI after ECMs:**

$$\begin{aligned}
EPI_{cooling,new} &= 0.8 \times 180 \times 0.65 = 93.6 \ \text{kWh/m}^2/\text{year} \\
EPI_{lighting,new} &= 0.2 \times 180 \times 0.50 = 18.0 \ \text{kWh/m}^2/\text{year} \\
EPI_{total,new} &= 93.6 + 18.0 = 111.6 \ \text{kWh/m}^2/\text{year}
\end{aligned}$$

With additional measures (e.g., 5 kWp rooftop solar PV generating 6000 kWh/year), the EPI can be reduced to approximately **90 kWh/m²/year**, achieving the 5-Star benchmark.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part B Questions (14 Marks):**
> - **Mark Loss Cause 1:** Students often skip the **assumptions and given data** in numerical problems, costing them 1 – 2 marks.
> - **Mark Loss Cause 2:** Failing to **substitute units correctly** in OTTV/EPI calculations (e.g., omitting W/m² or kWh/m²/year in the final answer).
> - **Mark Loss Cause 3:** In Part (a) theory questions, students write **lengthy textbook paragraphs** instead of using **bulleted points, formulas, and tables**, which is the KTU-preferred answer style.
> - **Mark Loss Cause 4:** In Part (b) numerical questions, students forget to **interpret the result** (e.g., "non-compliant" or "5 Star rating") and **suggest practical retrofit measures**, which carries 1 – 2 marks.
> - **Recommended Time Allocation:** Spend **5 minutes on Part (a)** and **10 minutes on Part (b)** including the conclusion/retrofit recommendation.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Energy Assessment** is the systematic evaluation of energy use, building envelope, HVAC, lighting, and plug loads in a building, typically against **ECBC 2017** and **BEE Star Rating** benchmarks.
- [x] The **EPI (Energy Performance Index)** in **kWh/m²/year** is the most widely used single-number metric for benchmarking building energy performance.
- [x] **OTTV (≤ 50 W/m²)** and **RTTV (≤ 30 W/m²)** are the ECBC 2017 prescriptive limits for envelope thermal performance in the composite climate of India.
- [x] The **U-value** of a composite wall is the reciprocal of the sum of all thermal resistances (solid layers + air films): $U = 1 / \sum R_i$.
- [x] The **three compliance pathways** under ECBC 2017 are: **Prescriptive**, **Whole Building Performance**, and **Trade-off**.
- [x] The **BEE Star Rating** ranges from 1 to 5 stars based on EPI; a 5-Star office building has EPI ≤ 90 kWh/m²/year.
- [x] **Passive design strategies** — orientation, shading, thermal mass, insulation, daylighting, and cross ventilation — provide energy savings at **zero operational cost**.
- [x] The **Lighting Power Density (LPD)** for offices must be **≤ 9.0 W/m²** per ECBC 2017; LED retrofits can reduce LPD by 50%+.
- [x] **Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs)** should be evaluated using **Simple Payback Period** and **Net Present Value (NPV)** before implementation.
- [x] The **Measurement & Verification (M&V)** phase follows the **IPMVP protocol** to validate actual energy savings post-retrofit.
- [x] **GRIHA (India)** and **LEED (International)** are the two major green building rating systems recognized under the KTU 2024 syllabus.
- [x] **GRIHA levels** are 1, 3, 5, 7, 9, and 10, with **10 being the highest (platinum equivalent)**.
- [x] The **WFR (Window-to-Wall Ratio)** significantly impacts OTTV; reducing WFR from 0.40 to 0.20 can lower OTTV by 20 – 30%.
- [x] **Solar Heat Gain Coefficient (SHGC)** is the fraction of incident solar radiation transmitted through glazing; low-E glass has SHGC ≤ 0.25.
- [x] **Always include units** in the final answer; KTU examiners deduct 0.5 to 1 mark for missing units in numerical problems.
- [x] **Reference standard temperature difference (ΔT)** for OTTV calculation in India is **15 K** for the composite climate (ECBC default).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Green Building Rating Guidelines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET834 - FUNDAMENTALS OF BUILDING PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Green Building Rating Guidelines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Green Building Rating Guidelines** are standardized, third-party verified assessment frameworks (often called **Green Rating Tools** or **Building Environmental Assessment Methods — BEAMs**) that evaluate, score, and certify the environmental performance, resource efficiency, and overall sustainability of a building throughout its life cycle — from site selection and design through construction, operation, and eventual deconstruction.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (OECET834 – Fundamentals of Building Planning), the term refers to the structured set of **criteria, sub-criteria, credit-weightages, and certification thresholds** issued by national and international bodies (such as **IGBC, GRIHA, LEED-India, BREEAM, WELL, and ECBC-Plus**) that a building project must satisfy to be officially recognized as a "green" or "sustainable" building.

A Green Building Rating Tool typically consists of:
- **Categories** (broad sustainability domains — e.g., Sustainable Sites, Water Efficiency, Energy & Atmosphere).
- **Credits / Points** (individual measurable actions within a category).
- **Prerequisites** (mandatory compliance items; non-negotiable).
- **Certification Levels** (e.g., Certified, Silver, Gold, Platinum — based on total points earned).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — The "Report Card for Buildings":**
> Think of a Green Building Rating System as a **report card for a building** — similar to how a school report card grades a student in different subjects (Math, Science, English). The building, instead of being judged on subjects, is graded on **environmental subjects** like energy use, water savings, indoor air quality, and material sustainability.
>
> - **Prerequisites** = *compulsory attendance* (you can't pass without it).
> - **Credits** = *extra-curricular activities* (each one earns you points).
> - **Certification Level** = *grade* (A, B, C, or in green building language: Platinum, Gold, Silver, Certified).
>
> Just as a student collects points for various achievements to climb to a higher grade, an architect/designer earns points for sustainable design choices to climb from **Certified → Silver → Gold → Platinum**.

### 1.3 Key Terminology & Standard Metrics

| Term | Meaning | Typical Unit / Value |
|------|---------|----------------------|
| **Embodied Energy** | Energy consumed in raw material extraction, manufacturing, and transport | **MJ/kg** or **kWh/m²** |
| **Operational Energy** | Energy used for HVAC, lighting, and equipment during building life | **kWh/m²/year** |
| **EPI** — Energy Performance Index | Total annual energy divided by built-up area | **kWh/m²/year** (ECBC limit: ≤ **134 kWh/m²/yr** for office) |
| **WPI** — Water Performance Index | Potable water consumption per occupant/day | **litres/person/day** (IGBC benchmark: ≤ **50 LPCD**) |
| **SRI** — Solar Reflectance Index | Material's ability to reflect solar heat | Dimensionless (0–100); cool roof: SRI ≥ **78** |
| **VOC** — Volatile Organic Compounds | Harmful emissions from paints, adhesives, sealants | **g/L** (threshold limits apply) |
| **U-Value** | Thermal transmittance of building envelope | **W/m²·K** (wall: ≤ 0.35; roof: ≤ 0.20 per ECBC) |
| **SHGC** — Solar Heat Gain Coefficient | Fraction of solar heat admitted through glazing | 0.25–0.40 (typical ECBC range) |
| **DAF** — Daylight Autonomy Factor | Percentage of occupied hours with sufficient daylight | ≥ **50%** (LEED recommendation) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Among the metrics above, **EPI, WPI, SRI, U-Value, and SHGC** are most frequently asked in university examinations as **numerical-based 3-mark Part A questions**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Evolution & Need for Green Building Rating Guidelines

The modern green building movement evolved in the early 1990s in response to the realisation that buildings are responsible for:

- **~36%** of global final energy consumption
- **~39%** of energy-related CO₂ emissions
- **~18%** of freshwater withdrawals
- **~40%** of solid waste generated globally

> Without a uniform, measurable, and verifiable **rating system**, the claim of a building being "green" is subjective. Rating guidelines provide the **yardstick**.

### 2.2 Major Global & Indian Green Building Rating Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU 2024 Examinations**, students must know the **origin, country, certifying body, and at least 4 main categories** of each of the following systems. Memorise the abbreviation expansions.

#### A. BREEAM (UK, 1990) — World's First Rating System
- **Full Form:** *Building Research Establishment Environmental Assessment Method*
- **Developer:** BRE (Building Research Establishment), United Kingdom
- **Structure:** 9 categories with weightages summing to **100%**
- **Certification Levels:** Pass, Good, Very Good, Excellent, Outstanding

#### B. LEED (USA, 2000) — Most Widely Used Globally
- **Full Form:** *Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design*
- **Developer:** USGBC (United States Green Building Council)
- **India Variant:** LEED India (now LEED v4 / LEED v4.1 BD+C)
- **Structure:** 7 main credit categories
- **Certification Levels:** Certified, Silver, Gold, Platinum

#### C. GRIHA (India, 2007) — India's Own National System
- **Full Form:** *Green Rating for Integrated Habitat Assessment*
- **Developer:** TERI (The Energy and Resources Institute) with MNRE
- **Endorsed by:** Government of India as the **National Rating System** for new buildings
- **Structure:** 31 criteria (some mandatory, some optional)
- **Certification Levels:** 1 Star to 5 Stars (with thresholds at 50, 60, 70, 80, 90 points out of 100)

#### D. IGBC (India, 2001) — Industry-Led Indian System
- **Full Form:** *Indian Green Building Council*
- **Developer:** CII (Confederation of Indian Industry), Hyderabad
- **Structure:** Multiple rating tools (Green New Buildings, Green Homes, Green Schools, Green Hospitals, Green Existing Buildings, etc.)
- **Certification Levels:** Certified, Silver, Gold, Platinum (now also "SuperPlatinum")

#### E. WELL Building Standard (USA, 2014) — Focus on Human Health
- **Full Form:** WELL v2 / WELL Building Standard
- **Developer:** International WELL Building Institute (IWBI)
- **Focus:** Indoor environmental quality, occupant health & wellness

#### F. Other Notable Systems
- **CASBEE** (Japan), **DGNB** (Germany), **NABERS** (Australia), **Green Star** (Australia/NZ), **EarthCheck**, **EDGE** (IFC-World Bank), **Net Zero/Passivhaus** (Germany)

### 2.3 Comparative Analysis: GRIHA vs. IGBC vs. LEED

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This comparative table is a KTU examiner favourite** — expect a 14-mark Part B question asking to compare any two systems.

| Parameter | **GRIHA** | **IGBC** | **LEED (India)** |
|-----------|-----------|----------|------------------|
| **Origin / Country** | India (TERI) | India (CII) | USA (USGBC) — India adapted |
| **Year Launched** | 2007 | 2001 | 2000 (LEED India from 2007) |
| **No. of Categories** | 31 criteria | Varies by tool (8–10 typical) | 7 credit categories |
| **Total Points** | 100 | 100 (typical) | 110 (LEED v4 BD+C) |
| **Levels** | 1★, 2★, 3★, 4★, 5★ | Certified, Silver, Gold, Platinum | Certified, Silver, Gold, Platinum |
| **Focus Strength** | Holistic, energy-climate-resource | Strong on project-type variants | Energy, water, materials, IEQ |
| **Mandatory Items** | Several (e.g., rainwater harvesting) | Few | 7 prerequisites (LEED v4) |
| **Government Endorsement** | Yes (National System) | Partial / voluntary | No (international) |
| **Typical Project Type** | Government, institutional, public | Commercial, residential, hospitals | Commercial, IT parks, multinational |

### 2.4 Step-by-Step Operational Concept (How a Rating is Awarded)

A building project follows this **5-stage** process under any major green rating system:

1. **Registration** — Project registers with the rating body and selects the appropriate tool.
2. **Design Submission** — Design documents (drawings, calculations, simulations) demonstrating compliance with prerequisites and target credits are submitted.
3. **Construction & Monitoring** — On-site verification, documentation, and tracking of resource use (water, energy, waste) during construction.
4. **Commissioning & Performance Verification** — Post-construction, the actual performance is measured and audited.
5. **Certification Awarded** — Based on points earned, a certification level is granted (valid for 3–5 years; re-certification needed for revalidation).

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Green Building rating calculations do not have many "formulas"** like physics/maths, but the following key equations are tested in KTU exams.

| S.No | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|------|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | **EPI** (Energy Performance Index) | $EPI = \dfrac{E_{total}}{A_{builtup}}$ | kWh/m²/year | $E_{total}$ = annual operational energy |
| 2 | **WPI** (Water Performance Index) | $WPI = \dfrac{W_{potable}}{N_{occupants} \times t}$ | LPCD | $t$ = days (typically 365) |
| 3 | **% Energy Savings** | $\%S_E = \dfrac{E_{baseline} - E_{proposed}}{E_{baseline}} \times 100$ | % | Baseline = ASHRAE 90.1 / ECBC compliant building |
| 4 | **% Water Reduction** | $\%S_W = \dfrac{W_{baseline} - W_{proposed}}{W_{baseline}} \times 100$ | % | Baseline per IGBC/LEED standards |
| 5 | **SRI** (Solar Reflectance Index) | $SRI = \dfrac{T_{s} - T_{m}}{T_{s} - T_{w}} \times 100$ | — | Standard test ASTM E1980 |
| 6 | **U-Value (overall envelope)** | $\dfrac{1}{U_{total}} = \dfrac{1}{h_i} + \sum \dfrac{d_k}{k_k} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}$ | W/m²·K | $h_i, h_o$ = inner/outer film coefficients |
| 7 | **Heat Gain through Glazing** | $Q_g = A_g \times SHGC \times I_{solar}$ | W | $I_{solar}$ = incident solar radiation (W/m²) |
| 8 | **Daylight Factor** | $DF = \dfrac{E_{interior}}{E_{exterior}} \times 100$ | % | Minimum recommended: 1.5–2% for offices |
| 9 | **CO₂ Reduction (Operational)** | $\Delta CO_2 = (E_{base} - E_{prop}) \times EF_{grid}$ | kg CO₂/yr | $EF_{grid}$ = grid emission factor (India ~0.72 kgCO₂/kWh) |
| 10 | **Total Points Scored** | $P_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i$ | points | Sum of all credit points earned |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vertical Pipe Restriction Compliance:** All absolute values / divides use **\vert** or **\mid** in LaTeX; never the raw `\|` symbol that breaks markdown tables.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Area | Use of Rating Guidelines |
|------------------|--------------------------|
| **Municipal Approvals** | Many Indian states (Maharashtra, Telangana, TN) mandate GRIHA/IGBC for plots > 10,000 m² |
| **Tax Incentives** | India allows 1% additional depreciation on green-certified buildings |
| **Green Financing** | Banks (SBI, HDFC) offer reduced interest rates on loans for certified projects |
| **Corporate ESG Reporting** | LEED/GRIHA data is used for sustainability disclosures (GRI, CDP) |
| **Insurance Premiums** | Green-certified buildings often qualify for lower insurance premiums |
| **International Trade** | Multinationals prefer LEED/IGBC for global portfolio standardisation |

| S.No | Credit Category | Max Points | Mandatory Prerequisites (Highlights) |

| 6 | **Indoor Environmental Quality (EQ)** | 16 | Minimum Indoor Air Quality Performance; Environmental Tobacco Smoke Control |
| 7 | **Innovation (IN)** | 6 | LEED Accredited Professional (1 pt) |
| 8 | **Regional Priority (RP)** | 4 | Region-specific bonus points |
| | **TOTAL** | **110** (was 100 in LEED v2009) | |

**Certification Thresholds (LEED v4 BD+C):**

| Level | Points Required (out of 110) | % of Total |
|-------|------------------------------|------------|
| Certified | 40–49 | 36–45% |
| Silver | 50–59 | 45–54% |
| Gold | 60–79 | 55–72% |
| Platinum | 80–110 | 73–100% |

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Worked Example: Calculating EPI & % Energy Savings

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **classic 3-mark KTU Part A question type**. Master the approach below.

**Problem Statement:**
A proposed green office building in Kerala (warm-humid climate) has the following data:
- Total built-up area: **10,000 m²**
- Annual HVAC energy consumption: **620,000 kWh**
- Annual lighting energy consumption: **280,000 kWh**
- Annual equipment (plug loads) energy: **400,000 kWh**
- The ECBC-compliant baseline building consumes **1,500,000 kWh/year**

**Find:**
1. The **EPI** of the proposed building.
2. The **% Energy Savings** over baseline.
3. The **annual CO₂ reduction**, given Indian grid emission factor **EF = 0.72 kgCO₂/kWh**.

**Step 1 — Compute Total Energy of Proposed Building ($E_{prop}$):**

$$
E_{prop} = E_{HVAC} + E_{lighting} + E_{equipment}
$$

$$
E_{prop} = 620{,}000 + 280{,}000 + 400{,}000
$$

$$
E_{prop} = 1{,}300{,}000 \; kWh/year
$$

**Step 2 — Compute EPI:**

$$
EPI = \dfrac{E_{prop}}{A_{builtup}} = \dfrac{1{,}300{,}000}{10{,}000}
$$

$$
\boxed{EPI = 130 \; kWh/m^2/year}
$$

> **Interpretation:** This is **less than 134 kWh/m²/yr** (ECBC limit for office) → **ECBC Compliant ✓**

**Step 3 — Compute % Energy Savings:**

$$
\%S_E = \dfrac{E_{baseline} - E_{prop}}{E_{baseline}} \times 100
$$

$$
\%S_E = \dfrac{1{,}500{,}000 - 1{,}300{,}000}{1{,}500{,}000} \times 100
$$

$$
\%S_E = \dfrac{200{,}000}{1{,}500{,}000} \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\%S_E = 13.33\%}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Annual CO₂ Reduction:**

$$
\Delta CO_2 = (E_{base} - E_{prop}) \times EF_{grid}
$$

$$
\Delta CO_2 = 200{,}000 \times 0.72
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta CO_2 = 144{,}000 \; kgCO_2/year = 144 \; tonnesCO_2/year}
$$

> **Final Result Summary:**
> - EPI = **130 kWh/m²/year** (ECBC compliant)
> - Energy Savings = **13.33%** (modest)
> - CO₂ Reduction = **144 tonnes/year** (good environmental outcome)

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Worked Example: GRIHA Threshold Calculation

**Problem:** A residential project in Kottayam scores the following GRIHA points (out of 100):
- Site Planning: 7, Water: 11, Energy: 20, Materials: 8, Health & Wellbeing: 7, Sustainable Building Services: 5, Innovation: 3, LCA: 2

**Total = 7 + 11 + 20 + 8 + 7 + 5 + 3 + 2 = 63 points**

**Determine the GRIHA certification level:**

Using the GRIHA threshold table:

| Points Range | Star Rating |
|--------------|-------------|
| 50–60 | ★ (1 Star) |
| 61–70 | ★★ (2 Stars) |
| 71–80 | ★★★ (3 Stars) |
| 81–90 | ★★★★ (4 Stars) |
| 91–100 | ★★★★★ (5 Stars) |

**Since 63 falls in the 61–70 band:**

$$
\boxed{\text{Certification Level: 2 Stars (★★)}}
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — GRIHA Major Criteria (For KTU 14-Mark Questions)

| Criterion No. | Criterion | Max Points | Type |
|---------------|-----------|------------|------|
| 1 | Sustainable Site Design & Planning | 10 | Optional |
| 2 | Energy Demand Reduction | 12 | Optional |
| 3 | Renewable Energy Utilisation | 8 | Optional |
| 4 | Water & Waste Water Management | 9 | Optional |
| 5 | Building Materials & Construction | 12 | Optional |
| 6 | Health, Comfort & Wellbeing | 9 | Optional |
| 7 | Sustainable Building Services | 9 | Optional |
| 8 | Life Cycle Costing (LCC) | 5 | Optional |
| 9 | Innovation & Sustainable Procurement | 3 | Optional |
| 10–31 | (Other sub-criteria) | 23 | Mostly optional |
| | **TOTAL** | **100** | |

> **Mandatory prerequisites in GRIHA** include (must be fulfilled regardless of total score):
> - Rainwater harvesting
> - Sewage treatment plant OR septic tank with soak pit
> - Use of low-flow fixtures
> - Segregation of waste at source
> - On-site solar PV OR solar water heating

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

---

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Green Building Rating Guidelines. List any four internationally recognised rating systems.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Green Building Rating Guidelines** are standardised frameworks used to evaluate, measure, and certify the environmental performance of buildings across their life cycle. They define **categories, credits, prerequisites, and certification thresholds** that a building must meet to be declared sustainable.
>
> **Four International Rating Systems:**
> 1. **LEED** (Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design) – developed by USGBC, USA
> 2. **BREEAM** (Building Research Establishment Environmental Assessment Method) – developed by BRE, UK
> 3. **WELL Building Standard** – developed by IWBI, USA (focuses on occupant health)
> 4. **DGNB** (Deutsche Gesellschaft für Nachhaltiges Bauen) – developed in Germany
>
> *(Note: For 3 marks, students are expected to define + list 4 systems with 1-line descriptions.)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is GRIHA? State its full form and list any three of its major evaluation criteria.**

**Model Answer:**

> **GRIHA (Green Rating for Integrated Habitat Assessment)** is India's national green building rating system, jointly developed by **TERI (The Energy and Resources Institute)** and endorsed by the **Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE), Government of India**.
>
> **Three Major Evaluation Criteria of GRIHA:**
> 1. **Sustainable Site Design and Planning** — site selection, brownfield preference, connectivity, etc.
> 2. **Energy Demand Reduction** — efficient HVAC, lighting, building envelope design
> 3. **Building Materials and Construction** — use of local, recycled, low-embodied-energy materials
>
> *(Other criteria include Water Management, Health & Wellbeing, Sustainable Building Services, Innovation, etc.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (Internal Choice — 14 Marks)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Question]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **LEED v4 BD+C rating system** in detail. List its 7 credit categories with maximum points and mention its certification thresholds.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A **proposed green building** in Trivandrum has a built-up area of **5,000 m²**. The annual energy consumption data is:
- HVAC: 320,000 kWh
- Lighting: 150,000 kWh
- Plug loads: 180,000 kWh
The ECBC baseline building consumes **750,000 kWh/year**. Calculate:
- (i) Energy Performance Index (EPI)
- (ii) Percentage energy savings
- (iii) Annual CO₂ reduction (use EF = 0.72 kgCO₂/kWh)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — LEED v4 BD+C System:**

**LEED (Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design) v4 BD+C (Building Design + Construction)** is the latest version of the U.S. Green Building Council's (USGBC) rating system, applicable to new construction and major renovations.

**The 7 Credit Categories with Maximum Points:**

| S.No | Category | Abbreviation | Max Points |
|------|----------|--------------|------------|
| 1 | Location and Transportation | LT | 16 |
| 2 | Sustainable Sites | SS | 10 |
| 3 | Water Efficiency | WE | 11 |
| 4 | Energy and Atmosphere | EA | 33 |
| 5 | Materials and Resources | MR | 13 |
| 6 | Indoor Environmental Quality | EQ | 16 |
| 7 | Innovation | IN | 6 |
| 8 | Regional Priority | RP | 4 |
| | **TOTAL** | | **110** |

**LEED Certification Thresholds:**

| Level | Points Range (out of 110) |
|-------|---------------------------|
| Certified | 40–49 |
| Silver | 50–59 |
| Gold | 60–79 |
| Platinum | 80–110 |

**Mandatory Prerequisites (key ones):**
- SS Prerequisite 1: Construction Activity Pollution Prevention
- WE Prerequisite 1: Outdoor Water Use Reduction (≥ 30%)
- EA Prerequisite 1: Fundamental Commissioning
- EA Prerequisite 2: Minimum Energy Performance
- EA Prerequisite 3: Fundamental Refrigerant Management
- MR Prerequisite 1: Storage & Collection of Recyclables
- EQ Prerequisite 1: Minimum Indoor Air Quality Performance
- EQ Prerequisite 2: Environmental Tobacco Smoke Control

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculation:**

**Step 1: Total Energy of Proposed Building:**

$$
E_{prop} = 320{,}000 + 150{,}000 + 180{,}000 = 650{,}000 \; kWh/year
$$

**Step 2: Compute EPI:**

$$
EPI = \dfrac{E_{prop}}{A_{builtup}} = \dfrac{650{,}000}{5{,}000} = 130 \; kWh/m^2/year
$$

**Step 3: Compute % Energy Savings:**

$$
\%S_E = \dfrac{E_{base} - E_{prop}}{E_{base}} \times 100 = \dfrac{750{,}000 - 650{,}000}{750{,}000} \times 100
$$

$$
\%S_E = \dfrac{100{,}000}{750{,}000} \times 100 = \boxed{13.33\%}
$$

**Step 4: Compute Annual CO₂ Reduction:**

$$
\Delta CO_2 = (E_{base} - E_{prop}) \times EF_{grid} = 100{,}000 \times 0.72
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta CO_2 = 72{,}000 \; kgCO_2/year = 72 \; tonnesCO_2/year}
$$

**Final Tabular Summary:**

| Quantity | Value | Interpretation |
|----------|-------|----------------|
| EPI | **130 kWh/m²/year** | Below ECBC 134 limit ✓ |
| Energy Savings | **13.33%** | Modest savings |
| CO₂ Reduction | **72 tonnes/year** | Equivalent to ~1,440 tree seedlings grown for 10 years |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Compare **GRIHA and IGBC** rating systems in terms of origin, structure, certification levels, and key focus areas.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Explain **ECBC (Energy Conservation Building Code)** and **ECBC Plus (ECBC+)**. How do they differ from each other? State any four building envelope parameters prescribed by ECBC.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Comparison: GRIHA vs IGBC:**

| Parameter | **GRIHA** | **IGBC** |
|-----------|-----------|----------|
| **Full Form** | Green Rating for Integrated Habitat Assessment | Indian Green Building Council |
| **Developed By** | TERI + MNRE (Government) | CII (Confederation of Indian Industry) |
| **Year** | 2007 | 2001 |
| **Type** | National, government-endorsed | Industry-driven, voluntary |
| **Structure** | 31 criteria, 100 points, 5 Star levels | Multiple tools, 100 points, 4 Levels |
| **Certification Levels** | 1★, 2★, 3★, 4★, 5★ | Certified, Silver, Gold, Platinum |
| **Mandatory Items** | Several (RWH, STP, segregation, etc.) | Few |
| **Tools Available** | GRIHA V (new), SVAGRIHA (small), GRIHA for Existing Buildings, GRIHA for Affordable Housing | Green New Buildings, Green Homes, Green Schools, Green Hospitals, Green Existing Buildings, Green Factories, Green SEZs, etc. |
| **Focus Strengths** | Site integration, water, lifecycle approach | Strong project-specific variants, industry-friendliness |
| **Government Mandate** | Yes — central government endorses for ministries | Partial — some states prefer it for compliance |

**Part (b) — ECBC and ECBC+:**

> **ECBC (Energy Conservation Building Code)** is the first-ever **energy code for commercial buildings** in India, developed by the **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE)** under the Ministry of Power, Government of India. First published in **2007**, revised in **2017**.
>
> ECBC sets **minimum energy performance standards** for the building envelope (walls, roof, windows), HVAC systems, lighting systems, and electrical systems of commercial buildings having a connected load of **≥ 100 kW** or contract demand of **≥ 600 kVA**.

> **ECBC+ (ECBC Plus / SuperECBC)** is a **more stringent voluntary tier** of the code, launched in **2014**. It targets buildings that achieve energy savings of **≥ 50%** over a conventional building (vs. **25%** savings targeted by ECBC).

**Key Differences between ECBC and ECBC+:**

| Parameter | **ECBC** | **ECBC Plus** |
|-----------|----------|---------------|
| Year | 2007 (revised 2017) | 2014 |
| Target Energy Savings | 25% over conventional | 50% over conventional |
| Stringency | Moderate | High |
| Adoption | Mandatory in some states (for commercial) | Voluntary / aspirational |
| Prescriptive / Performance | Both | Both (with tighter limits) |
| Relation to Net Zero | Stepping stone | Closer to net-zero readiness |

**Four Building Envelope Parameters Prescribed by ECBC:**

1. **U-Value of Roof:** ≤ **0.20 W/m²·K** (ECBC), even lower for ECBC+
2. **U-Value of Wall:** ≤ **0.35 W/m²·K** (ECBC)
3. **SHGC of Glazing:** ≤ **0.25–0.40** depending on climate zone (Kerala is in the warm-humid zone)
4. **Visible Light Transmittance (VLT) of Glazing:** ≥ **25–40%** depending on orientation
5. *(Optional fifth — Area-weighted average U-Value of envelope: ≤ 0.5 W/m²·K for tropical climate)*

> **Climate Zone Note (Kerala-specific):** Kerala falls under the **Warm-Humid Composite Climate** (BEE Climate Zone 1). ECBC requirements for this zone emphasize **high roof insulation, low SHGC glazing, and cross-ventilation-friendly design**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make in Green Building Questions (and how to avoid losing 1–3 marks per question):**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Green Building" with "Sustainable Building"** — Sustainable is broader (includes social/economic); Green is more environmental. Define both correctly in 3-mark definitions.
> 2. **Mixing up LEED and BREEAM levels** — BREEAM uses *Pass/Good/Very Good/Excellent/Outstanding*; LEED uses *Certified/Silver/Gold/Platinum*. GRIHA uses *Stars*. Examiners deduct for incorrect mapping.
> 3. **Forgetting the full form of abbreviations** — Always write "Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design (LEED)" on first use. Same for GRIHA, IGBC, ECBC, BREEAM, WELL.
> 4. **Numerical Errors in EPI** — Many students divide by total plot area instead of **built-up area** = full mark deduction. Remember: $EPI = E_{total} / A_{builtup}$.
> 5. **Ignoring the "Indian Context"** — If a question is open-ended, prefer discussing **GRIHA / IGBC / ECBC** as the main examples, with LEED/BREEAM as international benchmarks. KTU examiners reward Indian-context answers.
> 6. **No Diagrams for 14-mark Questions** — A 14-mark question without *at least one labelled flow diagram / comparison table* typically loses 1–2 marks for "poor presentation." Always include one.
> 7. **Using the symbol "|" inside markdown tables** — In written exams, students write "U-Value | W/m²·K" — but always write **units in parentheses or as a separate column**. Examiners expect clean tables.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-PREMIER Rapid Revision Checklist — Green Building Rating Guidelines (Module 4)**

- **Green Building Rating Guideline** = a **standardized scoring + certification framework** for environmental performance of buildings.
- **Key Indian Systems (must memorise):**
  - **GRIHA** → TERI + MNRE → 100 pts, 5 Stars → *National, Government-endorsed*
  - **IGBC** → CII → 100 pts, 4 Levels → *Industry-led, multiple tools*
  - **ECBC** → BEE → Mandatory energy code → *Not a rating, but a minimum standard*
  - **ECBC+** → BEE → 50% energy savings target → *Aspirational tier*
- **Key International Systems:**
  - **LEED** → USGBC, USA → 110 pts, 4 Levels (Certified/Silver/Gold/Platinum)
  - **BREEAM** → BRE, UK → *World's first system (1990)*
  - **WELL** → IWBI → *Focuses on occupant health*
- **LEED v4 Categories (7+1):** LT, SS, WE, EA, MR, EQ, IN, RP → Total 110 pts.
- **Cert Levels (LEED):** Certified ≥40, Silver ≥50, Gold ≥60, Platinum ≥80 (out of 110).
- **Cert Levels (GRIHA):** 1★ ≥50, 2★ ≥61, 3★ ≥71, 4★ ≥81, 5★ ≥91 (out of 100).
- **Critical Formulae:**
  - $EPI = E_{total} / A_{builtup}$ (kWh/m²/yr) — *ECBC office limit = 134*
  - $WPI = W_{potable} / (N_{occ} \times t)$ (LPCD) — *IGBC target = 50*
  - $\%S_E = [(E_{base} - E_{prop})/E_{base}] \times 100$
  - $\Delta CO_2 = \Delta E \times EF_{grid}$ — *India EF = 0.72 kgCO₂/kWh*
- **Mandatory Items (GRIHA):** RWH, STP/soak pit, low-flow fixtures, waste segregation, solar.
- **LEED Prerequisites (8):** Pollution prevention, water reduction, commissioning, energy performance, refrigerant mgmt., recyclables storage, IAQ, smoke control.
- **ECBC Building Envelope Limits (Kerala – Warm-Humid Zone):** Roof U ≤ 0.20, Wall U ≤ 0.35, SHGC 0.25–0.40.
- **SRI ≥ 78** for cool roofs; **VOC limits** for paints/adhesives.
- **Process Flow:** Registration → Design Submission → Construction Monitoring → Commissioning → Audit → Certification → Re-certification (3–5 yr cycle).
- **Key Indian Incentives:** 1% extra tax depreciation, green financing concessions, mandatory in some states for >10,000 m² plots.
- **Kerala Specifics:** Warm-Humid climate zone → emphasis on **cross-ventilation, low SHGC glazing, high SRI cool roofs, rain water harvesting (1800+ mm annual rainfall).**
- **Always draw a labelled diagram** in 14-mark answers (process flow, comparison table, or category tree).
- **Always write the full form** of any rating system abbreviation on first use.

> **End of Module 4 — Green Building Rating Guidelines | OECET834 — Fundamentals of Building Planning | KTU 2024 Scheme**
